entries.initial_entry: Parent-teacher conferences tomorrow and I keep running through the talking points in my head. Mikkel says I over-prepare but I would rather have every answer ready than stumble in front of someone asking about their child.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if you do stumble?
entries.response_text: Nothing dramatic, I suppose. Anyway, I should probably get some sleep instead of thinking about it.

entries.initial_entry: The school announced budget cuts today and there were some worried faces in the staff room. I felt steady, actually. My position is permanent and I have been here longer than most.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel watching your colleagues worry?
entries.response_text: A bit guilty, honestly. I knew I was safe while some of the newer teachers were clearly doing mental math about their contracts. I offered to help one of them update her CV after work, though I am not sure it made either of us feel better.

entries.initial_entry:
Mikkel brought up the idea of moving to Berlin again at dinner. His company has an office there and he thinks his career would grow faster in a bigger city. We have talked about this before but he seemed more serious this time.

I listed the usual things: our flat is rent-controlled, my job does not transfer, we do not speak German well enough. He said we could figure it out. I said I would think about it but I have been turning it over for hours now and my stomach keeps tightening.
entries.nudge_text: What would you need to feel okay about considering it?
entries.response_text: A guarantee, probably, and I know that does not exist. The truth is I have built everything carefully here: the job, the savings, being close to my parents. Mikkel sees possibility but I see all the things that could collapse. I do not think either of us is wrong. But I also do not know how to bridge the gap between what he wants and what I can actually handle risking.

entries.initial_entry: I don't care what Priya thinks, I handled that IV alarm correctly. The patient was stable and I had already checked the line twice before she came over to "help." I've been on this unit for fourteen months now, I know the protocols.
entries.nudge_text: What made her stepping in feel different from just helping out?
entries.response_text: Because it was in front of the resident. Made me look like I didn't know what I was doing.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the Priya thing from earlier. I know I sounded annoyed in my last entry but there's more to it. She's been a nurse for nine years and sometimes she does this thing where she swoops in and takes over, and the doctors start looking at her for answers instead of me. I worked hard to get assigned to the step-down unit. Most new grads end up on med-surg for at least two years before they get a chance at cardiac, and I pushed for this placement because I want to be in the ICU by the time I'm twenty-five.

But here's what bugs me. When Mr. Okonkwo's wife was crying in the hallway after the cardiologist explained his prognosis, Priya was charting. I was the one who sat with Mrs. Okonkwo for twenty minutes, got her water, called her daughter. Nobody saw that. Nobody writes that in your evaluation. The doctors remember who looked competent with the IV pump, not who held someone's hand. And I did both today, but only one of those things is going to show up when they're deciding who moves to the ICU.

I'm not saying Priya's a bad nurse. She's great, actually. She taught me half of what I know about reading telemetry strips. I just wish the people making decisions about my career could see the full picture instead of whatever three-second snapshot they happened to catch.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned two things you did today — the clinical work and sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo. If you had to pick one to be known for, which would it be?
entries.response_text: Both. That's the whole point. I don't want to pick. But if I'm being honest, the Mrs. Okonkwo thing is the reason I became a nurse in the first place. The ICU stuff, the certifications — that's how you get taken seriously so you can keep doing the other part. At least that's what I tell myself.

entries.initial_entry: Three twelves in a row. Came home, microwaved leftover pasta, opened the CCRN study guide, and fell asleep on page four. Mom called but I let it go to voicemail. I'll call her back tomorrow.

Funny day. Got pulled into a rapid response on 4 North because they were short-staffed and the charge nurse remembered me from orientation. Patient was this 80-year-old guy, former high school football coach, and even while we were stabilizing him he was trying to crack jokes about his offensive line being tougher than his heart. We got him transferred to the ICU and the attending told me I did good work. Wrote it down in my phone immediately so I don't forget the exact words — "good clinical instinct." That's going in my ICU application.

Then I got back to my floor and found out my patient in room 412, Mrs. Patel, had been asking for me the whole time I was gone. She doesn't like the other nurses because she says they talk too fast and don't explain things. I felt bad. I sat with her for an extra ten minutes at the end of my shift even though I was already in overtime. My charge nurse gave me a look but Mrs. Patel smiled and said I remind her of her granddaughter, so whatever.
entries.nudge_text: You stayed late for Mrs. Patel even after the charge nurse's look. What was going through your head in that moment?
entries.response_text: It wasn't a big deal. Ten minutes. She needed someone to listen.

entries.initial_entry:
I'm so mad at myself. Mom needed me to drive her to her follow-up appointment today and I completely forgot because I swapped shifts to cover for Dana, who's going through a divorce and needed someone to take her Saturday. I've been telling everyone yes all week — yes I'll cover your shift, yes I'll precept the new grad, yes I'll sit on the unit council — and then I forgot the one thing that actually mattered.

My brother Tyler had to take the bus from school and then drive her in my car, and he's only had his license for three months. He was fine about it but Mom sounded so tired on the phone when she told me. Not angry, just tired. That's worse. If she yelled at me I could at least argue back but the quiet disappointment just sits in my chest like a rock.

The thing is, I covered for Dana because she was falling apart in the break room and I couldn't just walk away from that. And the new grad, Jess, she literally doesn't know how to titrate a drip yet and if I don't teach her someone's going to get hurt. And the unit council looks good on my resume for the ICU transfer. Every single thing I said yes to had a reason. But all those reasons don't change the fact that my mom sat in a waiting room chair that hurts her hip because I wasn't there.

I need to figure out how to stop doing this. I keep spreading myself across everyone else's emergencies and then there's nothing left for the people who were there first.
entries.nudge_text: You listed the reasons for every yes. Is there one you would take back?
entries.response_text: The unit council. That one was for me, not for anyone else. The rest I'd do again because people needed help. But the council meeting — I could have said next quarter. I just didn't want to lose the spot.

entries.initial_entry:
Called Mom after my shift ended. She said the appointment went fine, the surgeon is happy with her range of motion, and Tyler did a good job driving. She told me not to worry about it.

I ordered grocery delivery to her house for the week and signed up for a meal prep service for Tyler so he doesn't just eat ramen every night. Set a recurring reminder in my phone for all her upcoming appointments. I won't miss another one.

Studied for three hours this morning before my shift. Went through the hemodynamic monitoring chapter and took a practice quiz — scored 82%, which is passing but not where I want to be. My target is 90% on every practice test before I schedule the real exam. I made flashcards for the concepts I missed and I'm going to review them on my breaks this week.

At work, had a good shift. Got assigned to a fresh post-cath patient and managed the sheath pull site without any complications. Documented everything meticulously because I know the ICU charge nurses pull charts when they're reviewing internal transfer candidates. My manager Denise mentioned that there might be an opening on the ICU in March. She didn't promise anything but she said my name had come up.

After work, drove to Mom's even though it wasn't Sunday. Tyler had a rough day at school — some issue with a group project where his partners aren't pulling their weight. I helped him outline a plan for talking to his teacher about it. He's so much like me, takes everything on himself instead of asking for help. I told him it's okay to flag when something isn't working. The irony of giving that advice was not lost on me.
entries.nudge_text: What irony were you thinking of?

entries.initial_entry:
Had coffee with Priya before shift today. She told me she's leaving — transferring to a hospital in Phoenix because her husband got a job out there. I was surprised by how much it bothered me. We're not close exactly, but she's the person I learned the most from on this unit. She said she thinks I'm ready for the ICU and that she told Denise the same thing.

I should feel good about that. And part of me does. But there's this other part that's uneasy. If Priya leaves, I'm suddenly one of the more experienced nurses on the floor, and we have two new grads starting next month. That means more precepting, more questions, more of me being the person everyone leans on. I already can't say no. What happens when there's literally no one else to ask? I keep thinking about last week, forgetting Mom's appointment because I was stretched too thin. If the workload doubles, something is going to break. I just don't know if it'll be my schedule or me.
entries.nudge_text: You said something will break. What would protecting yourself look like?
entries.response_text: I honestly don't know. Saying no, I guess. But saying no to a new grad who doesn't know how to read a rhythm strip feels like putting a patient at risk. And saying no to my family isn't an option. So I don't really know what's left to cut.

entries.initial_entry: CCRN practice test today, scored 88%. Getting closer. Tyler's coming over for dinner tonight, I'm making Mom's enchilada recipe.

Tyler told me over dinner that he's thinking about nursing school. He said he wants to be like me. I had to go to the kitchen for a minute because I didn't want him to see me tear up. It's the best thing anyone has said to me in months and also the thing that scares me the most.

Because what does he see? He sees me working hard, yes. He sees me helping people. But does he also see the dark circles and the missed appointments and the fact that I eat most of my meals standing up in a break room? I want him to have what I have — this feeling of doing something that matters — but I want him to do it without grinding himself down the way I do. I told him I thought it was a great idea and that I'd help him look at programs. And I meant it. I just hope he's better at the balance part than I am.
entries.nudge_text: What would you want him to do differently than you?
entries.response_text: Ask for help before he's drowning. Take a day off without feeling guilty. Remember that being good at your job and being there for your people shouldn't be an either-or thing. Basically everything I know I should do but don't.

entries.initial_entry: I don't care what Priya thinks, I handled that IV alarm correctly. The patient was stable and I had already checked the line twice before she came over to "help." I've been on this unit for fourteen months now, I know the protocols.
entries.nudge_text: What made her stepping in feel different from just helping out?
entries.response_text: Because it was in front of the resident. Made me look like I didn't know what I was doing.

entries.response_text: Ask for help before he's drowning. Take a day off without feeling guilty. Remember that being good at your job and being there for your people shouldn't be an either-or thing. Basically everything I know I should do but don't.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a call with Sami from the Aqaba branch about the delayed container shipments. Third time this month. He kept circling around the issue without giving me a straight answer about what went wrong on their end. I told him I need the incident report by Thursday and he agreed, but I could tell he was just saying what I wanted to hear.

Spent most of the afternoon reviewing Q1 numbers with Dina. She's been solid this quarter — her team's turnaround times are the best in the division. Told her I'd put her forward for the efficiency award at the next leadership meeting. She seemed surprised, which is odd. She should know she's earned it.

Picked up the girls from piano. Layla has her recital next week and she's been anxious about it — keeps saying she'll mess up the second movement. Sat with her after dinner and ran through the piece twice. She nailed it both times. She's better than she thinks she is but she won't believe me when I tell her.
entries.nudge_text: What's actually going on in Aqaba?
entries.response_text: Honestly I think Sami's lost control of his warehouse team. Two of his senior guys left in January and he promoted people who weren't ready. But if I say that out loud it becomes my problem to fix, and I've got enough on my plate.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time opening this today. Just got off the phone with my mother. She wanted to know if Fadi is "settling in okay" which is her way of asking whether I'm watching out for him. He's fine. More than fine — he closed the Zarqa supplier deal on his own last week and didn't even tell anyone until the contract was signed. I called him into my office this morning to tell him I noticed. The look on his face. Like he didn't expect anyone in management to actually see what he's doing day to day.

That's the thing that gets me. I remember being twenty-seven at my first real job and my manager barely knew my name. I told myself if I ever ran a team I'd make sure people felt seen. Not in some corporate poster way but actually noticed. Fadi's doing good work and he should hear it from someone who matters in the building.

Noor texted me a photo of Rania's math test — ninety-eight percent. Already thinking about how to celebrate at dinner. Maybe we do kunafa from Abu Salem's.
entries.nudge_text: Why does that matter so much to you?
entries.response_text: Because nobody did it for me. I had to figure everything out alone when I started. My first manager couldn't even remember which project I was on. I just don't want that for people under me.

entries.initial_entry: Got an email from HR about the VP role. They want me to "onboard" the new guy next week. Show him around, introduce him to the team leads. My team.

People keep asking me how I feel about the VP situation like they expect me to be bitter about it. I'm not bitter. The man hasn't started yet and I'm already being asked to hold his hand through orientation. Fine. I'll do it. I'll do it well because that's what professionals do and because everyone will be watching how I handle it.

Had the onboarding meeting today. His name is Khalil. MBA from London, five years at Maersk. He's polished. Asked good questions. I answered them thoroughly and introduced him to every team lead personally. Dina told me afterward she was impressed by how generous I was with my time. That word — generous. I don't know if that's what it was. I know how things work. The person who trains the new boss controls the narrative.

Fadi called me tonight to ask about a contract clause he wasn't sure about. Spent twenty minutes walking him through it. He thanked me three times. I told him to stop thanking me and just get it right.
entries.nudge_text: Was it generous or strategic?
entries.response_text: Both. I genuinely want the transition to go well for the company. But I'm not naive — if I'm the one who made Khalil's first week smooth, that positions me a certain way. I don't see anything wrong with that.

entries.initial_entry: Friday at home. Noor's at the clinic for a half day so I took the girls to Teta's house. My mother made mansaf and spent the whole lunch telling Rania she needs to eat more.

Khalil scheduled a "division alignment" meeting for Monday without telling me first. Found out from Dina who got the calendar invite. She assumed I already knew. I didn't. It's a small thing but it's the kind of small thing that tells you how someone sees the org chart.

Had lunch with Amjad from finance. He mentioned casually that there are rumors about restructuring — possibly merging operations and logistics under Khalil. I kept my face neutral but inside something tightened. I've built this team from twelve people to forty-two. The processes, the KPI framework, the vendor relationships — those are mine.

Came home and Noor could tell something was off. She asked and I said work stuff, the usual. She didn't push. She knows when to give me room. After the girls went to bed I sat on the balcony for a while thinking about what I'd actually do if they folded my division. Started going through my LinkedIn for the first time in months. Not seriously. Just looking.
entries.nudge_text: How serious is the LinkedIn thing really?
entries.response_text: It's not serious yet. I'm not updating my profile or messaging anyone. But I want to know what's out there in case I need to move fast. I refuse to be caught off guard.

entries.initial_entry:
Fadi came to me today looking shaken. Khalil pulled him into a meeting and started questioning the Zarqa deal — asking why procurement didn't go through the new approval workflow that apparently Khalil set up without consulting me or my team. Fadi didn't know what to say. He did everything by the book, by the process that existed when he closed the deal.

I went to Khalil's office and told him directly that if he's changing procurement workflows he needs to loop in operations first because my people are the ones executing these contracts. He smiled and said he appreciated the feedback. Appreciated the feedback. Like I'm filing a suggestion at a hotel.

I'm angry but what's worse is I feel like I let Fadi down. He came to me because he trusts me and I brought him into this company. I should have anticipated that Khalil would start making changes without understanding the existing structure. I should have shielded him from this. Now Fadi's second-guessing himself over a deal he should be proud of.
entries.nudge_text: Could you have actually prevented that?
entries.response_text: Probably not. Khalil was going to assert himself no matter what. But I could have warned Fadi, prepared him for the possibility that new leadership means new rules. I just didn't think it would happen this fast.

entries.initial_entry: Layla asked me at breakfast why I've been "grumpy." I told her I wasn't grumpy, just busy. But she's right. I've been bringing it home and that's not fair to them.

The restructuring rumor turned out to be exactly that — a rumor. CEO called me in on Thursday and told me operations stays independent, reporting to him, same as before. He also said he relies on my institutional knowledge and wants me on the quarterly strategy committee starting next month. I nodded, said thank you, kept it professional. Walked out of his office and called Noor immediately.

Took the whole family to Abu Salem's on Friday night to celebrate — didn't tell the girls why, just said we deserved it. Fadi texted me that evening asking for advice on a lease negotiation for his apartment. Nothing to do with work. I spent thirty minutes on the phone with him going through the clauses. Noor rolled her eyes but she gets it. Family is family, even at nine p.m. on a Friday.

My mother-in-law rearranged Sami's entire nap schedule without asking me. She told Tariq it was better for the baby to sleep earlier so she could take him to the park in the afternoon. Nobody asked what I thought. I had finally gotten him into a rhythm that gave me two hours in the evening to work on a logo project. That's gone now.

I stood there in the kitchen holding a cup of tea and I just didn't say anything. Tariq looked at me like he expected me to agree. His mother was already moving the crib blanket to the other room. I could feel my jaw clenching but I smiled. I hate that I smiled. Something about the way everyone just decided felt like they forgot I'm his mother, not a guest in this arrangement.
entries.nudge_text: You smiled but didn't want to. What stopped you?

entries.initial_entry: Sami had a fever all night. I was up every two hours. Tariq slept through it. His mother came in at 5am and took over, told me to rest. I should be grateful but it felt like being dismissed. I'm the one who knows what dose of medicine to give, what temperature means we go to the clinic. I didn't rest.
entries.nudge_text: What would the right kind of help look like?
entries.response_text: If she had asked. Just asked. "Can I sit with him so you can sleep?" instead of walking in and lifting him out of my arms. It's not the help. It's that nobody checks if I want it first.

entries.initial_entry:
Sami's fever broke. Normal day otherwise. Did laundry, made lentil soup, swept the hallway because nobody else notices the dust. Tariq came home and asked what's for dinner. I said soup. He said his mother already made something. So the soup is in the fridge now, I guess.

Finished a draft of the logo I've been working on. The client wants revisions but at least the base concept is solid. I had to work on it in the bathroom because Sami was asleep in the bedroom and the living room was occupied. Sat on the edge of the bathtub with my laptop balanced on the hamper. It's ridiculous but it's the only place I can close a door without anyone asking what I'm doing.

Can barely keep my eyes open. Sami is teething and it's been three nights of broken sleep. I had to email the client and ask for an extension on the logo revisions. Felt pathetic typing that out. My mother called to say my cousin Rania is getting engaged and when am I bringing Sami to visit. Everything requires something from me and I have nothing left to give right now.

Eid preparations are consuming the entire house. Tariq's mother has been cooking since dawn. She made ma'moul and knafeh and keeps sending me to the store for things she forgot. I don't mind the errands but she keeps correcting how I arrange the pastries on the tray. Told me that's not how they do it in their family. It's been two years, I still arrange things wrong apparently.

Tariq gave me money to buy a new outfit for Eid. I picked something I liked -- a dark green dress with embroidery on the sleeves. When I showed it to his mother she said it was pretty but maybe something more "appropriate for a young mother" would be better. I don't even know what that means. I put the dress in my closet anyway. I'll wear it.

My own mother sent a voice note about how she wishes I could spend Eid at their house this year. First Eid is always with the husband's family, she knows that, but she said it anyway. So now I feel guilty about being here and guilty about not being there. Sami won't remember any of this. I'm doing all of it for people who have opinions about trays and dresses.
entries.nudge_text: You said you'll wear the dress anyway. What's riding on that?
entries.response_text: It's just a dress. I liked it, I bought it, I'll wear it. Not everything has to be a statement.

entries.initial_entry: Later. Wore the dress. Tariq said I looked nice. His mother didn't comment. My sister-in-law Huda actually asked where I got it. Sami grabbed the embroidery and pulled a thread loose, which honestly made me laugh. Small thing but I felt like myself for the first time in weeks. I held Sami on my hip and I felt steady.

Got the payment for the logo project. Not a lot -- 45 JD -- but I earned it. Put it in the envelope I keep in my underwear drawer. I have about 200 JD saved now. I don't know what I'm saving for exactly. Maybe a better laptop. Maybe just proof that I can do something that isn't cooking and wiping noses.

Tariq doesn't know about the envelope. I'm not hiding it because I think he'd be angry, more because he'd want to "help manage it" and then it wouldn't feel like mine anymore. My father always handled my mother's money. She'd get her teacher's salary and hand it over and he'd decide what the household needed. She never complained about it. I think about that a lot. I don't want to not complain about it. I want a reason not to need to.
entries.nudge_text: What changes if Tariq finds that envelope?
entries.response_text: He'd probably be fine with it. Supportive even. But then it becomes a shared thing, a family decision. Right now it's the only thing in this house that belongs to just me. I'm not ready to give that up. Maybe that's selfish. I don't care.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Sami to the park near the mosque this morning. He's getting better at walking on his own, barely needs my hand now. He kept crouching down to look at ants. We stayed for almost two hours. I didn't bring my phone on purpose.

Ran into Um Fadi from the building. She asked if I was working these days, meaning a real job. I said no, just home with Sami. She nodded the way people nod when they think they're being polite but their face says everything. I used to let that bother me. Today I just picked Sami up and pointed at a bird and we moved on.

Tariq's mother made mansaf for dinner and I helped her roll the jameed. She showed me her mother's technique with the wooden spoon and for once it wasn't corrective, it was like she was sharing something. I actually paid attention. Her hands move fast for someone her age. The rice came out perfectly and she said "you're learning" and I think she meant it warmly. These moments confuse me because they make me forget why I'm frustrated. Or maybe they're exactly why I stay patient through the rest of it.

Friday. Tariq took the day off and we went to his parents' village with Sami to visit his grandfather. The old house has a courtyard with a lemon tree that Tariq's grandmother planted. His grandfather told Sami the same story he tells everyone about building the house stone by stone. Sami just chewed on a lemon leaf.

I sat with the women while the men did their thing. Tariq's aunt made sage tea the old way, on a brass tray over coals. It was good. Quiet. I sketched some patterns on a napkin -- the tile work in the courtyard is beautiful, these geometric interlocking shapes I've been wanting to use in a design project. Nobody noticed me drawing, which was the whole point.

New client reached out through Instagram. Wants a logo for a women's fitness studio in West Amman. Paying 80 JD. I said yes before thinking about when I'd actually do the work. Sami's not sleeping well again and the house is never empty. But 80 JD is 80 JD. I'll figure out the hours somehow. I always do.
entries.nudge_text: You keep saying you'll figure it out. Figure out what exactly?
entries.response_text: How to be two people at once. The one who takes care of Sami and the house and keeps everyone comfortable. And the one who wants to make things and get paid for it and have something that's mine. There's no schedule that fits both. I just keep squeezing one into the gaps of the other.

entries.initial_entry:
Tariq and I had an actual conversation last night. Not a fight. He asked why I've been staying up so late and I almost lied but I was too tired to come up with something so I told him about the freelance work. All of it. The clients, the envelope, the bathroom laptop sessions.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said he wished I'd told him sooner. Not because he wanted to control it but because he felt stupid not knowing. That stung. I told him it wasn't about trust, it was about needing something that was just mine. He said he understood that but it still felt like I didn't trust him. I don't know if he's wrong.

We didn't resolve anything exactly. He said he'd talk to his mother about giving me more space during the day so I don't have to hide. I told him I don't want him to fight that battle for me. He looked confused. I think he genuinely doesn't understand why I'd rather struggle alone than have him fix it. Honestly, I'm not totally sure I understand it either. But the thought of his mother knowing about the work and having opinions about it makes my chest tight. Some things I need to handle my own way, even if my way is harder.
entries.nudge_text: He offered help and you said no. What are you protecting?
entries.response_text: Can we not do this right now. I already spent all night turning this over in my head. I know it doesn't make total sense. I just need to sit with it for a bit.

entries.initial_entry: Same day, later. Tariq's mother asked me at dinner if everything was okay between us. She must have heard us talking last night. I said everything was fine. Tariq backed me up. She didn't push it. But the way she looked at me -- not unkind, just knowing -- made me feel exposed. I keep building walls in a house made of thin ones.

Got back from the workshop an hour ago and I'm still thinking about the order we took this morning. A couple from Ghent wants a set of tiles for their renovated kitchen -- they want something "modern but with a nod to Flemish blue." I know exactly what Opa would do with it. I know exactly what I'd do with it too, and they're not the same thing.

I spent the afternoon sketching options. The ones that feel most like me don't really look like what the shop is known for. The ones that look like the shop feel like I'm just tracing someone else's hand. Maybe there's a middle ground but I haven't found it today.
entries.nudge_text: What part of the shop's style feels most distant from your own?
entries.response_text: It's less about the style and more about -- I don't know, I'll figure it out when I start painting.

entries.initial_entry:
Finished the Ghent tile set. Went with a design that layers the old Delft patterning under more fluid brushwork -- sort of like the heritage is showing through underneath. Opa looked at them for a long time and then said "that's not bad." From him that's practically a standing ovation.

Choir rehearsal Wednesday went well. We're preparing pieces for the Ascension service in late May. Meneer Peeters gave me a small solo line in the Lassus motet which felt good. I've been singing these pieces since I was fourteen and there's something about the way the harmonies move that I can't get from any other kind of music. It's not about performing, it's about being part of something that's been going for hundreds of years.

Also got an email from a gallery in Brussels that saw my portfolio online. They want to talk about a group show in September. I should be thrilled but I feel weirdly hesitant, like showing the abstract work publicly might make it harder to keep one foot in the workshop world.
entries.nudge_text: What would change at the workshop if the gallery show happened?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing concrete, it's more a feeling. Let me think about it.

entries.initial_entry: Long day at the church restoration. The plaster on the south wall is in worse shape than the diocese estimated so the timeline is blown. Came home and Opa asked when the next batch of souvenir tiles would be ready and I just didn't have it in me to answer.

Writing again because I can't sleep. I know Opa wasn't trying to pressure me, he was just asking a normal question about the business. But it landed wrong. I'm stretched between the restoration job, the workshop orders, and now this Brussels thing, and I feel like everyone needs a piece of me that I haven't figured out yet.

My sister sent a photo of some mass-produced "Flemish style" coasters she saw at a tourist shop near the Markt. She thought it was funny. I didn't. That's what happens when the craft becomes just a product -- the hands behind it disappear. Maybe that's what bothers me about the gallery too, turning something personal into something on display for strangers to consume.
entries.nudge_text: Tourist coasters and a Brussels gallery feel different from the outside. Do they feel different to you?
entries.response_text: Yeah, I know they're different. The coasters erase the maker. The gallery at least credits you. I think what scares me is that once the work is out there, I can't control what it means to people.

entries.initial_entry: Workshop all day, filled six orders. Hands ache. Opa showed me a photo of his father working at the same bench in 1952 and I couldn't tell you what I felt exactly but it was a lot.

Called the Brussels gallery back today. Told them I'm interested but need to see the space first before committing. The curator was friendly, said the theme is "inheritance and rupture" which is almost too on the nose for my situation. She asked if my work engages with craft heritage and I said yes before I'd really thought about whether that's how I want it framed.

After the call I went to Opa's room to tell him. He nodded and said something about how his uncle once turned down a commission from a department store because he didn't want to see the family name on something cheap. I think he was trying to tell me he understands being careful about where the work goes, in his own sideways way.

I keep waiting for someone to tell me it's fine to do both -- the workshop and the gallery, the old patterns and the new ones. Nobody's stopping me except maybe myself. But the art world wants you to pick a lane and the family just wants the shop to keep going, and I'm twenty-two and trying to hold all of it.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'doing both' actually look like week to week?
entries.response_text: I don't know yet. Maybe I'll figure it out after I see the gallery space.

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because something small happened. Was cleaning brushes after the evening session and put on the Lassus recording from our choir rehearsal. Started humming my solo line and Opa came in and sat down in the corner chair without saying anything. He just listened for a while.

When the piece ended he told me my grandmother used to sing that same motet at Sint-Salvatorskathedraal before I was born. I'd never heard that before. It felt like finding a thread I didn't know was there, connecting her to me through the same music, the same stone walls.

Visited the Brussels gallery space today. It's beautiful -- high ceilings, raw concrete, very contemporary. Standing in it I felt like my workshop pieces would look out of place and my abstract work would look like it's pretending to be something it's not.
entries.nudge_text: What would your work be pretending to be in that space?
entries.response_text: Pretending to be detached from where it comes from, maybe. I don't know, it's hard to put into words.

entries.initial_entry:
Church restoration is eating all my daylight hours. The diocese wants the south wall done before Ascension and the plaster situation means I'm basically re-doing the ground layer from scratch. My shoulders are wrecked and I keep getting home too late to work on my own pieces.

Had a thought while I was up on the scaffold today: the anonymous craftsmen who painted these murals five hundred years ago never worried about whether their work belonged in a gallery. They just made what the community needed. Part of me envies that clarity and part of me knows I'd suffocate in it. That's the whole problem, isn't it.
Opa asked if I've decided about the Brussels show. I said I'm leaning yes. He said "good, your grandmother would have liked that." Didn't expect that at all.
entries.nudge_text: You sounded surprised by his reaction. What were you expecting him to say?
entries.response_text: I think I was expecting him to say the shop needs me here. Maybe I even wanted him to, so I'd have an excuse not to go. Hearing him connect it to Oma instead -- that knocked something loose.

entries.initial_entry:
Ascension service rehearsal today. Sang the Lassus and it went well but afterwards Meneer Peeters said the choir might switch to a more contemporary repertoire next season because attendance is dropping. A few of us pushed back, said the old music is the reason some of us are still here. He looked tired and said he'd think about it.

I don't understand why everything has to be either/or. Why does updating mean throwing out what came before? The whole conversation put me in a bad mood and I skipped the coffee hour after, which I never do. Mama will probably hear about that and ask what's wrong.

Finished the south wall restoration yesterday, just under the wire before Ascension. Stood back and looked at it for a long time. The colours are brighter now but the composition is the same one some painter laid down centuries ago. I added nothing of my own and that felt right for this particular work. There's a kind of discipline in faithful restoration that I'm starting to respect more than I used to.

Told the Brussels curator I'll do the show. I'm going to bring pieces that sit between the two worlds -- the layered tile work like the Ghent set, where the old patterning bleeds through into something new. Not pure abstraction, not pure heritage, but the conversation between them. She seemed genuinely interested.

Opa helped me crate the Ghent samples this evening. He wrapped each tile in newspaper the way he's done it for fifty years, slow and precise. Didn't say much. Didn't need to. I think he knows this is me trying to carry the thing forward, not away.
entries.nudge_text: A month ago the gallery felt threatening. What shifted?
entries.response_text: Opa's blessing, mostly. And finishing the church wall. I'll think about it more later.

entries.initial_entry:
Today was my parents' 40th wedding anniversary, and I spent most of the afternoon at their villa helping coordinate everything. My mother had very specific ideas about the flower arrangements and the seating chart — cousin Layla absolutely cannot be seated near aunt Fatima after what happened at Eid — and I found myself nodding along, rearranging the entire dining room twice to meet her vision.

The funny thing is, I didn't mind. There's something calming about fitting into the role everyone expects. My father pulled me aside after the guests arrived and thanked me for "being the reliable one," and I felt this warmth in my chest. My younger brother Samir flew in from London but spent most of the party on his phone, and I could see the disappointment in my mother's eyes. I don't want to be the source of that look.

But there was this moment, late in the evening, when the older relatives were doing their formal toasts, and I looked across the room at my wife Nadia. She caught my eye and did this little mock-yawn behind her napkin, and I had to bite my cheek not to laugh. We'd both rather have been at that new seafood place on the Palm with a bottle of wine, just the two of us. Instead, I sat through two hours of my uncle's stories about the old days.

I think I'm okay with it. The party was beautiful. Everyone was happy. And Nadia and I are going to that restaurant next weekend anyway — I already made the reservation. You can do both, I think. Be the person your family needs and still carve out space for the things that make life sweet.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. We had our quarterly review today and Hamza — my direct report who I've mentored for two years — threw me under the bus in front of the regional director. He presented his supply chain optimization idea as if it was entirely his own, when we literally developed it together over coffee last month. I was the one who told him to run the numbers on the Oman route!

The worst part is I just sat there. Smiled. Nodded along. When Ahmed, my boss, looked at me after the presentation, I said something supportive about Hamza's initiative. What else was I supposed to do? Create a scene? Interrupt him mid-presentation to claim credit? That's not how things are done. You don't embarrass a colleague publicly, especially one you outrank. It would look petty. Undignified.

But God, I wanted to. I wanted to say, "Actually, that was our idea, developed during our Tuesday coffees." Instead I shook his hand after and told him he did well. He had the grace to look slightly uncomfortable, at least.

Nadia says I should have spoken up privately to Ahmed afterward. She's probably right. But I keep thinking about how my father handled workplace conflicts — with patience, with letting things play out, with trusting that people eventually see the truth. Maybe Hamza will acknowledge it later. Maybe Ahmed already knows whose brain that idea came from.

Or maybe I'm just telling myself stories to feel better about being a coward. I don't know. I do know that I came home and ate an entire box of those pistachio sweets from that place in Sharjah, and I don't regret it one bit. Sometimes you just need something good after a bad day.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned Nadia's suggestion and your father's approach as two different paths. Which one feels more true to who you want to be?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I think I'm overthinking this. It's one meeting. Hamza is young, he got excited about presenting something that worked. The idea will get implemented either way, and that's what matters for the team. I'd rather focus on the fact that we're on track for a really good Q4, and I'm taking Nadia to Bahrain for New Year's. Some things just aren't worth the energy of picking apart.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep. It's 2am and I'm sitting on the balcony with my laptop because Nadia's breathing is usually soothing but tonight it's just reminding me that everyone else can rest except me.

My mother called today and mentioned — casually, as if she was discussing the weather — that cousin Layla's husband just got made partner at his firm and isn't that wonderful, they're buying a bigger house in Abu Dhabi. Then she asked when Nadia and I are going to "settle down properly" and have children. I'm 41. We've been married six years. She knows we've been trying. She knows about the appointments with Dr. Khalil. But she still asks, every single time, like maybe if she reminds me enough, my body will cooperate.

I didn't say anything. I made the appropriate congratulatory noises about Layla's husband. I changed the subject to the marina and the boat engine I'm having fixed.

What I wanted to say was: we might not be able to have children, and it's breaking Nadia apart, and I feel like a failure every time she cries in the bathroom thinking I can't hear her. But you don't burden your mother with things like that. You don't make her worry. You don't disrupt the pleasant fiction that everything is fine and her eldest son has everything figured out.

The thing is, I don't even know if I want children anymore. Is that terrible? I used to. I had the whole picture in my head — teaching my son to fish, taking my daughter to her first day of school. But lately I've been thinking about what our life looks like without them. The freedom. The travel. The late Sunday mornings. Is that selfish? It feels selfish. But it also feels honest.

I should go back to bed. Nadia's got that look lately when I stay up too late, like she's worried about me. I don't want to give her another thing to worry about.
entries.nudge_text: You described the picture you used to have and the one you're starting to imagine now. What would it take to talk about this with Nadia?
entries.response_text: That's the thing — I'm not sure I can. We've never actually said out loud that it might not happen. We talk around it. We talk about appointments, and procedures, and "next steps." But we don't talk about what it means if there are no next steps. I think I'm afraid that if I tell her I'm starting to be okay with the idea of just us, she'll think I've given up on her. On us. On the future she wanted. And I haven't given up. I'm just... adapting? Protecting myself maybe? I don't know. I think I need to figure out what I actually feel before I bring it to her. Right now it's all just noise in my head at 2am.

entries.initial_entry: Bahrain trip was good. Nadia and I finally talked about the fertility stuff. She cried but also seemed sort of relieved, I think? We're going to take a break from the treatments for a few months.
entries.nudge_text: That sounds like a meaningful conversation to have had together. How are you feeling now that it's out in the open?
entries.response_text: You know, mostly I'm just glad to be back at work tomorrow. The holiday period always feels long. We have the Q1 planning meeting on Thursday and I want to make sure the Oman route data is properly credited this time. Fresh start to the year.

entries.initial_entry:
Got home late from school and Leticia told me the water heater is making that clicking sound again. I went down to check and the pilot light was fine, but the tank had some rust along the bottom edge. Called Chema, our plumber, and he said he could come look at it Saturday. That is four days away.

I keep thinking about it. The thing is fourteen years old and I have been patching it along because replacing it is at least twelve thousand pesos we were not planning to spend this month. Sofia needs new uniform shoes and Valentina has her graduation fees coming up. I told Leticia we would figure it out but I could not stop checking the heater before bed, like staring at it would help anything. Too tired to think straight.
entries.nudge_text: What's the worst part — the heater or the money?

entries.initial_entry:
Cannot sleep. Leticia is out cold and I am sitting in the kitchen with the lights off because I did not want to wake anyone. I keep going over the numbers in my head. The aguinaldo comes in a couple of weeks but I already had that earmarked for Valentina's graduation expenses and the car insurance renewal in January. If the heater goes, I either pull from the emergency fund or I take on a Saturday tutoring group again, which I swore I would stop doing because it was eating into the only time I have with the girls.

What bothers me is that I should have replaced it two years ago when Chema first mentioned the corrosion. It would have been easier then. The repair fund had more in it and we did not have the graduation costs. I kept telling myself one more year, one more year. Now I am sitting here at midnight doing math on the back of a receipt.

Leticia would say I worry too much. Maybe. But somebody has to keep track of these things or they pile up until you cannot move. My father never had a plan for anything and I spent half my childhood watching my mother scramble. I will not put my family through that. I just wish planning ahead did not feel like carrying rocks uphill sometimes.
entries.nudge_text: When you say carrying rocks — is this the same weight your mom carried?
entries.response_text: Different rocks, same hill maybe. She was reacting to problems that already exploded. I am trying to stop them before they start. But the feeling of never being able to rest, of always watching for the next thing that could go wrong — yes, that part is probably the same. I never thought about it that way until just now.

entries.initial_entry:
Good day actually. Chema came early, turns out he had a cancellation, and the heater just needs a new thermocouple and some descaling. Eight hundred pesos. I could have kissed the man. Leticia made him a plate of pozole and he sat with us for a bit talking about his nephew who just started at the school.

Walked to the market with the girls after. Valentina tried on shoes at Don Beto's stall, the one where we always go because he gives the teachers a discount. Found a pair that fits her. Came home and sat on the patio while Leticia watered the plants. One of those afternoons where everything just fits into place and you do not have to push anything along.
The principal announced they are restructuring the math department next semester. Two positions may be consolidated. I have seniority but nothing official was said about who stays or goes.
entries.nudge_text: What went through your head when you heard that?
entries.response_text: Honestly my first thought was whether nineteen years counts for anything anymore. My second was checking how much we have in savings. Then I thought about Valentina's graduation in March and how I cannot afford any disruption right now. I did not say any of this out loud at the meeting. I just nodded and asked when we would get more details.

entries.initial_entry:
Leticia thinks I am overreacting about the department restructuring. She says my contract is solid and the union would never let them cut someone with my tenure. She is probably right. But she was not in that meeting. She did not see how the principal avoided eye contact with Profesora Marta, who has been there almost as long as I have and teaches the same subjects.

A colleague, Héctor, told me I should use this as leverage to finally apply to the Tec de Monterrey satellite campus. Better pay, better facilities. He has been saying this for years. I told him I did not want to start over at fifty-one, learning a new system, new administration, new commute. He laughed and said I was going to die at that desk. Maybe he is right too. But I would rather know what is coming than gamble on something looking better from the outside.

My mother called tonight and I did not tell her about any of it because she would worry. Told her the girls are doing well, which is true. Sofia got the highest mark in her English class. I held onto that for a few minutes after we hung up. Some things are solid. I just need the rest to stop shifting.
entries.nudge_text: Héctor's idea bothers you — is it because it's bad advice or because part of you agrees?
entries.response_text: Both, and that is the problem. I know the pay would be better and the school is newer. But I also know that private institutions restructure all the time and there is no union. At least here I have protections. The thing is, if I had applied five years ago when things were calmer, it would have been a measured decision. Now it would feel like running away from a fire, and decisions made while running rarely end well. So I stay and I wait for more information. That is not overreacting. That is just being careful.

entries.initial_entry:
The restructuring memo came out today. My position is safe. They are merging the two junior algebra sections and cutting one adjunct contract. I should feel relieved and I do, mostly. But I also feel something else I cannot name properly. Like I spent ten days bracing for impact and now that it did not come I do not know what to do with all the tension I was holding.

Héctor congratulated me and brought it up again about applying to the Tec. I told him I would think about it in the summer when things are quieter. He gave me that look like he knows I will not. And maybe he is right. But I also noticed I did not say no this time. I said later. I am not sure what the difference is yet but it felt like something shifted, even if just a little.

Started prepping canvases for the November show this morning. Same dimensions I always use for the parish hall walls -- 80 by 120, because the hooks are fixed in place and nobody is going to drill new ones into century-old plaster. There's something steadying about knowing exactly where each piece will hang before I've even mixed a color.

Spent the afternoon sketching from the bridge near Engis. The light this time of year turns the river into something between pewter and bronze, and I keep trying to pin it down the way Grand-mere did in her later work. I'm not sure I've gotten it right yet. Maybe I never will, but the attempt itself feels like the point. Brought the kids after school and let them toss stones while I worked. Ordinary afternoon.
entries.nudge_text: What does getting it 'right' mean to you here?
entries.response_text: Probably less about the painting and more about the river itself.

entries.initial_entry:
Parish hall booking confirmed for November 15-16. Same weekend as last year. Father Renaud said they're replacing the radiators in the vestibule, but the main hall should be unaffected. Spoke with Maman about the catering -- she'll do the tarte al djote again and Tante Sylvie offered to bring the boulets. This is the seventh year running we've kept to the same menu.

The framer in Jupille finished mounting three pieces. I went to collect them and he'd used the same molding profile Grand-mere specified in her notebook -- simple oak, no gilding. I didn't ask him to match it; he just remembered from previous years. Small thing, but it matters. There's a continuity in those details that makes the whole event feel less like something I organize and more like something that carries itself forward.

Spent the evening sorting through old catalogues from Maman's early exhibitions. Found a small pencil sketch she'd tucked inside the 1988 programme -- a quick study of the church door. I might frame it and include it in my show as a kind of anchor piece. Not sure yet. Need to ask her first.
entries.nudge_text: Why does asking her first feel necessary?

entries.initial_entry:
A gallerist from Brussels came by the studio yesterday. She'd seen some photos online and wanted to know if I'd be interested in showing at her space in Ixelles. Bigger audience, better lighting, actual opening night press. She said the parish hall thing was "charming" but suggested I was limiting my reach.

I know she meant it as a compliment, or at least as professional advice. But I didn't ask for professional advice. The November show isn't about reach. It's about the fact that people from the neighborhood come back each year and some of them remember Grand-mere's work on those same walls. That means something that a Brussels gallery opening with wine and small talk cannot replicate. I told her I'd think about it, but I already know the answer.

My daughter asked why we always have to go to Papy and Mamy's on Sundays. She wants to do football practice with her friends instead. I didn't have a good answer -- just that we go because we've always gone. Hearing it out loud, it sounded hollow even to me.

Still turning over what Elise said this morning. She's nine. She doesn't understand yet why we keep these rhythms, and I shouldn't expect her to. But the question caught me off guard because I've never had to justify it before. To anyone. Not even to myself.

My partner mentioned, gently, that maybe the Sunday lunches could shift to every other week. That the kids are getting older and their schedules are filling up. He wasn't attacking anything -- he was being practical. But it felt like the start of something eroding. If we skip one week, then two, then the meal becomes a monthly obligation that people rearrange their calendars around rather than something that simply is.

I know that's not rational. Things have to bend sometimes. I just don't want to be the one who let them break. Three generations of Sunday lunches in that kitchen. My grandfather built the long table specifically because he wanted everyone to sit together. It wasn't incidental. It was a decision, and decisions like that deserve to be honored even when they stop being convenient.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'letting them break' actually look like?
entries.response_text: Empty chairs. The table feeling too big. Maman cooking for two instead of twelve.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to the studio early. Needed to just paint without thinking about schedules or compromises. Worked on the largest canvas for the show -- the one based on the view from the mill bridge. It's the same view Grand-mere painted in 1971, and Maman in 1994, and now me. Three versions of the same bend in the river, each a little different because the trees change and the banks shift, but the bones of it stay.

A friend who paints digitally told me last week that I should try working on a tablet. Faster, cheaper, infinite undo. I told him I like the smell of linseed oil and the resistance of the canvas. He laughed like I was joking. I wasn't. The materials matter. The process matters. You don't inherit a craft by switching to whatever's newest. You inherit it by keeping your hands in the same medium.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the craft you're keeping, or something else?
entries.response_text: Both. The craft and the line of people who practiced it before me.

entries.initial_entry: We went to Papy and Mamy's yesterday. Elise came without complaint but spent most of the meal drawing on a napkin instead of talking. I watched her and wondered if I'm passing something down or just dragging her through motions that only mean something to me.

Took the finished canvases to the parish hall for a test hang. The light from the west window hit the river painting exactly the way it hits Grand-mere's piece in the catalogue photo from 1978. Same angle, same hour, same wall. Some things just line up when you let them.

Exhibition prep is wearing me thin. Maman called to say she might not do the tarte this year -- her hands are bothering her and she doesn't want to push it. I told her it's fine, but after I hung up I just sat in the studio staring at nothing for a while.
entries.nudge_text: What were you sitting with in that silence?

entries.initial_entry:
Twelve-hour shift again. My feet hurt and my back is screaming at me. There was a boy today, maybe seven years old, admitted with pneumonia. His mother was alone with him — the father works in Abu Dhabi and couldn't come. She looked so frightened sitting by his bed, not understanding what the doctors were telling her. I stayed past my shift to explain everything to her in Arabic, to help her fill out forms, to just sit with her while her son slept.

My own dinner went cold. Khalid texted me three times asking when I would be home. I could hear the frustration in his words even through the phone. But how do I leave a terrified mother alone? How do I explain that to him?

I am tired in my bones. Not just from standing all day but from carrying everyone's worry. Sometimes I wonder if there is anything left of me at the end of the day, or if I have given it all away.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned Khalid's frustration — what do you imagine he was feeling when he sent those texts?
entries.response_text:
He was worried, I think. Not angry. Khalid has been married to me for twenty-three years — he knows who I am. But lately I have noticed something in his eyes. A tiredness that mirrors my own. Last week he said, "The children are grown now, Fatima. When will it be our turn?"

I did not have an answer for him. I still do not. Because every time I think I can step back, there is another mother with frightened eyes, another child reaching for my hand. And I cannot make myself walk away from them. But I am also afraid that one day I will look up and find that Khalid has stopped waiting.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday. My day off. I woke up early anyway — the habit of years — and made mansaf for the whole family. Layla came home from medical school for the weekend, and Yousef brought his girlfriend Sarah to meet us for the first time. An American girl, very polite but clearly nervous. I watched my mother-in-law's face when Yousef introduced her. That slight tightening around her eyes. But then Sarah complimented her embroidered thobe, asked about the patterns, and I saw my mother-in-law soften.

The house was loud and full all day. Arguments about politics, laughter over old photos, my youngest Nadia showing everyone videos on her phone. At one point I stood in the kitchen doorway just watching them all. Khalid caught my eye and smiled at me across the room.

This is what I work for. This noise, this chaos, this fullness. When I am at the hospital giving pieces of myself to strangers' children, I am also doing it for my own. I want Layla to see that a woman can have both purpose and family. I want Yousef to know that love means showing up even when you are tired. I want Nadia to understand that our home will always be open, always warm.

My mother used to say: "A woman's heart is like a well. The more you draw from it, the more water rises." I did not understand her when I was young. I thought she was saying women should give endlessly. Now I think she meant something else — that love is not a finite resource. It grows when you share it. The exhaustion comes not from giving too much, but from forgetting to drink from the well yourself.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'drinking from the well yourself' look like for you?
entries.response_text:
I think that is a question I am still learning to answer. My sister Reem keeps telling me to take a spa day, get a massage. She does not understand that sitting still makes me anxious. I feel most myself when my hands are busy, when someone needs me.

Maybe that is the problem. Maybe I do not know how to receive. Even compliments make me uncomfortable. But this is too much philosophy for a Sunday evening. The dishes are not going to wash themselves.

entries.initial_entry:
The boy with pneumonia died tonight. Ahmed. Seven years old. I was not even on shift but the day nurse called me because his mother was asking for me. I held her while she screamed. I did not know what else to do.

I cannot stop seeing his small face. The way he used to smile at me when I checked his temperature. He called me "auntie."

I came home and hugged Nadia so tight she asked if something was wrong. I could not tell her. I just needed to feel her breathing against me. Alive. Healthy. Mine.

Khalid found me crying in the bathroom later. He did not say anything. Just sat on the floor next to me and held my hand until I stopped shaking. Twenty-three years and he still knows when words are not what I need.

I chose this work. I chose to love other people's children as my own. But tonight the cost feels unbearable.

Spent most of the morning grinding ink and laying out rice paper for the lotus series. Got through three decent studies before Xiaoli called to say Ma was refusing to eat lunch again. Took the bus over and sat with her for an hour, spooning congee and telling her about the lotus paintings. She ate about half the bowl, which is better than yesterday.

Back at the studio by three. The light was already shifting but I managed to finish one piece I had been circling for weeks. Something about the afternoon angle gave the petals the translucency I wanted. Xiaoli texted to thank me for going over. She worries she cannot handle Ma alone during the weekday shifts at the restaurant, and honestly she is probably right. But today both things worked out: Ma ate, the painting came together.

Small day. But the kind where you go to bed feeling like you showed up where it counted.
entries.nudge_text: What made today feel different from other days?
entries.response_text: Different? I do not think it was so different. Maybe I just had enough energy for once. Anyway the lotus piece still needs another layer, so tomorrow is already planned.

entries.initial_entry:
My daughter Yuwen called from Kaohsiung tonight. She has been offered a position in Singapore and she wants to take it. Two-year contract, some biotech firm. She was buzzing, talking fast the way she does when she has already made up her mind but wants me to say it is okay.

I said congratulations. I asked about the salary and the housing situation and whether she had looked into the health insurance. Normal questions. She said I sounded flat and I told her I was just tired from painting all day, which was half true. The other half is that Singapore is far and my chest got tight when she said January start date.

I do not think I am being unreasonable by needing a moment. She is thirty-four, she does not need my permission. But she called because she wanted something from me and I could not quite find it in the moment. I will call her back tomorrow when I have sorted myself out. People act like you should just immediately overflow with enthusiasm but sometimes you need to sit with the thing first.
entries.nudge_text: What were you hoping to say to her?
entries.response_text: That I am proud. That she should go. That I will be fine. All of which is true but none of which came out because I was too busy bracing for how quiet it will be once she is actually gone. Xiaoli has the restaurant, Ma has her routines, and Yuwen visits every other weekend. Without that I am just a woman alone in a studio talking to paper.

entries.initial_entry:
Called Yuwen back this morning like I said I would. Told her Singapore sounds like a strong move and that I want to hear everything about the lab once she has her first week under her belt. She sounded relieved. I could hear it in the way her voice loosened up.

After we hung up I went to the studio and started a new piece, something looser than the lotus series. Just playing with ink density on wet paper, watching the way it bleeds outward. No plan, no composition sketch. It has been months since I painted without a destination in mind and it felt like stretching a muscle I had forgotten about.

Dropped off steamed buns at Xiaoli's restaurant around noon. The lunch crowd was thin so we actually sat and ate together for twenty minutes. She asked about Yuwen and I told her. She squeezed my arm and said she is not going anywhere. I know that. But it was good to hear.

Had the calligraphy kids over today. Eight of them now, the youngest is six and keeps holding the brush like a pencil no matter how many times I adjust her grip. I should be more patient about it but something about watching her try so hard makes me want to get it right for her. I sat behind her and guided her hand through the first three strokes of her name. She laughed when the ink splattered and I let her keep the ruined sheet.

Their parents pick them up and sometimes linger to chat. Mrs. Huang brought tangerines for everyone and asked if I would consider teaching an adult class. I said I would think about it, but what I was really thinking about was how full the studio felt with eight small people breathing and concentrating in it. That kind of fullness is hard to manufacture.
entries.nudge_text: What does that fullness give you?
entries.response_text: Messy tables and ink on the floor, mostly. But I suppose a reason to set up and show up that is not just about finishing another painting for no one in particular. Anyway, Mrs. Huang's tangerines were excellent. I should ask her where she gets them.

entries.initial_entry:
Gallery owner Mr. Tsai called again. Third time this year. He wants to include three of my pieces in a group show in Taichung next March. I would need to deliver the work in person and attend the opening weekend, which means two nights away.

I told him I would check my schedule and he said Meiling, you always say that. He is not wrong. I know the exposure would be good and Xiaoli keeps telling me I should put myself out there more. But Ma has her bad stretches and I cannot just hand that off. Xiaoli has the restaurant to run. I am not making excuses, I am describing the situation. Two days away means two days where nobody checks if Ma ate her congee.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for those two days to feel possible?
entries.response_text: Probably just knowing someone would actually sit with Ma the way I do. Not just drop off food but sit there and talk to her and make sure she finishes. Xiaoli tries but she rushes. I do not blame her, the restaurant does not close itself. Maybe I could ask Mrs. Huang. Or maybe I am overcomplicating something that does not need to be this hard.

entries.initial_entry:
Ma fell this morning. Nothing broken, just a bruised hip. Xiaoli found her on the bathroom floor and called me in a panic. By the time I got there the paramedics had already checked her over and said to watch for swelling. We moved her to the couch and she complained the whole time that we were making a fuss.

Stayed with her until evening. Read her the newspaper, made her favorite sweet potato soup. She fell asleep around eight and I sat there watching her breathe for a while before Xiaoli came to take over for the night. I am tired in a way that has nothing to do with sleep. But she is okay. She is okay and that is the only thing that matters right now.
Xiaoli called after midnight crying, saying she cannot keep doing this. I told her we will figure it out together. I do not know what that looks like yet but I meant it.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'together' look like for you two?
entries.response_text: I do not know, I just said that. Probably me doing more of the morning shifts with Ma so Xiaoli can open the restaurant without worrying. We have always sorted things out between us, this will not be different.

entries.initial_entry:
Hired a part-time caregiver for Ma, three mornings a week. Her name is Ah-Fen, mid-forties, came recommended by Mrs. Huang's neighbor. Ma did not want a stranger in the house and told me so in Shanghainese so Ah-Fen would not understand. I told Ma to give it one week.

Ah-Fen brought her own apron and a thermos of ginger tea. She was gentle, did not talk too much, and knew how to help Ma to the bathroom without making her feel small. By the time I left, Ma was showing her the photo album from our old apartment in Shanghai. I drove to the studio and painted for four hours straight, the longest unbroken stretch in weeks. I am not sure if I feel relieved or guilty. Both, probably.
entries.nudge_text: Where does the guilt come from?
entries.response_text: From the same place it always comes from. Anyway, the important thing is Ma seemed comfortable with Ah-Fen by the end. If this works out, Xiaoli and I can both breathe a little.

entries.initial_entry: Yuwen flew in from Kaohsiung to say goodbye before Singapore. She brought me a new set of Kolinsky brushes, the kind I would never buy myself. We sat in the studio and painted together for the first time since she was a teenager.

New Year's Eve. Xiaoli closed the restaurant early and we all ate together at Ma's place. Yuwen leaves on the third. I keep looking at her across the table and memorizing how she holds her chopsticks.
entries.nudge_text: What are you holding onto?
entries.response_text: The way things are right now, I think. Everyone at one table. Ma complaining about the fish being overcooked. Xiaoli laughing too loud. Yuwen sneaking rice to the neighbor's cat. I know this exact configuration will not happen again for a long time and I am trying to press it flat like a leaf in a book. I have spent my whole life making sure the people around me are okay and now one of them is leaving and I cannot follow. That is the deal. You raise them to go.

entries.initial_entry:
Woke up early, first day of the year. The studio was cold so I boiled water for tea and just stood there holding the cup until my hands warmed up. Started grinding ink out of habit more than intention. Then I began painting: a small sparrow on a bare branch. Not sure why. It just came.

Yuwen stopped by before noon to drop off a bag of groceries she bought for me. She organized my fridge, threw out three containers of expired things, and lectured me about eating properly when she is gone. She sounded exactly like my mother thirty years ago and I almost told her that but stopped myself because she would have rolled her eyes. I just said thank you and walked her to the scooter.

Mr. Tsai's show is in March. I told Ah-Fen she might need to do a full weekend. She said no problem. Xiaoli said she would check on Ma both evenings. Yuwen said she would video call Ma every Sunday from Singapore. Everyone just stepped in without me asking. I have been holding all the pieces so tightly and it turns out some of them can hold themselves. The sparrow painting is still wet on the table. Small thing, but I think it is good.
entries.nudge_text: How does it feel to not be the one holding everything?

entries.initial_entry:
Stayed late at the lab again. Got home after the kids were asleep. Dina didn't say anything but I could tell from the way she left my dinner on the counter instead of the table. That's her signal.

The thing is I know I should've been home earlier. Layla had her first swimming lesson and I told her this morning I'd try to make it. "Try" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I didn't try. I got pulled into running another simulation batch and the hours just disappeared. And now my kid learned to float without me there and I can't get that back. I keep telling myself the PhD is for them, for our future, but some of these moments don't wait for dissertations to be finished.
entries.nudge_text: Which moments are you most afraid of missing?
entries.response_text: Honestly all of them. But the ones where they stop asking me to come. That's what scares me.

entries.initial_entry:
Monday again. Advisor meeting at 10, went fine I think, he wants the literature review chapter revised by Friday which is tight but manageable. Spent the afternoon in the lab calibrating the shake table sensors. Boring but necessary work, nobody else was going to do it.

Dina took Omar to the pediatrician because he's had this cough for two weeks. She texted me the update: nothing serious, probably allergies, they gave him a prescription. I should've gone with her but Mondays are impossible with the advisor meeting and the TA prep for Tuesday's section. She handled it fine. She always handles it fine. That's partly the problem I guess, she's so capable that I lean on her for everything outside the lab and at some point that stops being partnership and starts being something else.

Called my mother in the evening. She asked when I'm defending. When I said probably next spring she went quiet then started talking about my cousin Faisal who just got promoted to department head at the ministry back home. He's two years younger than me. She didn't say it outright but the comparison was loud. Told her I had to go because Omar needed a bath, which was true but also convenient.
entries.nudge_text: What does it start being?
entries.response_text: Just imbalance I think. Not worth spiraling about right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today which is unusual. Just got back from putting the kids to bed. Layla wanted me to read to her and I did three chapters of that Junie B. Jones book she's obsessed with. Omar fell asleep on my lap halfway through. Small thing but I needed it.

Dina and I actually talked after. Not about logistics for once. She told me about this cooking class she wants to take on Saturdays, Palestinian dishes from her grandmother's recipes. Her eyes lit up describing it. I told her she absolutely should and I'd handle the kids Saturday mornings. She looked surprised, like she wasn't expecting me to just say yes. That bothered me a little because it means she's gotten used to me being unavailable.

But I said yes and I meant it. Saturday mornings are mine with the kids and hers for the class. This is something I can actually hold onto. The dissertation won't collapse because I take three hours on a Saturday. And Dina deserves to have something that's just for her, same way the PhD is my thing. Though calling the PhD "my thing" feels strange when it's supposed to be for all of us. Whatever, the point is tonight felt right. I was present and it didn't cost me anything I'll regret.

Pulled two all-nighters this week to get the chapter revision done. Submitted it at 4am. Body is wrecked. Dina asked if I was okay and I snapped at her, said I was fine when I obviously wasn't. Feel awful about that. She doesn't deserve my stress dumped on her.
entries.nudge_text: What were you actually feeling when you snapped?
entries.response_text: Guilty. Because she was being caring and I resented it somehow. Like her concern reminded me I'd been neglecting everything that wasn't the dissertation.

entries.initial_entry:
Apologized to Dina this morning properly. Not just a mumbled sorry but sat down with her during the kids' cartoons and told her I knew I was being unfair. She said she knows the pressure I'm under but she needs me to not take it out on her. Fair. Completely fair.

Made pancakes for everyone after. Layla put chocolate chips in hers in the shape of a smiley face. Omar ate his with his hands and got syrup everywhere. Normal family morning and I was actually there for it instead of hunched over my laptop. Advisor can wait until tomorrow.

New month. Advisor approved the revised chapter with minor edits. Felt good to get that confirmation after the crunch. Started outlining the methodology chapter, should be more straightforward since it's describing work I've already done.

Dina started her Saturday cooking class last week. She came home with maftoul and it was better than my mother's, which I will never say to my mother. The kids and I went to the park while she was gone. Taught Layla to pump on the swing by herself. Omar mostly ate sand. Standard Saturday. This arrangement is working and I want to protect it.

Lab mate Chen got accepted to present at the ASCE conference in November. Good for him. His advisor fast-tracked the submission. Mine hasn't even looked at my abstract draft from three weeks ago. I know I shouldn't compare timelines. Chen doesn't have kids, doesn't have a family depending on him, works weekends without guilt. Different situation entirely. But sitting in the lab watching him celebrate while I'm still grinding through Chapter 3 revisions, I couldn't help doing the math on how far behind I am.

Dina asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't mention Chen. Didn't want to sound petty about it.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'behind' mean to you here?
entries.response_text: Just timeline stuff. Conference papers, publications. The CV metrics that actually matter for the job market.

entries.initial_entry: Layla asked me tonight if I'm going to be a student forever. She wasn't being mean, just genuinely confused. Told her almost done. But the question rattled around in my head after. Am I taking too long? Am I dragging this out because finishing means facing what comes next?
entries.nudge_text: What comes next that worries you?

entries.initial_entry:
My mother called and dropped the news that my father's been having chest pains. He went to the doctor last week apparently and nobody told me until now. When I asked why, she said they didn't want to "worry me during my studies." I'm 38 years old and they're still treating me like I'm a kid who can't handle bad news.

I'm angry but I also know exactly why they did it. Because every time I talk to them I'm stressed about the PhD, about money, about the kids. So they stopped telling me things. They shielded me and now I feel like I failed them by being so consumed with my own problems that they couldn't even lean on me. Dina found me sitting in the kitchen at midnight staring at my phone. She just sat with me and didn't say anything for a while. Then she said we could look into flights for winter break if I wanted to visit. She's already thinking about logistics while I'm stuck in my own head.
