I told my advisor I might need to go to Amman for a few weeks. He said it's fine as long as I keep writing. Of course that's what he said. Everyone needs something from me and I can't figure out how to be enough for all of them at once.

Spent the morning reviewing proposals from two potential investors. One offers more capital but wants a board seat and quarterly reviews. The other is smaller but completely hands-off. Everyone keeps telling me the bigger check is the obvious choice. But I keep thinking about three years ago when I walked away from a salary that would make most people's eyes water, all because I couldn't stand someone else dictating my calendar.

Maybe I'm being naive. Maybe smart founders take the bigger check and learn to play the game. But every time I imagine sitting in those quarterly reviews, justifying my decisions to people who've never built anything themselves, something in my chest tightens.
entries.nudge_text: What does that tightening in your chest usually signal for you?
entries.response_text: It's the same feeling I had in my last corporate job. Before I quit. Like I'm slowly being boxed in. When I was younger I thought I'd outgrow it, learn to compromise. But if anything it's gotten stronger. I think that feeling is my compass now—when it shows up, I know I'm about to betray something important about how I want to live.

entries.initial_entry: Took the smaller investment. Signed papers yesterday. Celebrated with Layla at that new Persian restaurant near the Marina. Felt right.

My mother called from Tehran today. She wanted to know when I'm getting married, when I'm having children, when I'm going to "settle down and stop playing." I'm almost forty. I've heard these questions my whole adult life. Usually I brush them off. Today it got under my skin more than usual.

I think it's because part of me wonders if she has a point. Not about marriage specifically—I'll figure that out when I figure it out. But about what I'm building toward. I work hard, yes, but I also make sure to enjoy the fruits of that work. Weekends in Oman. Good wine. A nice apartment. She sees all of that as frivolous, as avoiding responsibility. In her world, you sacrifice everything for family, for the future, for some distant reward.

But I've watched people sacrifice their entire lives for that distant reward and then die before they get there. Or they get there and realize they forgot how to enjoy anything. I don't want that. I refuse that. And yet—today, after we hung up, I sat on my balcony for an hour just staring at the water, wondering if my version of a good life will leave me alone at sixty with nothing but nice memories.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like you're weighing two different visions of what a meaningful life looks like. Which one feels more true to who you are right now?
entries.response_text: The one where I'm present. Where I'm actually living my days instead of deferring them. I've tried the other way—grinding through miserable weeks for some hypothetical payoff. It made me sick, literally. Migraines, insomnia. My body rejected it. So I know what's true for me. I just wish knowing made it easier to hear my mother's disappointment.

entries.initial_entry:
Layla and I got into it last night. She thinks I work too much. I think that's rich coming from someone who doesn't understand what it takes to run your own thing. When you're employed, you clock out and your problems stay at the office. When it's YOUR company, it follows you everywhere.

But then—I don't know. She made this comment about how I used to be more fun. More spontaneous. And it stung because she's not wrong. The last few months I've been grinding harder than usual, trying to prove to myself that taking the smaller check was the right call. Trying to show I can grow this thing without giving up control.

I hate that I'm becoming the person I swore I wouldn't become. The one who's always "busy."
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned you 'hate becoming the person you swore you wouldn't become.' What would that person look like if you fully became them?
entries.response_text: I don't want to think about that right now. I just needed to get this out. I know what I need to do—I need to take her somewhere nice for the holidays and actually be present. That's the plan.

entries.initial_entry:
We went to Muscat for three days. Left my laptop at home—Layla's one condition. First day I was restless, kept reaching for my phone. By the third day I had forgotten what I was even anxious about.

We hiked to this natural pool in the mountains. Cold water, complete silence except for birds. I floated on my back and watched clouds move and thought: this is why I built my own thing. Not for the money, not for the status. For moments like this, whenever I want them. The ability to disappear for three days without asking permission.

I need to protect this. The business should serve the life, not the other way around.
Back from holiday. January pipeline already overwhelming. Three new client requests, two urgent. I said yes to all of them because I'm still in growth mode. Already regretting it.
entries.nudge_text: When you say you're already regretting it, what specifically is showing up for you?
entries.response_text: The familiar dread. Knowing the next two months will be meetings and deadlines and no room to breathe. Knowing I just had a beautiful reset and I'm already overcommitting. It's a pattern. I recognize it. I just don't know how to break it when saying no feels like leaving money on the table.

entries.initial_entry:
My old colleague Faisal reached out. He's now a VP at a big consulting firm, wanted to know if I'd consider coming back to corporate. "Senior role, name your salary, you'd have a team of twenty."

I said no, obviously. But then he made this comment—"You're still doing the solo thing? I thought that was a phase." And I felt this flash of anger. Like I needed to justify myself. Started listing my clients, my revenue, my growth numbers. Trying to prove I wasn't some hobbyist.

Later I wondered why I cared so much. Why his opinion mattered. I left that world for a reason. I don't owe anyone an explanation.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think Faisal's comment touched on that made you want to prove yourself?
entries.response_text: Maybe the fear that I'm fooling myself. That everyone else sees something I don't—that I gave up a real career to play entrepreneur, and one day it'll all fall apart and I'll have nothing to show for it. I know that's not rational. My business is healthy. But there's this voice that sounds a lot like my father, saying I'm not serious enough. That real men build empires, not lifestyles. I thought I'd silenced that voice. Apparently not.

entries.initial_entry:
Funny thing happened today. One of my clients—a resort chain—asked if I'd relocate to Bahrain for six months to oversee an opening. Great money, interesting project. My immediate reaction was no. Not because of the work, but because I imagined six months without my Thursday dinners at that Lebanese place, without my morning runs along the creek, without the routines that make my life here feel like mine.

Then I caught myself and thought—wait, am I becoming rigid? I built this life precisely so I could be flexible. So I could say yes to adventures. But now I'm protecting routines like they're sacred.

Or maybe routines are sacred. Maybe I've earned the right to build a life I don't want to escape from. Maybe flexibility isn't about saying yes to every opportunity but about having the freedom to say no.

I don't know. I told them I'd think about it over the weekend.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned earning 'the right to build a life you don't want to escape from.' What did it cost you to earn that right?

entries.initial_entry:
Turned down the Bahrain project. Explained that I could consult remotely but wouldn't relocate. They accepted, which surprised me. I expected them to walk away. Maybe I've been underestimating my leverage. Maybe I've been saying yes to things out of scarcity when I could afford to negotiate.

Layla was relieved. She said, "You're finally acting like someone who knows what he wants." I think she's right. For years I was running from things—from my old career, from expectations, from boredom. Now I'm starting to run toward something specific. A life that actually fits. It's a subtle shift but it feels significant.

We're planning another trip for March. Somewhere new. The reward for good work isn't more work—it's more life.

Sat through the whole lab meeting biting my tongue while Felix presented my preprocessing idea as his own. Didn't say anything. Walked home in the rain and honestly the cold felt better than sitting in that room. At least I know the idea works — that's mine regardless of who gets credit.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from speaking up?

entries.initial_entry: People keep asking why I turned down Professor Hecht's placement like I owe them an explanation. It was a bad fit. I don't need to justify building something from scratch when the alternative is tweaking someone else's framework for two years. My project, my call.
entries.nudge_text: You sound sure, but you haven't told your family yet.
entries.response_text: Yeah, because telling them means a two-hour phone call where my mother explains how Professor Hecht's name on my CV would open doors. She's not wrong about that part. I just can't spend two years on work that doesn't feel like mine. I'll tell them once I have something to show for it — results shut down the lecture faster than arguments do.

entries.initial_entry:
Three days into building my own pipeline and I'm already behind on coursework. Stayed up until four last night debugging tokenizer issues that would've been solved if I'd just used the lab's existing toolkit. My officemate Nina asked if I wanted help and I said no before I even thought about it. That was stupid. She's good at this stuff and she wasn't going to steal the project.

I keep doing this — refusing help because I need it to be entirely mine, and then drowning quietly. The fellowship application is due Friday and I haven't started the personal statement because every time I open it I think about how I'm supposed to frame myself as a team player. I don't know how to write that without it feeling like a lie, but I also don't know how to explain that I work better alone without sounding like a problem.

The coordinadora pulled me aside after class and told me my reading circle format doesn't align with the ministry's new standardized framework. She wants me to switch to the workbook model by next week. I said I'd look into it but I'm not changing what's working.
entries.nudge_text: What happens when next week comes and you haven't switched?

entries.initial_entry:
People keep acting like I'm being difficult but I sat through two years of watching kids at the private school fill out comprehension worksheets with dead eyes. My students here actually talk about the books we read. They argue about characters. Last week Sofia, who barely spoke in September, stood up and defended her interpretation of the ending of the story to the whole class. That doesn't come from a workbook.

I talked to the coordinadora again today and told her straight up that I'd document my students' progress however she needs but I won't gut the program to fit a checkbox. She didn't love it. But she didn't say no either. My mom thinks I should just comply and keep my head down, which is rich coming from someone who argued with every teacher I ever had.
entries.nudge_text: Your mom told you to comply. How did that land?
entries.response_text: It stung more than it should have. She's the reason I push back on things in the first place.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Camilo through practice problems for the ICFES all Saturday morning. He's getting better at the reading comprehension sections, still shaky on the quantitative reasoning but that's not really my wheelhouse. I set him up with a YouTube channel that breaks it down step by step and told him to message me if he gets stuck. His mom, my tia Elena, called from Medellin to thank me, which was nice.

Went to abuela's in the afternoon. She'd made sancocho even though it was just the two of us, enough for ten people as usual. We watched a novela together and I fixed the latch on her back door that's been loose for months. She seems okay. A little quieter than normal maybe, but she always gets like that around the anniversary of abuelo's passing.

Long day. Parent meetings ran until six and then I stayed to reorganize the classroom library because nobody else was going to. Camilo called with a question about a practice test but I was too fried to be useful so I told him we'd go over it this weekend.
entries.nudge_text: You couldn't help Camilo tonight. Does that bother you?
entries.response_text: Yeah, a little. He doesn't ask unless he's actually stuck.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from the district office about a new mandate requiring all language arts teachers to submit weekly lesson plans using their template. The template is basically a fill-in-the-blank thing with pre-set learning objectives and approved activities. I forwarded it to my colleague Patricia and she just shrugged and said she'd start using it. I get it, she has two kids and no energy to fight bureaucracy. But I can't just do that.

The thing is, I know how this looks. I know people see me as the one who always has to do things her own way, the difficult teacher, the one who won't just go along. And maybe there's some truth in it. But I've also seen what happens when you hand over every decision about your classroom to someone who's never met your students. The kids in Ciudad Bolivar don't need another generic worksheet. They need someone paying attention to what actually reaches them.

I'm going to keep using my own plans and submit the template version as a formality. I've done this before. It's more work but I'd rather do double the planning than teach something I don't believe in.
entries.nudge_text: You said maybe there's truth in being the difficult one. What's that like?
entries.response_text: Lonely sometimes. Patricia used to push back with me but she stopped. I don't blame her but I notice it.

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today because I can't stop thinking about something that happened after school. One of my students, Diego, waited around after everyone left and asked me if I could help him write a letter to his dad who's working in Chile. He said his mom doesn't have time and he doesn't know how to start. So we sat there for forty minutes and I helped him figure out what he wanted to say. He wanted to tell his dad about a soccer game he played in and about a dog that follows him home from school every day.

It was one of those moments where you remember why you're doing this. Not the curriculum fights or the template emails or the district mandates. Just a kid who needed someone to sit with him and say yeah, that's worth writing down, your dad would want to hear about the dog.

I keep thinking about how many kids like Diego just need someone to stop and listen for half an hour. And then I think about how stretched thin I already am and how I told Camilo I'd help him this weekend and I promised abuela I'd come by Sunday and I still haven't finished planning next week's classes because I was busy making the fake template version for the district. There's only so much of me to go around.

Spent the morning with Camilo going through the quantitative reasoning section. He's definitely improving. We worked through the problems he'd flagged and he got about seventy percent right on his own before I stepped in. Told him that's real progress and he actually smiled, which he doesn't do much these days since he's so stressed about the exam. His mom is counting on him getting into a public university because they can't afford private tuition, so the pressure is real.

After that I went to abuela's. She was in better spirits today. She'd been to the market and bought ingredients for arepas de choclo, so we made them together. She told me stories about when she and abuelo first came to Bogota from the campo and how they used to make arepas on a clay stove. I've heard the stories before but I don't mind. She seems to need to tell them.

On the bus home I sketched out a rough plan for next week's reading unit. I'm going to have the kids write letters, inspired by Diego. They'll pick someone they want to tell something to and we'll work on voice and structure through that. It fits the ministry objectives if you squint at it the right way, and it's actually something the kids will care about.
entries.nudge_text: Diego's letter became a whole unit. Does that happen a lot?
entries.response_text: More than I realize probably. The best ideas come from paying attention to what the kids actually need, not from a planning document.

entries.initial_entry: Tia Elena called tonight and said Camilo bombed his practice test today, the one right after our session. She wasn't blaming me but it felt like it. I keep wondering if I'm actually helping him or just making myself feel useful.
entries.nudge_text: Making yourself feel useful — what made you put it that way?
entries.response_text: I don't know, I'm just tired. He'll be fine, he's smart enough.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the tech lead conversation with Martijn today. He laid out what the role would look like — running standups, making architecture calls, representing the team in cross-department meetings. I said I was interested, which is true, but the whole time I was thinking about how I'd actually handle it. I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is.

But I also know I did the right thing saying yes. Everyone on the team expects it, Noor would think I was insane to turn it down, and frankly it would look odd to pass on a promotion you're being handed. I can figure out the rest as I go. Probably.
entries.nudge_text: What part of it actually interests you?

entries.initial_entry: Long week. Spent most of it pairing with the new hire, walking her through the codebase and our deployment pipeline. Didn't get to touch my own backlog at all but she's ramping up faster than the last person we onboarded so I'll take it.
entries.nudge_text: Was it worth not having your own time?

entries.initial_entry:
Sprint retro got a bit heated. Devika suggested we rethink the approval flow for deployments because she thinks it slows us down. She's right, technically — it does add a half-day lag. I had a whole alternative sketched in my head while she was talking, a lighter-weight check that would still catch the critical stuff.

Didn't say it though. The senior architects set up the current flow and they were in the call. Felt like bringing up a redesign right there would come across as me trying to show off, especially with the tech lead thing floating around. So I just nodded and said I could look into Devika's point offline. She seemed a bit deflated that nobody backed her up more directly. I get it.

Weird day. Started normal — coffee, standup, tickets. Then around lunch Pieter from platform team pinged me about a side project he's been hacking on, this internal tool for tracking deployment health across services. He wanted to know if I'd help build the backend. Not sanctioned work, just something he thinks would be useful and wants to pitch to leadership once it's built.

First thought was yes obviously, this is the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on, building something new without twelve layers of sign-off. Second thought was that Martijn would not love me spending cycles on an unsanctioned project right when he's evaluating me for lead. Third thought was that Pieter didn't ask anyone else, he asked me, and that felt good in a way I don't fully understand. Like someone saw the part of me that wants to just build.

I told Pieter I'd think about it over the weekend. Which is code for "I want to do it but I need to figure out if I'm allowed to want to do it." That sounds ridiculous written down. I'm a grown man deciding whether to write some code on the side. But it feels more loaded than that right now.

Told Pieter I'd help with the tool. We set up a repo and I spent an hour last night after Noor went to sleep scaffolding the API layer. Felt more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint on the actual work.

Today was back to normal though — reviewing PRs, answering Slack threads, sitting through a planning session where half the decisions had already been made before we walked in. The usual. I'm not complaining, it's fine. I just noticed the contrast, that's all.
entries.nudge_text: You said it's fine twice now. Is it?
entries.response_text: Yeah. It's work, it doesn't all have to be exciting.

entries.initial_entry:
Noor and I went to dinner with her parents in Rotterdam on Saturday. Her dad asked about the promotion and I gave the standard answer — it's in progress, I'm grateful for the opportunity, etc. He seemed pleased. On the train back Noor said she was proud of me and I felt genuinely good about it. Like I'm on a track that makes sense to the people I care about.

Then on Sunday I stayed up way too late working on Pieter's tool and got completely absorbed refactoring the data ingestion layer. Noor asked what I was doing and I said "just work stuff" which wasn't really a lie but wasn't the whole truth either. I don't know why I didn't just explain it. Maybe because explaining it would mean admitting how much more alive that project makes me feel, and then someone might ask what that says about everything else.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean if you did explain it to her?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing bad. She'd understand. I think the issue is more that saying it out loud makes it real — that the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up. And then I'd have to figure out what to do with that.

entries.initial_entry: Martijn officially put me on the lead shortlist. Two candidates, me and someone from the other squad. I thanked him and said I'd put my best foot forward. Which I will. Whatever doubts I have aren't anyone else's problem to carry.

Team drinks after work. Everyone was congratulating me about the shortlist like it's already decided. I kept saying it wasn't confirmed yet but nobody listens. Devika made a joke about me being her future boss and I laughed but it sat wrong with me for the rest of the evening.

I don't think I'm being fake about it. I genuinely want to do well, and the role comes with better pay, more visibility, things that matter. But there's this gap between how I talk about it to other people and what actually runs through my head at 11pm when I'm staring at Pieter's repo. Not sure what to do with that. For now I'm doing both, which is sustainable enough.
Presented the deployment health tool to Pieter's skip-level manager today. She liked it. Asked if we'd want to bring it under the platform roadmap officially. Said yes without hesitating.
entries.nudge_text: That was quick for someone who usually thinks things over.
entries.response_text: Some things don't need overthinking.

entries.initial_entry:
Martijn pulled me aside after standup. Apparently someone mentioned to him that I've been spending time on Pieter's tool and he wanted to know if it was affecting my sprint commitments. It isn't — I've been keeping my velocity up — but the way he framed it made it sound like a warning. "Just want to make sure your priorities are clear," he said.

I told him it wouldn't be an issue and that the tool is now being folded into the platform roadmap anyway, so it's legitimate work. He nodded and dropped it. But I've been replaying the conversation all afternoon. The rational part of me knows he was just doing his job. The other part feels like I got caught doing something I wasn't supposed to enjoy. I hate that I felt the need to justify it. I hate more that I did justify it, immediately, without pushing back at all.
entries.nudge_text: What would pushing back have looked like?
entries.response_text: Saying something like "I'm delivering on everything you've asked, so what I do with my remaining time is my call." Which is true. But I keep imagining how that would land — confrontational, difficult, not a team player. So I default to the smooth version. And then I'm annoyed at myself for it later, which is its own kind of exhausting.

entries.initial_entry: Got the tech lead offer. Accepted on the spot because that's what you do. Texted Noor and she sent about fifteen celebration emojis. Calling my parents tonight.

Same day, later. Parents were thrilled, mum teared up a little. Had a glass of wine with Noor and she kept saying she knew it would happen. Everyone's reaction confirmed that this was the right move, or at least the expected one, and those two things feel like they should be different but tonight they're the same and I'm too tired to untangle it.

I keep coming back to something small. When Martijn told me, my first feeling wasn't excitement. It was relief. Relief that it's done, that I don't have to hold the performance anymore, that I can stop wondering and just be in the role. Maybe excitement comes later. Or maybe relief is enough. I genuinely don't know and I'm going to stop thinking about it and go to bed.
Long day. Lost the Hendricks account because their new facilities guy wants to go with a national chain. Grandpa built that relationship in '89 and I just watched it walk out the door.

Dad brought up the Hendricks thing at Sunday dinner. In front of everyone. Said maybe I should have driven out there in person instead of sending a proposal over email. Like I haven't been running seventy hours a week already.
entries.nudge_text: What stung most about your dad's comment?
entries.response_text: That he might be right. Grandpa would've driven out there.

entries.initial_entry: Closed the Meyer Medical Group contract today — biggest commercial deal we've landed this quarter. Stayed late to call Grandpa and tell him before the rest of the family heard. He said "that's my boy" and I don't think I've smiled like that in a month.

Had a call with the regional HVAC association about their young leaders program. They want me to apply for the board. On paper it's a great opportunity — good network, visibility in the industry, looks sharp on a resume. But the meetings are every other Thursday evening, which is when I do my site walkthroughs with the install crews.

Those walkthroughs aren't required. Nobody would notice if I stopped. But Grandpa drilled it into me when I started: you show up where the work happens, you shake the hands of the guys turning the wrenches. It's how you earn trust. Feels wrong to swap that out for a conference call with people trading business cards. But then I look at some of the other managers my age who are on three boards and speaking at conferences while I'm in a crawlspace checking ductwork.
entries.nudge_text: Which one would keep you up at night?
entries.response_text: The walkthroughs. No question. I'd never forgive myself otherwise.

entries.initial_entry:
Thinking more about the board thing from yesterday. Turned it down this morning. Polite email, said maybe next year. But now I'm sitting with this feeling like I just closed a door I shouldn't have. My buddy Jake from college is VP of Operations at a company half our size. He's thirty-two. He's on two industry boards, he did that leadership program at Northwestern, and every time I see his LinkedIn I feel this pull in my chest like I'm falling behind some invisible race.

I know comparison is poison. I know that. But it's hard when the scoreboard is right there on your phone. Jake doesn't have a grandfather who spent fifty years building something with his name on it. Jake doesn't sit at a dinner table every Sunday hearing about how things were done the right way. Jake just has a title and a trajectory and nobody asking him to preserve anything.

Meanwhile I'm trying to grow this company AND keep it feeling like the company it's always been. Like those two things are the same direction. Most days they are. But days like today they feel like opposite ends of a rope and I'm standing in the middle.
entries.nudge_text: Which parts of 'the right way' do you actually believe?

entries.initial_entry: Just realized I wrote twice in one day. Whatever. Came home and Jake posted about keynoting some supply chain conference. Good for him. I'm going to go prep my Q2 numbers because at least those are mine.
entries.nudge_text: What do your Q2 numbers prove that Jake's keynote doesn't?
entries.response_text: That I actually built something real. Forty-three paychecks.

entries.initial_entry:
Grandpa's 80th birthday party Saturday. The whole family came — uncles, cousins, people I haven't seen since I was a kid. Mom put together a slideshow with photos from when he first started the company. There's one of him standing in front of his first van, WHITFIELD HEATING hand-painted on the side. He was twenty-six in that picture. Three years younger than I am now.

Uncle Bill gave a toast about how the company was built on handshakes and showing up. Everyone raised their glasses. I watched Grandpa's face and he was trying not to cry. He looked at me across the table at one point and nodded, this small nod like he was passing something forward. I can't explain why that hit so hard.

Later that night I went out to the driveway to get some air and my cousin Travis followed me out. He works in tech in Seattle, does well for himself. He asked me if I ever thought about doing something else, something that was just mine. I told him the company IS mine. He kind of shrugged and said "you know what I mean." I do know what he means. But I also think he doesn't understand what it feels like to be part of something that started before you were born and will keep going after you're gone. There's weight to that. Some days it's an anchor. Other days it's the only thing keeping me steady.

Performance reviews this week. Had to sit down with each of my crew leads and go through their numbers. Most of the conversations went fine but Danny — he's been with us nineteen years, started under Grandpa — pushed back when I talked about the new scheduling software. Said it was "a solution looking for a problem" and that the whiteboard system works fine.

I wanted to snap at him. The whiteboard system loses us hours every week in double-bookings and missed follow-ups. I have the data. But instead I heard myself saying "let's give it a trial run and see how it goes, no pressure." Which is exactly what Grandpa would have done — never force it, bring people along. But part of me thinks Grandpa could afford to be patient because he had decades. I don't feel like I have decades. The market is moving and if we don't modernize the ops side we'll be the company that did great work in the '90s and couldn't keep up.
entries.nudge_text: What would it cost you to overrule Danny and just implement it?
entries.response_text: His respect. And every guy on the floor who remembers Grandpa.

entries.initial_entry: Danny used the new scheduling software today. Without being asked. Came in, saw the tablet on the wall, and just started putting jobs in. Didn't say a word about it. I didn't either.
entries.nudge_text: You didn't say a word — what were you holding back?
entries.response_text: Relief. Like I finally got permission to move forward.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday dinner. Grandpa wasn't feeling well so it was just Mom, Dad, and me. Without Grandpa at the table the whole energy was different. Dad talked about maybe expanding into the residential solar market. I've been thinking about it too — the margins are there, the demand is growing, it's a natural fit. But when I brought it up to Grandpa last month he said "we're heating and cooling people, that's what we do."

I didn't push it. How do you push it with an eighty-year-old man who built the thing from nothing? He earned the right to say no. But tonight Dad said something that's been rattling around in my head since — he said "your grandfather doesn't run the company anymore, Ryan. You do." And I know that's technically true. My name is on the decisions. But it doesn't feel true. It feels like I'm managing a museum and every choice I make is either a restoration or a renovation and I can never tell which one the family wants.

I keep going back to that nod at his birthday party. What was he passing to me exactly? The company as it is? Or the company as whatever I can make it? I genuinely don't know. And I'm scared to ask because what if the answer is something I can't live with.
entries.nudge_text: What would change if he said 'make it yours'?

entries.initial_entry:
Fourth of July at the lake house. The whole family thing — burgers, fireworks, Uncle Bill doing his annual speech about "what this country was built on." I usually tune it out but this year he looked at me during the part about "carrying the torch" and I realized he wasn't just talking about America.

Cousin Travis was there again. This time he asked how the company was doing and I gave him the numbers — up fourteen percent year over year, best Q2 since 2019, retention above ninety percent. He nodded and said "so it's doing well?" and I said yes. Then he asked "but are YOU doing well?" and I didn't have an answer. I said something about being busy, which is true but not what he was asking. The honest answer is I don't separate the two. The company does well, I'm doing well. The company struggles, I struggle. I don't know if that's dedication or if it's a problem.
entries.nudge_text: If the company disappeared tomorrow, what would be left of you?
entries.response_text: I honestly don't know. That's what scares me.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a weird moment today. Was driving back from a site visit and passed the old Whitfield Heating building — the original one on Division Avenue that Grandpa worked out of before we moved to the new place in '02. It's a laundromat now. The sign is gone, the awning's different, there's nothing left of what it was. Just a building someone else uses for something else. I pulled over and sat there for a minute. Not because I was sad exactly. More like I was looking at the future.

Someday the new building will be something else too. Someday the name on the trucks will change, or the trucks will be gone, or the whole industry will be different. Nothing I do is permanent. Nothing Grandpa did was permanent. The only thing that lasts is whether you did it right while you were doing it.

Something shifted sitting in that parking lot. Like maybe I've been so focused on holding everything together exactly as it is that I forgot the point was never the building or the sign. It was how you treated people, how you showed up, whether you left things better than you found them. Grandpa didn't preserve his father's business — he built his own. Maybe that's what the nod meant. Not "keep this the same." Just "do it right." I'm still not sure about solar. But I'm less afraid of the question now.
entries.nudge_text: What changed between last month and this parking lot moment?
entries.response_text: I stopped seeing it as either/or. It was never about furnaces.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.
entries.nudge_text: What was the quiet in the group chat about?
entries.response_text: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

entries.initial_entry: Supervisor meeting. Dr. Keane asked me to commit to one research question by next Friday and I heard myself say yes, absolutely, no problem. Walked home and could not stop thinking about the three other directions I had sketched out last month.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?
entries.response_text: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

entries.initial_entry: Library until six. Drafted the introduction for the narrowed thesis proposal, which took longer than it should have because I kept second-guessing paragraph transitions. Callum and I walked to the chippie on Clerk Street after.

Friday call with my mother went well for once. She asked about the thesis and I described the morphological analysis angle in simple terms — how computers learn to break Arabic words into their root pieces. She said it sounded useful, which from her is high praise.

I felt like myself on that call. Not performing, not deflecting her questions about when I am coming home. Just explaining something I actually care about to someone who wanted to listen. She even asked a follow-up about how the root system works differently in dialects. Small thing, but it settled something in me.

Second entry today because my brain will not shut off. After the call with my mother I went for a walk up Arthur's Seat. Halfway up I got this rush of clarity — or maybe it was just adrenaline — where I started thinking about what it would look like to pivot my thesis entirely. Not the careful narrowing Dr. Keane wants. Something bigger. Cross-linguistic transfer models for under-resourced dialects. Levantine, Gulf, Maghrebi. My heart was actually racing on the hill and it was not from the climb.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?

entries.initial_entry:
Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.
entries.nudge_text: What did you want her to say instead?
entries.response_text: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.
entries.nudge_text: Why print it out if you might not show it?
entries.response_text: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from my cousin Faris today, forwarding photos from his daughter's first birthday party back in Amman. The whole family was there — uncles, aunts, my mother holding the baby with that particular look she gets. I was not invited to skip it; I just was not there. No one said anything pointed but the group chat went quiet for a while after the photos dropped and I could feel the space where my name should have been.

Nothing happened, really. I ate leftover lentil soup, re-read a paper on morphological parsing, tried to focus. My flatmate Callum asked if I wanted to go to a pub quiz and I said no, then sat there wishing I had said yes.

entries.response_text: It is not that they are angry. It is that they have stopped expecting me to show up. That is worse, I think. Like I have been reclassified from "absent" to "gone."

entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from pushing back in the meeting?
entries.response_text: Because pushing back feels like making a scene, and I do not make scenes. My father never raised his voice in a meeting in his life. But I keep wondering if the question I pick will be the one that bores me by October.

Then I sat on the bench at the top and the wind hit me and I thought about what pivoting would mean. Another meeting with Keane where I contradict what I said three days ago. My mother asking again when I will finish. My uncle Hisham, who helped fund the first year, shaking his head at another delay. Everyone back home already thinks this PhD was an impulsive detour, and changing direction again would prove them right.

So I walked back down, made tea, opened the narrowed proposal draft. Still sitting here with it. The cursor is blinking at the end of paragraph three and I have not typed anything in forty minutes.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you brought the bigger idea to Keane?

entries.initial_entry:
Department social last night at The Pear Tree. I went because Dr. Keane mentioned it would be good to show face, and I know what that means in academic circles — it means attendance is optional in the way that prayer is optional at a family gathering. So I went.

Ended up in a long conversation with Priya from the NLP group. She is working on code-switching detection in Hindi-English tweets and the way she talked about it — with this specific, focused energy — made me jealous. Not of the topic. Of the certainty. She has her question, her method, her timeline. She did not seem to need a detour.

I told her about the dialect transfer idea. She said it sounded fascinating and asked why I was not doing it. I said timing, funding, the usual. She shrugged and said "those are real constraints" in a way that felt like she was politely letting me off the hook. I appreciated it and hated it at the same time.
entries.nudge_text: What did you want her to say instead?
entries.response_text: Something like "do it anyway." Or "those are excuses." Someone outside the family orbit giving me permission to be reckless. Which is ridiculous because I am thirty-eight years old and should not need permission from a colleague I barely know.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted the narrowed thesis proposal to Dr. Keane this morning. Forty-two pages, bibliography cleaned up, research questions scoped to morphological segmentation for Modern Standard Arabic. Felt like handing in a homework assignment I had done properly rather than one that kept me up at night, but there is something to be said for finishing.

Callum and I cooked mansaf tonight — or my version of it, which involves Greek yogurt because Edinburgh does not have decent jameed. He kept asking questions about the spices and I caught myself explaining things the way my mother does, with small corrections and zero patience. Made me laugh.

After dinner I sat with my laptop and, almost without thinking about it, opened a blank document and started outlining the dialect transfer idea. Not as a thesis. Just notes. No one has to see it. I wrote for an hour and it was the most alive I have felt in front of a screen in weeks.

My uncle Hisham called today. Not the usual Friday family call — a separate, pointed call. He asked how the PhD was going and I told him I submitted the proposal. He said good, asked about the timeline, and then said he hoped I was not "chasing new rabbits" — his phrase, not mine. Apparently my mother mentioned something about dialect research and he connected dots.

I told him everything was on track, the proposal was submitted, Dr. Keane was happy. All true. I did not mention the outline document from last night. It is not lying. It is managing information. People share what is relevant and I shared what was relevant.

Went to the Edinburgh Farmers' Market at Castle Terrace this morning, mostly for the za'atar bread from the Lebanese stall. Ran into Priya and her partner. We talked about the summer conference circuit and she mentioned a workshop on low-resource NLP in Copenhagen in September. She looked at me when she said it, like she knew.

I came home and emailed Dr. Keane asking for a meeting next week. Just to discuss, I wrote. I did not say what about. But I printed out the dialect transfer outline and put it in my bag. Maybe I will bring it, maybe I will not. It is in the bag though. That feels like enough for today.
entries.nudge_text: Why print it out if you might not show it?
entries.response_text: Because I need to know it is there. Like a door I have not walked through yet but I can see. If I leave it at home it is just a file on my laptop. In the bag it is something I chose to carry. I think I am going to show it. I think I have been working up to this for weeks and I just did not want to admit it because admitting it means it matters, and if it matters then Keane saying no will actually hurt.

entries.initial_entry:
Eleven hours behind the wheel today. My knees ache in a way that reminds me I am not the young man who once walked to the mosque before dawn without thinking twice. But the tips were good, and I have now saved enough for the lamb. Hassan does not know — he would insist on paying himself, and that is precisely why I cannot tell him.

My father slaughtered the lamb for my aqiqah with his own hands. I watched him do the same for my sons. Here in Michigan there is a halal butcher who will prepare everything properly. Different hands, same intention. I tell myself this is enough. Some nights I believe it.
Picked up a young couple from the airport. They asked if I minded them eating in the car. I said no. They left crumbs everywhere. I vacuumed at the gas station before my next ride. This is the job.

Layla asked me today why I still drive when my back gives me trouble. She said it gently, the way she says everything, but I heard what she meant: why don't you rest? Why don't you let Hassan take care of things?

I did not know how to explain that resting is not rest for me. My father worked until the week before he died. His father the same. A man provides. A man contributes. I know Layla was raised here and thinks differently about these things, and I love her for making space in her home for me, but I cannot sit in that house eating food I did not earn while my grandchildren grow up thinking their grandfather is just an old man who watches television.

She meant well. I know this. But I could not look at her when I answered, because if I had, she would have seen too much. I said something about needing the exercise, about enjoying the passengers. Lies dressed up as half-truths. The real answer lives in my chest where I cannot reach it with words.

Tonight I drove until midnight. Every fare felt like proof that I am still useful.
entries.nudge_text: You want them to see you're useful, but you're hiding the proof.
entries.response_text: Because if I show them the money, they will say I am too old to earn it. The secret is the only way the work stays mine.

entries.initial_entry:
Same-day entry, different weight. Woke up and my first thought was about the conversation yesterday. Then Fajr, then coffee, then Hassan asked if I wanted to come to his office for lunch because they are having some kind of celebration for a coworker. I said yes because he rarely asks.

His office is all glass and computers and young people in clothes I do not understand. They were kind to me. Someone asked what I did before driving and I said accounting and they seemed surprised, then interested. One young woman asked about Jordan and I talked about Amman in a way I had not talked about it in months. She listened.

On the drive home Hassan said "You should come more often, Baba." I am still thinking about why that made my eyes sting.

Three rides cancelled after they saw my name. Fourth passenger asked where I was "really" from. I answered. He said nothing for the rest of the trip. No tip. I am too tired to feel anything about this tonight. Tomorrow I will feel it.
entries.nudge_text: Is the tiredness about the hours, or about something else?
entries.response_text: Both. Always both. Not something I have words for right now.

entries.initial_entry:
My brother called from Amman to say I should come back. "What are you doing there, driving strangers around? You had a career. You had respect." He does not understand that respect here is different, that I am building something he cannot see from where he sits.

He thinks I left everything behind. He does not know that I brought everything with me — the prayers, the food, the way I greet the older men at the mosque. I did not abandon our customs. I carry them. The car is just a car. What I do between the rides, who I am when no one is watching, that is what matters.

I told him I was happy. He did not believe me. That is his problem, not mine.

The aqiqah was yesterday. I did not write then because I wanted to hold the day without examining it.

The lamb was perfect. The butcher did everything correctly and I stood with Hassan as we distributed the meat — a third to the family, a third to friends, a third to those in need. My grandson slept through most of it, which is as it should be. He will not remember this day. But I will remember his face, and Layla holding him, and the way Hassan looked at me when he realized I had paid for everything.

He tried to argue. I said: "This is my right as his grandfather. You will have your turn when your son has a son." He went quiet. Later, when the guests had left, he came to me in the kitchen and said only "Shukran, Baba." Thank you. Nothing more. It was enough.

My father would have been proud. Not of me — he did not give praise easily — but of the continuation. That we are still here, still doing what was done for us, still passing it forward. The country changed. The language around us changed. But the lamb was shared according to the old ways. That matters. That is the thread I refuse to let break.
entries.nudge_text: What else feels like that kind of thread for you?
entries.response_text: Friday prayers. The recipes my mother taught Layla. Teaching my grandson the Arabic words for grandfather and bread and home before he learns them in English. Small things. But small things are how the rope stays woven.

entries.initial_entry: Second entry today. Drove three hours after the evening prayers. Quiet roads. One passenger, an older woman going to the hospital to visit her husband. We did not speak much but she thanked me twice when I helped her with the door. Some shifts feel like service. Tonight was one.

Another steering committee meeting where half the room was on their phones. I run a tight agenda and still can't get people to care about infrastructure decisions that will haunt us for years. Nobody wants to own anything hard anymore.
entries.nudge_text: What does it cost you when they check out like that?
entries.response_text: It makes me wonder why I bother holding the line if nobody else will.

entries.initial_entry:
Found out today they picked Raj for the VP role. The announcement went out at 2pm in a company-wide email. I heard about it from my skip-level before that, which I guess was supposed to soften the blow. Raj has been here six years. I have been here nineteen.

I am not saying he is unqualified. He ran the platform migration last year and it went well. But I have been running the reliability council since before he joined the company. I built the incident response process from nothing. Three directors told me privately that they assumed the role was mine. So either those conversations meant nothing or something changed that nobody told me about.

Had a one-on-one with my manager afterward. She said the decision came down to "organizational energy" and "forward-looking vision." I asked what that meant concretely and she pivoted to talking about how much they value my technical depth. That phrase again. Technical depth. It is starting to feel like a box they put you in when they do not see you leading.
entries.nudge_text: Those three directors who assumed it was yours -- did any of them advocate for you?
entries.response_text: I actually do not know. I assumed they did but maybe assuming was the whole problem.

entries.initial_entry: Spent most of Saturday reviewing the Q3 reliability targets. Updated my tracking sheet for the team. Chen is slipping again, two missed deadlines this month.

Cannot stop thinking about the VP thing. Told myself I was over it but then I saw Raj's new title in the org chart and my chest got tight. Thirty years and I am still a "senior individual contributor." Hate that I care this much.
entries.nudge_text: You said you hate that you care. What would it look like if you didn't?
entries.response_text: Honestly I have no idea. I do not know who I am if I stop keeping score.

entries.initial_entry:
Weird thing happened at the barbecue yesterday. Tom from next door, he retired from Boeing last year, he was talking about building a deck. Not a fancy deck, just a simple one off the back of his kitchen. And he was describing the process of picking the lumber, getting the permits, measuring everything twice, and I realized I was genuinely envious. Not of the deck. Of the way he talked about it. Like it mattered to him for no reason other than he wanted to do it.

I have not built anything with my hands in years. Used to do woodworking in my twenties before the career took off. I still have my dad's old chisels in the garage somewhere, wrapped in an oilcloth. I think about them sometimes.

After the barbecue I went home and sat in the garage for a while. Found the chisels. They were right where I left them, maybe eight years ago. The handles are still good. I did not do anything with them, just held one and felt the weight. Karen came out and asked what I was doing and I said "nothing" which I guess was technically true. But it did not feel like nothing.

Long week. Raj scheduled a "vision alignment" meeting for the whole engineering org and I had to sit through it smiling. He is reorganizing the reliability council under his new structure which means my committee now reports up through him. Three years of building that group and now I am presenting to someone I used to mentor.

Karen noticed I have been staying up late. She asked if I was okay and I said I was fine, just busy. She did not push it. Part of me wanted her to push it.
entries.nudge_text: Why not just tell Karen what's actually going on?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent Sunday morning updating my LinkedIn profile. Added a few bullet points about the incident response framework and the reliability council. Looked at job postings out of curiosity. Principal engineer roles at three companies. Two of them pay more than what I make now.

Did not apply to any of them. Just screenshot the listings and saved them to a folder on my desktop called "Options." Have not decided if that is planning or procrastination.
entries.nudge_text: That folder -- when do you think you will actually open it again?

entries.initial_entry:
Had a skip-level with the CTO today. She asked me to lead a new cross-functional task force on AI readiness. Big scope, high visibility, reports directly to her. On paper it is exactly what I have been wanting. Direct line to the top, chance to shape strategy.

But I keep thinking about why now. Two weeks after passing me over they hand me a consolation project. Raj probably suggested it. I said yes because saying no would look petty. Not sure if I actually want to do it or if I just cannot stand to be seen turning down something important.

Took a half day and went to the hardware store. Bought some walnut stock and a new set of clamps. Nothing serious, just enough to try a small cutting board. Felt strange buying something that has nothing to do with work or credentials or org charts.

Spent the afternoon in the garage planing the wood down. My technique is rusty but the motions came back faster than I expected. I was out there for three hours and did not check my phone once. I cannot remember the last time that happened.
entries.nudge_text: Three hours without your phone. What was going through your head out there?
entries.response_text: Mostly just the grain direction and whether my measurements were right. That was the whole point I think. Just the thing in front of me.

entries.initial_entry:
First AI task force meeting was today. I spent all weekend preparing a charter document, twelve pages, frameworks, timelines, resource asks. I presented it and the CTO said "this is thorough" which I used to take as a compliment but now I wonder if it means "too much." She asked if we could "move faster and iterate." That phrase.

Raj sat in on the meeting. He did not say much but he was nodding along like he was evaluating me. Maybe I am reading into it. Probably I am reading into it. But I have been in enough rooms to know when someone is there to observe rather than participate.

Came home and Karen had dinner ready which was nice. She asked about my day and I gave her the short version. She said it sounded like a good opportunity. I agreed. We watched something on TV and I fell asleep on the couch before it ended. She put a blanket on me. I woke up at 2am still on the couch and went upstairs and she was already asleep. These are the kinds of things that keep happening and I am not sure either of us is noticing how much space there is between us now.
entries.nudge_text: That blanket she put on you -- when did you two stop talking about the hard stuff?
entries.response_text: We talk. Just not about the things that would require either of us to change something.

entries.initial_entry:
Finished the cutting board. Walnut with maple inlay, nothing fancy but the joints are tight. Rubbed it down with mineral oil and it looks good. Brought it inside and set it on the counter.

Karen picked it up and turned it over a few times. She said "You made this?" and I said yeah. She put it down and said "It's really beautiful, Gerry." Something about the way she said my name. Have not heard her say it like that in a long time.

Second task force meeting went better. I cut the charter down to four pages and proposed a two-week sprint to prototype something instead of planning for another month. The CTO seemed pleased. Raj was not there this time which helped. I could just run the room without wondering if I was being graded.

Chen came to me after the meeting and said he wants to transfer onto the task force. I was surprised. He has been underperforming on my reliability team but maybe that is because he does not care about reliability work. Maybe I have been measuring him against the wrong thing. I told him I would think about it but I think I am going to say yes.

Ordered more walnut and some cherry this time. Thinking about making a small side table next. Karen asked if she could watch me work in the garage sometime and I said sure. We are going to Portland together next weekend. Her idea but I said yes without hesitating which is new. The Options folder is still on my desktop. I have not opened it in two weeks. Not sure what that means yet but it does not bother me the way it probably should.

Warehouse inventory count done. Sumac shipment from Amman arrived three days late but everything accounted for. Mahmoud asked if we could advance his paycheck again—his sister needs surgery back home. I said yes without thinking, same as always. The new accountant raised her eyebrows at me when I told her. She has a point about cash flow, but what am I supposed to do, pretend I do not know the man's family?

Daughter called about the house listing in San Diego again. Sent me photos of the backyard. Palm trees. She says Layla would love it there. I looked at the photos while eating dinner alone. The kitchen in that house is twice the size of mine. I do not know what I would do with all that space.

Called my brother in Beirut after. He sounded tired. His granddaughter started school this year. He sent me her photo in uniform.

New year, everyone wants to talk about resolutions. The woman at the coffee shop asked me mine. I told her I wanted to worry less. She laughed like I had made a joke.

Meeting with the refugee resettlement coordinator today. They have a family from Sudan arriving next month and wanted to know if we had any openings. I said yes before checking with HR. We probably do not, honestly. But I remembered when I arrived in 1983 with two suitcases and a cousin's phone number. Someone gave me a chance. I cannot be the person who closes that door.

Fatima from the warehouse stopped me in the parking lot after. She wanted to thank me for the time off last month when her mother was in hospice. She cried. I did not know what to say so I just stood there. These moments—they are not comfortable. But they are the reason I still come to work at fifty-eight instead of sitting on some beach.

Nadia sent another email about succession planning. I deleted it without reading.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about the succession stuff you keep avoiding?

entries.initial_entry:
Good day. Really good day, actually.

Rashid—the young man from Yemen we hired last spring—closed his first wholesale deal today. A restaurant chain in Ann Arbor, twelve locations. He came into my office after with this look on his face, like he could not believe what he had just done. Three years ago this kid was living in a camp in Djibouti. Now he is wearing a blazer and shaking hands with purchasing managers.

I took him to lunch to celebrate. He told me about his plans—he wants to bring his mother over, get her a little apartment nearby. He asked me how I did it, building something from nothing. I did not have a good answer. Luck, maybe. Stubbornness. The right people believing in me when they had no reason to.

Driving home I thought about my father. He never got to see what this became. He died thinking I had made a mistake leaving Amman. Maybe I did. But I also built something here. Something that matters beyond profit margins.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think your father would say if he could see the business now?
entries.response_text: Honestly? He would probably still think I should have stayed. Taken over the family shop like my brothers. But he would understand Rashid. He would see himself in that story. My father always helped people, even when we had nothing. He would recognize what I am trying to do, even if he never would have done it this way.

entries.initial_entry: Mahmoud's sister died. Surgery was not enough. I sat with him in my office for an hour while he wept. What do you even say? There is no fixing this.
entries.nudge_text: How are you holding up after that?
entries.response_text: I am not, really. I keep thinking about whether we should have sent more money, faster. If it would have made a difference. Probably not. But the thought keeps circling. These are the things I carry home with me. Layla asks why I look tired and I do not know how to explain that I spent my afternoon holding a grown man while he cried for his sister six thousand miles away.

entries.initial_entry:
Daughter flew in for the weekend. We had dinner at the house, just the three of us—her, Layla, and me. She brought brochures. Retirement communities. Active senior living. There was one with a golf course.

I do not play golf. I told her that. She said I could learn. Layla just watched us go back and forth. At some point I realized I was gripping my fork too hard. I let go and changed the subject to her work. But it is still sitting in me, this feeling like I am being asked to disappear into some comfortable ending I did not choose.
entries.nudge_text: What would you choose, if it were entirely up to you?
entries.response_text: I do not know anymore. Maybe that is the problem. I have spent so long making choices for everyone else that I forgot how to want something just for myself. The honest answer is I would keep working. Keep showing up. But I know that is not fair to the people who want more of me. So I just keep not deciding.

entries.initial_entry:
Winter break finally started and I should be relieved. Instead I feel this strange hollowness. My cousin Rania announced her engagement yesterday at the family gathering—everyone was so happy, talking about wedding dates and apartments and furniture. I smiled and hugged her but inside I was somewhere else entirely.

I keep thinking about that email from my old friend Amir. He's starting an educational nonprofit in Morocco and asked if I'd consider joining for six months. Six months. The thought makes my chest tight in two directions—terrified of leaving my position, my apartment, my carefully built routine. But also something like hunger.

My mother would never understand. She still talks about how "irresponsible" Malaysia was. But I was 24 then. I'm 29 now. At what point do I stop blaming responsibility for my fear and fear for my responsibility?
entries.nudge_text: What would you actually lose?

entries.initial_entry: Spent the morning organizing my savings spreadsheet. I have enough for eight months of expenses if something goes wrong. That number used to make me feel safe. Today it just felt like a number. Had coffee with my sister. She mentioned a colleague looking for a roommate, assuming I might want to save on rent. Didn't correct her assumption.
entries.nudge_text: What changed about that number?
entries.response_text: I think I realized I've been saving for an emergency that might never come. Or maybe saving so I'd have an excuse not to take risks. "Can't leave, I'm building my safety net." But the net is built. So now what?

entries.initial_entry: First day back at school. Taught the same Nizar Qabbani poems I've taught for four years. The students were half-asleep. I was fully awake but pretending otherwise. Emailed Amir back asking for more details. Just details. Not committing to anything. Put my phone in my desk drawer so I wouldn't check it every five minutes.
entries.nudge_text: What are you hoping the details will tell you?
entries.response_text: Whether I can do this without burning everything down. I want to know if there's a version of this where I don't have to choose between feeling alive and feeling safe. Probably there isn't. But I need the details to prove that to myself.

entries.initial_entry:
The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.
entries.nudge_text: What makes you say it sounds pathetic?
entries.response_text: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.

entries.initial_entry:
Got pulled into a meeting about "organizational restructuring." They didn't say layoffs but the room felt like a dentist's waiting area. My manager kept using phrases like "right-sizing" and "strategic realignment." I sat there nodding, taking notes, asking no questions.

Afterwards I walked to the parking lot and just sat in the car for twenty minutes. Checked the bank app, checked the savings, did the math again. If they let me go tomorrow, we'd be fine for six months. Maybe seven if we cut the girls' tutoring. But that's not the point. The point is I shouldn't have to be doing this math.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned asking no questions. What would you have asked?
entries.response_text: Whether my department is on the list. Whether seventeen years counts for anything. But you don't ask those things in front of everyone. You wait, you read the room, you figure it out without making yourself a target.

entries.initial_entry:
No news on the restructuring. Business as usual. Finished the API migration ahead of schedule. Had lunch with Marwan from the infrastructure team. He's been here twelve years and seemed calm about the whole thing. Said he's not worried because his brother-in-law works in HR. Must be nice.

Rima's mother is visiting next week. Started cleaning the guest room. The girls are complaining about having to be "presentable" all weekend. I told them it's three days, they'll survive.

Rima's mother made a comment about how I should "think bigger" and maybe start something of my own like Fadi. I smiled and poured her more coffee. What does she want me to say? That I'm keeping her daughter's life stable while her golden nephew burns through investor money?
entries.nudge_text: What did Rima say when her mother said that?
entries.response_text: Nothing. She changed the subject to the girls' school. Which is its own kind of answer, I suppose. She knows I don't want to hear it, so she steers away. I'm grateful for that, but also wondering what she actually thinks.

entries.initial_entry:
A recruiter reached out on LinkedIn. Startup in Dubai, fintech, "ground floor opportunity." The salary would be higher but it's a two-year contract with no guarantees after that. I looked at the job description for maybe fifteen minutes, then closed the tab.

Rima would say I should at least talk to them. My father would have said a bird in the hand. I keep thinking about what happens if the restructuring goes through and I turned down the one door that opened. But I also keep thinking about what happens if I jump and there's nothing underneath.
entries.nudge_text: What would you need to know to feel okay about either choice?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I don't think any amount of information would make me feel okay about it. Some decisions just feel wrong no matter which way you go.

entries.initial_entry:
Thursday already. Week went by fast. Shipped the authentication module, got a thumbs up from the client. The restructuring email came out finally, my department is safe. No cuts in engineering. Marwan was right, his brother-in-law probably knew the whole time.

Funny how relief doesn't feel like happiness. It feels like unclenching. Like you've been holding your jaw tight for weeks and suddenly you notice the ache. Took the girls out for ice cream after work. Layla asked why I was being "weird and nice." Teenagers are brutal.

Team offsite today. The new VP of Engineering flew in from the head office in Riyadh and gave a presentation about "innovation culture" and "embracing disruption." Half the slides had stock photos of people jumping off cliffs. I wanted to ask him if he's ever actually jumped off anything, but I kept my mouth shut and clapped when everyone else clapped.

During the breakout session, one of the younger engineers, Sara, pitched an idea to rewrite our core platform in a new framework. She was passionate, articulate, the VP loved it. I could see at least fifteen ways it would break our existing integrations. But the room was electric, everyone was nodding, and I wasn't going to be the old guy who kills momentum.

After the session, my manager asked me privately what I thought. I told him the migration would take eighteen months minimum and we'd lose two major clients during the transition. He nodded and said "that's why I need you in the room, Nabil." Which is supposed to be a compliment but felt more like being told my job is to be the brakes on everyone else's engine.

I don't want to be the person who always says no. But someone has to think about what we'd lose.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to say what you think without being 'the brakes'?

entries.initial_entry:
Had the annual review. "Exceeds expectations" across the board. My manager wrote that I'm "the backbone of the engineering team" and "a stabilizing presence during periods of change." I should feel good about this. I do feel good about it.

Rima asked what they said and I told her. She smiled and said "of course." Like it was obvious. Like there was never any doubt. And maybe that's the thing that bothers me — not that people expect me to be reliable, but that nobody finds it surprising.
entries.nudge_text: What would you want them to find surprising?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe that I had an idea they didn't expect. That I pushed for something risky and it worked. But that's not me, and I'm not sure I actually want it to be. I just want the option to be seen differently, even if I never use it.

entries.initial_entry: Quiet week. Wrapped up documentation for the Q3 release. Took the girls to their grandmother's on Saturday. Drove back along the coast road instead of the highway. Rima fell asleep in the passenger seat. Good drive.

The office was quiet today, most of the team out for the holiday weekend. I stayed because there was a deployment window and someone needed to be there. Rima asked why I didn't just let the junior handle it. I told her it's not about trust, it's about making sure nothing breaks while the client's systems are live.

Seventeen years at this company. The chair in my office has molded to my shape. There's something comforting about that, even if it sounds pathetic written down. I know every hallway, every server room, every shortcut in the build system. That knowledge is worth something.
entries.nudge_text: What makes you say it sounds pathetic?
entries.response_text: Because my cousin Fadi started his own consultancy two years ago and everyone at family dinners talks about him like he discovered electricity. Nobody asks me about my work. I think staying in one place for this long makes people assume you had no other options.
