entries.initial_entry:
Spent the last two hours staring at a term sheet from a Lisbon-based startup that wants us to come in as operational partners. The numbers are solid enough but what actually caught me was the founder, this woman who walked away from a consulting gig to build a port logistics platform from scratch. She reminded me of how I felt fifteen years ago when I signed my first warehouse lease with money I did not really have.

The thing is, I told Ingrid I would slow down this year. We said that after the second exit. That was supposed to be the point where we had enough runway to just breathe for a while. And we do have the runway. But I keep circling back to this Lisbon deal like a dog that will not leave a bone alone.

I know what the smart move is. Park the capital, let the reserves do their work, stay disciplined. But I feel this pull and I cannot explain it rationally. It is not about the money. It is something about the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome.
entries.nudge_text: What does that edge feel like compared to the breathing room you mentioned?

entries.initial_entry:
Call with the Lisbon founder again today, third one this week. She pitched the expansion roadmap and honestly it is ambitious but not reckless. I ran the downside scenarios on a napkin while she was talking, which is a habit Ingrid would recognize and roll her eyes at. Worst case we lose about what I spent on that sailboat restoration three years ago, so it is recoverable.

Ingrid asked over dinner if I had decided. I said not yet. That is technically true but I think she could tell from my tone that I have already half-committed in my head. She did not push back, just said "make sure you are not chasing the feeling again." I told her I did not know what she meant, which was a lie.

Valentine's dinner last night, the Italian place near Erasmusbrug. We had a good time, actually managed to not talk about business for most of it. Ingrid brought up taking a trip to Gotland this summer to visit her mother, maybe staying a few weeks. I said yes immediately, genuinely meant it.

But then on the walk home I caught myself thinking about the Lisbon timeline and whether a few weeks in Sweden would slow down onboarding if we signed the deal. I did not say that out loud. I wonder when I started splitting like this, one half of me wanting to just be present and the other half always running the next scenario.
entries.nudge_text: When you say splitting, which half feels more like you?
entries.response_text: That is the problem, they both do. The planning half is not some unwanted intruder, it is the part that built everything we have. But the part that wanted to just walk along the water with Ingrid without calculating anything, that felt real too. I think they used to be the same thing but somewhere along the way they started pulling in different directions.

entries.initial_entry:
Saturday morning, sitting with coffee in the kitchen. The house is quiet. Ingrid went to the market early which means she is buying too much cheese again and I will pretend to be surprised when she gets back.

I made a decision last night. I am going to pass on the Lisbon deal. Not because the numbers are wrong or because the founder is not impressive but because I realized I was looking for a feeling, not a business case. The feeling of the leap. That rush of committing to something uncertain and then having to make it work. I know that feeling well and it has served me, but I do not think chasing it right now is the responsible move.

We have the reserves in a good place. The studio has three portfolio companies that need attention. Ingrid and I are in a solid rhythm. I do not need to blow that up to feel alive. At least that is what I am telling myself this morning. Ask me again next week when I am bored.

One of our portfolio companies, the cold storage outfit in Antwerp, just lost their anchor client. Found out this morning from a panicked call from the founder who I could barely understand because he was speaking so fast. This is the company where we parked the biggest chunk of operational capital.

I spent the entire day on calls, restructuring scenarios, talking to our lawyer about contract terms. By evening I had a workable plan but the whole time there was this tight feeling in my chest. Not about the money exactly, we can absorb the hit if we need to. It is more that I had just gotten comfortable with the idea of things being stable and now the ground shifted.

What irritates me is that I know this is part of the game. I have been through worse. The first company almost went under twice before we found our footing. But back then the instability felt like fuel. Now it just feels like a threat to something I have built up carefully. I do not know when that switch happened.

Antwerp situation is stabilizing. We found a replacement client, not as big but enough to keep the operation running while we diversify the pipeline. I spent three days in Antwerp this week working on-site with the team and it reminded me that I actually enjoy the problem-solving part of this work more than the deal-making part.

There is something specific about walking into a warehouse that is half-empty and figuring out how to fill it. Not the adrenaline of a new deal but the satisfaction of fixing what is broken. Maybe that is the version of this work that does not require me to keep jumping off cliffs.

Had a long phone call with Ingrid tonight and told her about the replacement client. She sounded relieved, which I noticed because she had been careful not to sound worried all week. She said she is glad I was there in person rather than just sending emails. She is right. The founder needed to see someone calm in the room. I think I needed to be that person for my own reasons too.

Got an email from the Lisbon founder today. She closed the round without us, brought in a Madrid-based firm instead. Good for her. But I would be lying if I said it did not sting. Not because we lost the deal, I walked away from it deliberately. It stings because reading her update about the progress they have made in the last three weeks made me feel like I am standing still.

I know that is irrational. The studio is doing fine, the Antwerp crisis proved the model works, we are in good shape. But "good shape" feels like treading water sometimes. Like I am maintaining rather than building. And maintaining is not something I have ever been good at sitting with.

Ingrid would say this is exactly the pattern she warned me about. I get things to a good place, feel restless, then go looking for the next thing to keep myself occupied. She is probably right. But knowing it is a pattern does not make the restlessness go away. I keep checking the inbox for something that will make tomorrow feel different from today.
entries.nudge_text: You said maintaining is not something you sit with. But you also chose it. What changed?
entries.response_text: Nothing changed. I just had a slow day and read an email at the wrong time. It is fine. The Antwerp thing took a lot out of me and I probably just need a proper weekend off. Ingrid suggested we drive out to Kinderdijk, maybe I will take her up on that.

entries.initial_entry: Did the Kinderdijk trip. Walked for hours, barely talked, ate pannenkoeken at that place near the windmills. It was exactly what I needed and I managed to leave my phone in the car for most of it.
entries.nudge_text: What was it like having the phone in the car?

entries.initial_entry:
Portfolio review meeting today with all three founders. The numbers are where they should be, maybe slightly ahead. The cold storage team in Antwerp has a real pipeline now and the other two are humming along. I should feel good about this and I suppose I do, in the way you feel good about a clean audit.

A guy I used to work with in Stockholm called afterward, wants to pitch me on a drone delivery concept for Nordic rural areas. My first instinct was interest. My second instinct was to check whether the first instinct was the problem. I told him to send the deck and I would look at it. Not committing to anything, just looking.

Stayed up too late reading the drone deck. It is actually well put together. The unit economics make sense for the regions they are targeting and the regulatory window is real. I caught myself building a financial model in a spreadsheet at one in the morning, which is exactly the kind of thing I do when something grabs me.

But I closed the laptop and went to bed. Ingrid was already asleep. I lay there thinking about the Antwerp scare and how it felt to have something we built nearly fall apart. I do not want to stretch us thin again. The reserves are there for a reason. Tomorrow I will look at the deck with fresh eyes and probably find three deal-breakers I missed because I was running on adrenaline.

Looked at the drone deck again over lunch. Found one real concern around the supply chain for replacement parts but the other issues I expected to find were not there. The founder has thought through most of the obvious failure modes.

Told Ingrid about it. She asked how much capital it would need and I gave her the number. She went quiet for a while and then said it was my call but that she wanted me to think about why the last time I felt this way about a deal I chose to walk away. I do not have a good answer for why this time feels different. Maybe it does not feel different. Maybe I am just further from the last scare.
entries.nudge_text: Is the distance from the scare the reason, or the excuse?
entries.response_text: Probably the excuse. I know that. Three weeks ago I was rattled by Antwerp and swore I would not overextend. Now I am sitting here telling myself this one is different because the deck looks solid. But every deck looks solid when you want it to. The honest answer is I miss the feeling of building something from nothing and I am trying to dress that up as a business decision.

entries.initial_entry: Called my Stockholm friend back and told him I need two weeks to think. He said fine. I keep going back and forth and I hate that I cannot just pick a lane and stay in it.
entries.nudge_text: What if both lanes are yours?
entries.response_text: Then I would need to figure out a speed that works for both of them. Ingrid said something like that once, that maybe the problem is not what I want but how much of it I want at once. I did not really hear it at the time but it is starting to make sense. Maybe there is a version of this where I do not have to choose between building and protecting, I just have to stop pretending one of them is the whole answer.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the last two hours staring at a term sheet from a Lisbon-based startup that wants us to come in as operational partners. The numbers are solid enough but what actually caught me was the founder, this woman who walked away from a consulting gig to build a port logistics platform from scratch. She reminded me of how I felt fifteen years ago when I signed my first warehouse lease with money I did not really have.

The thing is, I told Ingrid I would slow down this year. We said that after the second exit. That was supposed to be the point where we had enough runway to just breathe for a while. And we do have the runway. But I keep circling back to this Lisbon deal like a dog that will not leave a bone alone.

I know what the smart move is. Park the capital, let the reserves do their work, stay disciplined. But I feel this pull and I cannot explain it rationally. It is not about the money. It is something about the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome.
entries.nudge_text: What does that edge feel like compared to the breathing room you mentioned?

entries.initial_entry:
Call with the Lisbon founder again today, third one this week. She pitched the expansion roadmap and honestly it is ambitious but not reckless. I ran the downside scenarios on a napkin while she was talking, which is a habit Ingrid would recognize and roll her eyes at. Worst case we lose about what I spent on that sailboat restoration three years ago, so it is recoverable.

Ingrid asked over dinner if I had decided. I said not yet. That is technically true but I think she could tell from my tone that I have already half-committed in my head. She did not push back, just said "make sure you are not chasing the feeling again." I told her I did not know what she meant, which was a lie.

Valentine's dinner last night, the Italian place near Erasmusbrug. We had a good time, actually managed to not talk about business for most of it. Ingrid brought up taking a trip to Gotland this summer to visit her mother, maybe staying a few weeks. I said yes immediately, genuinely meant it.

But then on the walk home I caught myself thinking about the Lisbon timeline and whether a few weeks in Sweden would slow down onboarding if we signed the deal. I did not say that out loud. I wonder when I started splitting like this, one half of me wanting to just be present and the other half always running the next scenario.
entries.nudge_text: When you say splitting, which half feels more like you?
entries.response_text: That is the problem, they both do. The planning half is not some unwanted intruder, it is the part that built everything we have. But the part that wanted to just walk along the water with Ingrid without calculating anything, that felt real too. I think they used to be the same thing but somewhere along the way they started pulling in different directions.

entries.initial_entry:
Saturday morning, sitting with coffee in the kitchen. The house is quiet. Ingrid went to the market early which means she is buying too much cheese again and I will pretend to be surprised when she gets back.

I made a decision last night. I am going to pass on the Lisbon deal. Not because the numbers are wrong or because the founder is not impressive but because I realized I was looking for a feeling, not a business case. The feeling of the leap. That rush of committing to something uncertain and then having to make it work. I know that feeling well and it has served me, but I do not think chasing it right now is the responsible move.

We have the reserves in a good place. The studio has three portfolio companies that need attention. Ingrid and I are in a solid rhythm. I do not need to blow that up to feel alive. At least that is what I am telling myself this morning. Ask me again next week when I am bored.

One of our portfolio companies, the cold storage outfit in Antwerp, just lost their anchor client. Found out this morning from a panicked call from the founder who I could barely understand because he was speaking so fast. This is the company where we parked the biggest chunk of operational capital.

I spent the entire day on calls, restructuring scenarios, talking to our lawyer about contract terms. By evening I had a workable plan but the whole time there was this tight feeling in my chest. Not about the money exactly, we can absorb the hit if we need to. It is more that I had just gotten comfortable with the idea of things being stable and now the ground shifted.

What irritates me is that I know this is part of the game. I have been through worse. The first company almost went under twice before we found our footing. But back then the instability felt like fuel. Now it just feels like a threat to something I have built up carefully. I do not know when that switch happened.

Antwerp situation is stabilizing. We found a replacement client, not as big but enough to keep the operation running while we diversify the pipeline. I spent three days in Antwerp this week working on-site with the team and it reminded me that I actually enjoy the problem-solving part of this work more than the deal-making part.

There is something specific about walking into a warehouse that is half-empty and figuring out how to fill it. Not the adrenaline of a new deal but the satisfaction of fixing what is broken. Maybe that is the version of this work that does not require me to keep jumping off cliffs.

Had a long phone call with Ingrid tonight and told her about the replacement client. She sounded relieved, which I noticed because she had been careful not to sound worried all week. She said she is glad I was there in person rather than just sending emails. She is right. The founder needed to see someone calm in the room. I think I needed to be that person for my own reasons too.

Got an email from the Lisbon founder today. She closed the round without us, brought in a Madrid-based firm instead. Good for her. But I would be lying if I said it did not sting. Not because we lost the deal, I walked away from it deliberately. It stings because reading her update about the progress they have made in the last three weeks made me feel like I am standing still.

I know that is irrational. The studio is doing fine, the Antwerp crisis proved the model works, we are in good shape. But "good shape" feels like treading water sometimes. Like I am maintaining rather than building. And maintaining is not something I have ever been good at sitting with.

Ingrid would say this is exactly the pattern she warned me about. I get things to a good place, feel restless, then go looking for the next thing to keep myself occupied. She is probably right. But knowing it is a pattern does not make the restlessness go away. I keep checking the inbox for something that will make tomorrow feel different from today.

entries.response_text: Nothing changed. I just had a slow day and read an email at the wrong time. It is fine. The Antwerp thing took a lot out of me and I probably just need a proper weekend off. Ingrid suggested we drive out to Kinderdijk, maybe I will take her up on that.

entries.nudge_text: What was it like having the phone in the car?

entries.initial_entry:
Just got off a three-hour call with the fulfillment warehouse. They messed up the labels on two hundred units again. Second time this quarter. Spent the rest of the afternoon writing a detailed process document for them because apparently verbal instructions mean absolutely nothing to these people. My eyes hurt and I skipped lunch entirely.

Nothing dramatic happened today, just the slow grind of keeping everything from falling apart. Camilo was supposed to handle ops this week but he got pulled into a brand partnership meeting that ran long. So here I am, doing both. Normal Tuesday.
entries.nudge_text: Does Camilo know you covered his ops stuff?

entries.initial_entry:
Got into the Semana Emprendedora accelerator. Out of four hundred applicants, twenty-two got selected. I found out this morning and I just sat there staring at the email for a minute before telling anyone. Called my mom first. She cried. Then Camilo. Then Laura.

This is the first external proof that what we're doing is real. Not just two guys burning savings in a rented office. We got picked. Somebody looked at our numbers, our pitch, our traction, and said yes. That feels different from just believing in it yourself.

First week of the accelerator done. We presented our metrics in front of the other cohort companies and the mentors. Our customer retention was the second highest in the room. I know because I checked everyone's slides. Only that sustainable fashion startup from Lima had us beat, and they've been operating two years longer.

It matters to me that I know where we stand relative to the others. Some people in the cohort seem content to just be "learning from the experience." I don't understand that. You're here to win something—funding, connections, credibility. If you're not tracking how you compare, what's the point? The mentors kept saying it's "not a competition," but everything is a ranking if you pay attention.

After the session I stayed late talking with one of the mentors, Adriana. She built a fintech company that got acquired in 2021. She told me my numbers were solid but asked me what happens when I burn out. I told her I don't burn out, I recalibrate. She laughed. Not sure if she was impressed or thought I was naive.
entries.nudge_text: What did Adriana's laugh mean to you?
entries.response_text: Honestly, it doesn't matter what she meant. She's been through it and she's testing whether I've got the mindset. Mentor-founder dynamics are like that. You perform confidence. That's the job.

entries.initial_entry: Sunday dashboard session with Camilo. Revenue up 14% month-over-month and CAC is actually down for the first time since launch. Finally something moving in the right direction on both lines at once.

Coming back to this because Laura and I got into it after the dashboard session. She wanted to go to brunch with her sister and I said I couldn't because I still needed to prep slides for Monday's accelerator check-in. She said I always say that. I told her the accelerator is twelve weeks and it's not forever. She said I said the same thing about the pitch competition, and before that it was the product launch.

She's not wrong that there's always something. But she's wrong that it's the same thing. Each one of these is a step. The pitch comp got us visibility. The launch got us revenue. The accelerator is getting us in front of investors. These aren't distractions from life—this is the thing. This is what I'm building.

I didn't say all that to her because it would've turned into a bigger fight. I just said I'd try to be done by two and we could go for a late lunch. She said fine. We went. It was fine. But I was checking my email under the table the whole time.

Accelerator mid-point review. Each company got fifteen minutes with the mentor panel. They liked our growth but pushed back on our unit economics. Said our margins are tight and that scaling with the current supplier setup could squeeze us. I knew this already. I've been running those models for weeks. But hearing it said out loud in a room with the other founders stung more than I expected.

The sustainable fashion startup from Lima—Daniela's company—got praised for their margin structure. She came up to me after and said something supportive, which was nice, but I just kept thinking about why her numbers look cleaner. She has a family connection to a textile manufacturer. That's not hustle, that's a head start. I know comparing like that isn't productive but I can't stop the math from running in my head.

Camilo told me afterward that the feedback was constructive and that we're still in a strong position. He's right on paper. But I don't want to be in a "strong position." I want to be the one they point to when they talk about this cohort in two years.
entries.nudge_text: What would being the one they point to actually get you?
entries.response_text: Credibility. The kind that opens doors before you even walk through them. When investors hear "oh, he was the standout from Semana Emprendedora 2025," that changes the conversation before I even pitch. It's leverage. First-mover advantage in reputation.

entries.initial_entry: Skipped my cousin Mateo's wedding in Cartagena. Told my tia I had a work conflict. She didn't say anything but my mom called and I could tell she was disappointed.
entries.nudge_text: Was it a conflict or a choice?
entries.response_text: Both. The pitch competition was scheduled months before the wedding date was set. It's not like I picked one over the other—the timeline did.

entries.initial_entry: We placed second at the pitch comp. Second. Out of sixty companies. The judges said our presentation was the most polished. Camilo and I just looked at each other after and didn't say anything for a solid minute. Then we went and got arepas.

Two investor meetings came out of the pitch comp. One is a seed fund out of Mexico City, the other is an angel based in Miami. Both want to see our full deck and projections by Friday. I've been working on it since 6 AM and it's almost midnight now. Camilo's handling the financial model while I'm doing the narrative and market slide.

This is what all the prep was for. Every late night, every Sunday dashboard session, every fight with Laura about brunch. This is the payoff runway. If we close even one of these, we go from bootstrapped to funded. We go from "interesting startup" to "company." The distinction matters. People take you seriously when there's money behind you. Suppliers negotiate differently. Hires return your calls.

Laura sent me dinner because she knew I wouldn't eat otherwise. She left a note that said "go get it." I almost cried, which is stupid, but it hit me at the right time. She gets it when it matters. I need to remember that next time I'm annoyed about brunch.
Mexico City fund passed. Said they loved the team but the market size wasn't big enough for their thesis. What does that even mean. Our TAM slide was solid.
entries.nudge_text: What part of the rejection bothers you most?
entries.response_text: That I can't argue with it. It's not about our performance or our product. It's about their model. I did everything right and it still wasn't enough because of something I can't control. That's the worst kind of no.

entries.initial_entry: Miami angel came through. Signed the term sheet yesterday. Everyone keeps asking if I'm relieved and I keep saying I'm focused. Relief is for people who doubted whether it would happen.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you performing that confidence for right now?
entries.response_text: Maybe myself. The Mexico City rejection hit harder than I let on. For about three days I was running scenarios where both funds passed and we had to go back to bootstrapping for another year. I didn't tell Camilo or Laura. I just kept updating the spreadsheet and running the numbers. When the Miami call came in, the first thing I felt was my shoulders drop. So yeah. Maybe relief is exactly what it was.

entries.initial_entry: People act like delivery drivers don't know anything. I managed forty people at that factory. I know how to read a room, how to negotiate, how to plan logistics. Just because I'm on a scooter now doesn't mean I forgot everything.
entries.nudge_text: When did someone last treat you like you didn't know anything?

entries.initial_entry:
Decent week overall. Made about NT$18,000, which is slightly above average. The weather has been cooperating - no heavy rain, which means more orders and fewer accidents. I've been mapping out which restaurants have the fastest pickup times during dinner rush. Found that the smaller family-run places actually have better turnaround than the chain restaurants because they're not juggling ten delivery apps at once.

My wife mentioned that her store might cut hours in January. She said it casually, like it was nothing, but I saw her checking our savings account balance on her phone afterward. We have enough for three months, maybe four if we're careful. I've been taking the late-night shifts that nobody wants - the ones that end at 2 AM. The surge pricing is better then, and there's less competition from the younger drivers.

The app keeps pushing this "Diamond Driver" program where you get priority orders if you maintain high ratings. I'm at 4.92 right now. Would need to stay above 4.9 for two months straight. It's sort of worth it, I guess. Priority access means more consistent income.
entries.nudge_text: What's the part that makes it only 'sort of' worth it?
entries.response_text: Just takes a lot of energy to keep the smile on, you know? Anyway, it's fine.

entries.initial_entry:
My brother called tonight. He lives in Kaohsiung, works at a bank. He asked how the "side job" was going. I told him it's not a side job, it's my job. He made that sound, that little "ah" that means he doesn't believe me but won't argue.

He always does this. Acts concerned but really he just wants to feel better about his own choices. He stayed at the same bank for twenty years, never took a single risk. And now he acts like I failed at something when really the factory failed. It wasn't my decision to close it. I didn't make the products nobody wanted to buy anymore.

My son was home for the weekend and heard part of the call. He didn't say anything but I could tell he was uncomfortable. Later I heard him tell my wife that maybe I should "explore other options." What options? I'm 48. The HR screening software throws out resumes from anyone over 40 before a human even sees them. I know this because I used to be on the other side of that process.

I'm not going to apologize for doing what needs to be done to keep this family running. The gig economy isn't some failure - it's adaptation. That's what I taught my team back at the factory: adapt or get left behind. I'm adapting.
entries.nudge_text: What did it feel like hearing your son say that?

entries.initial_entry: Can't stop thinking about my old office. The chair, the window that looked out at the parking lot, the way people would knock before coming in. Nobody knocks for delivery drivers. They just grab the food and close the door.

Customer gave me one star because the restaurant forgot her drink. I explained it wasn't my fault. She said "whatever" and shut the door. My rating dropped to 4.88. Three weeks of perfect service, gone because of someone else's mistake.

Long day. Started at 11 AM, just got home at midnight. Made NT$2,800 which is okay. My back hurts from the scooter. The seat needs replacing but that's another NT$1,500 I don't want to spend right now.

Ran into Mr. Huang at a pickup. He used to work in purchasing at the factory - we'd have lunch together sometimes. He pretended not to recognize me. Put on his sunglasses even though we were inside, grabbed his order, walked away fast. I wanted to say something but what would I say? "Remember when we both had offices?" It's fine. People handle things differently. At least I'm not the one hiding.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually think he was hiding from you specifically?

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas. Working today because the surge pricing is excellent and most drivers take the day off to be with family. My wife wasn't happy but she understood. My son went back to Taichung for a friend's party. So it would have just been the two of us anyway.

I keep thinking about hierarchy. At the factory, I knew exactly where I stood - three levels below the director, two above the line supervisors. Clear chain of command. Now there's just the app. The app doesn't care that I have fifteen years of management experience. The app sees: delivery completed, time elapsed, customer rating. Three numbers. That's all I am now.

Some of the younger drivers formed a LINE group, share tips about which areas have better orders. They invited me but I don't participate much. They talk about this work like it's freedom - no boss, make your own hours. Maybe when you're 25 with no mortgage it feels that way. For me it feels like standing on ice that might crack any second.

My wife texted to ask when I'm coming home. She attached a photo of the dinner she made - nothing fancy, just three dishes and rice. The table looked empty with only one place setting out. I told her I'd be back by 10. It's 8:30 now and I'm sitting in my scooter outside a hot pot restaurant waiting for an order, writing this instead of going inside where it's warm.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you go inside where it's warm?
entries.response_text: Because inside I'm just a delivery guy waiting for order 847. Outside I'm just a man on a scooter. At least out here nobody's looking at me deciding what I am.

entries.initial_entry: Got home at 10:30. Wife had already put everything in the fridge. She didn't say anything, just handed me a plate and went to bed. The food was cold but I ate it anyway. I'm not sure what I'm actually saving all this money for.

Year-end is always busy. Everyone ordering food instead of cooking. I should be grateful for the work. I keep telling myself that.

Today a customer - young guy, maybe late twenties, nice apartment in Da'an district - tipped me NT$100. Then he said, "Uncle, you should take care of yourself. This weather is harsh." He meant well. I know he meant well. But something about being called "Uncle" by someone half my age, living in an apartment that costs more monthly than I made in three months at the factory, offering me NT$100 like it would make a difference... I said thank you. What else could I say?

I remember when I was his age. I had just been promoted to assistant manager. I thought I would keep climbing. Director by 45, maybe VP by 50 if I played my cards right. I had a plan. The plan had steps. Now the only steps I think about are the ones between the elevator and the apartment door, hoping there's not too many flights to climb with hot soup that's getting cold.

My son called to say he got a part-time job at his university. Research assistant for one of his professors. He sounded proud. I told him that's great, focus on your studies, don't worry about money. But of course he should worry about money. Everyone should. He's watching what happened to me and thinking: I won't let that happen. And I don't know if that makes me proud or ashamed. Probably both.
entries.nudge_text: What would make you lean more toward proud than ashamed?

entries.initial_entry: New year, new goals. Hit Diamond Driver status finally - 4.91 rating maintained for two months. Priority orders start Monday. Wife's hours weren't cut after all.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel when you hit Diamond?
entries.response_text: Good, I guess. It's just a status. Means more consistent orders.

entries.initial_entry: People act like delivery drivers don't know anything. I managed forty people at that factory. I know how to read a room, how to negotiate, how to plan logistics. Just because I'm on a scooter now doesn't mean I forgot everything.
