entries.nudge_text: What did it feel like hearing your son say that?

entries.initial_entry: Can't stop thinking about my old office. The chair, the window that looked out at the parking lot, the way people would knock before coming in. Nobody knocks for delivery drivers. They just grab the food and close the door.

Customer gave me one star because the restaurant forgot her drink. I explained it wasn't my fault. She said "whatever" and shut the door. My rating dropped to 4.88. Three weeks of perfect service, gone because of someone else's mistake.

Long day. Started at 11 AM, just got home at midnight. Made NT$2,800 which is okay. My back hurts from the scooter. The seat needs replacing but that's another NT$1,500 I don't want to spend right now.

Ran into Mr. Huang at a pickup. He used to work in purchasing at the factory - we'd have lunch together sometimes. He pretended not to recognize me. Put on his sunglasses even though we were inside, grabbed his order, walked away fast. I wanted to say something but what would I say? "Remember when we both had offices?" It's fine. People handle things differently. At least I'm not the one hiding.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually think he was hiding from you specifically?

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas. Working today because the surge pricing is excellent and most drivers take the day off to be with family. My wife wasn't happy but she understood. My son went back to Taichung for a friend's party. So it would have just been the two of us anyway.

I keep thinking about hierarchy. At the factory, I knew exactly where I stood - three levels below the director, two above the line supervisors. Clear chain of command. Now there's just the app. The app doesn't care that I have fifteen years of management experience. The app sees: delivery completed, time elapsed, customer rating. Three numbers. That's all I am now.

Some of the younger drivers formed a LINE group, share tips about which areas have better orders. They invited me but I don't participate much. They talk about this work like it's freedom - no boss, make your own hours. Maybe when you're 25 with no mortgage it feels that way. For me it feels like standing on ice that might crack any second.

My wife texted to ask when I'm coming home. She attached a photo of the dinner she made - nothing fancy, just three dishes and rice. The table looked empty with only one place setting out. I told her I'd be back by 10. It's 8:30 now and I'm sitting in my scooter outside a hot pot restaurant waiting for an order, writing this instead of going inside where it's warm.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you go inside where it's warm?
entries.response_text: Because inside I'm just a delivery guy waiting for order 847. Outside I'm just a man on a scooter. At least out here nobody's looking at me deciding what I am.

entries.initial_entry: Got home at 10:30. Wife had already put everything in the fridge. She didn't say anything, just handed me a plate and went to bed. The food was cold but I ate it anyway. I'm not sure what I'm actually saving all this money for.

Year-end is always busy. Everyone ordering food instead of cooking. I should be grateful for the work. I keep telling myself that.

Today a customer - young guy, maybe late twenties, nice apartment in Da'an district - tipped me NT$100. Then he said, "Uncle, you should take care of yourself. This weather is harsh." He meant well. I know he meant well. But something about being called "Uncle" by someone half my age, living in an apartment that costs more monthly than I made in three months at the factory, offering me NT$100 like it would make a difference... I said thank you. What else could I say?

I remember when I was his age. I had just been promoted to assistant manager. I thought I would keep climbing. Director by 45, maybe VP by 50 if I played my cards right. I had a plan. The plan had steps. Now the only steps I think about are the ones between the elevator and the apartment door, hoping there's not too many flights to climb with hot soup that's getting cold.

My son called to say he got a part-time job at his university. Research assistant for one of his professors. He sounded proud. I told him that's great, focus on your studies, don't worry about money. But of course he should worry about money. Everyone should. He's watching what happened to me and thinking: I won't let that happen. And I don't know if that makes me proud or ashamed. Probably both.
entries.nudge_text: What would make you lean more toward proud than ashamed?

entries.initial_entry: New year, new goals. Hit Diamond Driver status finally - 4.91 rating maintained for two months. Priority orders start Monday. Wife's hours weren't cut after all.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel when you hit Diamond?
entries.response_text: Good, I guess. It's just a status. Means more consistent orders.

entries.initial_entry:
My advisor asked today why I chose the topic of hospitality rituals in Levantine households when there are "more contemporary angles" I could take. I told her it was methodologically sound and left it at that. I don't owe anyone an explanation for why this subject matters to me. She's Swedish. She studies migration patterns. The way my grandmother set a table is not something she would understand, and I'm not interested in making it legible to her.

Came home and made musakhan from scratch, the whole process — rolling the dough, roasting the sumac onions until they were dark and sweet. Ate it alone at the kitchen table while my mother watched her show in the other room. The apartment smelled right. That was enough of an answer for one day.
entries.nudge_text: You said you don't owe an explanation. But you came home and spent hours making your grandmother's dish. What was that about?
entries.response_text: I like cooking. It's not that deep. The musakhan was good and I hadn't made it in a while. Sometimes dinner is just dinner.

entries.initial_entry: Spent twelve hours in the library and barely got through two chapters of my lit review. My back hurts, my eyes hurt, and I keep wondering what possessed me to do this at forty-nine. Going to bed.

My brother Faisal made a comment at Friday lunch about how I'm "still playing student" while his sons are already building careers. His eldest is twenty-six and works at a bank. I smiled and passed the hummus. What I wanted to say was that I spent twenty years running hotel operations so our family name meant something in the hospitality industry, and now I'm doing something for myself, and that should be allowed.

But it wouldn't have been the right moment. My mother was sitting right there, and she gets anxious when Faisal and I disagree. So I stayed quiet, helped clear the plates, and drove home listening to Fairuz with the windows down. The breeze off the hills felt good. I don't need Faisal's approval. I know what I'm doing and why.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you'd actually said it?
entries.response_text: My mother would have gone quiet. Faisal would have gotten louder. His wife Rania would have changed the subject to the children, which she always does. And on the drive home I would have felt worse, not better, because I'd have disrupted the one gathering that keeps us together. The lunch matters more than being right. That's something Faisal wouldn't understand because he's never had to choose.

entries.initial_entry: Can't stop thinking about what Faisal said. Opened a bottle of that Jordanian red wine I've been saving and I'm sitting on the balcony. My mother would not approve of the wine but she went to bed early and what she doesn't see doesn't trouble her.

Had a strange afternoon. After seminar I went to the old souk downtown instead of going home to write. I just walked. Bought a bag of roasted pistachios from the same vendor my father used to take me to when I was a boy. The man running it now is probably the vendor's grandson, same spot, same brass scale. I sat on a bench near the mosque and ate them slowly, one by one, listening to the call to prayer echo off the stone.

I haven't prayed regularly in years. My mother doesn't know this. She assumes I go to the mosque near campus on Fridays, and I don't correct her. The truth is I stopped feeling anything during prayer around the time I turned forty. The motions are still in my body — I could do them in my sleep — but the connection isn't there. And yet sitting there today, hearing the adhan bounce off those old walls while I cracked pistachios, I felt something close to what prayer used to give me. Stillness. Presence. The taste of salt and the sound of something ancient happening around me.

I don't know what to do with that. I can't tell my mother I've replaced prayer with pistachios and a bench. That would break her heart. But I also can't pretend the mosque still does for me what it used to. I'm stuck between what's honest and what's kind.
entries.nudge_text: You described that moment on the bench as close to what prayer used to give you. What do you think changed?
entries.response_text: I think I stopped believing that anyone was listening. Not God necessarily — I haven't worked that out — but the structure of it. The imam, the rows, the routine. It started to feel like I was performing for an audience that included my dead father and my living mother but not me. On the bench I wasn't performing for anyone. I was just a man eating pistachios in the late sun, and that was enough. Maybe what I lost wasn't faith. Maybe I lost the ability to feel things inside a frame someone else built, and I can only feel them when I stumble into them on my own. That's a lonely thought. But it felt true sitting there.

entries.initial_entry:
My cohort went out for dinner last night at a rooftop restaurant in Jabal Amman. I almost didn't go. I had reading to finish and my mother had made lentil soup, and leaving her eating alone feels wrong even when she tells me to go. But I went, and I'm glad I did.

There were eight of us, all at different stages. Layla is twenty-seven and finishing her masters. Khaled is thirty-three and already published twice. And then there's me, the oldest by a decade and a half, ordering an extra plate of mezze because the lamb was extraordinary and the arak was cold and the evening air on that rooftop was exactly right. Layla asked me once why I came back to school so late, and I told her the truth — I wanted to, and I finally could, and life is too short to keep postponing the things that interest you.

She looked at me like I'd said something profound. I don't think it was. I think it was just unusual for someone my age in our context to say out loud that wanting something was reason enough to pursue it. Most of the men my brother's age would find that embarrassing. A man approaching fifty should have his affairs settled, not be starting over in a seminar room. But I'm not sure I care about what those men think anymore. The lamb was perfect. The conversation was good. I walked home through the old streets and felt awake in a way that the library doesn't give me.
entries.nudge_text: You said you're not sure you care anymore what men your brother's age think. When did that start to shift?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning transcribing interviews for my fieldwork chapter. One of my subjects, a woman in her seventies from Irbid, described how she prepares mansaf for her family exactly the way her mother taught her, down to the specific way she folds the bread under the rice. She said she doesn't think about why she does it that way. She just does. Her hands know.

That's the thing I'm trying to capture in this dissertation and I keep circling around it without landing. The knowledge that lives in practice, not in explanation. My advisor wants me to theorize it and I understand why, but there's something that gets lost when you turn a grandmother's hands into a framework. I don't have a solution yet. I just know the gap is there and it bothers me.

My mother made knafeh today. She does this maybe twice a year, always unannounced, and when I came home from campus the whole apartment smelled like orange blossom and melted cheese. She was standing at the stove watching it with that focused expression she gets, and I sat at the kitchen table pretending to read but really just watching her.

She's seventy-six. Her hands shake a little now but she still moves through the kitchen with this sureness that I don't have anywhere in my life. She poured the syrup in one smooth motion, tilted the pan, pressed the edges with a spoon. Every step inherited from her mother, who learned from her mother before that.

I ate two pieces standing at the counter before she'd even plated it properly and she swatted my hand and called me greedy. I am greedy. I wanted a third piece and I took it. The sweetness was so intense it almost hurt, that good kind of too-much where your whole mouth is alive. My mother watched me eat and looked satisfied in a way that goes deeper than food. She was feeding her son. That's a complete sentence in her world.

Later I sat with my laptop open and thought about how this is exactly what my dissertation is about, but I can't write about my own mother. It would feel like a betrayal. Like turning something sacred into data. But she's the reason I'm writing about any of this in the first place.
entries.nudge_text: You called it a betrayal to write about your mother. What would it mean if you did?
entries.response_text: It would mean she becomes a subject. A case study. Her knafeh becomes "an example of embodied intergenerational knowledge transfer" and the orange blossom becomes a footnote. I'd be taking something that belongs to our kitchen and putting it in a seminar room for people who have never tasted it to analyze. My advisor would call that rigorous. I'd call it taking something warm and making it cold. I know that's not a rational objection. I just feel it in my chest when I think about writing her name in an academic paper. She deserves better than being theorized.

entries.initial_entry:
Canceled my library session today and drove to the Dead Sea. Told myself it was for thinking but really I just wanted to float. Paid for one of the resort day passes, the expensive one with the private beach and the buffet lunch. My fellowship stipend doesn't really cover that kind of thing but I did it anyway. Lay in the water for an hour, completely weightless, staring at the sky.

The buffet was ridiculous. Grilled prawns, tabbouleh, four kinds of baklava. I ate slowly and deliberately and didn't check my phone once. Drove home in the early evening with the salt drying on my skin and this lightness I haven't felt in weeks. I know I should have been writing. The chapter is overdue. But I can't bring myself to feel guilty about it.
entries.nudge_text: You said you can't bring yourself to feel guilty. Is that new?
entries.response_text: Not really. I've always been someone who needs a good meal and a change of scenery to function. The Dead Sea was beautiful and now I'm recharged. I'll write tomorrow. It's fine.

entries.initial_entry: Sat down to write after the Dead Sea trip. Got four hundred words down, all of them bad. My mother asked if I'd been to campus today and I said yes. First time I've lied to her about something this small. Too tired to think about what that means.

Got an email from my advisor asking me to justify why my fieldwork methodology is "sufficiently distanced" from my own background. She phrased it politely but the implication is clear — studying my own culture's domestic practices introduces bias. As if the Swedish anthropologists who study Swedish folk customs don't exist. As if proximity is only a problem when you're from the region being studied.

I wrote a professional reply explaining my positionality framework and reflexive methodology. It was measured and appropriate. But sitting here now, I want to throw something. I left a career. I'm spending my savings. My mother eats dinner alone three nights a week because I'm at the library. And someone who has never set foot in a Jordanian kitchen is telling me I'm too close to the food. I need her approval to move forward with this chapter. But some nights the cost of fitting myself into their frame feels like it's eating the thing that brought me here.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the cost of fitting yourself into their frame. What's the thing you're afraid of losing?
entries.response_text: The feeling. That's what I'm afraid of losing. The reason I walked into a classroom at forty-nine wasn't the degree or the title. It was because something about the way my mother cooks, the way that woman in Irbid folds bread, the way my father used to pour coffee for guests with both hands — those things mean something, and I wanted to understand why they move me the way they do. But the more I translate that into academic language, the further I get from the feeling that started it. I'm terrified that by the time I finish this dissertation, I'll have a perfect theoretical framework for something I can no longer feel. And then what was the point? I'll have turned something alive into something correct. My advisor would say that's the job. But I don't want that to be my job. I want to hold both — the rigor and the warmth. I just don't know if the institution lets you do that.

entries.initial_entry:
A former coworker called me today. She left the hospital two years ago to do travel nursing out in California and she's making almost double what I make. She told me about surfing on her days off, about renting this little bungalow in San Diego with ocean sounds at night. She wasn't bragging, just talking. But something about the way she described her mornings hit me in a way I wasn't ready for.

I almost asked her how the contracts work. I got as far as opening a browser tab. Then Ellie came in asking for help with her spelling homework and I closed it. I told myself it was because the timing was wrong, but really I think I was afraid of what I'd find out. That it might actually be doable. That's the part I don't want to sit with.
entries.nudge_text: What were you afraid you'd find out?
entries.response_text: That the money works. That Kyle could find jobs out there. That the kids are young enough to adjust. Basically that there's no real reason not to except that I don't want to give up what I already know.

entries.initial_entry: Meal prepped for the whole week. Got the kids' lunches sorted through Friday. Kyle fixed the dryer vent that's been rattling since October. Checked the HSA balance and we're on track to max it out by March. Nothing special about today but everything's where it should be.

Kyle's buddy offered us his cabin up north for New Year's, last minute, like they always do. Free stay, just bring food. Kyle's face lit up and the kids were already asking about the lake. And I wanted to say yes. I really did. But the drive is four hours each way in December, the roads up past Duluth are unpredictable, we'd have to pull the kids out of their routine, pack for cold weather, hope the cabin's pipes aren't frozen.

Kyle said I was overthinking it. Maybe I am. But somebody has to think about whether the car insurance covers off-road conditions up there, whether we'd have cell service if something happened, whether the kids' asthma inhalers are enough if we're that far from the nearest hospital.

I said no. Or I said "I don't think it's a good idea right now" which is the same thing but lets me pretend I'm being reasonable instead of just scared. Kyle shrugged and said okay, but his shoulders dropped. The kids went back to their tablets. And now I'm sitting here wondering if I just took something good away from them because the version of the weekend that sounded fun was also the version I couldn't fully control.

I keep doing this. Choosing the safe answer and then feeling lousy about it after.
entries.nudge_text: When's the last time you said yes to something like that?

entries.initial_entry:
Worked a double because two people called out. New Year's Eve in the ICU is always rough. Had a guy come in from an ATV accident, 24 years old, and it was touch and go for a few hours. He stabilized around 10 PM. His girlfriend was in the waiting room the whole time, shaking.

Got home at one in the morning and Kyle had left a plate in the microwave. The kids were asleep. There was confetti on the kitchen floor from whatever small celebration they had without me. I ate standing up at the counter, too tired to sit down properly. Didn't feel like New Year's. Felt like any other late shift except the drive home had more drunk drivers to watch out for.

Going to bed now. Tomorrow is another shift.

Woke up and saw a group text from nursing school friends. Half of them have moved on to different things. One's a nurse practitioner in Portland. One left healthcare entirely and does medical device sales now, making three times what I make. And here I am about to drive back to the same unit for another twelve-hour shift. Something about New Year's makes this sting more than usual.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the money or the change that stings?
entries.response_text: Probably both. I don't know. Forget it, I'm just tired from the double yesterday.

entries.initial_entry:
First full normal week back after the holidays. Got into a good rhythm. Three day shifts in a row, all manageable patient loads. Came home each night by 7:30, had dinner with the family, helped Owen with his reading log.

Kyle and I sat down Sunday morning and went through the January budget. Mortgage is on autopay, car payment goes through Friday, we've got enough set aside for the furnace inspection next month. Put fifty extra into the emergency fund. Not a lot, but consistent.

The house felt right this week. Quiet mornings, packed lunches on the counter, everyone knowing where they need to be. I like when things click like that. I know it won't always be this smooth, but when it is, I want to just let myself notice it.

Slow day at work, which is rare. Spent most of my downtime restocking supply carts and updating patient charts. One of the newer nurses asked me if I ever get bored doing the same unit for this long. I told her no without thinking about it. Not sure that was completely honest.

Still thinking about what that nurse asked me today. Bored. Like it's about entertainment. I'm not here to be entertained. I'm here because the pay is solid, the benefits are excellent, and I know the protocols well enough that I can keep people alive in their worst moments without second-guessing myself. That counts for something.

But then I drove home and passed that new urgent care clinic they're building off Highway 7 and I thought about how a friend from my cohort just became the clinical director at one of those places. Regular hours. No nights. She said it's like a different career. And I felt something I didn't want to feel, which was jealousy. Not at the title or the money, but at the fact that she let herself want something different and then actually went after it.

I don't know why that's so hard for me. Kyle would support it. My manager would write me a recommendation. The kids would barely notice. There's nothing actually stopping me except that the idea of starting over somewhere new, learning new workflows, being the person who doesn't know where anything is, makes my stomach drop. I'd rather stay where I can do the job with my eyes closed than go somewhere I'd have to prove myself all over again.

That's not ambition. But I'm not sure it's laziness either. It's just where I am.
entries.nudge_text: What's the worst that happens if you're the new person somewhere?
entries.response_text: I think the worst thing isn't failing. It's that I'd miss the version of myself that had everything figured out. Even if that version is just standing still.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week but a good one. Had a patient who'd been in the unit for twelve days finally get transferred to a regular room. His wife hugged me in the hallway and I had to duck into the break room for a minute after because it hit harder than I expected.

Kyle picked up the kids from school all week so I could take the early shifts. The fridge is stocked, the laundry's done, the mortgage auto-drafted on time. Owen lost a tooth at school on Wednesday and the tooth fairy remembered on the first night for once.

I'm tired in my body but not in my head tonight. That's the best kind of tired. Tomorrow's Saturday and I'm off. No plans except pancakes and whatever the kids want to do. That's enough.

People at the plant keep asking why I don't apply for the regional director position now that Héctor is retiring. I know exactly why — the travel, the relocating, none of that sits right with me. My team runs well, my commute is twenty minutes, and I sleep fine at night.
entries.nudge_text: Is there any part of you that's curious about it?
entries.response_text: Not really. Maybe five years ago I would have thought about it differently, but I watched Héctor miss his daughter's quinceañera because he was in Monterrey for a plant inspection. That told me everything I needed to know.

entries.initial_entry: Lucía mentioned her sister's husband lost his job at the autoparts factory in León. Just like that — twenty-two years and they called a meeting on a Friday afternoon. I checked our savings account twice after dinner, which is not something I normally do on a weekday.
entries.nudge_text: What were you hoping to see when you checked?

entries.initial_entry: Spent Saturday morning fixing the back fence with my compadre Tomás. Felt good to work with my hands for once instead of staring at spreadsheets. Lucía brought us agua de jamaica and we sat in the yard afterward just talking about nothing important.

Corporate sent an email Friday about "restructuring opportunities" in the operations departments across all three plants. The language was carefully neutral, all about efficiency and modernization, but I have been doing this long enough to recognize what that kind of memo actually means. Somebody is going to lose headcount.

I spent part of Sunday looking through my employment contract — the termination clauses, the severance calculations, the notice periods. Lucía noticed me reading it at the kitchen table instead of doing our usual budget review and asked if something was wrong. I told her I was just being thorough, which is true but also not the whole picture.

The thing that bothers me is not the restructuring itself. Companies do this. What bothers me is that I had filed my contract in the closet three years ago and forgot exactly where. It took me twenty minutes to find it. That should not have happened. I need a better system for keeping important documents where I can reach them quickly.
entries.nudge_text: You spent a lot of time on the contract. What did you find?
entries.response_text: The severance is decent — four months for my tenure — but decent is not the same as comfortable. We could cover things for ten months total if I combined severance with savings, but that assumes nothing else breaks. The car is twelve years old, the roof needs work eventually. Ten months sounds like a lot until you start listing what eats into it.

entries.initial_entry:
My direct report Andrés came in excited about some new automation software he wants to pilot on the production line. He showed me a demo and honestly it looked impressive — would probably cut processing time by fifteen percent. But the implementation would mean shutting down Line 2 for three weeks and retraining half the floor staff. Three weeks of lost output during our busiest quarter.

I told him to write up a proposal and I would review it, but the truth is I already know I am going to push it to next fiscal year. Maybe the quarter after that. I could hear how flat my voice sounded when I said it and I think he noticed too.
entries.nudge_text: What made your voice go flat?
entries.response_text: Because I know I am probably being the bottleneck. Andrés is sharp, the software looked solid, and fifteen percent is not nothing. But shutting down a line for three weeks when corporate is already talking about restructuring — that is the kind of disruption that puts a target on your department. I would rather deliver predictable numbers and keep everyone employed than chase an improvement that could backfire at the worst possible time. I just wish I could explain that to Andrés without sounding like I have given up.

entries.initial_entry: Got the water bill and it was almost double the usual amount. Probably a leak somewhere. Called a plumber but the earliest appointment is next Thursday, so I have been turning the main valve off at night just in case.

Plumber came, turned out it was just a faulty meter reading — no actual leak. Lucía laughed about me turning the water off every night for five days. I told her better cautious than flooded, and she kissed my forehead and said "sí, mi amor, así eres tú."

The restructuring memo turned into a town hall meeting this week. They are consolidating some administrative roles at the Querétaro plant but operations at our site stays as-is for now. My boss Carlos pulled me aside afterward and said my numbers were part of the reason they kept Guadalajara intact. Sixteen years of consistent output apparently counts for something.

I reorganized the filing cabinet this weekend. Employment contracts, insurance policies, property deed, car title — all in labeled folders now, easy to grab. Lucía thinks I am being excessive but she also put her own documents in the system without me asking, so I think she sees the point even if she would not say it directly.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel hearing Carlos say that?
entries.response_text: Relieved, obviously, but also something I did not expect — vindicated, maybe. I have spent years being the manager who says no to flashy ideas, who keeps the same routines, who does not chase promotions. Sometimes I wonder if people think that means I lack ambition. But steady output is what saved those jobs. Not innovation, not bold moves. Just showing up and delivering the same thing every quarter. It is not exciting but it held.

entries.initial_entry:
Lucía's mother called this morning to say she wants to sell the family house in Morelia and move into one of those assisted living places. Lucía was quiet for a long time after hanging up. Her mother has lived in that house for forty years — it is where Lucía grew up, where we had our wedding reception in the backyard.

The practical side of it is complicated. Lucía's sister wants to buy the house but does not have enough for a down payment and asked if we could co-sign a loan. I have never co-signed anything in my life. My father told me when I was nineteen: never put your name on someone else's debt, no matter who they are. But this is Lucía's family, and the look on her face when her sister called tonight was not something I can just calculate my way around.

I told them both I needed a few days to think. Lucía did not argue, which means she understands my hesitation even if she hopes I land somewhere different than where I am leaning.
entries.nudge_text: Where are you leaning?
entries.response_text: Toward no. I know how that sounds, and I know what it might cost with Lucía's family. But co-signing means if her sister cannot pay, we pay. And if we pay, that is the emergency fund, that is the roof repair, that is everything I have built to make sure we never end up like Lucía's cuñado in León. I would rather help them find another way — maybe lend a smaller amount directly, something we could absorb if it disappeared. But putting our name on a bank loan is a different thing entirely.

entries.initial_entry: The gallery wants to change the whole color palette for the installation, said it's "too somber for the spring collection." Spring collection. Like my work is a handbag.
entries.nudge_text: What matters most to you about this piece?
entries.response_text: It's about plastic waste in the North Sea. If I make it cheerful and decorative, I'm just adding to the noise. The discomfort is the point.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the weekend at the refugee art workshop. We had twelve participants this time, up from eight last month. One woman, Fatima, has been coming consistently since October. She doesn't speak much Dutch yet, but her drawings are extraordinary - detailed botanical illustrations of plants from her home region in Syria that she does from memory.

I brought some of my old nature journals from when I was her age, thinking we might find common ground. We sat together for most of the afternoon, not really talking, just drawing side by side. At one point she reached over and corrected my shading technique on a leaf. I was the student.

The session ran two hours over. I had planned to work on my installation sketches this evening, but I sat in the car for twenty minutes before driving home, just thinking about those plants that no longer exist where she once lived. The commission deadline feels less urgent somehow, even though I know it shouldn't.
entries.nudge_text: What shifted for you in that car?
entries.response_text: I was thinking about permanence. My gallery piece will be dismantled in three months. Fatima's drawings preserve something real that's been destroyed. I started questioning whether my environmental commentary means anything compared to someone actually documenting loss.

entries.initial_entry:
Just got off a call with my sister. She asked when I'm going to "get serious" about retirement planning. I'm 52. I have a studio, commissions, work that matters. But because I don't have a pension and a holiday fund, I'm apparently not a real adult.

She means well. I know she worries. But every conversation becomes a referendum on my life choices. Her husband works in insurance, they have a nice house in Utrecht, the kids are in good schools. And I have paint under my fingernails and a second-hand car. Different doesn't mean wrong.
Later the same day. Maybe the call bothered me more than I admitted. I do think about money sometimes. Not having it, I mean.
entries.nudge_text: What does security actually look like to you?
entries.response_text: Not what my sister has. But maybe enough to not panic when a commission falls through. I've been pretending money doesn't matter because wanting it feels like a betrayal of why I do this.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. The gallery accepted my color choices after I sent them a three-page explanation of the concept. They didn't love it, but they agreed. Small victory. I should feel satisfied but mostly I feel tired.

The nonprofit board meeting ran three hours last night. We're losing our main funder and no one can agree on next steps. Half the board wants to chase corporate sponsors, half thinks that compromises our mission. I sat there thinking about how much simpler it was when I just showed up and ran workshops. Now there's strategy and stakeholders and "capacity building."
entries.nudge_text: What's getting squeezed out by all this?

entries.initial_entry: Saw the question about what's getting squeezed. I'm not avoiding it. I just don't have a neat answer. My studio time, obviously. Maybe that's fine for now.
Good day in the studio. Lost track of time, which hasn't happened in weeks. The installation is finally taking shape the way I imagined it.

Installation opens next week and I'm behind. Skipped the workshop on Saturday to finish the main panel, which was the right choice for the deadline but felt wrong in a different way. Fatima asked after me, apparently. One of the other volunteers said she seemed disappointed.

I've been telling myself that the artwork has meaning beyond just me, that it might change how someone sees the world. But sitting here at midnight with resin fumes and sore hands, I wonder if I'm just making excuses for choosing my career over the people I said I cared about.
entries.nudge_text: Is this a one-time choice or a pattern?
entries.response_text: Pattern. I know it's a pattern. The teaching job, my marriage, now this. I keep choosing work. I tell myself it's because the work matters, but maybe I just find people harder.

entries.initial_entry: Opening night went well. Several pieces sold. A journalist from NRC wants to interview me about the environmental themes.

Same day, later. The opening was a success by every external measure and I spent the last hour sitting in my studio in the dark.

A collector asked me to explain the piece about North Sea plastic. I gave my usual explanation - the scope of the problem, the research, the materials I used. He nodded, bought the piece, and then asked if I could do something similar but "more hopeful" for his holiday home. I smiled and said I'd think about it.

I should have said no. I should have said art isn't interior decoration. But I need the money, and he wasn't wrong that people want hope. And maybe I'm the one who's wrong, clinging to this idea that discomfort creates change when actually people just look away.

I keep thinking about what I said in the last question - that I choose work because people are harder. But tonight I chose people, in a way. I performed the version of myself that makes sales and connections. And I hate it, but I also felt this relief that it was working, that maybe I don't have to be so uncompromising about everything.
entries.nudge_text: What would uncompromising cost you right now?
entries.response_text: Probably the commission. Maybe more. But also - and I hadn't thought this through until now - it might cost me this idea that my choices are purely principled. Some of my stubbornness is just fear of doing it wrong in a different way.

entries.initial_entry:
Went back to the workshop today. Fatima was there. She showed me the drawing she finished while I was away - a complete botanical study of a flower I don't recognize, maybe something that grew near Aleppo. We didn't talk about my absence.

I'm tired in a good way tonight. The kind of tired from doing something rather than just worrying about all the things I'm not doing. I emailed the collector to say I'd think about his commission but couldn't promise "hopeful" - I could only promise honest. He hasn't replied. That's probably fine.

The meeting with Diego didn't go how I planned. He sat there scrolling through my routing proposal on his laptop, barely looking up, and when he finally said something it was about the formatting. The formatting. Three months of pulling delivery data, testing alternate corridors, calling warehouse contacts in Medellin -- and he wants to talk about fonts.

I should have pushed back harder. Instead I just nodded and said I'd revise it. The whole walk back to my desk I was already rewriting my argument in my head, all the things I should have said. The numbers speak for themselves. Our current route through Bucaramanga adds two days and the clients in the southern corridor keep complaining.

Part of me wonders if I backed down because he's been here fifteen years and I've been here eleven months. That shouldn't matter when the data is right there. But I sat quiet like it did matter, and now I'm stuck doing cosmetic edits on something that should already be approved.
entries.nudge_text: Why do you think you went quiet?

entries.initial_entry:
Still at the office. Everyone else left at six but I'm going through the client satisfaction numbers from Q4 because nobody else is going to do it. Found three accounts where response times slipped below target because the team was running the old dispatch sequence I already flagged two months ago. Two months.

My mom called while I was pulling the spreadsheets. I let it go to voicemail. She'll ask why I'm still at work, then she'll ask when I'm coming home for New Year's, and I don't have answers to either one right now.
entries.nudge_text: Are you going home for New Year's?
entries.response_text: Probably not. There's too much to clean up here before January. She'll understand. She usually does.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent New Year's Eve at Lucia's apartment with a few people from the office. Nothing big -- arepas, aguardiente, the countdown on the TV. I didn't check my phone once the whole night, which felt strange at first but I stopped noticing after a while. Lucia made fun of me for not knowing any of the songs on the playlist.

On January first I went for a run along the ciclovia and came back with an idea for restructuring the southern corridor drops. Not the full routing plan Diego shut down -- something smaller, a pilot with just the Cali deliveries. If I can show a cost reduction on one leg, the rest of the argument builds itself. Nobody has to approve a pilot. I can just run it with the three drivers who already told me the current route doesn't make sense.

I drafted the pilot scope on my laptop sitting on Lucia's couch while everyone was watching a movie. Felt good to have something concrete again instead of waiting for permission. I want to own how this unfolds, start to finish.

The Cali pilot is running. Three drivers, rerouted through Palmira instead of the standard loop. I didn't tell Diego. I told the drivers it was a test week and tracked the first four deliveries myself from the dashboard. Average time dropped by six hours per run. Six hours. That's not a font issue.

I built a one-page summary with the numbers and sent it to the full operations chat, not just Diego. Maybe that was aggressive. Lucia said I should have gone through him first, and she's probably right in terms of politics. But if I go through him it sits in his inbox for two weeks and nothing changes. I've watched that happen before.

He hasn't responded yet. Part of me is nervous but mostly I feel like I did the thing I actually wanted to do instead of waiting around editing slide decks. If the numbers hold through Friday, the argument is over. I want this to stand on the work, not on who approved it.

Diego pulled me into his office yesterday and I thought he was going to tear into me about the pilot but he just asked for the full data set. No commentary, no lecture, just "send me the spreadsheet." I sent it within the hour. He forwarded it to the VP of ops in Mexico City. I know because the VP emailed me directly asking for a call next week.

That call could be anything. Could be a reprimand, could be an expansion. Either way the work is out there now and it's mine. I keep refreshing my email like that's going to make the calendar invite appear faster. Need to stop doing that.
entries.nudge_text: What are you hoping she says?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Something that makes the last three months feel like they counted.

entries.initial_entry:
Call with the VP happened. Forty-five minutes. She asked detailed questions about methodology, cost projections, scalability to other corridors. She knew what she was talking about, which made it feel real -- not like presenting to someone who just wants to check a box.

At the end she said she wants me to present to the regional leadership group in February. A full presentation, not a one-pager. My stomach dropped when she said it, not because I'm scared but because I've never had that kind of stage before and I want it to go perfectly. I already know I'm going to over-prepare.

Called my mom after to tell her. She said "mija, eat something, you sound wired." She's not wrong. I had an empanada for lunch and nothing since. But this is the thing I've been pushing for since I got here and it's actually happening. Not because someone handed it to me -- because I built it and put it out there myself.
entries.nudge_text: What does a perfect presentation look like to you?
entries.response_text: Numbers that nobody can argue with, questions I already have answers for, and walking out knowing they couldn't have gotten that analysis from anyone else on the team.

entries.initial_entry: Stayed up until three working on the deck. Caught myself copying Diego's formatting from an old presentation because I thought it would make the leadership group take it more seriously. Closed the file and went to bed feeling off about it.

Lucia told me today that some people on the team think I'm "trying to skip levels." She said it like she was doing me a favor by telling me, and maybe she was, but it stung. I've been busting myself on this project and the fact that doing good work gets read as political maneuvering makes me want to throw my laptop into the Magdalena.

I asked her who said it and she wouldn't tell me. Fine. But now I'm sitting here wondering if I should tone it down for the February presentation or just go harder. Toning it down feels like giving up ground I earned. Going harder might prove their point. I don't know.

Finished the presentation draft this morning. Fourteen slides, every number sourced, three scenario projections. I ran through it out loud twice in my apartment and timed it at eighteen minutes, which leaves room for questions. My neighbor probably thinks I'm losing it.

Went to my abuela's for almuerzo after. She made sancocho and didn't ask about work at all, just told me about her friend Doris who's fighting with her landlord. Sat there eating soup and listening and realized I hadn't thought about the presentation for almost two hours. That's probably the longest I've gone without thinking about it in weeks.
entries.nudge_text: How did the two hours feel?
entries.response_text: Good. Like my brain got to stop running for a bit. I forgot what that was like.

entries.initial_entry:
Diego sent me "feedback" on the presentation draft. Half of it is adding his name to the second slide as a co-contributor. He didn't contribute. He sat on my original proposal for weeks and only engaged after I went around him. Now he wants credit on the version that works.

I know the smart move is probably to add his name and keep the peace. Everyone keeps telling me to "play the game." But I didn't build this by playing the game. I built it by ignoring the game and doing the actual work. If I put his name on it, what am I even presenting? Someone else's stamp on my project. I'm going to push back. Maybe that's stupid. I don't care.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually not care?

entries.initial_entry: Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

I don't care.
