entries.initial_entry: Presentation went well. The VP asked me to lead a corridor optimization working group across three countries. Diego shook my hand after and didn't mention the co-credit thing. I slept eleven hours last night and I'm still tired, but it's the kind of tired where you know you did the thing.

Can't stop thinking about today. There was this older man, Mr. Hernandez, and he reminded me so much of my tio — the way he called me "mija" and told me about his grandchildren back in Honduras. He needed his blood pressure medication but couldn't afford the copay, kept apologizing like he was asking for something unreasonable. And I just... I knew we have samples. I know where they are. I could have grabbed a month's supply and no one would have noticed or cared.

But I didn't. Followed protocol. Gave him the paperwork for financial assistance that takes six weeks to process. He thanked me anyway, squeezed my hand, said God would bless me for my kindness. Kindness. I filled out a form.

The rules exist for a reason, right? That's what my supervisor says. That's what I tell myself when I'm lying in bed at 3am staring at the ceiling instead of sleeping. The system works when everyone follows it. My abuela used to say you earn respect by doing things the right way, even when the right way is hard. She volunteered here for decades and never took a single bandaid home that wasn't accounted for.

But also — what is the system actually doing for Mr. Hernandez? He's 68, worked construction his whole life, and now he can't afford pills that cost the hospital pennies. I went into nursing because I wanted to help people. Real help. Not paperwork.

I keep telling myself I'll feel better about this tomorrow. I won't.
entries.nudge_text: What would your abuela have done?
entries.response_text: She would have followed the rules. She always did. But things were different then — there wasn't so much bureaucracy, people looked out for each other more. Anyway, it doesn't matter. I have to get ready for my shift.

entries.initial_entry: Told my mom I can't come home for Christmas this year because of work. It's not entirely a lie — I could have requested off earlier. Just didn't.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you?
entries.response_text: Because part of me wanted an excuse. Everyone will be there — tias, primos, the whole family asking when I'm getting married, why I'm not going to church anymore, if I'm eating enough. I love them but I come back feeling like I'm failing at being who they expect me to be. At least at the hospital I know what I'm supposed to do.

entries.initial_entry:
Good shift today. We had a Guatemalan family come in — the grandmother only spoke K'iche' and was terrified. I don't speak it fluently but I know enough from my own abuela to say the important things: you're safe, we're going to help you, your family is right outside.

She grabbed my hand during the IV and prayed. I prayed with her, quietly, in Spanish. Felt right. The daughter cried when I came out to update them. Said they'd never been treated with such dignity at a hospital before. That's the job. That's why I'm here.

I hate the new scheduling software. It's supposed to be "fair" and "optimized" but it has me working Christmas Eve AND Christmas Day, back to back twelve hour shifts. Meanwhile Sarah who has no family obligations gets the whole week off because she requested it first. Cool. Great. Love that for her.

Called my mom to complain and she just said "Ay mija, that's how it is. At least you have a good job." Which I know is true. I know I'm lucky. I know people would kill for this position. But I'm allowed to be annoyed, right? I haven't had a Christmas off in three years. Three years of sending gifts instead of being there. Three years of video calls while everyone eats together.
entries.nudge_text: What's the hardest part of not being there?
entries.response_text: Watching them on the screen and knowing they're making memories without me. My little cousin is seven now and she barely knows who I am. Last year she asked my mom if I was the one who sends the presents. Not tia Valentina. Just "the one who sends presents." I'm becoming a stranger to my own family and I can't do anything about it because someone has to cover the holiday shifts and I'm the one without kids.

entries.initial_entry: Worked sixteen hours. A man died on my floor — no family, no one to call. I held his hand at the end because no one should die alone. My family sent me a video of them opening presents and I couldn't watch it.
entries.nudge_text: You could be there for him but not for them?

entries.initial_entry: Everyone's going out tonight. I'm eating leftover tamales my neighbor brought me and going to bed early. Told myself I'd call my mom at midnight her time but I'll probably be asleep. Another year of choosing this life over the one I was supposed to have.
entries.nudge_text: What life were you supposed to have?
entries.response_text: Married by now, probably. Living close to home, seeing my family every Sunday, raising kids who speak Spanish as their first language and know how to make tamales and go to mass. That's what my mom did, what her mom did. Instead I'm alone in an apartment eating someone else's cooking and pretending this is enough because I'm "making a difference." I don't know if I'm brave for building something new or just selfish for leaving everything behind.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole afternoon with Abu Mahmoud at his farm outside Karameh. Three hours sitting on plastic chairs drinking tea while he showed me where the irrigation canal used to run before the allocation changed. His hands, the way he traced the old waterline in the dirt like drawing a map from memory. Forty years he worked that land. Now his grandson is driving trucks in Amman because there is nothing left to grow.

I recorded everything but the recording will not capture how quiet he got when I asked about the cooperative's petition. How he looked past me toward the orchards, half of them dried stumps now. Dr. Khalil wants regression models and policy impact matrices. What I have is Abu Mahmoud saying "we wrote letters until we ran out of paper." This is what I came here for. Not the degree, not the credentials. This.
entries.nudge_text: What made you leave engineering for this?
entries.response_text: Thirty years I designed systems to move water from where it was to where it was needed. Good work. Useful work. But always I was working for someone else's priorities, someone else's calculations about who matters. The last project, we diverted a whole tributary for a development that displaced six villages. I signed my name to those drawings. Came home and could not sleep for a week. Told my husband I needed to understand the other side of my own blueprints.

entries.initial_entry:
Committee meeting today. Dr. Khalil brought up timeline again, asked when I would have my methodology chapter finalized. I told him the fieldwork is taking longer because I am doing it properly, not rushing through interviews like they are survey forms. He gave me that look, the one he gives when he thinks I am being difficult. Said something about how at my stage of career I should know how to work within constraints.

At my stage. Meaning old. Meaning I should be grateful they let me in at all, should play by the rules without questioning. He does not say this directly but I hear it. The younger students in my cohort, they nod along, they revise when told to revise. Maybe that is smarter. Maybe I am wasting everyone's time insisting my approach matters. But every time I sit down to write the chapter his way, my hands just stop. Something in me refuses.
entries.nudge_text: What specifically about his way makes your hands stop?
entries.response_text: He wants me to treat the farmers as data points. Sample size, response rates, coding categories. I understand the logic, I am not stupid about methodology. But when I sit down to write it that way, I keep seeing Abu Mahmoud's face. He trusted me with his story. Reducing it to a variable in a regression feels like betraying that trust. Like I am doing the same thing I did as an engineer, just with different paperwork.

entries.initial_entry: Roommates had people over until 2am again. Studied at the library until they kicked me out, then sat in my car in the parking lot reading by phone light. Called Farshad and he laughed, said I am the most stubborn person he knows.
entries.nudge_text: What were you reading in the car?
entries.response_text: Papers on participatory action research. Found this scholar from Brazil who worked with fishing communities fighting industrial pollution. Her whole methodology is about the community being partners in the research, not subjects. Reading her citations like following a trail. If Dr. Khalil will not accept my approach, maybe I can build enough of a foundation that he cannot dismiss it as naive.

entries.initial_entry: Presented the participatory framework to the committee today. Dr. Khalil called it unconventional. Dr. Haddad said she found it compelling. Got approval to continue with modifications. Walked out of that room feeling like I had earned something that was mine.

Had the kids do their Chuseok craft projects and half the class glued their hanbok cutouts upside down, which was honestly kind of funny. Spent twenty minutes scraping dried glue off the tables after they left. My co-teacher asked if I wanted to split the cleanup but she had bus duty so I just did it myself. Not a big deal.

Mom called during lunch to confirm Sunday's menu, she's making japchae and wants me to bring the sweet potato starch noodles from that specific shop near my apartment. Already bought them yesterday because I knew she'd ask. She does this every year before the holiday. I don't mind the predictability of it, actually.
entries.nudge_text: You already knew she'd call about the noodles?

entries.initial_entry: Chuseok at my parents' place went fine but my cousin brought up how she's moving to Melbourne for a master's program and everyone kept congratulating her. Sat there smiling but my chest felt tight the whole time.
entries.nudge_text: Tight chest about what exactly?
entries.response_text: I think it's that nobody ever talks about what I'm doing like it's impressive. I've been at the same school for six years, I live close to home, I do what's expected. And that's fine, I chose this. But when everyone lights up about someone leaving, it makes the staying feel small, even though I know it isn't. Or I think I know.

entries.initial_entry:
One of the newer teachers, Miss Shin, asked me at lunch why I didn't apply for the curriculum coordinator position that opened up. She said it like it was obvious I should want it. I told her I'm happy in the classroom, which is true. I like knowing exactly what my day looks like. I know my students, I know the parents, I know which copy machine jams less. There's something to be said for that.

But she kept pushing, saying I'd be "great at leadership" and how it's a step up. A step up from what? I'm not stuck. I chose to stay in this role. The coordinator position means late meetings, district politics, and honestly a barely noticeable pay bump that isn't worth the headache. My contract is stable, my pension is on track, I know what I'm doing here.

I mentioned it to my mom on the phone later and she agreed, said there's no reason to fix what isn't broken. She reminded me that my uncle switched careers three times and now he's borrowing money from grandma. I laughed but it landed differently than she probably intended.
entries.nudge_text: Why did the uncle comment land differently?
entries.response_text: Because mom used him as a cautionary tale, someone who took risks and failed. And I'm supposed to feel reassured that I'm not him. But part of me wondered if at least he tried something. That's an ugly thought. He's struggling. I don't actually want that. I think I just don't want my only proof that I made the right choice to be someone else's failure.

entries.initial_entry:
Checked my savings account this morning before work, just out of habit. The number is fine, it's growing the way it should. Emergency fund fully topped up, separate account for the apartment deposit whenever I decide to actually buy. But standing in the bathroom brushing my teeth and staring at the banking app, I thought, what am I saving toward exactly? A place that looks like my parents' apartment ten minutes away?

One of my students drew a picture of an airplane today and told me she's going to be a pilot. I said that's wonderful. I meant it. But I kept thinking about it through afternoon classes, the certainty she had, like there was no question the world would let her do it.

Spent Saturday morning reorganizing my lesson plan binder for October. Color-coded tabs, printed calendar, all the supplementary worksheets hole-punched and filed. Took almost three hours and I didn't need to do it until next week but it felt good having everything lined up.

Went to my parents' for dinner after. Dad asked if I'd looked into the apartment listings he sent. I said I'd get to it. The listings are all in this neighborhood, which makes sense practically. Close to school, close to them. I bookmarked them. I'll probably go to a few open houses next month, when the semester pace slows down a bit.
entries.nudge_text: Will you actually go to those open houses?
entries.response_text: Probably yes. My dad will ask about it and I'll feel guilty if I haven't. And honestly the practical reasons are real, the commute would be easy and I could still do Sunday dinners without driving. It just feels like I'm filling in a template someone else drew.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the kids do their Chuseok craft projects and half the class glued their hanbok cutouts upside down, which was honestly kind of funny. Spent twenty minutes scraping dried glue off the tables after they left. My co-teacher asked if I wanted to split the cleanup but she had bus duty so I just did it myself. Not a big deal.

Mom called during lunch to confirm Sunday's menu, she's making japchae and wants me to bring the sweet potato starch noodles from that specific shop near my apartment. Already bought them yesterday because I knew she'd ask. She does this every year before the holiday. I don't mind the predictability of it, actually.
entries.nudge_text: You already knew she'd call about the noodles?

entries.initial_entry: Chuseok at my parents' place went fine but my cousin brought up how she's moving to Melbourne for a master's program and everyone kept congratulating her. Sat there smiling but my chest felt tight the whole time.
entries.nudge_text: Tight chest about what exactly?
entries.response_text: I think it's that nobody ever talks about what I'm doing like it's impressive. I've been at the same school for six years, I live close to home, I do what's expected. And that's fine, I chose this. But when everyone lights up about someone leaving, it makes the staying feel small, even though I know it isn't. Or I think I know.

entries.initial_entry:
One of the newer teachers, Miss Shin, asked me at lunch why I didn't apply for the curriculum coordinator position that opened up. She said it like it was obvious I should want it. I told her I'm happy in the classroom, which is true. I like knowing exactly what my day looks like. I know my students, I know the parents, I know which copy machine jams less. There's something to be said for that.

But she kept pushing, saying I'd be "great at leadership" and how it's a step up. A step up from what? I'm not stuck. I chose to stay in this role. The coordinator position means late meetings, district politics, and honestly a barely noticeable pay bump that isn't worth the headache. My contract is stable, my pension is on track, I know what I'm doing here.

I mentioned it to my mom on the phone later and she agreed, said there's no reason to fix what isn't broken. She reminded me that my uncle switched careers three times and now he's borrowing money from grandma. I laughed but it landed differently than she probably intended.
entries.nudge_text: Why did the uncle comment land differently?
entries.response_text: Because mom used him as a cautionary tale, someone who took risks and failed. And I'm supposed to feel reassured that I'm not him. But part of me wondered if at least he tried something. That's an ugly thought. He's struggling. I don't actually want that. I think I just don't want my only proof that I made the right choice to be someone else's failure.

entries.initial_entry:
Checked my savings account this morning before work, just out of habit. The number is fine, it's growing the way it should. Emergency fund fully topped up, separate account for the apartment deposit whenever I decide to actually buy. But standing in the bathroom brushing my teeth and staring at the banking app, I thought, what am I saving toward exactly? A place that looks like my parents' apartment ten minutes away?

One of my students drew a picture of an airplane today and told me she's going to be a pilot. I said that's wonderful. I meant it. But I kept thinking about it through afternoon classes, the certainty she had, like there was no question the world would let her do it.

Spent Saturday morning reorganizing my lesson plan binder for October. Color-coded tabs, printed calendar, all the supplementary worksheets hole-punched and filed. Took almost three hours and I didn't need to do it until next week but it felt good having everything lined up.

Went to my parents' for dinner after. Dad asked if I'd looked into the apartment listings he sent. I said I'd get to it. The listings are all in this neighborhood, which makes sense practically. Close to school, close to them. I bookmarked them. I'll probably go to a few open houses next month, when the semester pace slows down a bit.
entries.nudge_text: Will you actually go to those open houses?
entries.response_text: Probably yes. My dad will ask about it and I'll feel guilty if I haven't. And honestly the practical reasons are real, the commute would be easy and I could still do Sunday dinners without driving. It just feels like I'm filling in a template someone else drew.

entries.initial_entry: Finished the authentication module for the logistics client ahead of schedule. Took a different approach than what they spec'd out — used a token-based flow instead of the session model they wanted. Should be fine once I walk them through it on Monday.
entries.nudge_text: What made you change the approach?
entries.response_text: Their session model had a scaling problem they had not noticed yet. I flagged it in a comment two weeks ago but nobody responded, so I just built what would actually work. If they push back I have the benchmark numbers ready.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a long call with my brother Faisal today. He has been at the same bank for twenty-two years now and just got promoted to department head. The whole family is celebrating. My mother called separately to tell me how proud she is of him, which — fine. He earned it. I am not bitter about that.

What got under my skin was when Faisal said, near the end of the call, something like "you should think about going back to a real company, you know, before it is too late." He did not mean it cruelly. He genuinely thinks he is helping. But the assumption underneath it — that what I do is somehow less real because I do not sit in an office with a title on my door — that has been following me around since I left the telecom job.

The thing is, I make roughly the same money he does. I pick my own projects. I took three weeks off last November without asking anyone for permission. I built a small open-source tool last year that people in four countries actually use. None of that registers as success in his framework, and honestly, it does not need to. I just wish the conversations did not always circle back to the same place.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for those conversations to stop bothering you?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing they could do. I think the honest answer is that some part of me still measures myself by their yardstick even though I walked away from it. If I were fully settled about the path I chose, Faisal's comment would roll off. It mostly does. But "mostly" is not "completely" and that gap shows up at 11pm when I am lying in bed running the conversation back.

entries.initial_entry: The logistics client wants me to come on-site three days a week starting next month. Three days. In an office, at a desk they pick, on their schedule. Nadia says I should consider it because the contract is good but I feel like my chest is tightening just reading the email.
entries.nudge_text: What part of the on-site request bothers you most?
entries.response_text: The loss of control over my own hours. Right now I start when I want, break when I want, and structure my day around when my brain is actually working. Sitting in their office from 9 to 5 means pretending to be productive during the two hours after lunch when I am basically useless, and then having to leave at five when I am finally hitting a good rhythm. It is just a stupid way to work.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent most of today going back and forth with the logistics client about the on-site requirement. Their project manager, a guy named Khaled, kept framing it as a "collaboration opportunity." I told him I have been collaborating fine over Zoom for six months. He said upper management wants to "see bodies in the building." That phrase — bodies in the building — is exactly why I left corporate work in the first place.

I offered a compromise: one day a week on-site, the rest remote. Khaled said he would take it up the chain but his tone suggested it was not going to fly. Part of me is already drafting a mental exit plan. I have two other clients and a lead on a new project through my old colleague Rami. The money would be tighter for a couple months but manageable.

Nadia thinks I am being rigid. She might be right. But there is a difference between being rigid and knowing what makes you actually function well. Every time I have forced myself into someone else's structure for long enough, the work gets worse, not better. I tried explaining that over dinner and she nodded but I could tell she was thinking about the school fees.

Khaled came back with their answer: two days on-site minimum, non-negotiable. I told him I would need until Sunday to decide. In the meantime, I reached out to Rami about the new project — it is a fintech startup building a payments API. Smaller scope, fully remote, three-month engagement.

Ran the numbers tonight. If I drop the logistics contract and pick up Rami's project, I lose about fifteen percent of my monthly income for the next quarter. After that it evens out if the fintech extends. Not comfortable but not catastrophic either.
entries.nudge_text: How are you feeling about the decision itself?
entries.response_text: Fine, honestly. The math either works or it does not. I should probably focus on getting Rami to confirm the rate before I commit to anything.

entries.initial_entry: Cannot sleep. Keep thinking about whether I am making the smart move or the stubborn one. Nadia did not say anything tonight but she left the school fee spreadsheet open on the kitchen table, which said plenty.
entries.nudge_text: Is Nadia's concern about money or something else?
entries.response_text: Both, I think. The money is real — the kids' school is not cheap and we do not have a fat cushion right now. But underneath that, I think she worries that I will always choose the harder path when an easier one is right there. And she is probably not wrong. I just cannot make myself take the easier path when it means someone else deciding how I spend my days. That is not stubbornness, that is — I do not know what to call it. It is just how I am built.

entries.initial_entry:
Turned down the logistics contract this morning. Told Khaled it was a good run and I appreciate the work but the on-site requirement does not fit how I operate. He was professional about it. Rami confirmed the fintech project at a slightly lower rate than I hoped, but I negotiated a performance bonus clause that could make up the difference.

Nadia and I talked properly last night, not the tense kitchen-table version but actually sitting down after the kids went to bed. I told her I know the numbers are tight and I am not dismissing that. She said she was not asking me to take the on-site deal — she just wanted me to be honest about the trade-offs instead of pretending it was a clean choice. Fair point. It is not a clean choice. I gave something up to keep something I need. That is the deal and I can sit with it.

Took a half-day today because my girlfriend and I had reservations at this omakase place we have been wanting to try for months. My manager gave me this look when I submitted the leave request, like I was abandoning ship during a crisis. We are not even that busy right now. I completed everything on my plate before noon.

The meal was worth it. Twelve courses, and each one was like a small surprise. Jun and I barely talked during the first half because we were so focused on the food. By dessert, we were laughing about how serious we had been. Walking out, I felt genuinely recharged. My manager will survive. I needed that afternoon more than the company needed me at my desk pretending to look occupied.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned your manager's look and feeling like you needed that afternoon. What do you think drives that expectation to always be visibly busy, even when the work is done?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I think it is just the culture here. A lot of the older staff measure commitment by hours, not output. They see leaving at 6pm as not caring, even if you finished everything. My manager came up that way, so he probably assumes everyone else should too. But I do not want to spend ten years proving myself by sitting around after my work is done. I would rather prove it by actually delivering, then having a life outside.

entries.initial_entry:
My dad asked me over dinner last night if I was taking my job seriously. I told him I just got a satisfactory rating on my performance review, and he said that was not the point. He meant whether I was staying late, whether I was sacrificing for the company the way he did at my age. I did not know how to explain to him that I work differently. I finished my projects ahead of deadline, I cover for my team when it matters, and I still make time for things I care about. That should count for something.

I spent today a bit distracted, replaying that conversation. Part of me wonders if he has a point, if I am missing something by not grinding harder. But then I think about my uncle, who worked sixty-hour weeks for thirty years and retired bitter and exhausted. I do not want that. I want to be good at my job and still have energy left for everything else.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned wondering if your dad has a point. What would it take for you to feel confident that your approach is the right one, without needing his validation?
entries.response_text: I guess I would need to keep proving myself over time. If I can keep getting promoted and still leave at reasonable hours, that should speak for itself eventually. We will see.

entries.initial_entry:
Sat in the parking garage after class for probably twenty minutes before I could make myself drive home. Stats exam went okay I think, but my brain is just done. Got a text from my sister right after saying Mom had a rough morning and kept asking where Dad was. He's been gone eleven years. Lisa sounded wrecked on the phone. I told her I'd come up this weekend to give her a break.

Now I'm staring at a stack of reading for my practicum seminar and all I can think about is whether I should have moved back to Duluth instead of doing this degree. Some days this all feels like a selfish detour when the people who need me are somewhere else.
entries.nudge_text: What makes it feel selfish to you?
entries.response_text: Because Lisa's up there handling everything alone while I'm down here highlighting textbooks. She didn't sign up for that. We were supposed to figure out Mom's care together and instead I enrolled in a program. I keep telling myself this degree will help me help more people long-term but honestly? Right now it just means I'm not there when my mother doesn't recognize her own daughters.

entries.initial_entry:
Drove up to Duluth Friday night after my last class. Lisa looked thinner than the last time I saw her, which was only three weeks ago. She's not eating enough. I spent Saturday doing Mom's laundry and sitting with her while Lisa went to get her hair done, which she hasn't done in months.

Mom was calm most of the day. She called me by my name twice, which felt like a gift. We looked through old photos and she remembered the cabin in Brainerd. Small things, but they matter. I made a big pot of chicken wild rice soup and left enough in Lisa's freezer for a week. It's the least I can do when I can't be there every day.

My cohort advisor emailed about missing a group meeting last Friday. I was in Duluth taking care of my mother. Some things are more important than a Zoom call. I'll make it up but I'm not going to apologize for being there.
entries.nudge_text: What's it like juggling those two worlds?
entries.response_text: It's fine, I manage. People do harder things. The advisor is understanding, I'm probably overreacting because I'm tired from the drive.

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas Eve and I'm wrapping presents at the kitchen table while Tyler's on the couch watching something on his phone. He's been quiet lately. I asked him if he was okay and he said yeah, just stressed about early decision. He applied to UW-Madison and won't hear back until February.

I keep wanting to fix it for him. Check his portal, email admissions, do something. But he's seventeen and he needs to learn to sit with uncertainty. That's the hardest part of caring about someone, I think. Knowing when to step back. I'm not very good at it. I'd rather stay up all night worrying on someone's behalf than let them worry alone. I don't know if that's love or just a habit I can't break.

Weird thing happened today. I was reading for my human development course, this chapter about attachment theory and caregiving patterns, and I just started crying at the kitchen table. Tyler walked in and asked what was wrong and I said nothing, just allergies, which is such a mom thing to say and we both knew it was a lie.

The chapter was about how some people develop their identity almost entirely around being needed by others. How it starts in childhood, usually with a parent who needed caretaking themselves, and it becomes this pattern where you feel most yourself when you're giving to someone else. And I'm sitting there with a highlighter in my hand thinking, this is literally my biography.

I called Lisa after Tyler went to bed. Told her about the chapter and she laughed and said yeah, no kidding, you've been like that since you were ten. She reminded me how I used to make lunches for both of us when Mom was going through her rough patches. I was in fourth grade. I don't remember it as a burden though. I remember feeling useful. Important, even.

I guess what's getting to me is wondering whether I chose social work because I genuinely want to help people or because I literally don't know how to exist without someone to take care of. That's a scary thought to put on paper. But my practicum supervisor keeps saying self-awareness is the first tool, so here it is. I'm aware. Now what.
entries.nudge_text: Does it have to be one or the other?
entries.response_text: Maybe not. Lisa said something similar, that both things can be true. That I can genuinely care about this work and also recognize the pattern. I just don't want to end up burning out because I never learned how to sit still without a to-do list for someone else. I watched that happen with Mom before she got sick. She ran herself ragged for everyone and then there was nobody left to run for her.

entries.initial_entry:
After that breakdown earlier I decided to actually be productive. Finished the attachment theory chapter and three of the discussion questions. Emailed my practicum site to confirm January hours. Called Mom's memory care coordinator to check in on the transition plan for when she needs more support, which might be sooner than we thought based on what Lisa's been describing.

The coordinator said they have a spot opening up in the assisted living wing in February. Lisa and I need to talk about it. It would take the daily pressure off Lisa but Mom would hate it. She still has enough awareness to know she's being moved, and I think that would devastate her. But Lisa can't keep doing this alone and I can't move back to Duluth right now, not with one semester left.

Made a list of things to handle this week: Tyler's FAFSA, my spring course registration, groceries for New Year's, and calling the insurance company about Mom's coverage changes. Also need to pick up Lisa's Christmas gift, which I completely forgot to order. She wanted that weighted blanket. I'll find one at Target tomorrow.

I noticed I feel calmer after making the list. That's the pattern again, isn't it. Anxiety happens and I make a list of ways to help other people and suddenly I can breathe. At least I can see it now even if I can't stop it.

Lisa just told me she's thinking about taking a leave from her job to handle Mom full-time. She can't afford that. I should be the one doing this but I'm stuck here finishing a degree that doesn't pay anyone's bills yet.

Had a long phone call with Lisa last night. We talked for almost two hours, which we haven't done in months because usually our calls are logistics. Where's the insurance card, did you call the pharmacy, can you come up this weekend. But this time we actually talked.

She told me she doesn't resent me for going back to school. I didn't realize how much I needed to hear that until she said it and I couldn't talk for a minute. She said she's proud of me, which made me feel about six years old in the best way. Then she said she's also exhausted and scared and doesn't know how long she can keep doing the day-to-day with Mom by herself.

We made a plan. I'll come up every other weekend starting this month, and we're going to look into the assisted living option together. Not making any decisions yet, just touring the place and talking to Mom's doctor. Lisa also said she wants me to stop apologizing every time we talk. She said, "You're doing what you need to do. Stop acting like you're abandoning us." I wrote that on a sticky note and put it on my laptop.

Tyler overheard part of the conversation and came in after to ask if Grandma was okay. I told him the truth, that she's not getting better but we're figuring it out as a family. He said he'd come with me to Duluth next time. He didn't have to offer that. Moments like that make me think I must have done something right with him.
entries.nudge_text: What did it feel like to hear Lisa say she's proud of you?

entries.initial_entry:
A classmate in my cohort made a comment during seminar about how people who go into social work are usually "working out their own stuff." She said it like it was a joke but half the room went quiet. I wanted to say something but I just sat there. She's twenty-six and has never had to take care of anyone but herself.

That's not fair, I don't know her story. But it bothered me more than it should have. Maybe because I know she's not wrong, at least in my case. But there's a difference between understanding your own patterns and having some kid with no life experience reduce your entire motivation to a punchline. I'm here because I've lived this. That counts for something.
entries.nudge_text: You said she's not wrong. What part felt true?
entries.response_text: The part about working out your own stuff, obviously. But everyone does that. Every doctor who lost a parent to cancer, every teacher who had a bad one growing up. That's not a weakness, that's fuel. I just didn't appreciate the delivery.

entries.initial_entry:
Drove up to Duluth Saturday morning. Tyler came with me. He sat with Mom for an hour while Lisa and I toured the assisted living wing. It's clean, the staff seemed attentive, there's a garden area for warmer months. Mom would have a private room.

Lisa and I agreed we'll bring it up with Mom's doctor at the next appointment. Neither of us said it out loud but we both know this is probably happening in the next few months. On the drive home Tyler was quiet. Then he said, "She remembered my name today." I just reached over and squeezed his hand.

Made it home by eight. Did laundry, packed Tyler's lunch for Monday, prepped my notes for practicum. Ordinary things that felt good to do.
Spring semester started. First class back and I actually felt ready. Not fixed, not finished figuring anything out. Just present. Lisa texted me good luck this morning with a heart emoji. I saved it.

Arjun's old company reached out to him about a relocation package to Bangalore. Good salary, housing allowance, school fees covered for Meera. He brought it up over dinner like it was just information, but I could tell he was testing the water. If we moved, I'd have to give up my lab placement and start over somewhere, if I even could.

The sensible thing would have been to say let's talk about it seriously. He's been carrying us financially for four years now and the offer was generous. Instead I just said I'm not ready to leave yet. He didn't push it. But I spent the rest of the night doing the math in my head — how much longer the PhD will take, how much we've burned through savings, whether Meera even remembers what it was like when we weren't watching every expense. I keep choosing this and I don't fully know why anymore.
entries.nudge_text: What would finishing feel like, if not relief?
entries.response_text: Like proof that it was worth it. That I didn't just burn four years and all our savings on something selfish. But sometimes I wonder if I'm just trying to outlast the doubt at this point, like if I keep going long enough the answer will show up on its own.

entries.initial_entry: Submitted the revised chapter draft to my supervisor this morning. Picked Meera up from after-school care, stopped at the wet market for vegetables, made dal and rice for dinner. Nothing remarkable. Normal day.

Meera drew a picture of me at my desk with all these little papers flying around and wrote "Amma working hard" underneath. I stuck it on the wall next to my monitor. Some days that's enough to keep going.

Got an email today from a former colleague, Deepa, about a senior position opening at the water authority back in Chennai. Permanent role, government pension, the whole thing. She said she thought of me immediately. My mother would cry with happiness if I took it. Appa would finally stop asking pointed questions about "what the plan is." Arjun's parents would stop whispering about how their son married a woman who can't settle down.

I sat with the email open for maybe twenty minutes. I could picture the whole life — the flat in Adyar, Meera going to the same school I went to, weekend lunches at my parents' house. Predictable. Comfortable. Everything mapped out for the next thirty years. And I felt this thing in my chest, not panic exactly but something close. Like the walls of that life were already closing in even though I haven't said yes to anything. I know the stipend is barely enough. I know Arjun is tired of being the only one earning properly. I know my savings are basically gone. I know all of this.

But I closed the email and went back to my data. I'll probably tell Deepa thanks but not now, maybe later, which is what I always say. The part I can't explain to anyone is that the uncertainty here feels more honest than the certainty there. That sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud but there it is.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'honest' mean here — honest about what?
entries.response_text: About what I actually want, I think. The Chennai job would be fine. Good, even. But it would be someone else's version of my life. The one everyone expected me to have. Here I'm struggling but at least the struggle is mine.

entries.initial_entry: Lab equipment broke down again. Spent the whole day troubleshooting instead of running samples. Came home to Meera already asleep and leftover rice in the cooker.

Amma called tonight and casually mentioned that Deepa's husband just bought a new flat in OMR. Then she said, "You know Deepa was asking about you, whether you've thought about that position." So apparently the whole family knows now. Great.

I told her I'm focused on my research and she did that thing where she goes quiet for a few seconds and then says "of course, of course, we just worry." Arjun was listening from the other room and afterward he said maybe we should at least talk about a timeline. He wasn't being pushy about it but the fact that he's now aligned with my mother is — I don't know. I told him my timeline is when I finish and he said that's not a timeline, that's a hope. He's not wrong. But the alternative is giving up something I've worked four years on because everyone else is tired of waiting. I'm tired too. But tired isn't the same as done.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for tired to become done?

entries.initial_entry:
Had a good meeting with my supervisor today. She said the fieldwork chapter is strong and that the analysis is heading in the right direction. First time in weeks I've felt like I actually know what I'm doing. She also mentioned a conference in March where I could present, which would be good for the CV.

Afterward I walked to the hawker centre near campus and got mee goreng and just sat there eating by myself for a while. It was raining and the whole place smelled like wet concrete and frying oil. I watched the aunties running the stalls — same thing every day, same dishes, same rhythm — and I thought about how there's a kind of steadiness in doing work you've chosen, even if nobody else sees why you chose it.

I called Arjun on the way home and told him about the conference. He sounded genuinely happy for me, which I needed more than I realized. I know this path doesn't make sense on paper. The money is bad, the timeline is uncertain, and every aunty and uncle back home thinks I've lost the plot. But today felt like one of those days where I remembered why I started. That has to count for something.

My brother-in-law made a comment at Sunday dinner about how I must enjoy "having so much free time now" and I've been thinking about it all week. Free time? I coordinate three different after-school activities, manage the household budget down to the krone, and I've been teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master. Just because there's no office title attached doesn't mean I'm sitting around watching television.

I know I shouldn't let it get to me. But there's this part of me that keeps wanting to list all the things I've done this month, all the skills I've picked up, all the ways I've improved how our home runs. The furniture business actually got two new orders last week. That's something, isn't it? Sometimes I feel like I'm running faster than I ever did at the firm, just with nobody keeping score anymore.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like that comment really stayed with you. What does "keeping score" mean to you now, outside of the traditional work context?

entries.initial_entry:
Finished the oak sideboard restoration today. Client picked it up and actually teared up a bit when she saw it. Said it had been her grandmother's and she'd been looking for someone to bring it back properly for years. Took about forty hours total across two weeks.

Started sketching plans for a home workshop expansion. The garage layout isn't working anymore, especially with winter keeping me indoors more. Found some modular storage systems from a German supplier that could work. The twins helped me measure the space after school. Emma is surprisingly good at holding a tape measure steady. I'm thinking if I reorganize everything by March, I could take on bigger pieces. Maybe even start documenting the process to teach others eventually.
entries.nudge_text: That's a meaningful project completion. What was most satisfying about the sideboard work - the technical challenge, the client's response, or something else?
entries.response_text: Honestly? The technical part. Matching the original veneer grain pattern took me three tries and about five hours of research into 1920s Danish furniture styles. When I finally got it right, I took photos before and after just for my own records. The client's reaction was nice, genuinely touching even, but I knew it was good work before she saw it. The satisfaction came earlier, when I stepped back and could see I'd actually pulled it off. The workshop expansion isn't just about space. I want to be able to take on restoration challenges I can't even attempt right now with the current setup.

entries.initial_entry:
My brother-in-law made a comment at Sunday dinner about how I must enjoy "having so much free time now" and I've been thinking about it all week. Free time? I coordinate three different after-school activities, manage the household budget down to the krone, and I've been teaching myself upholstery techniques that would take most people years to master. Just because there's no office title attached doesn't mean I'm sitting around watching television.

I know I shouldn't let it get to me. But there's this part of me that keeps wanting to list all the things I've done this month, all the skills I've picked up, all the ways I've improved how our home runs. The furniture business actually got two new orders last week. That's something, isn't it? Sometimes I feel like I'm running faster than I ever did at the firm, just with nobody keeping score anymore.

The satisfaction came earlier, when I stepped back and could see I'd actually pulled it off. The workshop expansion isn't just about space. I want to be able to take on restoration challenges I can't even attempt right now with the current setup.

entries.initial_entry:
Started the new term today. My Primary 3 class has 32 students this year, and already I can tell Jayden in the back row is going to be a handful. Had a meeting with Mrs. Tan, my mentor, about my classroom management plan.

She said my lesson on fractions last week was "creative but needed tighter pacing." I stayed until 7pm revising the slides. Arjun texted asking if we're still going for Korean BBQ on Saturday - I said yes but I'm already thinking about how much marking I need to clear.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned saying yes to Korean BBQ but your mind went straight to marking. What's making you think about work when you're making weekend plans?

entries.initial_entry:
I've been thinking about something Mrs. Tan said during our feedback session. She mentioned that I spend more time on "fun activities" than other beginning teachers and asked if I was "prioritizing student engagement over curriculum coverage." It stung more than it should have.

The thing is, my kids actually look forward to my lessons. Aisha told me yesterday that math is her favorite subject now because of my fraction pizza activity. But I also notice the other teachers leaving at 5pm while I'm still cutting out laminated shapes at 6:30. Maybe I'm doing this wrong. Or maybe they're just faster because they've been doing it longer. I don't know.
entries.nudge_text: That comment from Mrs. Tan clearly landed hard. What is it about her feedback that got under your skin?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I think I'm just tired. It's the end of term and everyone's stressed. She probably didn't mean anything by it - she's been teaching for 20 years and has seen a hundred new teachers come through. I'll focus on pacing next term and it'll be fine.

entries.initial_entry:
Last day of term. I just got home and it's 9pm. End-of-year reports took forever because I insisted on writing personalized comments for all 32 students instead of using the template phrases. Mrs. Tan actually complimented my reports today - said the parents would appreciate the detail. Small win, I guess.

But I'm so tired. Arjun and I were supposed to go to the Christmas market at Gardens by the Bay tonight but I cancelled. He was understanding but I could hear the disappointment. This is the third thing I've cancelled this month. Last week was my cousin Meera's birthday dinner, and before that it was supposed to be a spa day with my college friends.

Everyone keeps telling me the first year is the hardest. My colleague Rachel, who's in her second year, said she barely saw her husband for six months. Is that supposed to make me feel better? Mum called yesterday and when I told her I was exhausted, she said "but this is what you wanted, no?" And yes, I did want this. I love being in the classroom. Today during our class party, watching the kids exchange cards and eat cookies, I felt so happy. Dinesh, my quietest student, actually came up and gave me a handmade card that said "Best Teacher."

I just wish I could have both - be good at this job AND have a life outside of it. Right now it feels like I'm choosing one over the other and somehow still failing at both.
entries.nudge_text: You said it feels like you're failing at both work and life outside it. But you also mentioned getting compliments on your reports and a handmade card from a student. What would 'not failing' actually look like to you?
entries.response_text: I think not failing would mean... being present. When I'm at work, fully at work without guilt about what I'm missing. And when I'm with Arjun or my friends, actually being there instead of mentally going through my to-do list. Right now I'm always half somewhere else. The card from Dinesh was beautiful but even in that moment part of me was thinking about how I still needed to input grades into the portal.

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas Eve. Arjun and I are at his parents' place in Johor for the long weekend. His mum made mutton biryani and there's way too much food as usual. His younger sister Kavitha is back from Melbourne so there's been a lot of catching up.

We went to a night market yesterday and I bought these ridiculous matching couple keychains shaped like curry puffs. Arjun pretended to be embarrassed but I caught him showing his sister later. Today we just lazed around - watched a movie, played cards with his grandma, ate leftovers for lunch. His parents have this swing seat in their garden and I spent an hour there just reading a novel. An actual novel, not a teaching resource.

The contrast from last week is stark. I haven't thought about school once today. Well, except when Kavitha asked about my job and I ended up talking about Dinesh's card for ten minutes. But that was nice talking, not stressed thinking.

Arjun asked if I was feeling better. I said yes. He said "good, you looked really worn out" and honestly, I hadn't realized how bad it had gotten until I stepped away from it. Tomorrow we're driving to Malacca for the day to try this famous chicken rice ball place his colleague recommended.

Back in Singapore. The Malacca trip was lovely - we walked through Jonker Street, ate cendol, took photos by those colorful murals. Arjun said we should do more spontaneous trips like this. I agreed, but already my mind is drifting to school reopening next week.

I've been thinking about what "presence" means. Over the past few days I felt like myself again - laughing at dumb jokes, being curious about things that aren't work-related, enjoying food without rushing. But now that I'm home, sitting in my apartment with my laptop open, I can feel the familiar anxiety creeping back. I haven't opened my email yet but I know there will be things waiting. Part of me wants to check "just to know what's there." Part of me wants to protect this feeling a little longer.
entries.nudge_text: You're sitting with the choice of whether to open your email right now. What do you think would happen if you waited until tomorrow?
entries.response_text:
Probably nothing catastrophic. School doesn't officially start until next Wednesday. But there's this voice that says I'll be behind, that other teachers are already preparing, that Mrs. Tan might have sent feedback I need to act on. The rational part of me knows one more day won't matter. The anxious part is already composing a to-do list.

I'm going to close the laptop. Tomorrow is soon enough.

entries.initial_entry:
First week back and I'm already feeling the pressure. Today Mrs. Tan called me into her office to discuss my "development goals" for the year. She brought up the MOE promotion framework and asked where I saw myself in five years. I panicked and said "senior teacher track" because that's what you're supposed to say, right?

But sitting here now, I'm not even sure that's true. The senior teachers I see are in meetings constantly, doing administrative work, mentoring others. They're hardly ever in classrooms with kids anymore. And isn't that the whole point? Isn't that why I became a teacher?

I called my friend Meilin who teaches at another school. She's the same age but already coordinating the Science department's enrichment programs. She sounded excited about it - talked about "visibility" and "building a portfolio." When I told her about feeling conflicted, she said "Priya, you can't stay a classroom teacher forever. That's just how the system works."

So now I'm confused and also a little annoyed. Why can't I just be good at teaching? Why does everything have to be about climbing some ladder? I keep thinking about Dinesh and how he's finally starting to participate in class. That matters to me. More than being on some committee or getting a title.

But then there's Mum and Papa, who video called on New Year's Day asking about my "career progression." And Arjun's parents, who are so proud that both their kids have "professional jobs." And Mrs. Tan, who genuinely wants to help me succeed by her definition of success.

I feel like I'm disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned feeling like you're disappointing everyone by wanting something simpler. But who exactly decides what 'success' means for your career - them or you?
entries.response_text: That's a loaded question. In theory, me. In practice, there are real consequences to not playing the game - slower pay progression, fewer opportunities, being seen as lacking drive. It's not as simple as "follow your heart" when the system is designed around a specific path.

entries.initial_entry:
Something good happened today and I want to remember it. We started a new unit on creative writing and I let the kids write about anything they wanted. Most wrote about their holidays, pets, favorite games. But Aisha - the one who said math was her favorite subject - wrote this whole story about a girl who could fly but chose to walk because she liked how flowers smelled at ground level.

I pulled her aside after class to tell her how much I loved it. She got so shy and happy at the same time. Her mom picked her up today and I mentioned the story - she looked surprised, said Aisha usually hates writing.

Moments like this are why I teach. Not for reviews or promotions or what it looks like on paper. For a 9-year-old discovering she can create something beautiful with words. I don't know how to put that in a "development goal" but it's the truest thing I know about myself right now.
Long Sunday. Arjun and I had brunch at Atlas then walked around Bugis. Nice day but my mind kept wandering to tomorrow, to meetings, to whether I'm doing enough.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned wondering if you're doing enough. Enough for what, or for whom?
entries.response_text: For myself, I think. Or the version of myself I thought I'd be by now. When I was in NIE, I imagined being this effortlessly great teacher who also had time for friends and hobbies and travel. Instead I'm constantly choosing between being mediocre at everything or good at one thing while the rest falls apart. Today was supposed to be relaxing but I couldn't stop calculating how many hours until Monday. That's not who I want to be.

entries.initial_entry:
My mother called to remind me about the novena for Tía Carmen's anniversary next week. Didn't need the reminder — it's been circled on my calendar since January. When I was small I used to think these gatherings were just adults talking over coffee, but now I understand them differently. Nine nights of prayer, nine nights of family filling up the house. There's something steady about it.

I taught my third graders how to fold paper flowers today for the altar at school. One little boy asked why we do it every year and I told him the same thing my grandmother told me: because the dead don't really leave if we keep making space for them. He seemed satisfied with that. Sometimes children accept what adults forget — that repetition isn't emptiness, it's a choice to keep something alive.

Carlos asked to meet my family this weekend. I made up something about it being a busy week with the novena. He looked hurt. I feel like I'm lying to two worlds at once — telling him we just need more time, telling my mother I'm too focused on work to date. Neither story is true. The true story is that I don't know how to bring a divorced man into a family that still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you just told your mother the truth?
entries.response_text: She wouldn't yell. That's not how she operates. She'd get quiet and then start asking gentle questions that feel like tiny knives — How long has this been going on? Has he been to confession? What does his family think about all this? And I'd have no good answers. I don't even know if I want to marry him. I just want space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations pressing down on me before I've made up my own mind.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the evening at Abuela's preparing food for tomorrow's final night of the novena. Chopped onions until my eyes burned. My cousin Daniela brought her new baby and everyone passed him around the kitchen like a small warm blessing. The prayers start at seven. I'll be there early to set up chairs like I have been every year since I was fourteen.

The new principal at school announced we're "modernizing" the curriculum. Tablets for third graders. Interactive lessons instead of textbooks. She presented it like everyone should be excited but I sat in that meeting thinking about Sister Magdalena who taught at this school for forty years and how her chalkboard handwriting was so beautiful my mother still talks about it. I'm not against technology — I use my phone constantly, I'm not a dinosaur — but something about the way the principal said "we need to move past old methods" made me want to dig my heels in.

After the meeting Roberto from fifth grade asked what I thought and I said it sounded fine. I don't know why I didn't say what I was really thinking. Maybe because I didn't want to be the young teacher defending old ways. Maybe because I don't even know if I'm right. The kids might love the tablets. Sister Magdalena isn't here to ask.

I keep thinking about how my mother taught me to read with a book of saints' stories, turning actual pages, her finger tracing the words. What gets lost when everything becomes a screen? Or am I just afraid of change and calling it something nicer?
entries.nudge_text: You said you didn't speak up. What would you have said if you had?
entries.response_text: That maybe we should pilot it in one classroom first instead of rolling it out everywhere at once. That the older teachers who've been here for decades deserve to have their methods respected, not treated like problems to be solved. That learning isn't just information transfer — it's also ritual, routine, the physical feeling of a pencil in your hand. But standing up in a staff meeting to say "I think ritual matters" sounds ridiculous. So I stayed quiet and now I'm typing it into my phone at 11pm instead.

entries.initial_entry: My friend Valentina says I'm "too attached to the past." She means it gently but it still stings. She left Guadalajara for Monterrey and barely comes back for holidays now. Good for her. I'm not running from anything by staying. This is where I'm needed. My grandmother is eighty-three. My students know me. The roots here are mine.
entries.nudge_text: What about her comment bothered you most?

entries.initial_entry:
Carlos and I had a real fight. Not yelling — we don't do that — but the cold kind where you both know something broke. He asked if I was ashamed of him and I didn't answer fast enough. I'm not ashamed. I'm scared. There's a difference but I couldn't find the words to explain it while he was standing there looking at me like he'd already made up his mind about what my silence meant.

My parents have been married for thirty-two years. They met at church, got engaged with my grandfather's blessing, had the wedding everyone still talks about. That's the script. I don't know how to write a different one and still feel like myself. Carlos doesn't fit the script but neither does walking away from him. I lay awake until three trying to figure out which betrayal I could live with.
entries.nudge_text: Is there a version of this where nobody gets betrayed?
entries.response_text: I want to say yes. The optimistic answer is that my family loves me and they'll accept whoever makes me happy. My cousin got pushback about her civil ceremony and now everyone's fine at Christmas dinner. But I know my mother. She carries things quietly for years. She'd show up to the wedding and smile and then I'd catch her crying in the kitchen when she thinks no one's looking. I don't know if I can build a life on top of that image. Maybe that's my problem — I'm not choosing between Carlos and my family. I'm choosing between the version of me who disappoints people and the version who disappears herself to keep everyone comfortable.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to confession today for the first time in months. Didn't plan to — I was walking past the church after buying bread and the door was open. Father Miguel has known me since my first communion so there was no hiding behind anonymity. I told him I've been lying to my mother about something important. He didn't ask what. Just waited.

I ended up talking for twenty minutes about Carlos, about feeling stuck, about how the rules I grew up with don't seem to have instructions for this situation. He said something I keep turning over: "The tradition doesn't ask you to stop living. It asks you to bring your life to it honestly." I don't know exactly what that means yet but it felt like permission to stop pretending there's a perfect answer.

After confession I sat in the pew where my grandmother always sits and just breathed. The church smelled the same way it did when I was seven. Some things don't change and today that felt like comfort instead of a cage.

School starts back next week. Spent the afternoon organizing my classroom. Put up the same San Francisco de Asis poster my mother gave me my first year teaching. The corners are getting worn. Considered replacing it but decided against it. Some things are allowed to show their age.

I told my mother about Carlos. Not everything — not the divorce — but that I've been seeing someone. She asked when she could meet him and I panicked and said "soon" which is what I always say. She knows me too well. She could hear the hesitation and asked what I wasn't telling her.

I almost said it. The words were right there. But then my abuela came into the room asking about dinner and the moment passed and I felt relief and shame in equal measure. Relief because I got more time. Shame because I'm a grown woman hiding in the kitchen of my childhood home, still arranging my life around moments that are safe.

Carlos deserves better than being my secret. I deserve better than feeling like a coward. Something has to break.
entries.nudge_text: What would breaking look like for you?
entries.response_text: Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after. Not performing okay-ness at the next family gathering. Letting my mother feel her feelings without me trying to manage them. Maybe it looks like a difficult Sunday dinner where Carlos sits across from my father and answers questions about his first marriage. Maybe it looks like my grandmother going quiet in that way she does when she disapproves. I can survive that. I've survived other hard things. What I can't survive is another six months of living in two separate worlds that never touch.

entries.initial_entry:
Brought Carlos to Sunday lunch at my parents' house. My hands were shaking when we walked through the door. My mother had set an extra place — she knew I wouldn't come alone after our last conversation. She's always known more than she lets on.

It wasn't easy. There were silences that stretched too long and my father asked pointed questions about where Carlos sees himself in five years. But my grandmother held his hand when he talked about his daughter from his first marriage, and said children are always a blessing no matter the circumstances. That's not the same as approval but it's not rejection either.

After dessert, while Carlos helped my father clear plates, my mother pulled me aside and said "I don't understand all of it but I can see he's good to you." That's enough for now. Maybe that's what Father Miguel meant — bringing my life to the tradition honestly doesn't mean it fits perfectly. It just means I stop hiding the pieces that don't.

- Finally shipped the offline-sync feature for the rural schools module
- Took three sprints longer than planned but it actually works on 2G now
- Ravi sir from the Hubli pilot sent a voice note saying the kids were using it during lunch breaks
- That voice note is doing more for me right now than any sprint review ever has
entries.nudge_text: What was it about that voice note specifically?
entries.response_text: He sounded surprised. Like he genuinely did not expect it to work. And the kids laughing in the background, just messing around with the app like it was a game. That gap between what we built and what they turned it into, I don't know, it reminded me of when Suresh sir first showed me a terminal in college.

entries.initial_entry: Priya got her campus placement offer today. It is a decent product company in Hyderabad with good benefits. Amma cried at lunch, the happy kind, and then immediately started worrying about Priya living alone. I told her Priya would be fine. Honestly I am just relieved one less thing is on the table financially.

Coming back to this because something has been sitting with me since the afternoon. When Priya showed me the offer letter I noticed the salary. It is nearly the same as mine, fresh out of college. I am not jealous of her, that is not it. She earned it completely. But it made me do the math I keep avoiding.

I have been at this startup for three years and the increments have been modest. The work is the most fulfilling thing I have done but I also know Amma's knees are getting worse and the consultation she needs is not cheap. Priya's new salary helps but she will have her own rent and expenses. I keep circling back to the same question without finishing the thought.
entries.nudge_text: What's the question you keep not finishing?

entries.initial_entry: Had coffee with Nikhil today. He moved to a fintech company six months ago, says the work is mostly CRUD but the pay let him put his parents in a nicer flat. He was not bragging. He looked tired actually. I kept nodding and thinking about Amma's knee and the 2G kids in Hubli at the same time.
entries.nudge_text: Can you have both or is that the problem?
entries.response_text: I think the honest answer is I don't know yet and that scares me more than either option. If I leave for more money I know I'll resent sitting in standups about payment conversion funnels. But if Amma needs that surgery and I am still making idealistic-startup money, then what was all of it for? I keep framing it like a binary but maybe the part I can't say out loud is that I'm afraid whichever I pick I'll feel like I failed someone.

entries.initial_entry:
Came back to write more because I could not sleep after that last entry. Nikhil also mentioned that his old manager from the gaming company reached out to me apparently. Said they are looking for someone to lead backend on an accessibility project, adaptive interfaces for visually impaired users. The pay would be significantly better than what I make now and the work would not feel empty the way pure fintech might.

But here is the thing. If I leave the startup, the offline-sync project does not have a clear owner. Karthik could take it over eventually but he joined four months ago and the codebase is, frankly, held together with duct tape and my institutional memory. The Hubli pilot is supposed to expand to three more districts by January. If that rollout stumbles because of a handoff gap, actual kids lose access.

I know I am not irreplaceable in some cosmic sense. People leave jobs and the world continues. But the timing feels like it matters. Priya's placement means I could take a risk on staying. Or it means I finally have the breathing room to go somewhere that pays properly. Same fact, two completely different stories depending on which direction I face. Lying here staring at the ceiling fan trying to figure out which story I actually believe.

Amma asked me at breakfast why I have been quiet lately. I said work stress, which is not a lie but is not the whole thing either. She said she hopes I am not overworking myself for her sake. The way she said it made me think she knows more than she lets on. That made my chest tight for the rest of the morning.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she actually knows?

entries.initial_entry: The Saturday bootcamp kids presented their projects today. One of them, Farah, built a bus-route finder for her neighborhood because her grandmother takes the wrong bus sometimes. It is buggy and the UI is rough but she demoed it to the group with so much confidence. I almost lost it in front of twelve teenagers.
entries.nudge_text: What got to you about Farah's project?
entries.response_text: She reminded me of why I started this in the first place. She was not building it for marks or a portfolio. She built it because her grandmother needed help and she figured out she had the skills to do something about it. That is the whole thing, right there. That is the part I keep forgetting when I am up at night running salary comparisons.

entries.initial_entry:
- Got home from the bootcamp feeling good then opened LinkedIn and saw three more recruiter messages
- One of them literally said "stop leaving money on the table"
- Closed the app but the phrase is stuck in my head
- Maybe I should just turn off notifications for a while, I cannot think clearly with all this noise

Told Nadia she could take the summer to think about London but that we would discuss it seriously in June. She looked relieved but I felt nothing. I keep wondering if I am just delaying because I do not know how to say no to her face.
entries.nudge_text: What would saying no actually cost you?
entries.response_text: More than the argument itself. I think it would mean admitting the business is not enough to hold the family together the way it held us when Baba was running it.

entries.initial_entry:
My cousin Faisal called to say his son is taking over his construction firm this year. He did not say it to make a point, but it landed like one. His kid is twenty-three and already stepping in. Meanwhile I cannot even get Nadia to visit the warehouse.

People keep acting like I should just let her go do whatever she wants. Rania says I am being stubborn. But nobody asks what happens to this company in fifteen years. Nobody asks who carries it forward. I did not choose this either, but I showed up.
entries.nudge_text: Did you want someone to ask you that back then?
entries.response_text: That was a different time. There was no question to ask.

entries.initial_entry:
The new Italian distributor wants us to rebrand. Sleeker labels, English-only packaging, drop the family crest. He said the current look feels "regional." I almost hung up on him. Regional. My father designed that label with a calligrapher from Salt in 1982. It has been on every bottle we have ever shipped.

I brought it up with Samir, my operations manager, and he shrugged and said maybe the Italian has a point. That the European market responds to minimalism. I asked him if he would strip his grandmother's embroidery off the wall because a decorator said it clashed with the furniture. He laughed. I was not joking.

Rania told me later I am letting sentiment run the business. Maybe. But if I strip away everything that connects this company to where it came from, what exactly am I running? A logistics operation with my name on it. That is not what Baba built and it is not what I agreed to carry.

Could not sleep last night. Kept turning over the rebranding conversation. Went down to the kitchen at two in the morning and found Baba's old notebooks in the drawer where Mama keeps the good tablecloths. He used to write down every buyer's name, where they were from, what they ordered. Pages of it in his careful handwriting.

I sat there reading for an hour. He wrote about a French buyer in 1991 who wanted to change the oil blend and he refused. Lost the account. Got it back three years later when the buyer admitted the original was better. I do not know if that story is supposed to comfort me or scare me. The market is different now.

The thing that unsettles me is not the Italian distributor. It is that part of me can see his point. The rational part. And I do not know what it means that I can see it and still feel like acting on it would be a kind of betrayal. Like I would be the one who finally broke the thread.
entries.nudge_text: What does that thread hold together for you?
entries.response_text: Everything. The way we do business, yes, but also who I am in relation to my father. If I change the way he built things, I am saying his way was not good enough. And I am not ready to say that, even if the numbers eventually force me to.

entries.initial_entry:
Nadia brought home a prospectus from the London film school. Left it on the kitchen table like it was a magazine. Rania pretended not to notice. I noticed.

She has been building her case quietly. Showing us short films she made on her phone, mentioning alumni who work at Al Jazeera and BBC Arabic. She is smart about it. She knows a direct argument will not work with me so she is laying evidence like a lawyer. I would admire it if it were not aimed at dismantling the one plan I had for this family's future.

My brother Kamal called and said I should let her go, that holding her back will only push her further away. Easy for him to say. He moved to Dubai fifteen years ago and calls on holidays. He has no idea what it takes to keep things together here. To sit at Mama's table every Friday and hold the center while everyone else drifts toward their own orbits. Someone has to be the anchor and apparently that someone is always me.
entries.nudge_text: Is being the anchor something you chose or something that happened to you?
entries.response_text: Both, I think. It happened because I was the eldest and stayed. But I also chose it every single time I could have left. The problem is I chose it so many times it stopped feeling like a choice and started feeling like who I am. I do not know how to separate the two anymore.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Two shipments delayed because of port congestion in Aqaba, had to personally call four buyers to explain. Spent most of Thursday driving to Irbid because Mama's water heater broke and my brother the Dubai expert could not exactly fix it from there.

Came home to Rania and the kids eating mansaf without me. They saved me a plate. Ate it cold at ten o'clock watching the news. Just one of those weeks where everything takes twice as long and nothing feels finished.

Actually, the mansaf was good even cold. Rania uses Mama's recipe, the one with the dried yogurt from the women's co-op in Ajloun. Small thing but it reminded me that some things do carry forward without anyone forcing them.

Quarterly numbers came in and they are flat. Not terrible, but flat in a market that is growing means we are losing ground. Samir put together a presentation about expanding into the Gulf retail market. New packaging, smaller bottles, gift sets for Ramadan. I told him to go ahead with the proposal.

I did not fight it this time. Maybe because I am tired, maybe because the port delays last week reminded me how fragile our European pipeline really is. Diversifying makes sense. I told myself the Gulf packaging can look different from the European line without touching the original label. A compromise. Baba would have called it something else.

Nadia asked me at dinner if she could shadow a documentary crew in Amman next week. Some university project. I said fine without really thinking about it. Rania gave me a look, like she was surprised I did not put up a fight. I did not have one in me tonight.
entries.nudge_text: Are these concessions or decisions?

entries.initial_entry:
Took the kids to Friday prayers at the old mosque near Jabal Amman instead of our usual one. The imam there knew my father. Afterward he told my youngest, Omar, a story about how Baba once donated an entire shipment to a refugee camp in the eighties. Omar's eyes went wide. He had never heard that one.

Drove home with all three kids in the back seat arguing about whether Jiddo was a hero or just generous. Nadia said both. I did not correct anyone. It was a good morning.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have corrected?
entries.response_text: That he was not being generous. He was being obligated. In our family, in our community, you give because that is what you do. It is not heroism, it is just the way things work. But watching Omar hear it as something extraordinary made me realize maybe that way of doing things is not as obvious to them as it was to us. And that worries me.

entries.initial_entry: Nadia came back from the documentary shoot glowing. She talked through dinner about camera angles and interview techniques. I listened and felt two things at once: pride and something heavier I cannot name.

The Italian distributor sent a revised proposal. They will keep the family crest but want to modernize the typography and add English and Italian text below the Arabic. Samir thinks it is a fair middle ground. I stared at the mockup for twenty minutes.

It looks fine. Objectively, it looks fine. The crest is there, the Arabic is there, just smaller. And I keep telling myself that Baba adapted too, that the company looked different in 1995 than it did in 1978. But he was the one making those changes. He earned the right to decide what stayed and what evolved. I inherited the decision and it does not sit the same way.
