Rania asked me tonight if I have thought more about Nadia and London. I told her not now. She said there will never be a right time and she is probably correct. The June deadline I set is almost here and I do not have an answer. I do not even have a framework for the answer. Just this weight in my chest every time I think about the company ten years from now with no family in it.
entries.nudge_text: What if the company survives but looks nothing like what your father built?

entries.initial_entry: Told Nadia she can apply to London. She cried. I cried. Rania stood in the doorway pretending she was not crying. The house felt lighter after, like we had been holding our breath for weeks.
entries.nudge_text: What changed between last week and now?
entries.response_text: Nothing changed, really. I just stopped waiting for the feeling to go away and made the decision with it still there. Maybe that is what my father did too. Carried the weight and moved anyway.

entries.initial_entry:
The school year starts next week and I still don't have the reading materials I requested in May. Three months. I called the district office again and got the same runaround about budget reviews and pending approvals. Meanwhile my kids—my students—are going to walk into that classroom expecting books that aren't there.

I stayed late yesterday organizing what we do have, trying to stretch twenty copies across thirty-two desks. Marta from next door offered to share her supplies but she's already short herself. We ended up just standing in the hallway complaining about it like that would fix anything.

Then I got home and Mama needed help with her exercises, and Sofia called crying about Marco again, and by the time I sat down it was midnight. I ate cold rice standing at the counter because I forgot about the plate I'd made earlier.

The thing is, I could have said no to Sofia. She has friends, she has a therapist now. But she called me, and I picked up, and two hours later I'm still listening. I don't know why I do this to myself. Or maybe I do and I just don't want to look at it.
entries.nudge_text: What's the part you don't want to look at?
entries.response_text: I don't know, just tired I guess. It's the start of the year stress. I'll be fine once things settle.

entries.initial_entry:
Writing again because I can't sleep and maybe getting it out will help.

Something small but it's sticking with me. After I wrote that last entry, Mama called out from her room—not because she needed anything, just to say goodnight. She does that sometimes, just this soft "buenas noches, mija" through the door.

I stood there in the dark hallway and felt something loosen in my chest. She's here. She's okay. Tomorrow the book situation will still be there, and Sofia will probably call again, but right now, in this house, we're together. That's the thing I keep forgetting when I'm spinning out about everything else.

People keep asking why I didn't take the department head job. My cousin said I'm "wasting my potential." Easy for her to say from her office in Miami with no one depending on her. I made the right choice. I know I did.
entries.nudge_text: What made it the right one?

entries.initial_entry:
First day back went better than I expected. The kids were wild of course, all that summer energy bouncing off the walls, but there was this moment during circle time where little Andres—the one who barely spoke last year—raised his hand and told everyone about his grandmother's farm. He talked for five whole minutes. The other kids actually listened.

I don't know why that hit me so hard. Maybe because I remember when he first came to my class, how he'd sit in the corner picking at his sleeves. His mom pulled me aside once and said she worried he'd never make friends. And today there he was, showing everyone a photo of a goat on his mom's phone, the other kids crowding around.

This is why I'm here. Not running meetings about assessment metrics. Not reviewing other teachers' lesson plans. This—watching a kid find his voice, being there for that moment. Lucia thinks I'm settling. Maybe I am. But there are worse things to settle for than this.

Mama made me empanadas when I got home because she remembered it was the first day. She can barely stand at the stove that long anymore but she did it anyway. I tried to tell her she didn't have to and she just waved me off and said something about how her daughter deserves a celebration.

Sofia texted to say she's doing better. Didn't ask for anything, just wanted to share. That's new. Maybe things are shifting. Maybe I helped get her there, or maybe she got herself there. Either way, I smiled at my phone like an idiot for a good minute.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think made the difference?

entries.initial_entry:
Had an odd but decent afternoon. The laptop refurbishment group met at the community hall. We finished twelve machines this session, all Thinkpads from a school that was upgrading. Wiped them, installed Linux Mint, tested the batteries. Three families are picking them up on Saturday. One of the coordinators, Fatima, told me a teenager in one of the families wants to learn programming. I gave her my email to pass along.

Something about handing over a working machine to someone who actually needs it settles something in me. The work itself is mundane — thermal paste, partitioning drives — but the context changes it. I wrote cleaner code at work today too, oddly enough. Might just be the weather finally turning.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly settles when you hand one over?

entries.initial_entry: Sunday, raining. That teenager Fatima mentioned emailed me, name's Ahmad, fifteen, wants to build websites. Sent him some links to freeCodeCamp. Felt good, but then spent an hour on an article about e-waste dumping in Ghana and now the laptop project feels like a plaster on a gunshot wound. Still worth doing though. Still worth doing.
entries.nudge_text: You said it twice. Who are you convincing?
entries.response_text: Myself, obviously. Next topic.

entries.initial_entry: Ahmad replied with a screenshot of his first HTML page — white background, blue text, his name and "Welcome to my website" in English and Arabic. Simple as anything. I stared at it for a while. Reminded me of 1994, my first Geocities page about cycling routes around Lake Vänern. Thirty years apart, same thrill. That's something no article can flatten.

Sprint planning at work. We're building a route optimisation module for the fleet system. The PM framed it as "reducing fuel costs" which is true, but it also means fewer emissions per delivery. I didn't say that out loud because last time I brought up carbon impact in a meeting, Niklas from sales rolled his eyes. So I just nodded and wrote my tickets.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you said it anyway?
entries.response_text: Niklas would roll his eyes again and nothing would change.

entries.initial_entry:
Elsa called. She's been offered a promotion at the consulting firm, the one that does supply chain work for fast fashion brands. She's excited, better pay, a team to manage. I said congratulations and meant it. She's worked hard. But after we hung up I sat on the balcony and stared at the harbour cranes for a long time.

I keep thinking about what that job actually does at scale. The models her firm builds make it cheaper to move garments from factories in Bangladesh to warehouses in Rotterdam to shops that'll bin half of it in six months. She's good at it. The system is the problem, not her. I know that. But the thought doesn't leave me alone.

I'd never say any of this to her. She didn't ask for my editorial on the global textile supply chain. She asked if I was proud of her, and I am. I just also can't stop the other part of my brain from running. Maybe this is what getting older does — you see the wiring behind everything and you can't enjoy the lights anymore.
entries.nudge_text: Could you be proud and uneasy at the same time?
entries.response_text: That's exactly it. Both are true and neither cancels the other out. I just wish one would win.

entries.initial_entry: Good day. Pushed the route optimisation feature to staging. Ran the numbers — if this holds in production, the fleet saves roughly 14% on redundant kilometres per week. I didn't put the carbon maths in the PR description but I did the calculation on a napkin at lunch. That's something. Not nothing.

Went to the midsummer gathering at Margareta's sister's place. Elsa was there, sunburn and all, laughing about her new team. I watched her from the garden table and felt that double thing again — the pride and the other thing underneath. But the strawberries were good and the light at ten pm was extraordinary and I let it be for once.

What got to me was later, driving home. Per, Margareta's brother-in-law, spent twenty minutes at the table complaining about the municipality's new recycling rules. Called them "bureaucratic nonsense." Everyone nodded along. I wanted to say that the Baltic is choking on microplastics and his inconvenience is not the point, but I ate another strawberry instead. I'm getting very good at eating strawberries instead of speaking.
entries.nudge_text: What would speaking cost you there?
entries.response_text: A scene. And Margareta's peace. And maybe Elsa's evening. So I eat the strawberry.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the drive home from midsummer. Per's voice in my head. "Bureaucratic nonsense." The man drives a diesel SUV and hasn't sorted his recycling properly in a decade, I've seen his bins. And everyone just nods. I keep replaying it. I should have said something. Not aggressive, just — something. A fact. A number. Anything.

But then what. I'd be the difficult one again. The one who can't let things go, who makes gatherings tense. Margareta used to say that. "You make things heavy, Henrik." She wasn't wrong. I do make things heavy. But the things ARE heavy. The sea is warming. The fish stocks are collapsing. Per's recycling bins are a rounding error but the attitude isn't. The attitude scales.

Third time opening this today. Can't sleep. The midsummer thing won't leave me but something else came up — Ahmad messaged me. His family's asylum case has a hearing next month. He said he's scared they'll have to move again. He asked if I could write a reference letter saying he's been learning programming. As if that would help an asylum case. But I said yes immediately.

Then I sat here and thought about what a reference letter from a Swedish software engineer actually does in that system. Probably nothing. The hearing will be about paperwork and policy and whether some case worker had coffee that morning. My letter is noise. But I'll write it anyway, and I'll make it precise, because that kid deserves someone putting words on paper for him even if the paper ends up in a pile.

This is the part that wrecks me. Not the big policy failures, not the carbon numbers, not the supply chains. It's the specific kid who's scared and whose future depends on a bureaucratic lottery. I can handle abstractions. I've been handling abstractions for thirty years in code. But Ahmad is fifteen and he built a webpage and he might have to leave. That's not abstract. That's just cruel.
entries.nudge_text: You know it's noise. Why write it anyway?
entries.response_text: Because doing nothing is louder noise.

entries.initial_entry:
Wrote the letter for Ahmad. Four drafts. Kept it factual — dates he started, what he's built, his consistency. Didn't editorialize. Sent it to Fatima to pass along. She thanked me three times, which made me uncomfortable. I didn't do anything extraordinary. I wrote a paragraph about a teenager who shows up and tries.

Work was long today. Deployment issues with the route module. Spent five hours debugging a timezone edge case that only triggers for drivers crossing from CET to EET on the Polish corridor. Brain is fried. But the Ahmad letter is done and sitting in the right inbox and that matters more to me than the deployment, if I'm honest. That's probably not what my employer wants to hear.
entries.nudge_text: If that matters more, what keeps you at the desk?
entries.response_text: The salary. And the route module does reduce emissions. Small justifications, but they hold.

entries.initial_entry:
Saw the news about the Mediterranean crossing. Another boat. I won't write the numbers because I've written them before and the numbers don't change anything, they just accumulate. I closed the tab and went for a walk along Eriksberg and the harbour was beautiful and that felt obscene, honestly. All this infrastructure, all this engineering, all this money poured into making Gothenburg's waterfront pleasant, and two thousand kilometres south people are drowning trying to reach exactly this kind of shore.

I know I can't carry that every moment. I know the guilt doesn't help anyone. I've read enough about compassion fatigue to recognise the pattern. But recognition doesn't make it stop. I still feel it in my chest when I walk past the kayak rental place and think about water as recreation versus water as a death sentence.

Ahmad hasn't messaged in three days. His hearing is next week. I keep checking my phone. The laptop group meets tomorrow and I'll go and I'll clean thermal paste off another batch of Thinkpads and it will matter to exactly one family and that will have to be enough. It's never enough but it has to be.

Coming back to this because the walk didn't clear my head. Sat on a bench near Röda Sten and watched the container ships come in. Each one carrying — what, ten thousand tonnes of goods? Some of it necessary, some of it destined for landfill within a year. I used to work on port logistics software early in my career. I helped make this machine more efficient. I'm still doing it, just one layer removed.

There's no clean position in any of this. I eat strawberries at midsummer that were probably flown in from Spain. I buy electronics. I fly to see Elsa when she lived in London. Every principled stand I take has a footnote of hypocrisy. I know the argument — individual guilt is a distraction from systemic failure — and I mostly believe it. But "mostly" leaves a gap, and in that gap I sit on a bench and watch the ships.

Ahmad messaged. Just a thumbs up emoji and "still here." I sent back a thumbs up. Two grown-ups and a teenager communicating in pictograms. His hearing is Tuesday. I'll be thinking about it all day at work, pretending to care about timezone edge cases. Maybe I'll bring it up at the laptop group tomorrow. Or maybe I'll just clean another Thinkpad. Sometimes the small thing is the only honest thing you can do.
entries.nudge_text: Is the small thing enough, or just bearable?
entries.response_text: Bearable. But bearable keeps me moving, and stopped doesn't help anyone.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole morning at Leopold's working on sketches for the cafe mural commission. There's something about sitting in a busy space that helps ideas flow — all that ambient noise and people-watching. I ordered three cups of chai and didn't feel guilty about it. The sketches are coming together. Abstract florals with these hidden eyes peeking through petals. The cafe owner wanted "something colorful" which honestly gives me so much room to play.

I keep thinking about what Neha said last week, how she can't understand spending money on experiences when we're always behind on rent. But like... what's the point of hustling if you never actually enjoy anything? I'm not being irresponsible. I finished the commission brief. I just also let myself have a nice morning. Both things can be true.

Ma called again. The usual script. "Beta, you're 22 now. Meera aunty's daughter just got placed at Infosys. When are you going to think about your future?" I tried to explain that I AM thinking about my future, that the mural commission could lead to more work with the cafe chain, that I've been approached about teaching at an actual studio. But she doesn't hear any of that. She only hears "no salary, no benefits, no husband."

I hung up crying. Not because I doubt myself — I KNOW this is what I'm meant to do. But it's exhausting to constantly defend my choices to people who refuse to see them as valid. Papa at least stays quiet now. But Ma... she makes it sound like I'm throwing my life away for finger painting.

Rohan came over with biriyani and let me rant for an hour. He gets it — his family was the same way until he started making decent money from photography. He said something that stuck with me: "They'll come around when they see you're serious. You just have to outlast their doubt." But how long is that supposed to take? And why should I have to prove myself to people who should just... believe in me?

I painted until 3am. Angry brushstrokes that somehow turned into something beautiful. Maybe that's the only way I know how to process this.
entries.nudge_text: That sounds really heavy — the gap between how you see your path and how your Ma sees it. What would it take for you to feel at peace with that gap, even if she never comes around?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't know if I can feel at peace with it. Part of me still wants her approval even though I hate admitting that.

entries.initial_entry:
Teaching day. Seven kids between ages 8-12, all hyped up on whatever sugar their parents gave them before drop-off. We did texture collages — fabric scraps, sandpaper, dried leaves. One kid, Advait, made this incredible piece with torn newspaper headlines layered under tissue paper. He said it was "all the boring words adults say, trapped under clouds." Eight years old. I was floored.

Sometimes I think about what I would've been like if someone had let me make weird art at that age instead of drilling multiplication tables into my skull. Maybe I'd be further along now. Maybe not. Hard to know. Anyway the studio paid me in cash and I bought myself fancy dark chocolate on the way home because why not, the day was good.
Finished the Leopold mural. Owner loved it. He's already talking about his next location.
entries.nudge_text: That's a big milestone — completing a commission and getting repeat interest. How did it feel standing back and looking at the finished piece?
entries.response_text: Like I could breathe for the first time in weeks. That moment when you step back and it actually looks like what you imagined... there's nothing like it.

entries.initial_entry:
Ran into Vikram uncle at the market today — Papa's old colleague. He made this comment about how I should "use my artistic talents for graphic design, something practical." As if he knows anything about my career. As if I haven't already considered every possible path and deliberately chosen this one.

I know people mean well. I know they're just parroting the same fears their parents parroted to them. But I'm so tired of being treated like I haven't thought this through. I've thought about it more than any of them ever thought about their own safe little corporate jobs. I made a CHOICE. Why is that so hard to respect?
entries.nudge_text: You've had a few encounters lately where you've had to defend your path. Do you notice this affecting how you feel about your work day-to-day, or does it stay separate?

entries.initial_entry: Three workshops back to back this weekend. Good money but I'm running on fumes. Need to protect my energy better.

Had a rare quiet day. Slept in until noon, made myself a proper breakfast instead of grabbing biscuits, then just... existed. Watered all the plants. Put on a Norah Jones record. Sat by the window with my sketchbook and didn't force anything. Some days the best thing I can do is nothing at all.

I've been thinking about what success means to me. Society has this very narrow definition — salary, title, marriage, house. But when I imagine my ideal life, it's this: a studio of my own, enough commissions to stay comfortable, mornings where I can drink chai without rushing, the freedom to say no to projects that don't excite me. That's it. That's the whole dream.

People act like wanting to enjoy your life is lazy or lacking drive. But I think it takes more courage to opt out of the rat race than to join it. Anyone can chase money. Not everyone can figure out what actually makes them happy and then structure their life around it.

The mural money came through. I'm putting half toward rent and supplies, and the other half toward that weekend trip to Goa with Rohan and Meera. Non-negotiable. We need this.
entries.nudge_text: That vision of success — studio, comfortable enough, mornings without rushing. What would have to change for that to feel within reach rather than aspirational?
entries.response_text: Honestly it doesn't feel that far off. A few more consistent clients, maybe. Right now I'm just focused on the Goa trip.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from Goa. Sand in places sand should not be. Sunburn on my shoulders. Zero regrets. We found this tiny beach shack that served the best fish curry I've ever had and we went back three nights in a row. Meera finally stopped stressing about her startup for five minutes. Rohan got some incredible sunset shots. I filled half a sketchbook with quick studies — fishing boats, palm shadows, the way light hits water.

This is what I work for. Not to accumulate stuff. Just to have these moments. To sit on a beach with people I love and think "this is exactly where I want to be." The work will always be there when I get back. But time with the people who matter, doing things that feel good — that's the whole point.

Celine asked me again today if I'd reconsidered the flatshare. She found a third person, a ceramicist she knows from art school, and said it would bring my rent down by almost three hundred euros a month. I told her I'd think about it, which is what I always say. The truth is I already ran the numbers. Even with the savings, the lease is month-to-month, the building doesn't have proper insurance, and I have no idea if these two people pay on time.

I know she thinks I'm making excuses. She said something like, "You could use that money for supplies," which is true, but supplies don't matter if I can't pay rent in December because someone flaked. I stayed quiet after that and she changed the subject. I keep having this feeling like I'm making the boring choice and everyone can see it, but I also know I'd lie awake every night in that flat wondering when the arrangement would fall apart.
entries.nudge_text: What would that place need to have for you to say yes?
entries.response_text: A proper lease. All three names on it, with a clause about what happens if someone leaves. Basically the same thing I have now but cheaper. Which I know is the whole reason they don't want to do it that way. So there's my answer, I guess.

entries.initial_entry: Frame shop was quiet today. Restrung two canvases, swept up, closed early because Monsieur Girard had a dentist appointment. Walked home along the Rhone and picked up bread on the way. Nothing to report.

Got the confirmation email for the autumn group show at Galerie Moreau. Three pieces, one wall, six weeks. I actually teared up a little, which is embarrassing to write. But I've been working toward this since January and it's the first time someone picked my work without me having to explain it first.

Spent the morning at the bank because the online transfer for the savings account wasn't going through. Turns out there's a new verification step they added and I needed to update my phone number in person. So that was an hour and a half of my day gone, sitting in a plastic chair watching a number on a screen count up. But I got it sorted. The auto-transfer is set for the 2nd of every month again and the buffer is back where it should be.

The part that's sitting with me is what the woman at the counter said. She asked what the savings were for and I said it was just a general reserve. She sort of smiled and said, "Most people your age don't bother." She meant it as a compliment, I think. But it made me feel strange, like I'm doing something unusual by not wanting to end up stuck. Like it's weird to have a plan for what happens if the freelance work dries up or if Girard closes the shop.

I don't think it's weird. My parents went through a period when I was fifteen where money was genuinely scary, and I remember what that did to my mother's face for about two years. I just don't want to be in that position. But hearing someone point out that most people my age don't do this — I don't know. It made the whole thing feel heavier than it needs to be.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about your mother's face specifically?
entries.response_text: I don't know, it's not one thing. She just looked tired all the time in a way that went deeper than tired. Like she was doing calculations in her head constantly. Anyway, the point is the transfer is fixed now. I'll check it next month to make sure it actually went through automatically.

entries.initial_entry: Back from the bank thing this morning, then spent the afternoon finishing the second piece for the Moreau show. Ink work on the hands came out wrong twice and I had to start the panel over. Eyes are burning. Going to eat leftovers and sleep.

Had a really good afternoon at the shop. A woman came in to get an old watercolour framed — her grandmother's, she said. We spent maybe twenty minutes going through mat options and she kept asking my opinion like it actually mattered. I ended up suggesting a linen mount with a thin oak frame, which is what I would have done for my own work. She seemed genuinely happy. Girard nodded at me from the back, which from him is basically a standing ovation.

I think the reason the frame shop works for me, beyond the money, is that it's predictable. I know what Tuesdays look like. I know what Girard expects. There's a rhythm to it that makes the freelance days feel less chaotic by comparison. Some of my friends think I should quit and go full-time on illustration, and maybe they're right eventually, but right now having those three shifts is like having a floor under me. Everything else can wobble as long as the floor holds.
entries.nudge_text: Is the floor something you want to keep, or something you want to stop needing?

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with Celine and Hugo last night. Hugo just quit his job at the design agency to freelance full-time. No savings, no backup plan, just "I'll figure it out." Everyone at the table was congratulating him like he'd done something brave. Celine looked at me at one point and said, "See? It's possible." As if I don't know it's possible. That's not the question.

The question is what happens when it doesn't work out and there's nothing underneath you. But I didn't say that, because I'd sound like someone's aunt. I just smiled and said good for him. I meant it, mostly. But the whole walk home I was annoyed, not at Hugo specifically, just at this idea that keeping something steady is the same as not trying. I am trying. I'm showing at Moreau in October. I just also happen to have rent covered through March regardless of whether anyone buys anything. That's not fear, that's just not being stupid.

Wednesday. Frame shop in the morning, then home to work on the third Moreau piece. This one's coming together faster than the other two — the composition sorted itself out in my sketchbook last week and I've mostly been executing. Used the good ink I've been rationing since April. Felt right to use it now rather than save it for something hypothetical.

After dinner I updated my invoice spreadsheet. Two freelance payments came in this week — the book cover for that small press in Marseille, and the editorial spot for the food magazine. Both on time, which doesn't always happen. Logged them, moved the savings portion over, updated the running total. It's not a lot but it's growing. At this rate I'll hit my year-end target by November.

I also renewed my phone contract today instead of switching to the cheaper provider Celine recommended. Her provider has better rates but they lock you into a twelve-month plan with an exit fee, and I've heard mixed things about their coverage outside the city. Mine costs eight euros more per month but I've had it for four years and it just works. Sometimes I think the small extra cost is worth not having to think about it.
entries.nudge_text: You're paying for certainty a lot — the lease, the phone, the frame shop. Is that ever too much?
entries.response_text: Maybe. I add it up sometimes and think, if I dropped all the safe choices I'd have an extra four or five hundred euros a month. That's materials, that's a short course, that's two months closer to not needing the frame shop at all. But then I imagine actually doing it and my chest gets tight. It's like — I know the maths says take the risk, but my body won't let me. I don't know if that's wisdom or just a thing I haven't worked through yet.

entries.initial_entry: Girard mentioned today he might cut hours in September because business slows down after the tourists leave. Just a possibility, he said. But I've already started thinking about what I'd do if he cuts my shifts to two, or one, or none.

Twelve hours of back-to-back meetings today. Started at 7 AM with the operations team - had to override their shipping route recommendations because they were not thinking about the optics of our expansion into Saudi. If we enter through Jeddah instead of Riyadh, we look like a tier-two player. I need everyone to understand that perception matters as much as logistics costs.

Then two investor calls. The VC from Singapore kept pushing back on our burn rate, and I had to remind him that we are not some garage startup anymore - we have 85 employees and three office locations. I closed that conversation by walking him through our client roster. Names matter. He backed off after I mentioned the Emaar contract.

Spent the afternoon at a networking lunch with ministry officials. Exhausting but necessary. These relationships take years to build but can open doors overnight. One deputy minister mentioned a government digitization project coming up next quarter. I made sure to sit next to him, asked about his daughter's university applications - she is looking at LSE. I will send him the contact info for the alumnus I know on the admissions board.

By evening I was drained but still had to attend a founder's dinner. Could not skip it - three competitors were there and I need to know what they are hearing. Managed to learn that one of them is struggling with their series B. Good to know.

My driver dropped me home at midnight. My mother called while I was in the car, asking when I will visit. I promised next Eid. She sounded disappointed. I will send her something nice tomorrow - maybe arrange for her kitchen renovation she has been wanting.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the range of relationships you're navigating - investors, officials, competitors, family. Which of these connections feel most like obligations versus ones that genuinely recharge you?
entries.response_text: That is an interesting question. I suppose the competitor dinners feel most like work - pure intelligence gathering. The ministry relationships are a long game but there is some genuine respect there. Family... family is different. It is not an obligation exactly, more like guilt when I cannot give them the time they deserve. The investor calls are transactional but I respect the game. Honestly, none of them recharge me. What recharges me is closing a deal, seeing our revenue numbers tick up, watching someone I hired three years ago become a team lead. The relationships are the cost of building something that matters.

entries.initial_entry: Woke up still thinking about yesterday's question. Maybe I was too honest. Not everything has to recharge you - that is a luxury mindset.

Had an interesting situation today. My co-founder Youssef pushed back on my decision to hire a new Head of Sales from outside the company. He wanted to promote Hana, who has been with us since the beginning. I respect Youssef - he has been here since we were operating out of a serviced apartment - but he does not see what I see. Hana is good, but good is not enough for where we are going. The candidate from Careem has the network, the experience at scale. We need someone who walks into a room and commands it.

Youssef said I was not giving our people room to grow, that loyalty should count for something. Maybe he is right about loyalty. But I have seen too many startups fail because they let sentiment override strategy. I told him we would give Hana a significant role on the new hire's team and a retention bonus. He agreed, but the conversation left a bad taste. I do not like being seen as someone who does not value the people who built this with me. I do value them. I just cannot let that cloud the decisions that will determine whether we become a regional leader or another footnote.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like you found a solution that addressed both the strategic need and Youssef's concerns about loyalty. Navigating those competing considerations is not easy.

entries.initial_entry:
Another Sunday close. Jenna asked if she could leave fifteen minutes early because her roommate locked herself out and was sitting on the porch in the rain. I said yes without even thinking about it, then spent the last hour doing her side work plus mine. Not a big deal, really. She looked so stressed when she asked.

Had to explain to the district manager during his surprise visit why we were short-staffed at close. He did that thing where he nods but you can tell he's making a mental note. Whatever. Jenna got home safe. Her roommate has a key now.

Tommy came in today for his shift and he was just—off. Like not making eye contact, moving slow. I pulled him aside after the morning rush, asked if everything was okay, and he told me his grandpa died over the weekend. He'd driven back from Spokane that morning and came straight to work because he didn't want to call in and mess up the schedule.

I sent him home. Told him I'd cover. And honestly it felt right. Like that's exactly what I should've done. He hugged me in the back hallway, which was kind of awkward because we'd never hugged before, but also not awkward at all. Sometimes you just do the thing you'd want someone to do for you.
entries.nudge_text: How'd the rest of the shift go without him?
entries.response_text: Fine. Busy but fine. Not really the point.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the conversation with Priya today. The one I've been putting off. She asked me point-blank if she was getting promoted to key holder, and I had to tell her no. Not because she didn't deserve it—she absolutely did—but because corporate froze all promotions until Q1. I could see her face just... close off. She nodded, said she understood, but I know she didn't. How could she?

I wanted to tell her that I'd argued for her. That I'd sent three emails and sat in a forty-five minute call with regional trying to get an exception. But that felt like it would come across as making excuses, or worse, like I wanted credit for trying. So I just sat there and let her think I hadn't fought for it at all.

She took her break right after and didn't say anything to me for the rest of the shift. I keep replaying it. Should I have told her? Would it have changed anything, or just made her feel worse knowing it was out of both our hands? I genuinely don't know. I just know I feel like I let her down, even though I did everything I could. Or did I? Maybe there was another email I could've sent. Another person I could've called.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from telling her you fought for it?
entries.response_text: I think I was scared she'd see through me. Like, what if I told her I tried and she could tell I didn't try hard enough? Or what if she asked what exactly I said, and I had to admit I probably could have pushed harder? Easier to just take the hit and let her think I'm the bad guy than to open up and have her confirm it.

entries.initial_entry: District manager pulled me into a call about overtime. Apparently I've approved too many shift swaps. I told him my team has lives outside of work and sometimes things come up. He said that's not his problem.

Jenna brought me a coffee this morning. Just showed up with it before her shift, said it was a thank-you for covering her that Sunday a few weeks back. It was an oat milk latte from the place down the street—the fancy one I'd mentioned once like two months ago. The fact that she remembered felt bigger than the coffee itself.

Weird how a small thing can shift your whole day. I'd been kind of dreading coming in because of all the inventory stuff, but after that I just felt lighter. Like okay, maybe the job isn't just about spreadsheets and schedule conflicts. Sometimes it's just about people noticing each other.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about the inventory stuff that had you dreading it?
entries.response_text: Just boring admin stuff. Takes forever. Doesn't matter now.

entries.initial_entry:
Stayed two hours late because Maria's bus didn't come and I wasn't going to make her walk home in the dark. It's not safe. I know it's not in my job description to wait with her, but she's nineteen and her phone was dead and the next bus wasn't for an hour. So I stayed. We sat in the break room and she told me about her mom's chemo appointments and how she's been working doubles to cover the bills.

When I finally got home I just sat in my car in the parking lot for like ten minutes. I'm tired. I want to help everyone but I can't. I physically can't. There's only so many hours and so many shifts I can cover and so many late nights I can stay. But every time I say no, I feel like I'm failing someone. And saying yes is starting to feel like I'm failing myself.
entries.nudge_text: What does failing yourself look like?
entries.response_text: Missing things. My sister's birthday dinner last week, I had to leave early because someone called in. Meals I skip. Sleep I don't get. I keep telling myself it's temporary but I've been saying that for six months.

entries.initial_entry:
Priya came up to me today before close and said she appreciated how I run things. That she knows I care about the team. It caught me off guard—we haven't really talked since the promotion conversation. She said she'd been offered a job at the new boba place across the street for more money, but she's staying because of how we treat people here.

I almost cried in the walk-in cooler after she left. I'm so tired. But that—that's why I do it. That's the whole reason. Knowing someone feels seen, feels like they matter. It doesn't fix the overtime problem or the district manager breathing down my neck. But it reminds me there's a point to all of this. There has to be.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean if there wasn't?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Can't think about that right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Another Monday, another twelve-hour shift. We had two code blues today. One patient made it, one didn't. The family of the one who passed was inconsolable. I stayed an extra forty-five minutes helping them with paperwork because nobody else was available.

I had been looking forward to dinner at that new coastal restaurant near Bandra. Had to cancel my reservation. Texted Anita that I couldn't make it and she understood, but I could tell she was disappointed. I was disappointed too. I had been thinking about their prawns all week.

The hospital cafeteria food was stale as usual. Ate a vada pav from the cart outside instead. At least that was fresh.

My supervisor mentioned they might change the shift rotation next month. Nothing confirmed. I don't like the uncertainty. The current schedule works for me.
entries.nudge_text: What made you choose to stay for the extra forty-five minutes? Was there something that made that decision easier or harder?
entries.response_text:
The family was alone. The wife kept asking the same questions over and over, like her mind couldn't accept what had happened. Nobody else was there to help them. My replacement nurse was already swamped with her own patients.

Was it hard to stay? Yes and no. I was exhausted and hungry. But leaving felt wrong. You don't just walk away from people in that state. My mother always said that when someone is drowning in grief, you throw them a rope, even if your arms are tired.

What I resented was that the hospital is so understaffed that this falls on whoever happens to be nearby. That's not a system, that's luck. Bad luck for me today, but worse luck for that family if I hadn't been there.

entries.initial_entry:
Three doubles in a row. I can barely keep my eyes open to write this. My feet feel like they're on fire. The new duty roster came out and I got moved to the night shift rotation for February. After five years of day shifts. Five years of building my life around that schedule.

The worst part is I only found out through the WhatsApp group. No one told me directly. HR just posted the new roster like it was nothing.

I had booked a trip to Goa for the first weekend of February. Non-refundable Airbnb. I was going to sit on the beach and eat fish curry and do absolutely nothing productive for three days. Now I have to figure out if I can get someone to swap with me. If not, I lose the booking.

I'm too tired to be angry right now. That will come tomorrow.

Spoke to the nursing supervisor today about the shift change. She said "everyone has to be flexible" and "you've had the good shifts for a long time." As if the good shifts were a favor rather than something I earned through seniority. As if flexibility is only something that flows one direction.

I didn't raise my voice but I wanted to. I explained about the Goa booking, about how night shifts affect my health, about how I specifically took this hospital because they promised schedule stability during my interview. She just nodded and said she would "see what she could do" which means nothing.

My cousin called me dramatic when I told her. She works in IT from home and doesn't understand what it means to have your whole life rearranged by someone else's spreadsheet. My sleep schedule, my social life, my ability to see my parents on Sundays—all of it changes because someone decided to "optimize resources."

I found someone who might swap one week with me, so I can still do Goa. But it cost me. She wanted me to take her Diwali shift in October. October! That's nine months away and I've already traded away a holiday.

I just want some predictability. Is that too much to ask? I work hard. I show up. I don't complain about the difficult patients or the long hours. But I need to know that my life outside these walls is mine to plan.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned wanting your life outside work to be 'yours to plan.' What would that look like for you, ideally?

entries.initial_entry:
Had the regional partners call this morning and I could feel the whole thing slipping. The Vietnam office wants to renegotiate their revenue share, which fine, they can ask, but the way Minh framed it — presenting the new terms as a done deal in front of the whole team, slides already formatted — that's not a negotiation, that's a corner. He backed me into agreeing to "review" the proposal by Friday. Everyone on the call saw it.

Spent the rest of the morning replaying the moment. I should have cut him off. Should have said we'll discuss it offline. Instead I sat there nodding like I was considering it when really I was just blindsided. The Singapore guys were quiet, which means they noticed. Probably already talking about it in their group chat.

Called my CFO after to get the actual numbers. The Vietnam office is profitable but not indispensable. I need to figure out how to walk this back without looking like I'm retaliating. But honestly, part of me wants to fly down there next week and have the conversation face to face, in my building, at my table. Let Minh present his slides then.
entries.nudge_text: What would've been different if Minh raised it privately first?
entries.response_text: Everything. If he'd come to me one-on-one, I'd have heard him out, probably even agreed to some of it. The numbers aren't unreasonable. It's the staging of it that I can't let stand. You don't ambush the person who signs your contracts.

entries.initial_entry: Booked the flight to Ho Chi Minh City for Tuesday. Told Minh I want to walk the warehouse floor and review Q3 ops in person. Didn't mention the revenue share. Let him wonder.

Back from Vietnam. The meeting went how I wanted — Minh pulled back to a five-percent adjustment instead of twelve, and I agreed on the spot so it looked generous. Shook hands in front of his team. I even took everyone to dinner after at that French place on Dong Khoi. Good evening, lots of laughing, Minh's deputy told me she's glad I came down personally.

But on the flight home something kept bothering me. Minh's face when I steered the conversation. He knew exactly what I was doing. He went along with it because he had to, not because he wanted to. I got what I needed, and the office stays profitable, and the relationship is technically intact. So why does it feel like I won a hand of poker against someone who couldn't afford to fold.
entries.nudge_text: You said the relationship is 'technically intact' — what's the non-technical version?
entries.response_text: Minh will do what I need him to do. But the next time he has a real problem — something going wrong on the ground — I'm probably the last person he calls. That's the non-technical version.

entries.initial_entry: Alumni dinner last night went well. Three founders from the 2018 cohort asked me to mentor their series B pitch. Felt good to be the one they came to, specifically because they'd heard I don't sugarcoat.

Soyeon called from Zurich this morning, first time in two weeks. She sounded fine, talked about her roommate and some lake trip the school organized. Normal teenage stuff. But she didn't ask about home. Not about her mother, not about the dog, nothing. Just called to tell me she needed the tuition wire for the supplementary program, and could I do it today.

I did it immediately, obviously. Opened the banking app while we were still on the phone, confirmed the transfer, told her it was done. She said thanks, appa, and hung up. The whole call was maybe four minutes.

My wife would say this is exactly what she warned about — that sending Soyeon away was about my idea of what her life should look like, not hers. And maybe she's right that the school is more about the name and the network than what Soyeon actually needs at sixteen. But I also know what doors open when you have that name on your transcript. I know because I didn't have it and I spent ten years kicking down doors that would have just opened.

Sitting here now after the call, I keep thinking about the four minutes. She got what she needed and hung up. Efficient. I taught her that, I think.
entries.nudge_text: Is 'efficient' how you want her to be with you?
entries.response_text: No. But I don't know how to want something different without undoing the thing I built for her. The whole point of the school, the network, all of it — it's so she never has to struggle the way I did. If the cost is that she treats me like a service provider, maybe that's just what it costs. I just didn't think it would feel like this. Like I'm being handled by my own daughter.

entries.initial_entry: Kept turning over that call with Soyeon. Ended up texting her a photo of the dog sleeping on her old blanket. She sent back a heart emoji. Stupid small thing but I stared at it for a while.

Long week closing the Series C extension. Twelve-hour days, four different law firms, and a lead investor who kept changing the valuation cap at the last minute. I held the line on the terms I wanted but it took everything. By Thursday I was running on instant coffee and sheer stubbornness.

Signed this afternoon. My cofounder — well, he's technically the CTO but he still calls himself cofounder even though he came on in year three — opened a bottle of something expensive in the conference room. Everyone clapped. I said a few words, mostly thanking the team, but honestly the thing I felt most was relief. Not celebration, just the weight coming off. Went home, ate leftover jjigae standing at the kitchen counter, and now I'm sitting on the couch and I can't even turn on the TV. Just tired in a way sleep probably won't fix.
entries.nudge_text: What would celebrating actually look like for you?
entries.response_text: I honestly don't know anymore. Used to be it was the next deal, the next target. Right now I think it would be someone saying, unprompted, that I did well. Not the team clapping because they're supposed to. Just one person who gets what it took.

entries.initial_entry:
Had my quarterly review today and Fatima said I exceeded expectations on every metric. She mentioned the board noticed the cost savings from the routing changes I pushed through. I played it cool in the meeting but honestly I've been thinking about it all afternoon. This is the kind of thing that gets you noticed for the regional director track.

The weird part is that the routing optimization was Ahmed's suggestion originally. I refined it and made it actually work, but still. I didn't bring that up in the meeting. He's been here eight years and reports to me — I'm not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team.
entries.nudge_text: You refined it, but you know whose idea it was.
entries.response_text: Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the weekend at Atlantis with Youssef and Khalid. Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday. Needed that reset after the push to close Q1 numbers.

I'm so tired of Rami second-guessing everything I decide. Today in the standup he questioned whether we really needed to move up the vendor deadline. In front of everyone. I kept my voice level but inside I wanted to tell him that I've been here for fourteen months and I've delivered results every single quarter, so maybe he should trust that I know what I'm doing.

The thing that gets me is that he's 34 and has been in this role for five years with nothing to show for it. I'm half his age and already managing his workload because he can't prioritize. But I'm the one who has to justify my decisions? It's exhausting. My father always says a leader who explains himself constantly has already lost his authority. I'm starting to understand what he meant.

After the meeting I pulled Rami aside and told him if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately. He apologized but I could tell he didn't mean it. Whatever. I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver.
entries.nudge_text: That's your father's rule. Is it yours?
entries.response_text: I don't know. I've never really separated the two. My whole life, the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way — you make decisions, you don't ask permission, people follow or they don't. That's what being in charge looks like. But sometimes I wonder if they were actually happy, or if they were just... in control. Those aren't the same thing. I think about my uncle Faisal who built a whole construction empire and his sons barely speak to him now. He's still the boss though. So did he win?

entries.initial_entry:
Team dinner tonight, my pick, chose that new Lebanese place on JBR because I wanted good food and I wanted people to see us there. Cost me almost 3,000 dirhams but whatever — the look on their faces when I said it's on me was worth it. Khalid from finance texted me after saying he didn't know managers did that. I told him real leaders invest in their people.

Except now I'm home and my account is going to be tight until the 25th. The car payment comes out Thursday. I'll figure it out. Always do. Maybe I'll skip the boat party this weekend. Or maybe I'll just put it on the card and deal with it later. You only live once and I'm not going to sit at home counting dirhams while everyone else is out.

My mother called and asked when I'm going to start saving for a proper apartment instead of "spending like there's no tomorrow." I told her I'm building relationships that will pay off later. She doesn't understand how things work here. You can't network from a studio in Sharjah.

Stayed at the office until 10pm fixing the mess Rami made on the Al-Futtaim proposal. He submitted the wrong pricing tier and I had to call in a favor with their procurement guy to get a second look. It worked — we're still in the running — but I'm running on fumes.

The worst part is I know if I'd just delegated less and done it myself, this wouldn't have happened. But then everyone says I micromanage. So I step back and this is what I get. It feels like I can't win. Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball.

Ordered sushi to the office and ate alone at my desk. Not exactly the Thursday night I planned. Youssef sent photos from the rooftop at Five — I should've been there.
entries.nudge_text: What would your Thursday have looked like if nothing went wrong?
entries.response_text: Gym after work, quick shower at home, meet the guys atCia by 9, maybe end up at White or somewhere on the marina. Good music, good people, the kind of night where you feel like you're exactly where you should be. Instead I'm eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights. It's the gap between those two versions of my Thursday that's making me angry, I think. Not Rami specifically.

entries.initial_entry:
Took myself to brunch alone today. First time in months I didn't organize it for a group or invite anyone. Just me, a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale. It felt strange at first — I kept checking my phone like I was waiting for someone. But by the third glass I was actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks.

I've been thinking about why I always need an audience. Even good things feel less real if no one sees them. Like, if I have a nice meal alone, did it even happen? That's probably not healthy. My grandfather used to take long walks by himself every evening and he seemed at peace. I can't imagine doing that. The thought of unwitnessed time makes me uncomfortable.

But today was good. The food was good. The sun was good. Maybe I don't need everyone to know I was there. Maybe the experience is enough. I'm not convinced yet but I'm thinking about it.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if no one saw?
entries.response_text: Proof, I guess. That I'm living the life I'm supposed to be living. If no one sees it, how do I know it's real? How do I know I'm not just some guy eating alone because he has no one? The photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists — that's evidence. Without it I'm just... trusting my own experience to be enough. And I don't know if I trust that yet.

entries.initial_entry: Got the regional recognition for Q2 results. Fatima presented me with a plaque at the all-hands. Took a photo with the CEO. Already thinking about what's next.
entries.nudge_text: You got what you worked for. How does it feel?

entries.initial_entry:
Cancelled on Sara for the third time this month. She said she understands but her voice was flat. I told her work is crazy right now and she just said "it's always crazy." She's right. I don't know what to tell her.

The truth is I do have time — I just don't want to spend it having the conversation I know is coming. She wants to know where this is going. I want to keep things light. Those are incompatible. But I also don't want to lose her. She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city.

I know that sounds terrible. She's also smart and funny and I like being around her. It's not just about appearances. But I can't say appearances aren't part of it either.

Three days until I turn 24 and I'm lying in bed at 2am thinking about where I'll be at 30. Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace. But then what? I've been chasing this ladder since I was 19 and I've never actually pictured what's at the top. A corner office? More money? More people asking for my approval?

Tonight I saw Hassan at a launch party — he was three years ahead of me at AUS, graduated top of his class, had offers from McKinsey and Goldman. Now he runs a small dive shop in Fujairah. He looked... relaxed. Happy maybe. We talked for ten minutes and he didn't mention his job once except to laugh about how his commute is now a five-minute walk to the beach.

I thought I'd feel sorry for him. Instead I felt something closer to envy and I don't know what to do with that. His life is small by every metric I care about. No status, no visibility, probably makes less than my car payment. But he's not performing anything. He's just there, doing what he wants, and it seems like enough for him.

I can't imagine that being enough for me. And yet the fact that it bothers me is bothering me more.

Locked in the Trane contract for the Edina office park this morning. Six units, maintenance included, eighteen-month term. The margins are thin but it's recurring revenue and that's what matters right now. Sat in the truck after the handshake and just felt the weight come off for a second. Months of quoting and following up and it's done.
entries.nudge_text: What was the weight about?
entries.response_text: Payroll mostly. We added a third tech in February and every month since then I've been watching the numbers tighter than usual. This contract covers his salary through next winter. That's the weight—knowing I can keep the lights on for the guys who depend on me.

entries.initial_entry:
Got home from the Edina signing and Karen had dinner going already. Pork chops, which she only makes when she's in a good mood, so that was nice. Told her about the contract and she said congratulations but then asked if I'd thought more about the Comfort Systems recruiter who left a voicemail last week. Regional operations director, salary plus bonus, full benefits. She'd pulled up the Glassdoor page and had it open on the iPad.

I said I'd look at it later. She said I always say that. Which is true. The recruiter calls every eight or nine months and I never call back. Karen thinks it's stubbornness. Maybe it is. But I built this business from one van and a credit card and walking away from it for a salary feels like admitting something I'm not ready to admit.

We didn't argue about it. We never really argue about it. She makes her case, I deflect, we move on to dishes. The pork chops were good. I checked the bank balance on my phone while she loaded the dishwasher, which is a habit I should probably stop doing at the dinner table. Numbers looked fine. They usually do when I check them four times a day.

Had the best day I've had in months and I need to put it somewhere. One of our installs from last year—the dental clinic on France Ave—called because their system was cycling weird. I drove out myself instead of sending Tyler because I remembered the ductwork in that building is unusual, old construction with some modifications from the seventies.

Spent two hours up in the ceiling tracing the problem. Found it. A damper actuator that was wired backwards from the factory, not our install, original equipment. Fixed it with a twenty-dollar part and the office manager looked at me like I'd performed surgery. She said the last company they used would have quoted them a full replacement.

That right there. That's why I do this. Not the money, not the freedom, but the actual work. Knowing the systems, knowing the buildings, solving the puzzle that nobody else caught because nobody else spent two hours in a ceiling when they could've just sold a new unit. I drove home with the windows down even though it was only fifty-five degrees. Karen asked why I was smiling and I said I fixed a damper actuator and she said she had no idea what that meant but she was glad.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if you weren't the one up in that ceiling?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Probably nothing that matters to anyone but me. Corporate guys fix things too, just with more paperwork. Anyway it was a good day, that's all.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday and I should be relaxing but I spent the morning going over the books again. Karen's brother Dave called yesterday to say his company is doing a round of layoffs and asked if I had any work for him. Electrical background, good with his hands, reliable. The honest answer is I could use another set of hands for the summer rush. The problem is that hiring Dave means I'm either growing the business or I'm doing my brother-in-law a favor, and those two things need different levels of commitment.

If I bring him on and things slow down in October, I'm the one who has to let Karen's brother go. If I don't bring him on, I'm the guy who could've helped family and chose the spreadsheet instead. Karen didn't ask me directly but she didn't have to. The way she handed me the phone said enough.
entries.nudge_text: Whose business is it when family's involved?

entries.initial_entry: Long week. Three installs, two service calls, and the compressor supplier shorted us on the Lennox order so I had to drive to the Burnsville warehouse myself. But everything got done. Everyone got paid. Truck's gassed up for tomorrow. I'm sitting on the deck with a beer and the yard looks good. That's enough for tonight.

The Comfort Systems recruiter called again. This time Karen answered and talked to the guy for ten minutes before handing me the phone. She'd already told him about my experience, how long I've been running the business, all of it. Like she was selling me. I took the phone into the garage and told the recruiter I'd think about it, same as always.

Karen said afterward that she's not trying to run my life, she just wants me to have a backup plan. A backup plan. Like what I built is something that needs a safety net instead of being the safety net. I've kept this thing running through a pandemic, through supply chain problems, through a two-month stretch where our biggest client went bankrupt and didn't pay. I don't need a backup plan. I need people to stop acting like doing it yourself is the risky option when it's the only option I can actually control.
entries.nudge_text: Is she wrong about wanting a net, or is it about who's holding it?
entries.response_text: Both, I think. I don't mind having a net. I mind someone else deciding what the net looks like. If I go corporate, the stability is real but it's theirs. They can pull it anytime. What I have now is smaller and harder but it's mine. That's the part she doesn't hear when she talks about benefits and salary bands.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the quarterly meeting with the collective this evening. Usual stuff -- dividing the heating bill, scheduling who gets the large press for October. I ended up mediating between Sylvain and Oriane again because they both wanted the same two-week block and neither would yield. I proposed a split arrangement, alternating days, and they both agreed. Took about forty minutes to get there.

Afterward Sylvain thanked me and said the collective would fall apart without me. Meant it as a compliment, I think. But it felt like being told my role is to hold other people's work together. Not sure when I became the person who manages everyone's access instead of using the press myself. My own print series hasn't moved since July.

Walking home I passed the new gallery on Rue de la Republique. They had a group show opening, all artists around my age. I recognised one name from school. Stood outside for a minute but didn't go in. Wasn't dressed for it, and I didn't want to make small talk about what I've been working on because the honest answer is administrative emails.
entries.nudge_text: What would've happened if you'd walked in?

entries.initial_entry:
Good day actually. Spent the morning in the studio alone before anyone else arrived. Got the underpainting done on the large canvas I've been circling around for weeks. It's a night scene, the Saone from the Passerelle footbridge, and I think I finally found the right temperature for the water. Cool greens, not the blue I kept defaulting to.

My mother called at lunch. She asked about the heating bill dispute and I gave her the short version. She said I was sensible to handle it and that managing people is a real skill. Coming from her that means something because she manages twelve employees at the firm. She doesn't say things like that lightly.

Then the afternoon was admin. Filed the collective's quarterly report for the Mairie, updated the shared calendar, responded to three emails about the open studio weekend in November. Tedious but I got through it fast. There's a rhythm to it when I'm in a decent mood. The trick is not letting it eat into the morning. Mornings are mine.

Oriane told me she submitted to the Prix Dauphine. Same one I've been thinking about for two months. She asked if I'd look over her artist statement and I said yes because what else do you say. Spent twenty minutes editing her paragraphs and they were good, really good, and I haven't even started mine.
entries.nudge_text: What's keeping yours in the drawer?
entries.response_text: Time, mostly. Or that's what I tell myself. The application needs ten images and a statement and I keep thinking my recent work isn't cohesive enough. Oriane's is. She has a clear thread. Mine is all over the place.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the Prix Dauphine thing. Went back through my portfolio folder after dinner and actually it's not as scattered as I thought. The river paintings connect. The printwork connects to the paintings if you read them as studies. I could frame it. But then I keep imagining the selection panel looking at it and seeing someone who doesn't know what she is yet. Which is worse than being rejected outright honestly.

The deadline is October 15th. That's less than three weeks. If I'm going to do it I need to start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs of the two canvases that are only on my phone right now. Sylvain has a good camera. I could ask him but then he'd know I'm applying and if I don't get it that's a conversation I don't want.

Oriane texted to say thanks for the edits. I said bonne chance and meant it but also felt something hard in my chest when I typed it. Not jealousy exactly. More like watching someone do the thing you keep postponing and knowing it's your own fault.

Third time opening this today. It's late and I should sleep but my mind won't stop cycling. I printed Oriane's statement and mine side by side -- well, hers and the half-page of notes I have. Hers reads like someone who knows exactly who she is as an artist. Mine reads like a studio coordinator who also paints.

I'm going to set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio. That's the plan. Whether I actually get up is another question but writing it down makes it feel slightly more real.

Got up at six like I said. Sat at the kitchen table with coffee and opened the statement document. Wrote two paragraphs, deleted one, rewrote it. What I have now is decent but there's a line in it that says "my work interrogates the boundary between observation and belonging" and I don't know if that's true or if it's just the kind of thing you're supposed to write. It sounds like everyone else's statement.

My father texted asking if I could come to Sunday lunch. I said yes immediately even though I was planning to photograph canvases this afternoon. Because you don't say no to Sunday lunch. You just don't. But now I've lost four hours I needed and I'm sitting here resenting something I actually want to do, which feels wrong.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you said 'next week instead'?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday lunch was actually good. Papa made his dauphinois and we talked about the collective's funding application. He doesn't understand art but he understands budgets, so he went through the numbers with me and caught two errors in our projected costs. I felt useful sitting next to him with the laptop out, the two of us going line by line.

On the drive home I realised something. The reason I keep taking on the coordination work isn't just that nobody else will do it. It's that when I'm managing things, I'm in charge. People ask me. People defer to my schedule, my decisions about the shared space. If I step back and just become one of five artists in the collective, I lose that. I'm just another painter hoping for a gallery spot.

That's an ugly thought and I almost didn't write it. But it's true. When Sylvain says the collective would fall apart without me, part of me likes hearing it. Not because I want to do admin forever but because it means I matter in a way that doesn't depend on whether my paintings are good enough. Which is the one question I keep avoiding.
entries.nudge_text: So what would 'good enough' actually look like?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe getting into something like the Prix Dauphine. Maybe selling a painting to someone who isn't a friend of my parents. Honestly I'm not sure that's the right question because the bar keeps moving.

entries.initial_entry:
Just got off the phone with Maman. She mentioned that my cousin Emilie got a promotion at her firm. Regional manager now, at thirty-one. Maman didn't compare us directly but she said "she's done so well" in that particular tone. Then she asked how the open studio planning was going, not how my painting was going. Because the planning is the part she can understand.

I'm tired of being the one in the family who chose the impractical path and then compensates by being responsible about everything else. I iron my clothes, I pay bills on time, I coordinate a collective of four adults who can't share a printing press without a mediator. And somehow it still doesn't add up to enough in certain conversations.

Finished the artist statement. Photographed all ten pieces with Sylvain's camera -- told him it was for the collective website, which is technically true since two of them are on there. Application is drafted. Just need to press send.

Submitted the Prix Dauphine application six days ago. Haven't told anyone except writing it here. Results aren't until December so there's a long stretch of not-knowing ahead. I've been filling it by throwing myself back into the collective stuff -- finalised the open studio weekend schedule, ordered new ink for the press, had a long call with the Mairie about our lease renewal.

Part of me knows I'm using the admin to avoid sitting with the fact that I put my work out there and it might not be enough. But another part thinks this is just what I do: I keep things running while I wait. It's not avoidance if the work genuinely needs doing. Or maybe I'm making excuses. Hard to tell from the inside.

Oriane asked at the studio yesterday if I'd ever considered applying for anything. I said "maybe at some point" and changed the subject to the November schedule. She nodded and moved on. I could have told her. I chose not to. It felt safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things. There's less to lose that way, or at least that's what I keep telling myself.

Fired the second batch of the celadon tea set today and the glaze came out exactly as Master Kwon taught me. Took one piece straight to Mother's room. She held it up to the window light the same way my father used to examine finished work.
entries.nudge_text: What went through you when she did that?
entries.response_text: It was just a nice moment. Anyway, the rest of the batch needs trimming before Thursday.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from the Gyeonggi Cultural Foundation. They want to feature my work in a contemporary ceramics exhibition in Seoul next March. The catch is they want me to collaborate with a digital artist who projects animations onto pottery surfaces.

I said I would review the proposal. The money is good and the exposure would help, but the whole concept feels like it turns the pieces into screens. I keep rereading the email instead of answering it.
entries.nudge_text: What would Master Kwon say about it?
entries.response_text: He would probably say the clay does not need decoration it did not ask for. But he also told me once that celadon survived because potters adapted across dynasties. I do not know which version of his advice applies here.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent Sunday at the Icheon Ceramics Festival committee meeting. They asked me to join the planning board for next year and I said yes immediately. My father served on that board for eleven years and his teacher before him. Sitting in that room felt like slotting into a space that was kept warm for me.

After the meeting a few of the younger potters invited me for soju. They were talking about selling through Instagram and shipping internationally, and I found myself actually listening instead of dismissing it. One of them, Minji, makes moon jars and photographs them beautifully. She said something about honoring the form while finding new eyes for it, which I have been turning over since.

It is strange how the committee meeting and the conversation afterward both felt right, even though they pull in different directions. The board is about preserving what we have. Minji's approach is about carrying it somewhere else. I suppose someone has to do both.
Called my sister Eunji in Vancouver for her birthday. She asked if I would visit in the spring. I told her I would think about it, same thing I say every year.
entries.nudge_text: What keeps the answer the same each year?
entries.response_text: Someone needs to be here for Mother. And honestly, seeing Eunji's life there, her kids barely speaking Korean, the way she has just folded into something else entirely. It is easier to love her from here.

entries.initial_entry: A gallery owner from Gangnam visited today and suggested I could triple my prices if I made the pieces more "accessible." Smaller, simpler, pastel glazes. I smiled and showed him the door. My kiln is not a factory floor.
entries.nudge_text: Did any part of his pitch land, even slightly?
entries.response_text: The part about reaching more people, maybe. But not at the cost of making things I would be embarrassed to put my name on. There is a difference between sharing what you do and diluting it until it is unrecognizable.

entries.initial_entry: Mother mentioned at dinner that Eunji wants to hold a small Christmas gathering this year. She asked why we never do that here. I did not know what to say. We do Seollal and Chuseok. That has always been enough.

Preparing the clay for the new year's first firing, which I always do in the last week of December. Same schedule my teacher kept, same schedule his teacher kept. There is something about the rhythm of it, wedging clay while the house smells like Mother's kimchi jjigae, the mountain visible through the workshop window still holding a little snow.

Minji sent me a photo of one of her moon jars displayed in a café in Itaewon. It looked beautiful, actually, warm light on white clay. I saved the photo. I would not do what she does but I am glad someone is doing it. The work keeps moving even if I stay in one place, and I think that is fine.
entries.nudge_text: What made you save that photo?
entries.response_text: It was a good photograph. The lighting was well done. Anyway, I need to finish wedging before it gets too cold in the workshop tonight.

entries.initial_entry: Eunji sent photos of her kids opening presents. My nephew was wearing a hanbok she had sewn herself. It was slightly wrong, the goreum tied on the left side. I almost texted her about it but stopped myself. At least she is trying.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from sending that text?
entries.response_text: She would have felt bad, and it is Christmas over there. It does not matter. The kids looked happy.

entries.initial_entry:
Supervisor meeting today. She said my literature review needs "tightening" before she'll sign off on the methods chapter. What does that even mean? I asked for specifics and got a vague hand gesture toward the citation list. Spent the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft to the review sections of papers that made it into top journals. They're all cleaner somehow, more confident in what they leave out.

Ingrid asked how it went when I got home. I said fine. She's been picking up extra client work to cover the gap our stipend leaves, and I couldn't bring myself to tell her I might be another month behind schedule. She looked tired. I should have said something real.
entries.nudge_text: Why the 'fine'?
entries.response_text: Because admitting I'm struggling feels like admitting I made the wrong choice. We gave up a good apartment, a pension plan, weekends that didn't involve reading deadlines. If I'm not making visible progress, what was all that sacrifice for?

entries.initial_entry: Revised twenty-three pages of the literature review. Supervisor meeting rescheduled to next week because she's at a conference in Berlin. Department coffee hour was cancelled again due to low attendance.

Grant application rejected. The feedback mentioned "insufficient preliminary data" and "unclear pathway to completion." I've been working on this for eighteen months. How much more preliminary can you get?

The younger students in my cohort seem unbothered by these setbacks. They shrug, submit elsewhere, go for beers. Maybe they haven't calculated their monthly burn rate. Maybe they're not watching their wife skip the organic vegetables at the supermarket because the regular ones are cheaper. I keep thinking about my old desk in Stockholm, the ergonomic chair the company provided, the predictable paychecks.
entries.nudge_text: What makes the difference for them?
entries.response_text: They can afford to fail. They're twenty-six, living in student housing, no one depending on them. I'm trying to build something at an age when most people have already built it.

entries.initial_entry:
Something small happened today that I keep thinking about. One of my students from the tutorial group, a first-year named Willem, stopped me after class to ask about the regression model I'd explained. He'd been confused during the lecture but didn't want to ask in front of everyone. I walked him through it again on the whiteboard, step by step, and watched his face shift from furrowed to open.

Teaching wasn't supposed to be the point. It's a requirement they attach to our funding, time carved out of research hours. But explaining something clearly enough that another person suddenly gets it, there's a satisfaction in that I hadn't expected. Reminded me of training junior analysts at my old firm, the ones who actually wanted to learn. I was good at that. People would request me for their onboarding.

Walked home the long way along the canal afterward. The light this time of year stays until past nine. For once I wasn't calculating anything. Just watched the boats and felt okay about being here.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different from the grant rejection?
