entries.response_text: The grant was about me being judged. This was about someone else learning. I couldn't fail at helping Willem because there was no scoreboard. It was just useful or it wasn't.

entries.initial_entry:
Couldn't sleep last night. Ran the numbers again on our savings. If I don't secure additional funding by September, we're looking at difficult choices. Ingrid doesn't know I've started browsing job postings in finance again. Not seriously, just looking. Just in case.

The thought of going back after everything we invested in this feels like a specific kind of defeat. Not just professional, something more personal. Like proof that I can't handle uncertainty, that I need someone else's structure to function. My father worked the same job for thirty-four years. I used to think that was limiting. Now I wonder if he just understood something I didn't.
entries.nudge_text: What did your father understand?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe that wanting more than one thing at once is a recipe for misery.

entries.initial_entry:
Department seminar today. One of the second-year students presented preliminary results that were, honestly, sloppy. Half the citations were missing page numbers, the statistical approach was questionable at best. But the professors smiled and nodded and said it was "promising early work."

When I presented six months ago, I got twenty minutes of pointed questions about my methodology. I spent three weeks revising based on their concerns. I'm not saying he didn't deserve encouragement, but the standards seem flexible in ways I don't understand. Maybe they see him as someone with time to improve. Maybe they see me as someone who should know better by now.

Same day, later. Ingrid found the job postings in my browser history. I expected anger but she just sat down on the bed and asked if I wanted to talk. Told her everything. The grant rejection, the supervisor feedback, the runway calculations, the feeling that I'm competing against people who have nothing to lose.

She listened and then said something I hadn't considered. She said we came here so I could find out if I could do this, and that finding out includes the possibility that I can't, and that wouldn't make the choice wrong. It would just make it complete. I don't know if I agree with her logic, but hearing someone say it out loud made the weight feel different. Lighter, somehow. Like the worst case scenario has been acknowledged and we're still sitting here, still together, still figuring it out.

Supervisor meeting today. She said my literature review needs "tightening" before she'll sign off on the methods chapter. What does that even mean? I asked for specifics and got a vague hand gesture toward the citation list. Spent the rest of the afternoon comparing my draft to the review sections of papers that made it into top journals. They're all cleaner somehow, more confident in what they leave out.

Ingrid asked how it went when I got home. I said fine. She's been picking up extra client work to cover the gap our stipend leaves, and I couldn't bring myself to tell her I might be another month behind schedule. She looked tired. I should have said something real.

entries.response_text: Because admitting I'm struggling feels like admitting I made the wrong choice. We gave up a good apartment, a pension plan, weekends that didn't involve reading deadlines. If I'm not making visible progress, what was all that sacrifice for?

entries.response_text: They can afford to fail. They're twenty-six, living in student housing, no one depending on them. I'm trying to build something at an age when most people have already built it.

Now I wonder if he just understood something I didn't.

entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe that wanting more than one thing at once is a recipe for misery.

entries.initial_entry:
Department seminar today. One of the second-year students presented preliminary results that were, honestly, sloppy. Half the citations were missing page numbers, the statistical approach was questionable at best. But the professors smiled and nodded and said it was "promising early work."

When I presented six months ago, I got twenty minutes of pointed questions about my methodology. I spent three weeks revising based on their concerns. I'm not saying he didn't deserve encouragement, but the standards seem flexible in ways I don't understand. Maybe they see him as someone with time to improve. Maybe they see me as someone who should know better by now.

Same day, later. Ingrid found the job postings in my browser history. I expected anger but she just sat down on the bed and asked if I wanted to talk. Told her everything. The grant rejection, the supervisor feedback, the runway calculations, the feeling that I'm competing against people who have nothing to lose.

She listened and then said something I hadn't considered. She said we came here so I could find out if I could do this, and that finding out includes the possibility that I can't, and that wouldn't make the choice wrong. It would just make it complete. I don't know if I agree with her logic, but hearing someone say it out loud made the weight feel different. Lighter, somehow. Like the worst case scenario has been acknowledged and we're still sitting here, still together, still figuring it out.

People keep telling me I need to set boundaries, like it's so easy. My friend Ana was saying how I should stop lending money to Diego because he never pays me back. But she doesn't understand—he's family. His girls needed new shoes for school and what was I supposed to do, just say no? He's trying his best with that construction job but the hours aren't steady.

I know I can't save everyone. I know that. But when someone in my life needs help and I have even a little bit to give, how do I look them in the eye and say sorry, I'm saving for myself? Maybe I'm the fool here but I'd rather be broke and know I showed up for people than comfortable and wondering if I did enough.
entries.nudge_text: You mention maybe being 'the fool' here—what would it look like if you weren't?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't know if I'd recognize myself. Like, the version of me who doesn't pick up the phone when Diego calls, who doesn't drive 40 minutes to help tia Elena with her groceries... that person feels like a stranger. Sometimes I wonder if I help because I want to or because I'm scared of who I'd be if I stopped. My mom was the same way. She gave and gave until there was nothing left. I don't want to end up like that but I also don't know any other way to be.

entries.initial_entry: Twelve hours of driving today. Picked up tia Elena's medication on my break because the pharmacy was on my route anyway. Just want to sleep.
Diego paid me back today, which surprised me. He said his girls wanted to give me their piggy bank money for helping them. I told them to keep it but I cried in the car after.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about that moment that moved you?
entries.response_text: I don't know, it was just sweet. Kids being kids. Anyway, it was a good day—better than most this week.

entries.initial_entry: Made tamales with tia Elena for the holidays. She taught me her mother's recipe, the one with the olives. Spent the whole afternoon together, just us and the masa.
My brother called from Bogota—he's struggling with rent again. I already sent what I could last month but I'll figure something out. He's my little brother; that's just what you do.
entries.nudge_text: When you say you'll 'figure something out'—what does that usually mean for you?
entries.response_text: It means I pick up extra shifts, maybe skip the new tires for another month. It's fine, I've done it before. My brother didn't ask to be born into this situation, you know?

entries.initial_entry: Tia Elena fell yesterday. She's okay, just bruised, but I wasn't there. I was doing a delivery across town and didn't see her call until an hour later. I should have been there.
entries.nudge_text: You were working—how could you have known?
entries.response_text: I know, I know. But she lives alone. What if next time it's worse? I've been thinking about asking her to move in with me but my place is so small and she's proud, she wouldn't want to feel like a burden. I don't know.

entries.initial_entry:
New Year's Eve was nice, actually. Ana came over with some of the other girls from the building and we did the grape thing at midnight—you know, eating twelve grapes for twelve wishes. Tia Elena made it too, even with her hip still sore. Diego's kids were running around screaming happy new year and setting off these little firecrackers in the parking lot.

I caught myself just standing in the kitchen at one point, watching everyone eat my food and laugh, and I felt... full. Not from the pernil, though that was good too. But like, okay, this is why I do everything. These people, this noise, this mess. Ana asked me what my resolution was and I said to take better care of myself but honestly I don't even know what that means. I've never been good at that part. Taking care of other people is easy—there's always a clear next thing to do. But taking care of myself feels selfish, or maybe I just don't know how.

My cousin said something that stuck with me though. She said you can't pour from an empty cup. I've heard that before but this time it hit different. Maybe because I've been running on empty for months and it's starting to show. Snapping at people in traffic, too tired to cook some nights, just ordering delivery even though I can't afford it. I want to do better this year but I don't know where to start.
entries.nudge_text: What's one small thing that would feel like taking care of yourself?
entries.response_text: I keep thinking about Sunday mornings. When I was little, before my mom got sick, she used to make herself coffee and sit on the porch alone for like an hour. She called it her "moment." I never understood it then—I thought she was just being lazy. Now I get it. I don't have a porch but I could... I don't know, maybe just sit with my coffee before I start driving. Not check my phone, not worry about who needs what. Just ten minutes of quiet. It sounds so small but I honestly can't remember the last time I did nothing without feeling guilty about it.

entries.initial_entry: I tried the coffee thing this morning. Five minutes in, Diego texted asking if I could watch the girls after school. I said yes before I even finished reading.
entries.nudge_text: What would it have felt like to finish your coffee first?
entries.response_text: Probably bad, honestly. Like I was choosing myself over them. I know that's not how it works but that's how it feels. Anyway, the girls are fun to have around. It wasn't a sacrifice, just a change of plans.

entries.initial_entry:
The faculty review came back today and my department scored highest on the new pedagogical alignment rubric. Twenty-eight years and it still lands when the numbers confirm what you feel in the classroom. I printed the summary and put it in the drawer with the others.

Ran into Katja on the way out, one of the newer teachers I meet on Mondays. She was upset about a parent complaint and I stayed an extra forty minutes walking her through how to frame the follow-up email. Got home late again but Brigitte had already eaten. Didn't say anything about it. I think she's stopped expecting dinner together on school days.
entries.nudge_text: What did Brigitte not saying anything feel like?
entries.response_text: Honestly, worse than if she had complained. When she used to get frustrated I could at least argue I was doing something that mattered. The silence feels like she's decided it's not worth the effort. And I don't know what to do with that because the work genuinely does matter to me, but so does she. I just can't seem to make the hours stretch.

entries.initial_entry:
Markus got the state teaching prize nomination. Markus. He's been at the school nine years and I recommended him for the department two years ago because I saw potential. Which is fine, good for him, I even wrote a reference. But I keep circling back to the fact that nobody thought to put my name forward and I've been building this department since before he finished university.

Maybe it's that the committee wants fresh faces. Or maybe my work has become furniture, something everyone relies on but nobody actually sees. I spent an hour tonight reorganising the spreadsheet of student outcomes to see if I'm slipping. I'm not. The numbers are solid. But the numbers don't seem to matter to whoever fills out the nomination form.
entries.nudge_text: You recommended him and he got the nomination. Where does that leave you?

entries.initial_entry:
Monday coffee with Katja, Leon, and Simone. Katja brought a lesson plan she'd redesigned using a framework I introduced two years ago and it was genuinely good. I told her so. Leon asked if I'd review his assessment rubric by Wednesday. Simone needed help navigating a difficult student's parents. I said yes to both without thinking.

Walking home I realised I hadn't touched my own conference paper all week. The submission deadline is in three weeks and I've been telling myself I'd carve out evenings for it. Instead the evenings go to other people's problems. I don't resent them for asking. I resent myself for not being able to say not this week.

Parent evening ran until nine. One father spent twenty minutes questioning why his son got a B+ instead of an A on the Cold War unit and I had to sit there with my folder of rubrics defending a grade I know is fair. This is the part of the job that drains me. Not the teaching, the performing.

Got home and Brigitte asked if I'd remembered to call my sister for her birthday. I hadn't. Sat on the couch for ten minutes not moving before I picked up the phone. Hilde sounded fine but I could tell she noticed I was going through the motions. Another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything.

Sunday. Actually managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop. Brigitte and I drove out to the Eifel, walked a trail we used to do when the kids were small. She pointed out the bench where Thomas fell asleep once, years ago, and we both laughed. It felt like coming back to something.

On the drive home I told her about the conference paper and how behind I am. She said maybe I should let the Monday coffees go for a few weeks. I know she's right in a practical sense. But those three depend on me right now, especially Katja, and I think what I give them matters more than another line on my CV. Or maybe I just tell myself that because it's easier than sitting alone with a half-finished draft.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you told them you needed a few weeks?

entries.initial_entry: Markus won the prize. Everyone clapped at the staff meeting and I clapped too. My hands felt mechanical.
entries.nudge_text: What were you thinking while you clapped?
entries.response_text: That the cake they brought was dry. No, I don't know. It's done, he earned it, I should move on.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the evening reviewing Leon's rubric instead of the conference paper again. It's becoming a pattern I can see clearly but apparently not interrupt.

Submitted a request to attend the European History Educators Conference in June. The head approved it immediately, said I'm long overdue for professional development. That felt decent. It's been four years since my last conference.

Called Hilde properly this time, no agenda, just to talk. She told me about her garden renovation and I actually listened instead of mentally drafting lesson plans. She said I sounded different, more present. I'll take that.

Second thought today. After calling Hilde I sat with my conference paper draft and got two decent pages written. The argument is about how we teach democratic participation as abstract principle rather than practiced skill, and once I started pulling examples from my own classroom it flowed better than I expected.

Brigitte came in with tea around ten and read over my shoulder for a minute. She said it sounded like me at my best, which is the kind of thing she doesn't say often enough for me to take it lightly. I saved the file and actually closed the laptop before midnight. Small accomplishment but I'll count it.
entries.nudge_text: What made tonight different from the other evenings?

entries.initial_entry:
Monday coffee again. This time I told the three of them I'd need to scale back for the next two weeks because of the paper deadline. Katja immediately said of course, Leon nodded, Simone looked a bit lost but said she understood. Nobody collapsed. Nobody resented it, at least not visibly.

Walking home I felt lighter than I have in weeks but also oddly guilty, as though I'd broken an unspoken contract. Brigitte would say that contract only exists in my head. She'd probably be right. I sat down with the draft again after dinner and got another page done. The structure is taking shape.
entries.nudge_text: Where does that unspoken contract come from?
entries.response_text: My father, probably. He was a teacher too, and his door was always open. Students, colleagues, neighbours. I grew up believing that being good at what you do means being available to everyone who needs you. The problem is you can spend a whole career being available and still feel like you haven't done the thing you were actually capable of. I don't want to retire with a drawer full of other people's thank-you cards and an empty shelf where my own work should have been.

entries.initial_entry:
Brigitte said something last night that I keep turning over. We were in the kitchen and she mentioned, casually, that she'd stopped inviting the Fischers for dinner because I always cancel or sit there half-present checking my phone for student emails. I started to argue but she cut me off and said she wasn't angry, just adjusting. That word, adjusting, hit harder than any argument.

I've been telling myself the work matters, and it does. The department results prove it, the younger teachers tell me constantly how much the Monday sessions help. But Brigitte adjusting means she's already factored in my absence as the default. My sister noticed the same thing on the phone last week. I don't think I'm neglecting them on purpose. It's more that every time I choose between something with a measurable outcome and something that just asks me to be present, I pick the one I can point to later and say, see, this counted.

I don't know when being present stopped counting for me. Or if it ever did count and I just never built the habit of valuing it properly. The conference paper is going well now and I should feel satisfied. But Brigitte adjusting is sitting in my chest like a stone and I can't spreadsheet my way out of it.

Got back from Nashik at ten, meeting ran five hours longer than planned. Supplier wants to renegotiate the whole contract because of raw material costs going up again. My back is aching from the drive but the deal isn't done so I'll be back there Wednesday. At least the evening puja helped settle my mind before the drive home.
entries.nudge_text: What about the Nashik deal has you going back so soon?
entries.response_text: It's just business. These things take time. Anyway, the real issue is that my production manager called in sick today and we've got a client audit on Friday.

entries.initial_entry:
Aarav called from Singapore this morning, first time in two weeks. He sounded good, talking about some new project at work, some kind of payment infrastructure thing. I don't understand half of what he describes but I can hear when he's fired up about it. He asked about Maa and I told him she's fine, that she made his favorite kaju katli last week and kept a box aside even though nobody's coming to collect it. He laughed but I don't think he caught what I was saying.

It's strange because I built this company partly so there would be something for the next generation to step into. My father had the first small shop, I turned it into a proper operation, and the idea was always that Aarav would take it further. He knows the business, he grew up in it. But he looked at it and chose something else. I respect that, I do. But there's a gap now in how I think about what comes next.

Had a good meeting with the sustainability consultants after lunch. They think we can get the biodegradable line certified by Q3 if we commit the capital. Numbers look solid. I told them I'd decide by end of month. Thirty years ago I would have said yes on the spot but now I sit with things longer. Not sure if that's wisdom or just fatigue.
entries.nudge_text: Is the gap about the company or something bigger?

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I can't sleep and writing seems better than staring at the ceiling fan. Went back to Maa's for Thursday dinner even though it's only Wednesday. She didn't ask why, just set an extra plate. She made dal the way Baba used to like it, with the whole red chilies left in. Sitting at that table, same steel plates we've used since I was a boy, I felt like things made sense for a moment. The dal tasted exactly right.

Came back and sat with the Q1 numbers. Revenue is up eleven percent but margins are thinner because of the raw material situation. The Nashik supplier is squeezing us and he knows we don't have many alternatives for that grade of recycled fiber. I've been in business long enough to know when someone has leverage and is using it. Need to find a second source, but that takes time and relationship-building, and right now I'm stretched.

I keep coming back to what Aarav said on the phone, that he's building something from scratch. I did that too, once. Took my father's small operation and built it into something real. Maybe that's what he needs to do on his own terms. I just wish his terms didn't put him seven thousand kilometers away. Anyway. The dal was good tonight. That's enough for one day.

Rajiv Kulkarni just got written up in Business Standard. They did a full profile, his logistics company apparently crossed the five-hundred-crore mark. We went to COEP together, started our companies the same year. He's always been three steps ahead, or at least that's how it reads in the papers. My company does good work, honest work, but nobody's writing profiles about steady growth in packaging materials. I know comparison is pointless but I looked at that article for too long this morning.

Then I caught myself wondering if Aarav would have stayed if the company was bigger. If there was something more impressive for him to inherit. That's an ugly thought and I know it, but it sat there in my chest for a while before I could push it aside. The truth is the company is solid. We employ over two hundred people, we pay on time, we've never missed a delivery commitment in six years. That means something. It just doesn't make the business papers.

Had the Nashik meeting. Drove back and went straight to the factory to check on the audit prep. Suresh had it mostly handled, which was a relief. Got home past nine. Shobha had kept dinner but I could tell she was irritated I didn't call. She's right. I should have called. I just get tunnel vision when I'm in it.
entries.nudge_text: You called that thought ugly. Why did it stick?
entries.response_text: Because it means I'm measuring my own son's loyalty against a revenue number. And that's exactly the kind of thinking I always told myself I wouldn't fall into. My father never once talked about the size of his shop. He talked about the quality of his paper, the customers who came back for twenty years. Somewhere I started keeping score differently and I don't like what that says.

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha brought up the Varanasi trip again at breakfast. Her mother's eightieth birthday is in April and the whole family is expected. She's already booked the train tickets. I told her I'll come for the weekend portion but can't take the full week off. She went quiet in that way she does when she's decided I'm wrong but isn't going to argue about it in the morning.

The thing is, I want to go. I actually do. Shobha's mother has always been kind to me. The ghats in the morning, the aarti at Dashashwamedh — there's a stillness in Varanasi that I don't find anywhere else. And the whole family gathers, cousins I haven't seen in years, the children running around. But the biodegradable line decision needs to happen and I can't hand that off. It's a forty-lakh capital commitment and some things you sign yourself.

Called the Nashik supplier after lunch. Offered a two-year lock-in at current rates. He said he'd think about it. Also interviewed a candidate for the assistant production manager role. Decent on paper but too eager to impress, kept dropping the names of companies he's worked with instead of talking about what he actually did. I'll see two more candidates next week.
entries.nudge_text: When's the last time you sat in that stillness?

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha told her sister I'm only coming for the weekend and apparently the whole family knows now. Got a call from her brother asking if everything is okay with us. Everything is fine. I'm running a company. People act like taking time off is some kind of moral test. My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two and nobody questioned his commitment to the family. He showed up when it mattered and so do I.

Signed off on the biodegradable line investment today. Forty-two lakhs when you include the testing certification. It's the right call. Suresh and I went through the projections twice. If the pilot customers convert, we'll break even by Q1 next year. Felt good to make the decision. Some days the work is the clearest thing I have.

Maa asked me tonight if I'm happy. Just like that, over chai, no context. I said of course, what kind of question is that. She just looked at me and sipped her tea. Mothers see through everything. I changed the subject to the new dal recipe she's trying but it sat with me the whole drive home.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she was seeing?

entries.initial_entry:
Production line for the new biodegradable material started trial runs today. Watched the first batch come through and it's not right yet — the tensile strength is off, tears too easily. The engineers say it's a calibration issue, two or three more iterations. I've heard that before. But the raw material itself feels promising. Held a sheet of it and it had good weight, good texture. These are the moments I still like about this work, when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands.

Aarav sent photos of his apartment. It's small but he's furnished it well. He has one of Maa's old brass diyas on his bookshelf, the one she gave him when he left. I zoomed in on the photo just to make sure I was seeing it right. He didn't mention it in the message, just the new coffee table and the view. But he kept it. He put it where he could see it.

Had lunch with Patil from the industry association. He's retiring next year and asked if I'd consider taking his seat on the board. I said I'd think about it. Ten years ago I would have jumped at the visibility. Now I'm not sure I want another obligation. The days are already full. But Patil's father and my father knew each other, and there's something about continuing those connections that matters.

Varanasi trip logistics settled. I'll arrive Friday evening instead of Saturday morning. Shobha seemed pleased when I told her, though she didn't make a big deal of it. I moved two meetings and delegated the supplier follow-up to Suresh. The company will survive three days without me. Writing that down feels like something I need to remind myself of more often.

Biodegradable line second trial was better. Tensile strength improved after the temperature adjustment. Still not where we need it for the food packaging spec but trending the right direction. Told the team I want a third trial by next Wednesday with the modified polymer blend. Set a target of reaching spec by mid-April. If we hit it, we can go to the pilot customers with samples before monsoon season complicates the logistics.

Also called Aarav in the evening. Didn't have a specific reason, just wanted to hear his voice. We talked for twenty minutes about nothing in particular, his weekend plans, a temple he found near his flat that does a small morning puja. He said he's been going on Sundays. I didn't know that. I said nothing about it because I didn't want to make it into a thing, but after we hung up I sat on the balcony for a long time feeling something I can't quite name. Relief, maybe. Or recognition.
entries.nudge_text: Relief or recognition — which one sits closer?
entries.response_text: Recognition. Like seeing something of yourself show up in your child without you having to put it there. I never told him to go to temple. He saw me and Maa do it his whole life and now he's finding his own version of it, seven thousand kilometers away. That means something got through. Not the company, not the career path, but something underneath all of that.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from Varanasi. The trip was good. Shobha's mother was genuinely surprised we came a day early. We did the aarti on Saturday evening, the whole family standing along the ghat with the flames reflected on the water. My brother-in-law and I talked business for a bit but mostly we just ate too much and let the kids run circles around us. Slept better those three nights than I have in months.

Got to the factory this morning and Suresh had handled everything cleanly. One minor quality flag on a shipment that he resolved. The Nashik supplier accepted the two-year lock-in. Good. Checked the third trial results on the biodegradable line — we're within eight percent of target spec. Close. I told the team to push for one more round by Friday.
entries.nudge_text: You slept better in Varanasi. What was different?
entries.response_text: No alarm. No production numbers to check before anyone else gets in. I woke up to temple bells and Shobha already dressed and ready for the morning walk along the river. I think I've forgotten what it feels like to not be the first person solving problems each day. In Varanasi I was just someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle. There was nothing to prove to anyone. I didn't realize how tired I was of being the one who holds everything until I put it down for three days.

entries.initial_entry:
Shobha asked if maybe we could go to Varanasi more often, maybe once a quarter. I said that's not realistic with the business right now, especially with the new product line launching. She said she's not asking me to shut the factory down, just to take a few days. I know she's not being unreasonable. But there's a part of me that tightens up when someone asks me to step away from the work. Like if I stop, everything stops. I know that's not true. Suresh just proved that last week.

Patil called again about the board seat. I told him I'd accept. It felt like the right thing. His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago, and if I can help the next group of small manufacturers the way the association helped me when I was starting out, that's worth the time. Shobha raised an eyebrow when I told her I'd taken on something new the same week she asked for more time off together. She has a point. I'm going to have to sort that out.

Fourth trial on the biodegradable line hit target spec. Finally. The team earned a day off and I told them so. Sent Aarav a photo of the sample sheet. He wrote back, "Looks like the future." Three words, but they landed right.

Sat through the entire biostatistics review session without asking about the regression exercise even though I got a completely different answer from everyone at my table. Dr. Pham went around the room and each group shared their approach and all of them had the same output, the same neat little table, and mine was off by enough that it wasn't rounding. I kept waiting for someone else to flag something but nobody did so I just nodded along.

After class I went to the TA's office hours and she helped me find the mistake — I'd misread the variable coding in the dataset. A small thing. Ten-minute fix. But the whole two hours in that classroom I was turning it over and over, wondering if I should raise my hand, wondering what Dr. Pham would say, what the twenty-three-year-olds at my table would think about the oldest person in the room not being able to read a codebook properly.

Came home and made lentil soup, the one my mother used to make with cumin and a whole lime squeezed in at the end. Ate it standing at the counter reading next week's assignment. The apartment was quiet, which was fine. Called Patricia and she said the kids have a stomach bug so Sunday dinner might just be the two of us. That's okay too.
entries.nudge_text: What would've happened if you'd just asked?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing bad. Dr. Pham is patient, he wouldn't have made a scene. I think part of it is that at the clinic I spent thirty years making sure everything ran without friction — no surprises, no awkward moments for the doctors or the patients. Asking a question that might slow everyone down or reveal I'm behind, it feels like making a mess someone else has to clean up. Even when I know that's not how a classroom works.

entries.initial_entry:
Group project meeting went sideways. Kayla proposed we restructure the entire literature review two days before the draft is due and everyone just went along with it. I had already written my sections the way we agreed last week. I sat there while they talked about the new framework and I wanted to say this is not what we decided, we had a plan, but Kayla was so energized and the others were nodding and I didn't want to be the one who drags things backward.

So now I'm redoing three pages tonight. I should have said something. I'm not even sure the new structure is better, it just sounds more impressive. But what really bothers me is that I smiled and said sure, sounds great, like I didn't have an opinion at all. Like thirty years of working with people taught me nothing about when to open my mouth.
entries.nudge_text: You said you should've spoken up — so what stopped you?

entries.initial_entry:
I don't know why the group project is still eating at me. It's done. We submitted. The restructured version was fine, probably even better than what I had. So what's the problem. Kayla even texted afterward and said she appreciated how flexible I was, and I think she meant it genuinely.

The thing is, everyone keeps talking about how grad school is supposed to push you and make you uncomfortable and I don't think they mean this kind of uncomfortable. They mean presenting at conferences or learning R. Not sitting in a meeting biting the inside of your cheek because you disagree but saying it out loud would make everything tense. That's a different kind of hard and nobody gives you credit for it because from the outside it looks like you just went along.

My daughter would say I'm overthinking. She's probably right. She's lived here since she was nineteen and she learned fast that Americans speak up, they push back, they call it being assertive and they admire it. I learned different things. In the clinic we kept things running by reading the room, by knowing when to let something go. That wasn't weakness. But lately it feels like the skills I spent a lifetime building don't translate. Like I'm fluent in a language nobody here speaks.

Spent the afternoon in the library finishing the epidemiology reading. Three chapters on study design — cohort, case-control, cross-sectional. Made notes in the margins the way I always do, small stars next to things I want to revisit. The reading itself was straightforward. I've been working through the textbook slowly, one section at a time, and it helps to have that rhythm.

Ran into Miguel from the parish at the coffee shop on Guadalupe. He asked about classes and I gave the usual answer — busy but managing. He's been here longer than I have, twelve years now, and he said the first couple of years in any new system are the hardest. He wasn't talking about school specifically but it landed anyway.

Walked home through campus when the light was going gold. Students everywhere on the lawn, studying, throwing a frisbee. I bought a pan dulce from the bakery on the corner and ate it on the bench outside my building. Patricia texted that the kids are better and they want abuela's arroz con pollo this Sunday. That settled something in me I didn't know was unsettled.
entries.nudge_text: What did Miguel mean about 'any new system'?
entries.response_text: I think he meant that anywhere you go, there's a way things are done, and it takes time to figure out the unwritten parts. The rules nobody tells you. He was talking about immigration but it applies to school too — figuring out when to speak, how to disagree, what's expected versus what's just tolerated.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday dinner went long. Patricia, the kids, Miguel and his wife Estela. I made the arroz con pollo and Estela brought a tres leches. The kids chased each other through the apartment and knocked over the little cactus by the window but nobody minded. It was loud and full in the way I miss sometimes during the week when it's just me and the textbooks.

I've been thinking about what it means to be a student again at this point in my life. Not the academics — I can handle the reading, the exams. It's more the social part, figuring out where I fit with people half my age and whether fitting in even matters or if I'm looking for something else entirely. At the clinic I knew my place, I knew the rhythms. Here I'm still learning them. But tonight, scooping rice onto plates while Estela told a story about her neighbor's dog, I thought maybe the rhythms I already know are enough. They just show up in different rooms now.
entries.nudge_text: If fitting in isn't it, what are you actually looking for?
entries.response_text: Honestly I don't know. Maybe I'll figure it out when I stop asking.

entries.initial_entry: Handed in my notice at Zain last week and everyone keeps asking why I'd leave a senior role with stock options. I don't have a clean answer. Something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like.
entries.nudge_text: What does that deadness feel like when it starts?
entries.response_text: It's hard to describe. Like the air gets thick. Anyway, the new place starts in two weeks so I'm focused on that now.

entries.initial_entry:
First week at NovaPay. The stack is Rust and Go, neither of which I've used professionally before, so I've been staying up past midnight going through documentation. The CTO is 31 and talks faster than anyone I've ever met. The whole office runs on this nervous energy that I find either exhilarating or exhausting depending on the hour.

My wife Dina said I looked happier at dinner last night than I have in months. She's probably right. My mother called from Amman and asked if this is really the time to be starting over, at my age, with the kids' school fees what they are. I told her it's a lateral move, which isn't exactly true — I took a pay cut. But she doesn't need to know that right now.

Second entry today because I just got out of the team standup and I need to note this. They gave me ownership of the payment reconciliation service on day four. At Zain it took me eight months to get write access to the production repo.

Spent the day pair-programming with Farah, one of the junior engineers. She's 26 and already knows Rust better than most people I've worked with. I kept catching myself wanting to impress her with something clever instead of just learning the idioms properly. Old habit. I measure myself against everyone in the room whether I want to or not.

We got the reconciliation pipeline passing all tests by end of day, which felt good. The CTO, Samir, pinged me in Slack to say "nice velocity for week one." I screenshot that. I know it's a small thing but I needed it. At Zain nobody noticed when you shipped. You'd push a feature that handled millions of transactions and the response was silence or a Jira ticket for the next thing.

After work I went to the gym for the first time in weeks. Ran five kilometers on the treadmill and my knees reminded me I'm not thirty anymore. Dina and I took the kids to City Walk for dinner. Layla wanted sushi, Adam wanted burgers, we compromised on a food court. Normal evening. But I was actually present for it instead of mentally rewriting code in my head, which is rare for me during a new project. Maybe that's a good sign.

Got invited to speak at a Go meetup next month about the migration patterns we're using at NovaPay. Said yes immediately. Now I have to actually build something worth presenting about, which is either motivating or terrifying, can't tell which.
entries.nudge_text: What made you say yes before thinking it through?
entries.response_text: Because saying no feels like admitting I'm not the kind of person who does these things. And I want to be that person. The one who ships and talks about it and gets known.

entries.initial_entry: My old colleague Walid messaged me saying NovaPay's last CTO left after six months and the company has a "churn problem." He thinks I made a mistake. I think he's projecting because he's been at the same bank for fifteen years and can't imagine choosing otherwise.

The meetup talk is in three weeks and I haven't started the slides. I keep opening the deck and then getting pulled into something else — a new Kubernetes operator I want to try, a side project idea for a CLI tool that generates API contracts. My brain refuses to stay on one track. The talk would be good for visibility, I know this, and yet every time I sit down to work on it I feel this resistance like I'm doing homework.

Dina asked tonight if I've made any friends at the new company or if it's "all just code again." Fair question. I eat lunch at my desk most days. There's a group that goes to the shawarma place downstairs but I usually skip it because I'm deep in something. I told her I'll try to go next week. She gave me that look that says she's heard this before.

Performance review cycle started this week and Samir asked everyone to write self-assessments. I spent two hours on mine, rewriting it three times. The first draft was too modest, the second was too aggressive, the third I think landed somewhere honest. I listed the reconciliation service, the test coverage improvements, the documentation I rewrote. Five weeks of work and I already have a page of deliverables. That's the thing about joining somewhere new — everything you do is visible.

Had an odd moment during Friday prayers at the mosque. The imam was talking about the difference between ambition that serves your community and ambition that isolates you. I'm not especially religious but it struck a nerve. My father was a civil engineer in Amman who built the same kinds of bridges for thirty years and was beloved by everyone in the neighborhood. I can't imagine staying anywhere for thirty years. Does that make me restless or just different? He seemed content. I don't know what contentment feels like, honestly. The closest I get is the first few months at a new job when everything is a puzzle and I'm learning at full speed.

Dina read part of my self-assessment over my shoulder and said "you really need them to see you, don't you." She wasn't being mean about it. But it sat with me because she's right. I do need that. I've always needed that and I've never really asked myself why.

Gave the Go meetup talk last night. About sixty people showed up. The demo worked — the live migration ran without crashing, which honestly I was sweating about until the last minute. Got solid questions afterward and two people asked for my LinkedIn. Samir was in the audience and mentioned it in the team channel this morning.

Good day. Drove home with the windows down even though it was barely 18 degrees out. Put on Fairuz, which I only listen to when I'm in a specific mood. Dina had kept dinner warm. I told the kids about the talk and Layla said "baba, are you famous now?" which made me laugh more than it should have.
entries.nudge_text: What mattered more — the talk going well or people noticing?

entries.initial_entry:
Three months into NovaPay. The initial rush is starting to flatten. Not gone, but the problems are becoming familiar. I know the codebase now, I know the deployment pipeline, I know where the bodies are buried. That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you stayed this time?

entries.initial_entry: Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.
entries.nudge_text: What was it like when she went quiet?
entries.response_text: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.
entries.nudge_text: Dina's question — what do you think now?
entries.response_text: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

entries.initial_entry: Handed in my notice at Zain last week and everyone keeps asking why I'd leave a senior role with stock options. I don't have a clean answer. Something just goes dead inside me when I know exactly what the next six months will look like.
entries.nudge_text: What does that deadness feel like when it starts?
entries.response_text: It's hard to describe. Like the air gets thick. Anyway, the new place starts in two weeks so I'm focused on that now.

That feeling of every day being a discovery is fading into competence, which should be a good thing but it makes me uneasy.

I keep thinking about a conversation with my uncle Faris last Eid. He runs a carpentry shop in Amman, same shop for forty years. I asked him if he ever gets bored and he looked at me like I'd asked something absurd. He said the wood is different every time — different grain, different knots, different mood. Same tools, same hands, but never the same piece. I want to believe that about software but I'm not sure I do.

Had a one-on-one with Samir and he talked about making me tech lead for the new payments vertical. It's a big deal. More responsibility, more visibility, a path to VP if the company scales. On paper it's everything I've been working toward. But there's a part of me that immediately started thinking about what else is out there. A friend forwarded me a job posting for a robotics startup in Abu Dhabi doing computer vision for warehouse automation. Completely different domain. I bookmarked it and I don't know why. I have a good thing here. Why can't I just stay with it?
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you stayed this time?

entries.initial_entry: Dina found the Abu Dhabi job posting in my browser history. She didn't yell, just got very quiet, which is worse. I told her I was just looking, which is true. Everyone looks.
entries.nudge_text: What was it like when she went quiet?
entries.response_text: Like she was doing math in her head. Calculating how many times we've done this. The kids changed schools twice in four years because of me. I know she's tired of it. I'm tired of it too, honestly. But the looking feels like breathing — I don't know how to stop.

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I can't sleep. It's 1am and I've been lying here thinking about the tech lead offer. Samir wants an answer by Monday. The role is everything that would look right on a CV — leadership, equity, a team of eight reporting to me. My father would have understood this kind of trajectory even if he didn't understand the technology. Build something, get recognized, build more.

But then I picture myself in six months running the same standups, managing the same sprint cycles, dealing with stakeholder calls about roadmap timelines. The operational weight of it. I know the excitement would drain out and I'd be left holding a title that looks impressive but feels like a cage. This has happened before. At Zain, at the logistics company before that, at the consulting firm before that. The pattern is so clear that I could draw it on my whiteboard — six months of fire, six months of friction, then I leave.

I don't know what I actually want. I thought I wanted to prove I could build things that matter and have people notice. And I can. I have. But the moment it becomes routine I start suffocating. And I don't know if the problem is the jobs or if the problem is me. Dina asked me once, years ago, whether I'm running toward something or away from something. I laughed it off. Tonight it doesn't feel funny.
entries.nudge_text: Dina's question — what do you think now?
entries.response_text: I think I'm doing both. Running toward the next thing because it makes me feel alive, and running from the part where being good at something stops being enough and you actually have to sit with yourself. My father could sit with himself. I never learned how. Maybe that's what I'm actually avoiding — not boredom, but stillness.

entries.initial_entry: Advisor asked why I haven't applied to more conferences yet. I know I should be building my CV but lately I just want to curl up with a book that isn't required reading.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if you don't apply?

entries.initial_entry: Morning coffee at that new kissaten near campus, the one with the jazz records and the old wooden counter. Just sat there for an hour watching the light change through the window. Good start.

Second entry today because I want to remember this. After the coffee shop this morning, I went to the seminar and Professor Mori mentioned a summer research position in Kyoto. It's prestigious, competitive, the kind of thing everyone in the program is scrambling for.

I thought about it during the whole train ride home. The position would mean living in a cramped dorm, working six days a week, minimal stipend. But the name on the CV would be impressive. Meanwhile my friend Emi is planning to spend summer working part-time at a bookshop in her hometown and hiking on her days off.

I kept thinking about what actually sounds like a good summer. Not what looks good, what feels good. Kyoto in summer is humid and exhausting. The dorm has shared bathrooms. I'd be doing research I'm only moderately interested in.

I don't think I'm going to apply. There will be other opportunities. This summer I want to actually enjoy.
entries.nudge_text: What would Emi say about your decision?
entries.response_text: She'd probably be surprised I even considered the Kyoto position at all. Emi has always been better at knowing what she actually wants versus what she thinks she's supposed to want. I've been trained my whole life to chase the next impressive thing. It's only recently I've started asking whether I actually want the things I'm chasing.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Three seminars, two papers to review for workshop, one very awkward dinner with my mother who flew in from Osaka "just to check in" which means "just to ask about job prospects."

She kept steering the conversation toward practical matters. Have I thought about which companies are hiring? Do I have connections in publishing? What's my backup plan if academia doesn't work out? I just wanted to enjoy the tonkatsu. We were at that place in Shibuya I love, the one with the perfectly crispy cutlets and the house-made pickles, and I could barely taste any of it because I was so busy defending my life choices.
entries.nudge_text: What would that dinner have been like without those questions?
entries.response_text: We would have talked about the food, probably. Mom used to be more relaxed about this stuff. We'd go out to eat and she'd spend the whole time appreciating every dish, teaching me how to notice the details. Somewhere along the way she got more anxious about my future and forgot how to just be present with me. I miss that version of her.

entries.initial_entry:
Turned down the Kyoto position officially today. Sent the polite email declining to apply. Felt nothing dramatic, just a small relief.

Spent the afternoon reorganizing my bookshelves by color instead of subject because I wanted my apartment to look beautiful, not efficient. Took about three hours. Could have been writing. Could have been reviewing literature. Instead I arranged my books into a sunset gradient from red to orange to yellow to cream.

My labmate Kenji stopped by to borrow a book and was visibly confused by the arrangement. "How do you find anything?" he asked. I told him I usually remember what color the spine is anyway. He shook his head and muttered something about humanities students.

Made dinner from scratch even though I had instant ramen in the cupboard. Tamagoyaki with rice and miso soup. Sat by my window and watched the city lights come on while I ate. Ordinary evening but somehow exactly what I needed.
entries.nudge_text: What were you recovering from?
entries.response_text: I don't think it was recovery exactly. More like... recalibrating? I've spent so many weeks doing things because I should. The bookshelf thing was completely pointless from a productivity standpoint and that's exactly why it felt good.

entries.initial_entry:
Conference deadline week. Everyone in the lab is stressed. The shared office smells like convenience store coffee and desperation.

I finally submitted my abstract at 11pm last night, not because I wanted to but because my advisor made it clear this wasn't optional. The whole process felt hollow. I wrote about a topic I find moderately interesting using frameworks I find mostly tedious to impress reviewers I'll never meet.

The worst part is I used to love this subject. My undergraduate thesis on modernist Japanese literature was something I genuinely cared about. Somewhere in the grad school process the joy got squeezed out. Now it's all about metrics. H-index. Citation counts. Impact factors. Nobody asks if you're enjoying your research. They ask if it's publishable.

Came home at midnight and couldn't sleep. Made hot chocolate and watched old episodes of a cooking show until 3am. Felt guilty about it this morning but honestly it was the happiest I'd been all week.
entries.nudge_text: Why guilty about the happiest part of your week?

entries.initial_entry: I'm so tired of feeling like enjoying my life is something I need to earn. Like I have to suffer through enough work hours before I'm allowed to have a nice dinner or watch a movie without guilt.
entries.nudge_text: Who taught you that?
entries.response_text: Everyone? No one specific? It's just in the air in East Asian academic culture. Work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later. My grandmother worked herself to exhaustion running a small restaurant for forty years, always saying she'd rest "when things calm down." She died before they ever did. I don't want that to be my story.

entries.initial_entry:
Presented at the workshop today. It went fine. People asked reasonable questions. Advisor said it was "solid work." High praise from him, apparently.

Afterward a few of us went for ramen to decompress. The kind of evening I actually enjoy - warm food, easy conversation, no agenda. One of the third-years asked how I stay so calm about deadlines and I laughed because I don't feel calm at all. I just refuse to let grad school consume every waking moment. They looked at me like I'd said something radical.
entries.nudge_text: What did their reaction make you feel?
entries.response_text: A bit sad, honestly. These are smart, interesting people who have completely bought into the idea that suffering is necessary for success. They've given up hobbies, relationships, sleep - for what? A slightly better chance at a job that might not even exist by the time they graduate? I don't want to be preachy about it but I genuinely think there's another way to do this.

entries.initial_entry: Spent way too much on a nice dinner alone at that French place I've been wanting to try. Felt indulgent. Also felt necessary. Can't explain it.
entries.nudge_text: What made it necessary?
entries.response_text: I think I needed to prove to myself that I can do nice things for myself without justifying it with productivity. No celebration, no milestone, just a Wednesday. I ordered the wine pairing. The waiter didn't ask me why I was dining alone and I was grateful for that.

entries.initial_entry: Advisor wants to meet tomorrow about "trajectory." I know what that means.

The meeting with my advisor went about as expected. He's concerned I'm not "maximizing my potential." Translation: I'm not working enough weekends, not applying to enough conferences, not publishing enough papers. He pointed at his own CV, decades of relentless output, and asked if I understood what it takes.

I told him I understood, but I also told him something I've been thinking about for months. That I watched my grandmother work herself into an early grave. That I've seen colleagues burn out and leave academia bitter and broken. That I want a career, yes, but not at the cost of actually living.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said something surprising. He told me he missed his daughter's childhood. That his wife almost left him twice. That he sometimes wonders if it was worth it. He said he wouldn't advise anyone to follow his path exactly.

We didn't resolve anything concrete. I still need to publish, still need to be strategic about my career. But for the first time it felt like we were having an honest conversation instead of him just transmitting expectations.

Walked home the long way through the park. The ginkgo trees are that perfect yellow right now. Bought a taiyaki from the street vendor and ate it while it was still warm. Whatever happens with my career, I want to remember that there's a life happening alongside it.

Had my annual performance review with the principal today. She said I'm on track for contract renewal next August. That was the main thing I needed to hear. She mentioned some areas to improve—classroom management during transitions, parent communication frequency—but nothing that would jeopardize my position.

After the meeting, I reorganized my lesson plan binder for the rest of the semester. Having everything mapped out through December gives me peace of mind. I know exactly what materials I need, which units align with the standardized testing schedule, and when I can schedule parent conferences.

Miguel and I talked on the phone tonight. He got a small raise at the accounting firm. We updated our shared savings spreadsheet. At this rate, we'll hit our apartment down payment goal by next June. It feels good to see the numbers moving in the right direction.

[No nudge requested]

My colleague Sofia asked me again why I don't apply for that international teaching fellowship in Spain. She keeps saying it would be "an amazing experience" and that I'm "wasting my potential" staying here. I don't understand why she can't accept that I'm content with my situation.

Yes, the fellowship pays more. Yes, it would look impressive. But it's a two-year contract with no guarantee of renewal, in a country where I have no support system. What happens after those two years? I'd be starting over. Here, I have seniority building, pension contributions accumulating, and my mother nearby if anything goes wrong.

Sofia took a gap year after university and talks about it like everyone should do the same. Not everyone grew up with parents who could bail them out. Some of us had to figure things out early. My choices make sense for my life, even if they don't make sense to her.

[No nudge requested]

Stayed late grading papers. The empty classroom felt different tonight—quiet in a way that made me think. I wonder sometimes if I'll still be in this same room in ten years. The thought is comforting and unsettling at the same time. Maybe that contradiction means something.
entries.nudge_text: What about that contradiction feels unsettling to you?
entries.response_text: I think it's that I've built everything around wanting things to stay the same. But nothing actually stays the same forever, does it? Students graduate, teachers retire, policies change. Even the building gets renovated. Maybe I'm holding onto an idea of permanence that doesn't really exist. I'm not sure what to do with that realization yet.

entries.initial_entry: Supervisor meeting went fine. She said the lit review draft is solid but needs tighter framing around the methodology gap. Called Mama after, she asked when I'm coming home. Same conversation every Tuesday.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'same conversation' actually sound like?
entries.response_text: She asks when I'll visit, I say after the semester, she sighs, I change the subject to Noor's day at the shop. It's a script at this point. Anyway, the lit review feedback was useful — I should focus on that.

entries.initial_entry:
Three nights of maybe four hours' sleep each and I still didn't finish the conference paper draft on time. I emailed my supervisor asking for two more days and she replied with a single line: "Let's discuss priorities in our next meeting." I've read that sentence fifteen times. It could mean nothing. It could mean she thinks I'm falling behind. Both feel equally possible and I can't stop cycling through it.

Noor called tonight about the shop — the main printer broke down again and the repair estimate is more than what I usually send in a month. I told her I'd figure it out. Didn't tell her I've been eating rice and lentils for two weeks straight to keep my own rent covered. She doesn't need that. She's handling the shop and Papa's physiotherapy appointments and I'm over here unable to finish a twenty-page paper.

The worst part is I know people in my cohort half my age are publishing faster. Ahmed from the lab already has two journal submissions this year. I'm not jealous of him specifically, it's more that I keep measuring myself against a timeline that was never mine to begin with. I chose to come here late. I keep forgetting that.
entries.nudge_text: You said you'd figure it out for Noor. Have you actually figured it out?

entries.initial_entry:
Supervisor meeting was actually good. She wasn't upset — she wanted to talk about narrowing my scope so the paper is submittable by July. She drew this diagram on the whiteboard, crossing off two whole sections I'd been agonizing over. Felt like someone opened a window.

Walked home through the park after and sat on the bench near the pond for a while. Called Mama from there. Told her about the meeting and she said "habibi, you always worry too much." She's right, probably. Noor texted later that she found a cheaper repair guy through a friend at the souk. Small thing but I felt my shoulders drop reading it.

End-of-term presentations today. I went third. My slides were clean, my data was organized, I rehearsed four times this morning. And then during Q&A, Professor Chen asked a question about my sampling methodology that I couldn't answer clearly. I fumbled through something about stratified approaches and he just nodded politely. That polite nod — I know what it means. It means the answer wasn't good enough.

The frustrating thing is I knew the answer. I'd written about it in section four of my draft. But standing there with twelve faces staring at me, half of them twenty-six years old and already sharper on their feet, my mind just locked. I sat down after and my hands were shaking slightly. Nobody noticed, I think.

Came home and couldn't eat dinner. Opened the spreadsheet where I track my targets — publication by August, comprehensive exam in October, teaching assistantship application due next week. Every single line has a yellow or red flag next to it. I used to run project teams of forty people. I built a bridge overpass in Zarqa. And now I can't get through a fifteen-minute Q&A without my voice going thin. I keep telling myself this is worth it, that the degree changes everything, but tonight I'm not sure what "everything" even refers to anymore.
entries.nudge_text: When you first decided to come here, what was 'everything' supposed to look like?
entries.response_text: I think I pictured myself walking across a stage and my mother watching from the front row. Papa too, before the stroke. The degree was supposed to prove that leaving the career and the salary wasn't reckless — that I could build something bigger than a bridge. But now most days it just feels like I traded one kind of pressure for another, except this one pays nothing and nobody at home understands what a comprehensive exam is.

entries.initial_entry:
Quiet week for once. Submitted the revised paper draft, supervisor said it reads much better with the narrower scope. No major feedback, just a few citation formatting things. I spent Saturday catching up on the teaching assistantship application — recommendation letters are in, statement is drafted, just need to polish the research summary section.

Called home and for once it was a normal conversation. Mama talked about the neighbors' daughter's engagement party. Noor complained about a supplier who keeps shorting her on paper stock. Nobody asked me when I'm coming back. It felt different. Lighter.

I realized I've been here almost three semesters and I still eat dinner alone most nights. There's a departmental social on Thursday that I keep meaning to attend but I always find a reason not to. Last time it was the paper deadline. Before that it was needing to call home during the time slot. I'm not lonely exactly — I talk to my family every day and I see my labmates — but there's a gap between being around people and actually having someone here who knows me beyond my research topic. It's not urgent. It's just something I notice when the work quiets down enough to notice anything.

Ahmed asked me at lunch if I'd thought about switching to a more "applied" research track since I have industry experience. He meant it helpfully, I'm sure. But the way he said it — like the theoretical work is too much for someone who came in mid-career — sat with me all afternoon. I told him I didn't leave a perfectly good job to do a watered-down version of what I already know. He looked surprised. Maybe I was too sharp.

But I'm tired of the assumption that because I'm older and came from practice, I should be doing something "practical." I'm here because I wanted the harder version. I wanted to understand the theory underneath the things I built for twenty years. That's not a consolation prize, it's a deliberate choice. People keep framing it like I'm catching up when I'm actually trying to go deeper.

The thing I didn't say to Ahmed is that his question hit exactly where I'm already uncertain. The comprehensive exam reading list is dense and some of the mathematical modeling chapters make me read the same page four times. I am keeping pace, but barely. And when someone who's been in academia since undergrad casually suggests I take the easier route, it confirms the thing I'm afraid of — that maybe the gap between where I started and where I need to be is wider than effort alone can close. I don't actually believe that. But I didn't need him planting the seed.
entries.nudge_text: You said you don't believe the gap is too wide. But you also didn't push back on the thought.

entries.initial_entry:
Good day. Actually good. Spent the morning working through chapter nine of the modeling textbook — the section on finite element analysis that's been haunting me for weeks. Something clicked today, like the pieces rearranged themselves while I was sleeping. I worked through three practice problems without checking the solutions until the end. Got two out of three right. The third was a sign error, not a conceptual one. That matters to me even if it sounds small.

Went to the departmental social after all. Stood in the corner with a cup of tea for the first twenty minutes but then Priya from the structures lab came over and we ended up talking for an hour about her fieldwork in Gujarat. She studies soil liquefaction. I told her about the overpass project in Zarqa and she asked genuinely interested questions — not the polite academic kind, the kind where someone leans forward. It felt good to talk about the work I did before as something that adds to what I'm doing now rather than something I left behind.

Walked home and the air smelled like rain. Called Mama and told her about the social. She said "finally, you're making friends" in that tone she uses when she's been worried but doesn't want to say so. I laughed. She's not wrong. Noor sent a photo of the shop with a new sign she designed herself — clean lettering, the family name in green. I saved it to my phone's home screen.

Teaching assistantship results came in. I got it. Starting September, I'll be a TA for the second-year structures course. The extra stipend means I can send Noor more without skipping meals myself, which honestly matters more to me than the line on my CV.

Sat with that for a while this morning. Three semesters of tracking every deadline on that spreadsheet, color-coding my progress, comparing myself to people who started this path twenty years before me. And here's a concrete thing that says: you belong here, you can do this, someone looked at your file and said yes. It doesn't fix the comprehensive exam anxiety or make the reading list shorter. But it's something solid to stand on.

Called Mama tonight to tell her about the TA position. She cried. Then she told me Papa smiled when she repeated it to him. I don't know if that's true or if she said it for me, but I'll take it either way.
entries.nudge_text: What would it change if it were true?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing practical. He can't travel here, he can't come to a graduation. But it would mean he knows I'm not wasting this. That the years away and the money spent turned into something he can recognize. I think that's what I've been working toward underneath all the spreadsheets and deadlines — just him knowing.

entries.initial_entry: Turned down the director thing. Everyone at the office looked at me like I'd lost my mind, including Tomás who's been pushing me toward it for two years. I don't owe anyone an explanation for wanting to keep my Fridays.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about the way they looked at you?

entries.initial_entry:
Can't stop replaying dinner. Camila brought up the director thing again, not even an hour after I told her I said no. She had that voice, the one where she's being very calm and very patient like she's talking to one of her students instead of her husband. She asked what my "plan" was. I said the plan is the same plan it's always been, I do good work, I enjoy my life, and I don't let some title turn me into someone who eats sad lunches at a desk in a city I don't even like.

She didn't say anything for a while. Then she said maybe the problem isn't the job, maybe the problem is that I always need something new and nothing is ever enough. That sat weird. Because I don't think wanting to feel alive is the same as nothing being enough. But I also couldn't explain why she's wrong without sounding like I'm the one who doesn't get it.
entries.nudge_text: Do you think she has a point?
entries.response_text: Maybe a small one. I don't get bored with her. But I get bored with everything around us if it stays the same too long, and I think she takes that personally even when it's not about her.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the whole day at the distribution center running damage control on a shipment mix-up with the Monterrey warehouse. Five hours of calls and spreadsheets and a very annoyed client who kept asking to speak to "someone higher up." Normally this kind of chaos makes me want to throw my phone off the roof, but today I sort of got into it. There was a rhythm to it. I called in a favor from Beto at the trucking company, rerouted three pallets through Aguascalientes, and had the corrected order confirmed by four o'clock. The client's tone shifted completely once he realized I'd actually fixed it same-day.

Drove home with the windows down, cumbia loud, and stopped at the fish market in Chapala on the way back. Picked up red snapper and a bag of limes. Made a zarandeado on the grill while the sun went down. Camila came out and sat with me while I cooked and we didn't talk about the director thing or plans or any of it. She just ate and said it was the best fish I'd made all year. That was the whole evening and it was perfect.

I think the good days look like this. Something that gets my blood going during the hours I'm working, and then the reward after. The snapper, the lime, the smoke off the grill, my wife telling me I got the char exactly right. I don't need a bigger title for that. I just need the space to keep having days like this one.
Budget meeting ran long. Signed up for a weekend mezcal tasting in Oaxaca next month. Tomás asked if I was "collecting hobbies" and I told him I was collecting experiences, which shut him up.
entries.nudge_text: The mezcal tasting -- whose idea was that?
entries.response_text: Saw it online somewhere. Looked fun, just booked it.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday and I'm wiped. Camila's parents came down from Zacatecas for the weekend and I spent two days doing the good son-in-law routine. Don Aurelio wanted to sit in the living room and watch fútbol all afternoon and I sat there with him because that's what you do. Four hours of commentary about the ref and the state of Mexican soccer and me nodding along while my brain was climbing the walls. I kept thinking about the Sayulita forecast, perfect swell all weekend and I'm on the couch watching a replay of a game I already know the score of.

Camila noticed I was restless. She told me to relax and be present. I wanted to say that sitting still and watching something I've already seen is the opposite of present for me, but I didn't. I just went to the kitchen and made everyone coffee and let it go. By tonight I felt like I'd been holding my breath for 48 hours and only just exhaled.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you'd actually said that to her?
entries.response_text: She would have said I was being selfish in front of her parents. And honestly, maybe she'd be right. They drove five hours to visit. I just can't fake enjoying something I'm not enjoying, and pretending drains me more than anything else.

entries.initial_entry:
Took the day off Friday and drove out to Sayulita alone. The waves were clean, offshore wind, barely anyone out. I caught a left that held up for maybe six seconds and when I came off the board I was laughing out loud with nobody around to hear it. Ate ceviche at that stand near the plaza where the guy recognizes me now and gives me extra tostadas.

On the drive home I called my brother Ernesto in Puebla. He's been grinding sixty-hour weeks at his plant and sounded terrible. I told him to come visit, we'd go out on the boat, eat well, do nothing useful for three days. He said he couldn't, too many deadlines. He sounded exactly like the version of me I've spent five years trying not to become. But surfing alone on a Friday while he sat in a fluorescent office tells me I'm doing the right thing, even when Camila and Tomás think I'm wasting my potential. I'm not wasting anything. I'm spending it.

Turned down the director thing. Everyone at the office looked at me like I'd lost my mind, including Tomás who's been pushing me toward it for two years. I don't owe anyone an explanation for wanting to keep my Fridays.
entries.nudge_text: What was it about the way they looked at you?

Because I don't think wanting to feel alive is the same as nothing being enough. But I also couldn't explain why she's wrong without sounding like I'm the one who doesn't get it.

entries.response_text: Maybe a small one. I don't get bored with her. But I get bored with everything around us if it stays the same too long, and I think she takes that personally even when it's not about her.
