entries.initial_entry:
Took the day off Friday and drove out to Sayulita alone. The waves were clean, offshore wind, barely anyone out. I caught a left that held up for maybe six seconds and when I came off the board I was laughing out loud with nobody around to hear it. Ate ceviche at that stand near the plaza where the guy recognizes me now and gives me extra tostadas.

On the drive home I called my brother Ernesto in Puebla. He's been grinding sixty-hour weeks at his plant and sounded terrible. I told him to come visit, we'd go out on the boat, eat well, do nothing useful for three days. He said he couldn't, too many deadlines. He sounded exactly like the version of me I've spent five years trying not to become. But surfing alone on a Friday while he sat in a fluorescent office tells me I'm doing the right thing, even when Camila and Tomás think I'm wasting my potential. I'm not wasting anything. I'm spending it.

The landlord for the apartment we've been saving for called Ernesto and said he's raising the deposit by two thousand pesos. Just like that. We were so close. I had the whole timeline mapped out in my notebook and now the numbers don't work anymore. I keep telling myself we'll figure it out but my stomach won't stop tightening.
entries.nudge_text: What part of the plan changing bothers you most?
entries.response_text: It's not about one part, it's about the whole thing. We had a deal, basically. We shook hands on a number and now it's different. But I guess that's how landlords are. Ernesto says we just push the move-out date back a month. That's probably what we'll do.

entries.initial_entry: Sofia napped for almost two hours this afternoon, which never happens. I used the time to reorganize the kitchen cabinets at my suegra's house. She didn't ask me to but they were driving me crazy. Everything is in labeled containers now. Ernesto's mom didn't say much about it. Made arroz con leche after dinner. Normal day otherwise.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel reorganizing the kitchen in a house that isn't yours?
entries.response_text: Honestly, a little strange. I know it's her kitchen. She's been cooking in it for thirty years and I show up and start putting labels on things. But when things are out of order I can't think straight. It's like the mess creeps into my head. I think part of me is trying to make this place feel like mine because we're going to be here longer than I planned. Putting the lentils in a jar with a label is the one thing I can actually control right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Ernesto brought up the motorcycle idea again at breakfast. His coworker is selling one cheap because he's moving to Monterrey. It would save Ernesto bus fare, which is like eight hundred pesos a month, and it would cut his commute in half. He showed me the numbers and on paper it makes sense. I get that.

But we'd have to pull from the apartment savings to buy it. Not all of it, but enough that the timeline changes again. And motorcycles break down. Insurance costs money. What if he gets into an accident? His factory doesn't have great coverage for that kind of thing. I didn't say all of this out loud because I could tell he was excited and I didn't want to be the person who always says no.

I said I'd think about it. Which is what I say when I already know my answer but don't want to fight. My mom used to do the same thing with my dad. She'd say "vamos a ver" and then quietly make sure things stayed the way they were.

I looked at our savings notebook after he left for work. If we buy the motorcycle we'd need four more months to save back up for the deposit. Four months is a long time when you're sleeping in someone else's house and your daughter doesn't have her own room.

Made Sofia's nine-month checkup appointment for next week. Packed her bag the night before like I always do—extra clothes, snacks, her vaccination booklet in the zippered pocket. Ernesto laughed and said I prepare like we're going on a trip. Maybe. But last time we forgot the booklet and had to come back. That's not happening again. Felt good having it all ready.
entries.nudge_text: What goes through your mind when everything's packed and ready?
entries.response_text: It's like I can breathe for a second. When the bag is packed and the appointment is confirmed and I know exactly what time we need to leave, there's this little window where nothing can go wrong. Or at least, nothing that I could have prevented. That matters to me. My mom raised four of us and she was always scrambling, always forgetting things, always stressed. I don't want that for Sofia. I want her to feel like someone has it handled.

entries.initial_entry:
The water heater has been making a weird sound for about a week. My suegra says it's always done that but I know it hasn't because I would have noticed. I told Ernesto we should call someone to look at it. He said it's probably fine and that getting a repair person out on a weekend costs double.

So we're just going to wait. And I'm the one who bathes Sofia in that bathroom every evening, listening to the thing rattle, wondering if it's going to give out or worse. He's at work all day, he doesn't hear it.

I know I could call someone myself but it's not my house. It's his parents' house and their water heater. So I wait for Ernesto to decide it matters enough, and in the meantime I boil water on the stove just in case, which his mom thinks is ridiculous.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned boiling water just in case — what would need to happen for you to call someone yourself?

entries.initial_entry:
Sofia is teething again and didn't sleep more than two hours at a stretch last night. I lost count of how many times I got up. Ernesto tried to help but she only wanted me, crying harder when he picked her up, so he went back to bed because he had an early shift.

My suegra made us chilaquiles this morning and I almost fell asleep at the table. She took Sofia for a walk around the block so I could rest. I lay down but couldn't actually sleep. Just stared at the ceiling thinking about nothing specific.

Did laundry, folded everything, put it away. Made a grocery list for the week. The routine helps on days like this. When I'm too tired to think, my hands know what to do. Ernesto texted asking if I needed anything from the tienda on his way home. I said no. We have everything. I checked twice.

I know people look at my life and think I should be doing something different by now. My sister keeps sending me job listings for warehouse jobs with benefits, like I haven't already thought about that a hundred times. But she doesn't understand that those jobs aren't what they used to be. My buddy Darren took one of those positions six months ago and got his hours cut to part-time within three months. No warning. Just fewer shifts on the schedule.

At least with the apps, I can see exactly what I'm earning. I pulled $847 last week across all three platforms. Gas was $112, which was a little high because I had to drive out to Broomfield for a delivery surge. But I logged every mile, and I know what my take-home is. Nobody can cut my hours without me knowing it's coming.

People call this unstable, but what's more unstable than trusting some company to keep you employed? I've seen too many people get comfortable and then get blindsided. At least I know the ground I'm standing on, even if it shifts.

I set aside $200 this week for the car fund. The transmission has been making a slight whine when I shift into reverse. Probably nothing, but I'm not taking chances. That car is everything.
entries.nudge_text: You mention the ground 'shifting' under you - what does stable ground actually look like for you these days?
entries.response_text: Stable ground? It's not some fantasy about a corner office or a 401k matching program. It's knowing I have enough in the car fund if the transmission goes. It's knowing that if one app slows down, I've got two others. People think stability means one thing, but they're just describing someone else's version of it.

entries.initial_entry:
Solid week. $923 total, and gas was only $98 because I stayed closer to downtown. The weather helped - no snow, which always kills the afternoon delivery runs when people don't want to tip for food that took an extra twenty minutes.

Got the oil changed on Thursday. Mechanic said everything looks fine. Transmission whine was probably just cold weather affecting the fluid viscosity. He didn't charge me for the diagnostic check since I've been going there for years. Still going to keep the car fund where it is, though. You never know.

I cannot believe this. DoorDash sent me a notification that I've been temporarily deactivated pending a review of my account. Something about customer complaints, which is absolute garbage. I have a 4.91 rating. I've never stolen food, never been late without a legitimate traffic reason, never done anything wrong.

They give you no information. No specifics about what the complaint was. Just a form email telling you to submit an appeal and wait. Wait how long? They don't say. Could be days, could be weeks. Meanwhile, that's roughly 35% of my weekly income just gone.

This is exactly what I was talking about before. These companies have all the power. One random complaint from some customer who's mad their fries were cold and suddenly I'm scrambling. I spent three hours last night going through old emails looking for any warning signs. Nothing.

I'm going to have to push harder on Uber and Instacart. Already adjusted my availability to pick up early morning grocery runs. I hate doing those - all the heavy lifting for less pay - but I don't have a choice right now. Had to move $300 from my car fund to cover this month's health insurance premium since my earnings are going to be short. That hurt.

The worst part is the waiting. Sitting around not knowing if this is going to resolve in a few days or if I'm going to have to find a whole new income stream. I hate not knowing.
entries.nudge_text: You moved money from your car fund to cover insurance - what went through your mind in that moment?
entries.response_text: Honestly? It felt like breaking a promise to myself. That money is supposed to be untouchable. It's there for when things go wrong with the car, not for when things go wrong everywhere else. But the insurance is non-negotiable. If I let that lapse and something happens, I'm finished. So it was picking between two things I can't afford to lose. I told myself it's temporary, that I'll put it back the second DoorDash resolves this. But saying that out loud just reminded me how many times I've had to tell myself something is temporary.

entries.initial_entry:
DoorDash appeal is still pending. No update. Did twelve hours between Uber and Instacart today. Made $186, which isn't bad but my back is killing me from the grocery hauls.

Called my sister to let her know what happened. She immediately started talking about the warehouse jobs again. I told her I'd think about it. Easier than arguing. She means well, but she doesn't understand that taking a job like that right now, when I'm already behind, would mean starting at the bottom of the seniority list. First to get cut when things slow down. At least with the apps, I'm not depending on someone else deciding I'm worth keeping around.

Got the email from DoorDash this morning. Account reinstated. No explanation, no apology, just a generic message saying the review was complete and I can start accepting orders again. I logged in immediately and did a few deliveries just to make sure it was real.

Still down $300 in the car fund. Going to take a few weeks to build that back up. Planning to do six-day weeks until I'm back where I was. Added a reminder on my phone to check my DoorDash rating every single day now. If I can catch a complaint early, maybe I can get ahead of it next time. Not sure that's even possible, but it makes me feel like I have some control over this.
entries.nudge_text: You mention the daily rating checks give you a feeling of control - does it actually feel that way, or is it something else?
entries.response_text: It doesn't feel like control, not really. It feels like watching the weather forecast when you know you can't change the weather. But I'd rather know a storm is coming than get caught in it. The checking is just... something I can do. Something that's in my hands. Even if it doesn't change anything, at least I'm paying attention. That has to count for something.

entries.initial_entry: Lit the incense this morning before the client call. Small thing, but my grandmother would approve. Felt steadier going into the pitch.

Met up with Jason and Wei Liang for drinks after work. They kept asking about my "startup life" with that tone they always have, like it's some extended gap year before I grow up and take over the family business like I'm supposed to.

I didn't argue this time. Just said business is fine, ordered another Tiger, changed the subject to Jason's wedding plans. I'm tired of defending myself. They don't understand that I can run my own thing and still be a good son. Those aren't opposites.

Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's was the usual. She made her braised pork belly the way she's made it for forty years, using the same clay pot that belonged to her mother. I watched her in the kitchen for a bit while everyone else was in the living room watching the news. She moves slower now but she still won't let anyone help with the cooking.

My cousin Melissa brought her new boyfriend, some finance guy from Hong Kong. Ah Ma was polite but I could tell she didn't like that he only spoke English and didn't touch the pig's ear dish. Little things. After dinner she pulled me aside and asked about my business. Not in the way my parents ask, like they're waiting for me to fail. She just wanted to know if I was eating enough and sleeping enough. Then she said her father also left his village to try something new, and that took courage, but he always sent money home. She wasn't lecturing. Just telling me.

I don't know why that hit different. Maybe because she's the one person who doesn't make me feel like I'm choosing between two lives.
entries.nudge_text: What makes her different from the others?
entries.response_text: She's lived through actual hard things. The war, losing her first shop, raising five kids. I think she just has perspective that my parents don't. They grew up comfortable because she made it possible. So now they're anxious about protecting what they have. She already knows you can lose everything and rebuild.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about what Ah Ma said about her father. I looked up the village name after I got home. It's in Fujian, some small place I can barely find on the map. He left with nothing and built something in Singapore. Opened a provisions shop. That became my grandfather's import business. That became my father's company.

And I left that to start a tech consultancy. Three generations of building, and I walked away. At least that's how my father sees it. He's never said it directly but I can feel it every time we talk. Like I've broken some chain.

But Ah Ma's father left too. He left his family, his village, everything familiar. And that's why any of us are here. So why is my leaving different? Maybe because I'm not leaving out of necessity. I'm leaving because I want something else. Maybe that's what they can't forgive.
entries.nudge_text: Is it something they can't forgive, or something you can't?
entries.response_text: Both, probably. I don't know if I've actually forgiven myself for not wanting what they want for me. Every time the business does well, part of me wonders if I'm just proving I was right to leave. And every time it struggles, I wonder if I should have just stayed.

entries.initial_entry:
Closed the deal with the logistics company. Three-month engagement, decent budget. Should keep the lights on through Q2.

Called my mother to tell her. She said "that's good" and then asked if I'm coming back for Qing Ming next week. Didn't ask anything about the contract. I said yes, I'll be there.
entries.nudge_text: How did that feel, her not asking about it?

entries.initial_entry:
Qing Ming was fine. We went to the cemetery at 6am before it got too crowded. I helped my father carry the offerings, burned the paper money, did the three bows. Same as every year.

My uncle asked when I'm going to "stop playing" and help run the real business. Right there at my grandfather's grave. I just smiled and said business is good. Didn't want to cause a scene. But I kept thinking, would Gong Gong have been disappointed in me too? He built something from nothing. I'm building something from nothing. It's not the same thing, but it's not that different either. I wanted to say that. I didn't.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying it?

entries.initial_entry:
Fight with my father tonight. Real one, not the passive aggressive stuff. He came to my apartment unannounced, said he was "in the area" which is never true. He saw my laptop open with the client dashboard and started asking questions. Then he said he doesn't understand why I need to prove something when there's already a business that needs me.

I tried to explain that I'm not proving anything. I just want to build my own thing. He said his father didn't have the luxury of wanting. He had to build because there was no other choice. Then he said "you have a choice and you're choosing wrong."

I don't remember exactly what I said back but it was something about how maybe I'm not wrong, maybe he just can't see it because he never had to question what he was supposed to do. He left without finishing his tea.

My mother called an hour later to say he's just worried about me. I know. I know he's worried. But being worried doesn't make him right.
entries.nudge_text: What happened after you hung up with your mom?
entries.response_text: I sat on the couch for a while. Didn't do anything. Then I cleaned the apartment, which I do when I'm upset. Washed the teacup he didn't finish. Went to bed early but couldn't sleep. Kept replaying what I should have said differently.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to the temple with Ah Ma this morning. She goes on the first and fifteenth of the lunar month, always has. I don't usually join but I had time and she seemed pleased when I offered.

We lit joss sticks and she showed me how to hold them properly, three in both hands, bow three times. She does it with such certainty. I like that about her generation. They don't question these things. They just do them because their parents did them and their parents before that. I used to think that was mindless but now I think maybe there's something to just... participating. Being part of something older than yourself.

Had to turn down a potential client today. They wanted me to work on a project for their online gambling platform. Good money, probably six months of runway. But I kept thinking about what my parents would say if they found out. Not that they would, but still.

My grandfather's name is on my business registration. Lim Wei Keong Consulting Pte Ltd. I named it after him because I wanted to feel connected to something larger than just me and my laptop. So when I thought about taking money from a gambling company, it felt wrong. Like I was attaching his name to something he would have disapproved of.

I don't even know if he would have cared. He died when I was twelve. I barely remember his voice. But I remember my grandmother saying he never gambled, not even mahjong, because his own father lost everything that way. So I said no to the client. Probably stupid from a business standpoint. But it felt like the right thing.
entries.nudge_text: What do you actually remember about him?
entries.response_text: Mostly just impressions. The smell of his pipe tobacco. His hands, very rough, always moving. He used to let me stamp the shipping documents with the company chop when I visited the warehouse. Made me feel important. I remember his funeral more clearly than I remember him alive. Everyone crying. The paper house they burned for him. My father reading the eulogy in a voice I'd never heard before.

entries.initial_entry:
Mother's birthday dinner at the seafood restaurant in Geylang. The one we've been going to since I was a kid. Same lazy Susan, same chili crab, same argument about who's paying the bill.

Dad and I didn't talk much but it wasn't hostile. Just quiet. I gave her a jade bracelet, the kind she likes. She cried a little, said I didn't have to spend money on her. Then she asked if I'm eating properly. Same as always.
entries.nudge_text: Where do things stand with your dad now?
entries.response_text: Same as always. He's stubborn, I'm stubborn. We'll figure it out eventually. Or we won't. Family, right?

entries.initial_entry:
Client emergency all day. Their website went down during a promotion and I had to fix it remotely while pretending to work from a cafe because my home internet was out. By the time everything was stable it was 9pm and I'd missed Sunday dinner at Ah Ma's.

First time I've missed in maybe two years. I called to apologize and she said it's okay, she saved me some food. But I could hear something in her voice. Not anger, just... something. Like she was expecting this would happen eventually. Like she's been waiting for me to become the kind of person who misses family dinners for work.

I am that person now, I guess. The kind who chooses the client over the grandmother. My father probably missed a hundred family dinners building the company. My grandfather probably did too. So why does it feel different when I do it? Maybe because I was supposed to be different. I was supposed to be the generation that has it figured out. The one who doesn't have to sacrifice everything.
entries.nudge_text: What did you think you were supposed to be?

entries.initial_entry:
Picked up the leftover braised pork from Ah Ma's this afternoon. She'd kept it in the fridge all week, even though I told her she could give it to someone else. Stubborn woman. I sat with her for a while in the living room, watching her fold paper offerings for the upcoming Vesak Day.

She didn't mention me missing dinner. Just asked about business and whether I was sleeping. Then she told me a story about my grandfather I hadn't heard before. Apparently he used to sneak out of family dinners early to check on the warehouse. His own mother would scold him. "The goods won't walk away," she'd say. But he couldn't help it. She laughed telling me this. Said I reminded her of him. First time anyone's said that in a way that wasn't a complaint.
entries.nudge_text: What did it mean to hear that from her?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Good, I guess. Like maybe I'm not as lost as I thought. Or at least I'm lost in a way that runs in the family.

entries.initial_entry:
Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?

entries.initial_entry:
Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?
entries.response_text: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent Nochebuena with my cousins and their families. Marisol's mother made the tamales this year, and we all said they weren't as good as my tía's back home, but we ate them anyway and laughed about it. That's the thing about being far from home—you recreate what you can, and the imperfections become part of the new story.

Midnight mass was crowded. I sat between Marisol and her little brother, and when we sang "Noche de Paz," I closed my eyes and could almost pretend I was back in the cathedral in Guatemala City, the one with the blue walls and the golden altar. The incense smelled the same. Some things carry across distance.

Now it's New Year's Eve and I'm home early from a short shift. The city is loud with fireworks and parties, but I'm content to stay in. I cleaned my apartment, changed the water for my santos, and made a list of things I want to accomplish next year: get the condo, help Marisol settle in, call home more often, maybe learn to make mole the way my grandmother does.

It's strange to think about the year ending. I feel like I'm exactly where I should be—maybe not where I dreamed as a teenager, but where I'm meant to be. There's comfort in that. Knowing that I'm building something solid, something that will last.

Started the new year by setting up automatic transfers to my savings account. It's not much, but seeing that number grow a little each month makes me feel like I'm actually getting somewhere. Small steps.
entries.nudge_text: Where is 'somewhere' for you? What does getting there look like?

entries.initial_entry:
Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.
entries.nudge_text: Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?
entries.response_text: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

entries.initial_entry:
The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

I talked to Abuela today. Really talked—not just the usual check-in where we say we're fine and miss each other. I told her about the condo, about feeling stuck, about Rachel and the travel nursing offer and all of it. She listened for a long time without interrupting, which is unusual for her.

Then she told me a story I'd never heard. About when she was young, maybe a little younger than me, and she moved from her village to Guatemala City for work. She said she cried every night for months. She said the city people made fun of how she talked, how she dressed, the food she cooked. She said she thought about going home so many times she lost count.

But she stayed. She stayed because she could see something on the other side of the hard part—a life she could build, children she could raise in a place with more opportunities. She said the crying stopped eventually. Not because it got easier, but because she grew into the person who could handle it.

"Mija," she said, "you're already that person. You just don't see it yet."

I cried after we hung up. Good tears, though. The kind that clean something out. I don't know if I'll find a condo in that building or if Marisol and I will end up somewhere else entirely. I don't know if I'll ever stop feeling caught between here and home. But I know that whatever I build, it will have roots. It will have her in it, and Mami, and everyone who came before.

That's enough. That has to be enough.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like if it were simply 'enough'—without the 'has to be'?
entries.response_text: I think it would look like trusting that I'm not going to lose anything by being here. That keeping our ways alive doesn't require living in the same building as my cousins or calling Mami every single day. That the roots can stretch without breaking. I'm not there yet. But maybe I'm closer than I was this morning.

entries.initial_entry:
Long shift today—twelve hours on the pediatric oncology floor. Lost a patient, a seven-year-old boy named Diego. His mother was inconsolable, and I stayed an extra hour just sitting with her, holding her hand because that's what my own mother would have done. That's what we do in my family. You don't leave people alone with grief.

I came home and lit a candle at my altar. Said a prayer for Diego, then another for his family. It felt right to do something, even if it's small. The other nurses on my floor, the American ones, they cope by going out for drinks or hitting the gym. That's fine for them. But I need the quiet. I need the ritual. It centers me.

Called Mami afterward. She didn't say much, just listened while I cried a little. Then she reminded me that this work is holy, that caring for the sick is what God asks of us. I don't know if I believe exactly what she believes anymore, but I believe in that feeling—that this job matters, that being present for people in their worst moments is something sacred.

I'm exhausted but I don't regret the extra hour. I'd do it again tomorrow.

Had coffee with my cousin Marisol after work. We talked about maybe getting an apartment together next year—it would cut costs and she's family, so I trust her. It's nice to have someone nearby who understands why I do things the way I do.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean for you to have Marisol closer in your daily life?

entries.initial_entry:
Posadas season started this week. The hospital let me organize a small celebration for the pediatric patients who were well enough to participate. We made paper luminarias, sang "Pidiendo Posada," and I brought in tamales that my tía made. Some of the kids had never seen anything like it—they kept asking questions about why we go door to door, why we carry the figures of Mary and Joseph.

Explaining it to them made me realize how much I've been holding onto these things without really thinking about why. It's not just about religion, not really. It's about belonging to something bigger than myself. Something that connects me to my grandmother, my great-grandmother, and all the way back. When I sing those songs, I can hear my abuela's voice mixing with mine, even though she's thousands of miles away.

One of the other nurses, Jessica, said she thought it was "so cool" that I have all these customs. She said her family doesn't really do anything special for the holidays anymore. I felt sad for her. Not in a judgy way, just—I can't imagine not having that thread connecting me to where I come from. It would feel like floating.

I'm already planning what to bring for Nochebuena. The head nurse said I could have Christmas Eve off if I work Christmas Day, which is perfect because Christmas Eve is when my family has always gathered anyway.

My supervisor offered me a position on the travel nursing team. Better pay, opportunity to see different parts of the country. I told her I'd think about it but I already know my answer.

The money would help—I could send more home, save faster for the condo. But travel nursing means no consistent schedule, no knowing where I'll be month to month. My cousin is counting on me for the apartment plan. And I can't just leave Miami when my whole support system is here. Abuela wasn't thrilled when I left Guatemala; leaving again feels like I'd be proving something wrong.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like there might be more pulling at you here than just the practical decision. What feels hardest to say out loud?
entries.response_text: The hardest thing to say is that part of me is curious. Part of me wonders what it would be like to just go somewhere new, not have to be the one everyone depends on for a minute. But that's selfish. My family gave up a lot so I could be here. I'm supposed to be building something stable, not chasing adventures. That's not who I am.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent Nochebuena with my cousins and their families. Marisol's mother made the tamales this year, and we all said they weren't as good as my tía's back home, but we ate them anyway and laughed about it. That's the thing about being far from home—you recreate what you can, and the imperfections become part of the new story.

Midnight mass was crowded. I sat between Marisol and her little brother, and when we sang "Noche de Paz," I closed my eyes and could almost pretend I was back in the cathedral in Guatemala City, the one with the blue walls and the golden altar. The incense smelled the same. Some things carry across distance.

Now it's New Year's Eve and I'm home early from a short shift. The city is loud with fireworks and parties, but I'm content to stay in. I cleaned my apartment, changed the water for my santos, and made a list of things I want to accomplish next year: get the condo, help Marisol settle in, call home more often, maybe learn to make mole the way my grandmother does.

It's strange to think about the year ending. I feel like I'm exactly where I should be—maybe not where I dreamed as a teenager, but where I'm meant to be. There's comfort in that. Knowing that I'm building something solid, something that will last.

Started the new year by setting up automatic transfers to my savings account. It's not much, but seeing that number grow a little each month makes me feel like I'm actually getting somewhere. Small steps.
entries.nudge_text: Where is 'somewhere' for you? What does getting there look like?

entries.initial_entry:
Had an argument with my coworker Rachel today. She made a comment about how I'm "always on the phone with my mom" and said it in a way that felt like she was making fun of me. I told her that in my family, we actually talk to each other, and she got quiet after that. Maybe I was too harsh but I don't regret it.

I know Americans have different relationships with their families. I know not everyone has a mother they can call whenever they need to hear a familiar voice. But the way she said it—like I'm some kind of child who can't function without parental approval—that stung. I'm not asking for permission when I call Mami. I'm asking for connection. There's a difference.

And yes, maybe I do ask her opinion on things more than Rachel asks her mother. So what? My mother has lived through things Rachel can't imagine. She raised four children while my father worked two jobs. She kept our family together when half the neighborhood was falling apart. Her opinion matters to me because she's earned it.

I'm not going to change who I am to make Rachel comfortable. If she thinks I'm too close to my family, that's her problem. I'd rather be "too close" than whatever she has going on where she barely speaks to her parents once a month.
entries.nudge_text: Underneath the frustration, what did Rachel's comment touch in you?
entries.response_text: It touched the part of me that sometimes wonders if I'm holding on too tight. If maybe the way I was raised doesn't fit perfectly here. I don't want to question how my family does things—we've been this way for generations and it's gotten us through everything. But sometimes I feel caught between two worlds, and comments like Rachel's just remind me that I don't fully belong in either one.

entries.initial_entry:
The condo I was looking at fell through. Someone else made a cash offer and the seller took it. Marisol said we could just find another one, but she doesn't get it. That was the building where our other cousins live. That was the whole point.

Everyone keeps telling me it's fine, that there will be other places, but I had planned around this. I had a whole picture in my head: Marisol and me in our place, walking distance from Lucia and her kids, Sunday dinners that don't require an hour of driving. Now I have to start over and I don't even know what I'm looking for anymore.

I talked to Abuela today. Really talked—not just the usual check-in where we say we're fine and miss each other. I told her about the condo, about feeling stuck, about Rachel and the travel nursing offer and all of it. She listened for a long time without interrupting, which is unusual for her.

Then she told me a story I'd never heard. About when she was young, maybe a little younger than me, and she moved from her village to Guatemala City for work. She said she cried every night for months. She said the city people made fun of how she talked, how she dressed, the food she cooked. She said she thought about going home so many times she lost count.

But she stayed. She stayed because she could see something on the other side of the hard part—a life she could build, children she could raise in a place with more opportunities. She said the crying stopped eventually. Not because it got easier, but because she grew into the person who could handle it.

"Mija," she said, "you're already that person. You just don't see it yet."

I cried after we hung up. Good tears, though. The kind that clean something out. I don't know if I'll find a condo in that building or if Marisol and I will end up somewhere else entirely. I don't know if I'll ever stop feeling caught between here and home. But I know that whatever I build, it will have roots. It will have her in it, and Mami, and everyone who came before.

That's enough. That has to be enough.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like if it were simply 'enough'—without the 'has to be'?
entries.response_text: I think it would look like trusting that I'm not going to lose anything by being here. That keeping our ways alive doesn't require living in the same building as my cousins or calling Mami every single day. That the roots can stretch without breaking. I'm not there yet. But maybe I'm closer than I was this morning.

entries.initial_entry: Spent all morning fixing the matching algorithm instead of calling back the three tutors who said they might quit. Keep choosing the code over the people. Mom called twice, didn't pick up. The platform won't matter if nobody's left to teach on it.

Thinking about yesterday. I tell myself I built this thing to help kids but half the time I'm just hunched over my laptop ignoring everyone. Soyeon asked if I was okay and I said yeah, fine. I wasn't fine. I was annoyed she interrupted me.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you building this for right now?

entries.initial_entry:
One of the students, Minjun, sent me a message through the app. Just a short thing, maybe three lines. Said his math grade went from a D to a B minus and that his tutor Hyunwoo explains things in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid. I read it on the subway and my eyes got hot. I looked up at the ceiling so nobody would see.

This is why I do it. Not the pitch decks, not the investor meetings I keep fumbling. This kid in Incheon who doesn't feel stupid anymore. I saved the screenshot. I'm going to look at it on the bad days.
entries.nudge_text: What do the bad days look like?
entries.response_text: Days where nobody replies to my messages. Where I eat convenience store kimbap for dinner again and wonder if I should've just stayed in school like everyone else. Where I check our user numbers and they haven't moved.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, later. Had dinner with Dad. He drove all the way from Incheon, which means he had something to say. We went to the jjigae place near Hongdae station, the one we used to go to when I was still enrolled and he'd visit on weekends.

He didn't bring up school right away. Asked about the platform, how many students we have now. I told him forty-seven active pairs. He nodded, did the math in his head the way he always does. "That's forty-seven kids whose parents can't afford hagwon," he said. Then he got quiet for a while.

Eventually he said your mother worries. I said I know. He said she doesn't understand why you couldn't do both, school and this. I told him it's not that kind of thing, you can't half-build something like this. He looked at his soup and said I know, I'm just telling you what she says.

We walked to the subway together after. He didn't bring up re-enrolling. That's new. At the turnstile he put his hand on my shoulder for a second, didn't say anything, and went through. I stood there like an idiot watching him go down the escalator. I think that was him saying he gets it. Or at least that he's trying to.

My old classmate Eunji posted about getting into Samsung's graduate program. Good for her. Everyone in the group chat congratulating her. I didn't say anything. I'm not jealous, I just don't have anything to say about it. My path looks nothing like that and I'm fine with it.

Hyunwoo, the tutor Minjun likes so much, told me he's graduating next month and can't keep volunteering. He was apologetic about it, kept saying he'd try to find a replacement. I told him it's fine, don't worry about it, we'll figure it out. We'll figure it out is what I say when I have no idea what to do.

I spent the evening going through the waitlist of potential tutors. There are eleven people who signed up in the last month. I messaged all of them. Three bounced, two said they're too busy now, one asked if we pay. Five didn't reply. So that's great.

The thing is I could pay tutors. I have enough from the freelance work to cover maybe two or three stipends. But then I'd have to charge the families or find a sponsor and both of those options make me feel like I'm turning this into something it's not supposed to be. I started this because the whole point was that it's free. That nobody has to qualify or prove they deserve help. But I can't keep bleeding money either. Soyeon says I'm being stubborn about it. She might be right but I don't want to hear it from her right now.

I keep thinking about Minjun. Who's going to explain things to him in a way that doesn't make him feel stupid.
entries.nudge_text: What would you tell Minjun if he asked why his tutor left?
entries.response_text: That people move on and it's not his fault. That I'd find someone just as good. I'd be lying about the second part but he doesn't need to carry that. He's fourteen.

entries.initial_entry: Haven't slept properly in three days. Freelance deadline tomorrow, two tutor spots still empty, Mom sent a long KakaoTalk message about my cousin's wedding that I know is really about me being twenty-two and having nothing stable. I want to reply to her but I don't know how to explain any of this without starting a fight.
entries.nudge_text: What part of it would start the fight?
entries.response_text: The part where I say I don't regret leaving school. She hears that as me saying her life as a teacher wasn't enough. That's not what I mean but it always lands that way.

entries.initial_entry: Found a replacement tutor for Minjun. Engineering student at Yonsei, seems solid. Stayed up until 2am onboarding her. Soyeon brought me coffee this morning without me asking. I almost cried over a cup of coffee. I think I need to be better at letting people help me.

Okay so today was one of those days where everything just sort of piled up and I don't even know where to start really. Woke up early because Arjun had a nightmare, then couldn't go back to sleep because my mind started racing about the lunch I'm supposed to make for the pooja tomorrow. Mummyji has her specific expectations you know, the dal has to be a certain consistency, the rice has to be this particular grain she likes, and I keep thinking about all the relatives who will be there judging every single dish I put out.

But here's the thing that's been bothering me all day—Priya messaged the group chat about going to that new spa place next weekend. The one with the aromatherapy and the hot stone massage. And I just... I really want to go? Like my shoulders have been killing me for weeks, carrying Aanya around, sleeping in weird positions when she has colds. I haven't done anything just for myself in so long. The last time was probably Diwali when I bought that kurta and even then I felt guilty about spending on something that wasn't for the kids.

I typed out "I'm in!" three times and deleted it each time. Because what will everyone think? Mummyji will do that thing where she doesn't say anything but you can tell she's disappointed. She'll mention something about how she never had time for "all this spa-shpa" when she was raising three kids. And my husband will probably be fine with it but then I'll have to coordinate everything—who will give Arjun his evening milk, what if Aanya doesn't nap for anyone else, all of that. It just seems like so much effort for a few hours of relaxation that I'll probably spend feeling guilty anyway.

But also... don't I deserve to feel good? My body hurts. I'm tired all the time. My friends are going and they keep saying I never come to anything anymore. I miss laughing with them about nothing. I miss being Meera who had opinions and went places and wasn't just someone's mother and someone's wife and someone's daughter-in-law.

I don't know. I'll probably say no. It's easier.
entries.nudge_text: You deleted that message three times—what were you afraid would happen?
entries.response_text: That's... actually a good question. I think I was afraid they'd say yes. Does that make sense? If I had sent it and they said "great, come!" then I'd have to actually make a choice. Right now I can tell myself I never really wanted to go anyway, it was just a passing thought. But if I commit, then I have to face my mother-in-law's silence, have to justify wanting three hours for myself, have to look at my husband and ask for help instead of just handling everything quietly. It's easier to be the one who sacrifices. Everyone expects it. Nobody questions it. The moment I want something, suddenly I'm the difficult one.

entries.initial_entry:
Both kids have a stomach bug. I've been cleaning vomit since 3am. Lost count of how many sheet changes. Mummyji keeps hovering and suggesting home remedies that I know won't work but I just nod and say "yes, good idea" because I don't have the energy to argue. Husband took the morning off but had to go back for some urgent meeting so I've been solo since noon.

I keep thinking about that spa. Priya sent the confirmation today—they booked for Saturday. The message just sat there in my notifications while I was elbow-deep in laundry. I want to cry but even that feels like too much effort. All I want is to lie down somewhere quiet where nobody needs anything from me. Just for an hour. Is that too much to ask?
entries.nudge_text: What went through your mind when you saw Priya's message?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Jealousy. Burning jealousy. They're going to have mimosas and get massages and laugh and I'm here with sick children and sour laundry and my mother-in-law telling me for the fifth time that ajwain water fixes everything. I know it's not their fault. They don't have kids yet, their lives are different. But I just kept staring at that message thinking "that could have been me" and then immediately feeling guilty for even thinking that because my babies need me and I love them but god I'm so tired.

entries.initial_entry: Kids are finally better. Mummyji made khichdi today without me asking—small gestures like this remind me she cares in her own way. Still thinking about the spa thing, wondering why it's so hard for me to just ask for what I want.
entries.nudge_text: When's the last time you did ask?
entries.response_text: I genuinely can't remember. Maybe when I was pregnant and asked for extra pillows? But even that felt like it was for the baby, not for me. Somewhere along the way I stopped asking. It was just easier to figure out how to not need things. Or to need smaller things that nobody would notice. Like my morning chai—I wake up twenty minutes early just so I can have that without anyone else's needs interrupting. That's my one thing. Twenty minutes of silence with a warm cup. Maybe that's enough. Maybe it isn't.

entries.initial_entry: Sat on the balcony after dinner with a glass of something cold and the city was doing that thing where the light turns everything golden. I could hear Camille laughing on the phone inside. I nearly didn't come home after school because some of the other trainees were going for drinks, but I wanted this more. Sounds stupid maybe but I picked the right evening. The air smelled like bread from downstairs and my legs were tired in the good way, like I'd actually done something useful today. The kids were chaos as usual but we got through the maths block without anyone crying, which I'm counting as a win. Sometimes the small stuff is enough.
entries.nudge_text: What made you sure about coming home?
entries.response_text: Honestly I just knew I'd be sitting in a loud bar pretending to enjoy myself when all I really wanted was quiet. I like those people fine but I already spent all day performing. The balcony doesn't ask anything from me.

entries.initial_entry:
My mentor observed my lesson on Friday and said I need to be "firmer with boundaries." She means discipline. I know she's right, probably. But I let the kids talk during transitions because they're six and they just want to tell each other stuff. She wrote it up as an area for improvement.

The thing that's sitting wrong is I smiled and nodded and said I'd work on it. I didn't push back at all. Didn't even say what I actually think, which is that making them sit in silence for thirty seconds between activities is pointless. I just folded. Walked out of the meeting feeling like I'd agreed to be someone I'm not and now I have to actually do it on Monday.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying what you think?
entries.response_text: She's the one signing off on my certification. That's the honest answer. I don't want to be difficult. Everyone else in the programme just does what they're told and it works out fine for them. I don't know why I can't just do the same without it gnawing at me afterwards.

entries.initial_entry:
Tried the firmer boundaries thing today and it felt horrible. I made them wait in silence before moving to reading and one of the little ones looked up at me like I'd taken something away from him. Which I had. I kept a straight face and got through it and my mentor wasn't even watching.

I don't know who I did it for. Camille asked how my day was and I said fine. Didn't feel like explaining the whole thing because she'd just say I'm overthinking it, which is probably true but doesn't help. Ate leftover pasta standing up in the kitchen and went to bed at nine. Can't tell if I'm tired or just flat.

My mum called tonight and asked how the placement was going. I told her it was going well. She asked if I was eating properly and sleeping enough and I said yes to both even though the second one is debatable. She worries, and I don't want to give her anything to worry about, so I edit.

It's not lying exactly. It's just that if I say "actually I'm struggling with this one aspect of teaching" she'll spiral into whether I've chosen the right career, whether I should come home for a bit, whether Lyon is too far. I've watched her do it with my brother when he mentioned one bad week at his job and suddenly she was looking up flats near their house for him. I can't open that door.

So the call was fine. Pleasant. We talked about her garden and my aunt's birthday and whether I'd be back for it in September. I said probably, which is true. She sounded happy. I sounded happy. Everyone performed their part.

I don't think there's anything wrong with that. People manage their parents. It's normal. I just wish it didn't take so much energy, the constant calibrating of what to share and what to keep back. By the time I hung up I was more tired than before the call started. Made myself a cup of tea and watched something mindless. Fine evening, really. Nothing to complain about.
entries.nudge_text: Who are you performing for besides your mum?
entries.response_text: That's a bit much. I just meant we keep things light, it's how we've always been. Anyway the point is the call went fine, not everything needs to be a big revelation.

entries.initial_entry:
Absolutely shattered. End-of-module assessments for the kids this week and I've been prepping materials every evening. My eyes hurt from the screen. But there was this moment today during the reading assessment where Nour, who's been struggling all term, read a full page out loud without stopping. She looked at me after and I just gave her a thumbs up and she grinned so wide.

That's it. That's the whole thing I'm holding onto tonight. The rest of the day was paperwork and a tense staff meeting about next year's timetable where I sat there saying nothing because the permanent teachers were arguing and I'm still a trainee so I just kept quiet. Which is fine, that's my place right now. I don't have standing to weigh in on scheduling decisions.

But Nour's face. She's been so anxious about reading and she did it. I didn't even do anything special, just followed the programme and gave her time. Sometimes you don't need to reinvent things.

Camille brought home macarons from that place near her office and we ate them on the balcony even though it was too hot. The pistachio ones are unreasonably good. I told her about Nour and she said "see, you're good at this" and I let myself believe it for a minute. Going to sleep well tonight. Body's wrecked but my head is okay.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said at that meeting if you could?
entries.response_text: Probably that the Wednesday afternoon slot they're fighting over doesn't matter as much as they think it does. But I'd never actually say that. I barely know the politics and I don't want to step on anyone. It's smarter to just listen for now and figure out how things work before opening my mouth.

entries.initial_entry:
Friday night and I'm sitting here instead of going to the party at Thomas's place. Everyone from the programme is going. Camille went already, she looked great, asked me three times if I was sure. I said I was tired which is true but also I just didn't want to go and I couldn't say that because then it becomes a whole conversation about why not and am I okay and should she stay.

The real reason is last time at Thomas's I had too much wine and said something about our supervisor that wasn't mean exactly but was honest and I could feel the room shift. Mathilde changed the subject really fast. Nobody brought it up again but I've been thinking about it for weeks. The comment wasn't even bad, I just said she gives vague feedback, which she does, but saying it out loud in front of people felt like I'd broken some rule I didn't know existed.

So now I'm on the sofa with leftover macarons and a show about renovating houses in Normandy and I feel weird about it. Not the staying in part, I genuinely like this. But the fact that I'm staying in because of something I said three weeks ago that probably nobody remembers. That's the part that's sitting wrong. I keep replaying it and adjusting — what I should have said, how I should have laughed it off, whether I should have just kept my mouth shut like I usually do. The mouth-shut version of me has fewer problems but she also has fewer macarons and is standing in Thomas's kitchen right now pretending to enjoy bad speakers and warm beer.

Got my mid-placement review today. "Meets expectations." Not exceeds, just meets. My mentor said I'm reliable, well-prepared, good with the children, but I need to contribute more in team settings and be more assertive in classroom management. The same things again.

I wanted to say: I am good with the children precisely because I'm not assertive in the way you mean. I let them breathe. But I didn't say that. I thanked her for the feedback and said I'd keep working on it. She seemed satisfied. The review goes in my file and that's that.

Afterwards Thomas asked a few of us how it went and everyone said fine. Mathilde got "exceeds" in classroom management and was obviously pleased. I said mine went well too, which it did, technically. Meets expectations is fine. It's literally the standard. I don't know why I'm annoyed about it.

Actually I do. I'm annoyed because I think I'm better than meets expectations but the things I'm good at aren't the things they measure. They don't have a box for "made a scared kid feel safe enough to read out loud." They have a box for "implements behaviour management strategies consistently." I do implement them. I just don't enjoy it and apparently that shows.

Had dinner with Camille and we split a bottle of wine and she told me about some drama at her office and it was exactly what I needed. Not to think about my own stuff for an hour. Good food, good company, clean kitchen afterwards. The week is done.
entries.nudge_text: Is 'meets expectations' actually fine with you?
entries.response_text: No. But what am I going to do, argue with the rubric? I know what they want and I know I could give it to them if I tried harder. I just don't want to become that teacher. The one the kids go quiet around. I'd rather meet expectations and keep the classroom feeling like a place they actually want to be.

entries.initial_entry:
Marking forty-two exam papers and my eyes are giving out. Priya from Class 10 wrote something genuinely moving about her grandmother's village, but I cannot even enjoy it properly because the stack keeps growing and dinner still needs sorting.

Mummy-ji asked twice when I would be done with the table because she wants to lay out her medicines. I said ten more minutes both times and it was never ten minutes. Rajesh heated leftovers for everyone without being asked, which was something. Still at the table now. Still marking.
entries.nudge_text: Who got the short end tonight?
entries.response_text: Everyone, probably. The papers got half my attention, Mummy-ji got empty promises, and I did not even sit with Rajesh while he ate.

entries.initial_entry:
Parent-teacher meeting ran an hour over. Sharmaji spent twenty minutes arguing his son deserves higher marks in poetry analysis. I explained the rubric twice. He was not satisfied but eventually left.

Came home to find the washing machine had leaked again. Rajesh called the repair person. Bahu made chai without asking, which was nice. Ate late. Nothing else to say about it.
Same day but lying in bed now and actually feeling lighter than expected. The chai Bahu made — she added elaichi the way I like it without me mentioning. Small thing but it sat with me.
entries.nudge_text: What made that land so much?
entries.response_text: She noticed. Nobody asked her to, she just paid attention. Sometimes that is the whole thing.

entries.initial_entry:
Ritu called during lunch break, which never happens on weekdays, and for a second my chest seized because I thought something was wrong with the baby. But no — she just wanted to tell me that little Aarav said "Nani" for the first time. Clear as anything, she said, not a babble. She held the phone to him and he did it again and I was standing in the staff room corridor pressing the phone to my ear like an idiot with tears running.

The other teachers walked past and Sunita asked if everything was okay. I just nodded and waved her off. Could not explain it properly. Twenty-eight years of students calling me Meena ma'am and none of it hits the way one small boy saying Nani does.

Came home and told Mummy-ji and she got teary too. We sat together for a bit, just the two of us, which does not happen often these days because the house is always full of someone needing something. Rajesh came in and saw us and quietly backed out. He knows when to leave a room. That is its own kind of gift, knowing when not to interrupt.

Spent the morning helping Sunita draft her transfer application — she wants to move closer to her aging parents in Varanasi. Felt right, sitting with her and getting the wording correct, even though it means the department loses a good teacher.
entries.nudge_text: You're helping her leave. Does that sting at all?
entries.response_text: It is her parents, what else would I tell her. Anyway the application needed better phrasing, that was the immediate problem.

entries.initial_entry: Staff meeting about the new attendance software. Everyone complained. I kept quiet even though I also find it needlessly complicated — did not see the point in adding to the noise when the decision was already made.

Sunday reading circle went well. Kamla-ji finally came back after skipping three weeks because of her knee. She said she almost did not come today either but her daughter-in-law pushed her out the door. We are reading a Premchand collection and she had opinions about Nirmala that got everyone arguing in the best way.

I had set up an extra cushion near the door in case her knee was bad, and she noticed and patted my hand when she sat down. We have known each other since our children were in primary school. There is something about that length of knowing someone — you can set up a cushion without it being a fuss.

After everyone left I cleaned up the chai cups and rearranged the chairs. Rajesh offered to help but I told him to rest, his back has been bothering him. Did it myself in fifteen minutes. The room still smelled like cardamom and old pages. Good Sunday.

Neha from the English department asked if I would co-sign a letter to the principal about the new workload policy — extra periods without compensation. She has been drafting it for weeks and already has four signatures. I said I would think about it.

I agree with every word in that letter. The workload is unreasonable and the younger teachers are burning out. But the principal and I go back years, he came to Ritu's wedding, and this feels like it would land as a personal thing even if it is not meant that way. Neha looked disappointed when I did not sign immediately. She did not say anything but the pause said enough.

Now I am sitting here turning it over and I do not like how I handled that. The right thing and the comfortable thing are not the same here and I walked toward comfortable without even deciding to. I keep telling my students to speak up when something matters. Easier to say from behind a desk apparently.

Still have not signed Neha's letter. Rajesh says I am overthinking it and should just do what feels right, but that is exactly the problem — what feels right is staying out of it, and I am not sure that is actually right. Not everything needs to be a stand.
entries.nudge_text: What would signing cost you, specifically?
entries.response_text: It is not about cost. It is about... I do not know. Can we not do this right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Met with the Rotterdam curator today. She spent twenty minutes explaining the "cohesive vision" for the group show and showed me examples of what she's looking for from other artists. Clean lines, muted palettes, conceptual restraint. Everything I've been moving away from in my studio work.

I nodded along and told her I had some pieces in development that would fit perfectly. I don't. I have canvases full of saturated color and texture that would look completely wrong next to what she described. But this is the Kunsthal feeder gallery — a good showing here means better representation, better sales, a step closer to the kind of career I've been working toward for eight years. So I'll make something new. That's what professionals do.
Started three new sketches for Rotterdam. Muted greens, geometric forms, nothing that feels like mine but everything that fits. Deadline is October 15th. Plenty of time if I stay focused.
entries.nudge_text: What makes something feel like yours?
entries.response_text: Ownership is complicated in this industry.

entries.initial_entry:
My friend Sanne came by the studio today and asked why everything looked so different. I told her about Rotterdam and she got this look — not disappointed exactly, more like she was watching me make a choice she wouldn't make. She said "you could always say no" like it was simple. Like turning down the Kunsthal feeder show wouldn't ripple through every networking dinner and studio visit for the next two years. Like my gallerist wouldn't quietly wonder if I'm "difficult."

The thing is, I remember being 24 and thinking I'd never compromise on my work. I had this whole philosophy about integrity and staying true to my vision. But then you go to enough openings where the artists with gallery support have catering and yours has boxed wine you bought yourself. You watch people with half your skill get collected because they know how to make curators feel smart. You learn that the art world runs on relationships, and relationships run on making people comfortable.

I'm not selling out. I'm participating in a system that exists whether I like it or not. Sanne can afford her principles because her parents own three properties in Utrecht.
entries.nudge_text: You're explaining why it makes sense. I'm curious if it feels right.
entries.response_text: No. It doesn't feel right. It feels like I'm building a portfolio that looks like success while my actual work stays hidden in a corner of my studio. But "feeling right" hasn't paid my rent in eight years — meeting expectations has. I don't know how to want different things than I want.

entries.initial_entry: Worked twelve hours on the Rotterdam pieces. They're competent. That's the worst word I could use. Competent.

Quarterly review with my gallerist Pieter this morning. He went through the sales figures, upcoming opportunities, collectors who've expressed interest. I've sold more this year than last year. I should feel proud of that.

He mentioned Rotterdam and said it's "exactly the kind of strategic move" that builds long-term careers. He used the word "strategic" three times. I wanted to tell him that I've been sneaking into my studio at night to work on my own pieces — the ones that won't sell, the ones that feel alive — but instead I thanked him for his guidance and asked about the fair calendar for next spring.
entries.nudge_text: What happens in those night sessions?
entries.response_text: I paint without thinking about who's going to see it. Thick impasto, colors that clash, marks that look unfinished. It's probably worse than my commercial work technically. But I lose track of time when I'm doing it. That hasn't happened with the Rotterdam pieces once.

entries.initial_entry:
Got coffee with two artists from my residency cohort — Anke and Willem. Willem just turned down a show because the venue wanted him to "tone down the political elements." Anke nodded like this was obviously the right call. I sat there wondering if I'm the only one who does the math before every decision.

When I mentioned Rotterdam they both went quiet for a moment before Anke said "that's great for you" in a voice that clearly meant something else. I'm tired of being the person who plays by the rules in a room full of people who act like rules are optional. Some of us don't have trust funds or teaching positions or partners with stable incomes. Some of us need our art to actually function as a career.

I didn't say any of that. I just said I was excited about the opportunity and let them judge me silently over their flat whites.

Can't sleep again. Keep thinking about this conversation I had with my mother last week. She asked how the career was going and I listed my accomplishments like a CV and she said she was proud but then asked if I was happy and I said yes immediately without thinking about it. Was that a lie? I'm successful by every metric I set for myself ten years ago. Gallery representation before 30. Consistent sales. Press coverage. A studio I can afford. That's supposed to be the goal.

But then I think about the night sessions and how they're the only time I feel like I'm actually doing something instead of performing something and I don't know what to do with that. If I follow that feeling I lose everything I've built. If I ignore it I keep building something that looks like a life from the outside.
entries.nudge_text: What if it's not all-or-nothing?

entries.initial_entry: Rotterdam pieces are done. Sent photos to the curator. She responded within an hour saying they were "exactly what we discussed." Submitted everything to Pieter for documentation. Moving on.

Opening night for a small group show at a friend's project space. Nothing prestigious, just artists showing work they believe in to thirty people drinking cheap wine. I almost didn't go because I had grant applications to finish, but Sanne insisted.

The work was uneven — some pieces felt student-level, honestly — but there was this energy in the room that I haven't felt at my own openings in years. People were actually talking about the art, arguing about it, getting close to the pieces instead of networking in corners. One painter had hung these massive chaotic canvases that broke every rule about composition and I stood in front of them for ten minutes feeling something I couldn't name.

On the train home I thought about my Rotterdam pieces hanging in a pristine white gallery where people will nod approvingly and then talk about real estate. That's success. That's what I wanted. I got everything right.

I keep telling myself that's what I wanted.

The Rotterdam curator emailed asking for an artist statement. I've written hundreds of these. I could do it in my sleep. But I've been staring at the blank document for two hours because every phrase I type sounds like someone else wrote it.

"My practice explores the intersection of..." No it doesn't. My practice explores whatever will get me into the next show.

I called my mother tonight to tell her about Rotterdam and she cried a little and said she always knew I'd make it. I thanked her and hung up and then sat in my studio surrounded by my real work — the hidden canvases, the ones no one will ever see — and felt like I was standing in someone else's life.

I don't know how to want different things. But I'm starting to think I already do.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to let one of those hidden pieces out?
entries.response_text: One step at a time. Rotterdam first.
