entries.response_text: One step at a time. Rotterdam first.

entries.initial_entry: Another sprint planning meeting where they want me to estimate the same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years. I know exactly how long everything takes now. That is maybe the problem.
entries.nudge_text: What's bothering you about knowing?

entries.initial_entry:
The new architect they brought in, Jennifer, she wants to rewrite the payment gateway from scratch. Using Go instead of Java. Everyone in the room looked at me like I was supposed to object, since I built the original thing in 2019. But honestly? Part of me wanted to say yes, let us do it, let me learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever.

I did not say that though. I said we should consider the timeline and the risk to the December release. Which is true. The business needs stability. But I walked out of that meeting feeling like I had swallowed something heavy.
entries.nudge_text: You sided against yourself in there?
entries.response_text: It is not that simple. Jennifer does not know what she does not know about the regulatory requirements. Someone had to be the adult.

entries.initial_entry:
Elena surprised me with tickets to a tech conference in San Francisco next month. Not the corporate kind with vendor booths and networking bingo, but one of those unconference events where people just propose sessions in the morning and see what happens. She said she noticed I have been looking at conference websites lately but never registering for anything.

I should be excited. Five years ago I would have booked the flight before she finished her sentence. Now I am already thinking about the sprint commitments, about whether Marcus can handle the on-call rotation alone, about what my manager will say when I request the days off. The conference is on a weekend. I would only need to take Friday off. Still, I hesitated.

What bothers me most is not the hesitation itself. It is that I used to make decisions faster. Move to Austin? Two weeks to decide. Take the startup job over the stable bank position? One evening with Elena and a bottle of wine. Now I research, I weigh, I consider all stakeholders. My mother would say I finally grew up. But it feels more like something got dimmer.

I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes. I just wish the yes had come easier.
entries.nudge_text: When did decisions start feeling heavy?
entries.response_text: Maybe around the time my father got sick. 2018. Suddenly there were consequences everywhere I looked. Every choice felt connected to whether I would be there, whether I was reliable. He passed in 2019 and I thought it would lift, but it did not. The weight just found new things to attach to.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. Production incident at 3 AM, the payment gateway again. Not the part Jennifer wanted to rewrite, ironically, but the legacy integration with the bank's SFTP server. I was on call so I got the page. Took four hours to trace it to a certificate expiration that nobody had on their calendar.

By 7 AM I had fixed it and documented everything. By 8 AM I was in the standup explaining what happened. By 9 AM I was getting messages from the VP asking how we let this happen. Not thank you for catching it. Not good job on the quick fix. Just how did we let this happen.

I did not let anything happen. I inherited this system. I have been asking for budget to modernize the certificate management for two years. But I did not say that either. I just said we would add better monitoring and put the renewal dates in a shared calendar. Safe answer. Team player answer.

Elena asked me at dinner why I looked so tired. I said work was busy. I did not tell her about the VP message because then she would get angry on my behalf and I would have to either agree with her anger or defend the company that made me feel small today. Both options felt exhausting.

I need to sleep but my mind keeps rehearsing the incident like I missed something. I did not miss anything. The certificate was not in any documentation I had access to. But I keep checking anyway.
entries.nudge_text: You keep swallowing things down lately.
entries.response_text: Maybe I do. It is easier than the alternative. Every time I push back, I become the difficult one, the senior engineer who cannot adapt, the old guy who thinks he knows better. So I absorb it. I fix things quietly. I make myself small so the team can function. It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years. I used to think he was weak for that. Now I understand he was just tired.

entries.initial_entry: The morning after a bad night is always strange. Everything looks normal. Coffee tastes the same. I just feel like I am watching myself go through the motions from somewhere slightly outside.
entries.nudge_text: What motions are you watching yourself do?
entries.response_text: The usual routine. Checking Slack before I get out of bed. Reviewing the overnight alerts. Logging into the standup. Answering questions I have answered a hundred times. It is not bad, exactly. It is just automatic. Like I am not really needed there, just my pattern.

entries.initial_entry:
Flew to San Francisco yesterday for the unconference. Staying at a hotel near the Embarcadero with a view of the bay. Weather is cold and gray but I brought my good jacket.

The conference format is interesting. You show up in the morning, people pitch sessions on index cards, everyone votes with stickers, and then the schedule emerges. No predetermined speakers, no approved slides. Someone proposed a session on rebuilding legacy systems with LLMs. Someone else wanted to discuss leaving tech after 50. I almost proposed something about maintaining software versus maintaining yourself, but I did not have the index card ready in time.

I attended four sessions. The legacy systems one was good, practical advice I could use. The leaving tech one was harder to sit through. A woman around my age talked about how she realized she had optimized her whole life for a company that laid her off in an afternoon. She cried a little. Everyone was respectful.

Tonight I walked along the waterfront instead of joining the dinner group. Watched the ferries come and go. Called my mother. She asked if I was eating well and I said yes, even though I had skipped lunch. Some lies are kindness.

Tomorrow is the last day. I feel different here. Not better or worse. Just different.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you with that index card?
entries.response_text: I was still figuring out what I wanted to say. It felt too personal for a room of strangers. Maybe next time.

entries.initial_entry:
Back from the conference for a week now and people at work keep asking how it was. I say it was great, very inspiring, lots of good ideas. Which is true. But when they ask what I am going to do differently, I do not have a good answer. Learn Go? Propose a rewrite? Those feel like someone else's moves.

My manager suggested I do a lunch-and-learn to share what I learned. I said I would think about it. I probably will not do it. Public speaking was never my thing, and honestly, the sessions that meant most to me were the ones I cannot really present in a corporate setting. Nobody wants a slide deck about existential uncertainty in late-career engineers.
entries.nudge_text: What did you actually bring back from there?
entries.response_text: Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff.

entries.initial_entry:
December sprint started with three people out sick and a scope increase nobody agreed to. I have been working ten-hour days trying to keep us on track for the holiday release. Jennifer is frustrated because her Go rewrite keeps getting deprioritized. I am frustrated because I am the one who has to explain why to her without making leadership look bad.

Had dinner with Elena's cousin Miguel who is visiting from Monterrey. He runs a small construction company there. He asked me about my job and I tried to explain sprint planning and stakeholder management and he just laughed and said it sounds like I spend a lot of energy managing people's feelings about work instead of actually working. He did not mean it unkindly but it stuck with me. Maybe he is right. Maybe that is what corporate software engineering becomes after a while.
entries.nudge_text: What would actually working look like for you?
entries.response_text: Building something. Seeing it run. Not writing status reports about why it is not done yet. But that is not realistic at my level, I suppose.

entries.initial_entry:
I finally did it. I proposed to my manager that I move to Jennifer's team for the Go rewrite project. Not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer. Someone who writes code and learns the new language and maybe remembers what it felt like to not know everything about a codebase.

He looked at me like I had asked for a demotion. Which, technically, I had. He said he would need to think about it, talk to HR, consider the implications. He asked if everything was okay at home. I said yes, everything is fine, I just want to learn something new before I am too old to learn.

I do not know why I said it like that. I am not too old. But something in me wanted to say it out loud, the fear underneath. Like naming it would make it less powerful.

Elena was proud of me when I told her. She said she has been waiting for me to do something like this for years. I did not know she was waiting. I thought I was hiding the restlessness well enough. Apparently I was only hiding it from myself.

Now I wait to see what they decide. Maybe they say no and I stay in my maintenance role forever. Maybe they say yes and I flounder in a new language at 57. Both possibilities feel more alive than certainty.
They said yes. Contingent on finishing the holiday release first, but yes. I start on Jennifer's team in January. I have been studying Go tutorials at night, feeling like a student again.

Vikram got promoted to Staff Engineer today. Everyone's congratulating him on Slack, and yes, fine, he deserved it. But let's be honest—he's been here six months longer than me and his scope was smaller until Q2. I'm not bitter. I'm just aware of where I stand. My manager keeps telling me my time will come, but I've heard that before at my last company. You have to make your time come.

Called Appa tonight. He asked about work, and I told him everything was progressing well. No point worrying him with the details. He mentioned the temple renovation fund and I sent extra this month. These things matter. You take care of your family, you do what's expected—that's not a burden, that's just how it should be.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'making your time come' actually look like?
entries.response_text: Visibility. I need to be leading cross-functional initiatives, not just delivering my own sprint work. I've already started attending product roadmap meetings even when I'm not required. When the reorg happens in Q4, I want my name to come up naturally for the platform team lead role.

entries.initial_entry: Priya asked when I'm taking her to meet my parents. I said after Diwali, maybe. We both know I'm stalling but she didn't push.
Three nights of on-call rotations. The junior devs can't seem to handle basic incident triage without escalating to me. I don't mind being the one they depend on, but I need sleep.
entries.nudge_text: Is there a difference between being needed and being indispensable?
entries.response_text: Indispensable means they can't function without you. Needed just means they prefer having you around. I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep. Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced.

entries.initial_entry:
Quarterly review feedback came in. My manager said I have strong technical skills but need to demonstrate more "executive presence." I asked what that meant specifically. She said it's about how I communicate upward—being more concise in leadership meetings, framing problems in terms of business impact rather than technical detail.

I've always believed that doing excellent work should speak for itself. That's what my father taught me—results matter, not showmanship. But apparently that's not how it works here. The people who get ahead are the ones who know how to package themselves.

Spent the evening reading articles about executive communication. Downloaded a book on stakeholder management. I don't like that I have to play this game, but I've never failed at something I committed to learning. If this is what they want, I'll figure it out.

What frustrates me is that nobody told me the rules changed. At IIT, you studied hard, you got the marks, you got the placement. Clear metrics. Here it's all subjective—"presence," "influence," "visibility." How do you measure that? How do you know if you're winning?
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean if excellent work alone was never enough?
entries.response_text: Then everything I was taught about how success works would be wrong. My father worked for the same company for thirty years and retired as a senior manager—never played politics, just delivered results. But maybe that's why he stayed senior manager. Maybe there was a ceiling I didn't see. I don't want that ceiling.

entries.initial_entry: Presented the Q3 roadmap to the leadership team today. Kept it to seven slides, used business metrics instead of technical specs. VP nodded twice. Small win.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel to communicate that way?

entries.initial_entry:
Priya's friends are getting engaged left and right. She showed me photos from another mehendi last night and I could feel her watching for my reaction. I said the bride looked happy. That's not the response she wanted.

It's not that I don't want to marry her. I do. But there's an order to these things. My parents expect me to be settled—flat, savings, stable position—before I take that step. Amma specifically said not to repeat what her brother did, rushing into marriage without foundation and then struggling for years. I'm building the foundation. Priya should understand that this is for us, not against us.

My cousin Karthik called to announce he's getting married. He's two years younger than me, works at an MNC doing standard IT support, lives in a rented flat in Coimbatore. And yet he's the one Amma spent twenty minutes congratulating on the phone tonight. "Such good news, beta! When will we hear from you?"

I'm the one who sends money home. I'm the one who got into IIT, who moved to Bangalore for better opportunities, who's actually building something. Karthik posts cricket memes and calls it a day. But because he's checking the marriage box, suddenly he's the responsible one?

I'm not jealous of Karthik. His ceiling is that MNC job until retirement. I want more. But I can't help feeling like the family scoreboard doesn't track the things I'm actually achieving. It's all marriage, children, temple visits. The professional stuff—they're proud, sure, but they don't understand it. To them it's just "computer work."

What if I never make them understand? What if I keep climbing and the only thing they see is the boxes I haven't checked?
entries.nudge_text: Whose approval matters more to you right now?

entries.initial_entry:
The platform team lead role opened up officially. My manager submitted my name. I've been preparing all week—reviewing my contributions, documenting the initiatives I've led, practicing how to frame my scope expansion.

Interview is next Thursday. Five panelists including the CTO. I've never presented to him before. My manager said he values people who "think like owners"—whatever that means. I'm guessing it means speaking about company strategy as if you have personal stake in it. I can do that.

Called Priya to tell her. She was supportive but also asked if this meant more late nights. I said probably yes, at least initially. She went quiet. I told her once I get this role, things will stabilize. She said I said that about the senior engineer promotion too. She's not wrong, but what does she expect? This is how you build something. You sacrifice now for position later.

Appa would understand. He always said the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns. I'm still investing.

Interview went well, I think. CTO asked how I'd handle a direct report who consistently missed deadlines. I said I'd have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences. He seemed to like that.
entries.nudge_text: What's making you unsure about how it went?
entries.response_text: It's not uncertainty, it's just being realistic. You never know what other candidates said or what factors matter to them. I did my part. Now I wait.

entries.initial_entry:
Got the role. Platform Team Lead. Officially starts August 15th. Vikram sent me a congratulatory message that was probably through gritted teeth. Doesn't matter. I'm ahead now.

Manager said the CTO specifically mentioned my clarity about accountability. Direct conversations about expectations and consequences—turns out that's exactly his management philosophy. Lucky guess, or maybe I just think the way leaders do. Either way, I'm in.
entries.nudge_text: What does being 'ahead' give you?

entries.initial_entry:
Called home to share the news. Appa congratulated me, asked what the new title means in terms of responsibilities. Explained about headcount, budget ownership, technical direction. He said it sounds like a lot of work. Then asked if this means I'll be too busy to come for Pongal.

I said of course I'll come for Pongal. I always come for Pongal.

Amma asked about Priya. I said we're doing well. She said Karthik's wedding is in November and the family will be asking questions. I said I know. She said maybe Priya could come for the wedding, meet everyone. I said I'd think about it.

After the call, I sat with this feeling I couldn't name. I achieved exactly what I set out to achieve six months ago. Platform Team Lead before 32. Higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite. But the call home felt like two different conversations—me trying to share a professional milestone, them trying to pull me back to the family timeline.

Maybe both things can be true. Maybe I can lead the platform team and also bring Priya to Karthik's wedding and also send money home and also make Pongal. But it feels like I'm running two races on parallel tracks, and I'm not sure if either one knows the other exists.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like if both tracks knew about each other?
entries.response_text: I'd have to tell my parents what the promotion actually cost—the late nights, the stress, why I haven't proposed yet. And I'd have to tell Priya that family expectations aren't just background noise to me, they're load-bearing. I don't know if anyone wants that level of honesty. Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate.

entries.initial_entry:
My aunt called again about the civil service exam. She does this every Chuseok season, like clockwork — finds some cousin's daughter who just passed and drops it into conversation as though I asked. I said the same thing I always say. I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered.

She doesn't believe me. I know because she paused the way she does when she's holding something back, and then she said Umma worries. Which is just her way of saying she worries, but putting it on my mother so it lands harder. I didn't take the bait. Told her I had to go check the kiln.

There was nothing in the kiln. I just sat in the studio and threw a slab that I didn't need. The wheel was cold and the clay was too wet because I hadn't wedged it properly. It didn't matter. I wasn't making anything. I was just moving my hands so I wouldn't think about the conversation. Normal Wednesday.

Gallery owner asked if I'd consider doing a collaboration with a streetwear brand. Custom glaze finishes on sneaker displays. Good money. I said I'd think about it but the whole walk home I kept hearing halmeoni's voice — that clay remembers what your hands were thinking when you shaped it. Felt cheap even considering it.

Woke up to a message from Ji-hye. She got into the residency in Arita. The one we both applied to, the one that would have meant three months studying under a fifth-generation potter and learning noborigama firing. She got it and I didn't, and she told me by text at 7am like it was news about the weather.

I know I should feel happy for her. She's my closest friend in the studio and she works harder than anyone I know. But all I can think about is how I spent two weeks on that application, reworking my portfolio statement, asking my old professor for a letter, calling my mother to say I might be in Japan for the spring so don't plan the family trip yet. All of that for a standard rejection email.

The worst part is I can't even be angry about it openly. Ji-hye keeps texting me details — the studio setup, the housing, the firing schedule — and I keep sending congratulations emojis and exclamation marks. My face hurts from performing enthusiasm on a screen. I closed the chat and went back to bed. It's not even noon.
entries.nudge_text: What were you hoping would be different after Arita?
entries.response_text: That I'd come back with something my family could point to. Studying under a master in Japan — that's something even my aunt would understand. It would have bought me time.

entries.initial_entry:
Went to the studio after moping around all morning. Didn't want to, but the pieces for the January show won't fire themselves. Started trimming the set of moon jars I threw last week. The proportions on the third one are actually close to what I've been chasing — that slight asymmetry halmeoni always said separates hand from machine.

Nobody cares about that distinction anymore, probably. The gallery visitors want to know the price and whether it fits on a shelf. But I held the jar up to the window light and the walls were even, the foot was clean, and the curve of the shoulder did exactly what I meant it to do. That matters to me even if it doesn't matter to anyone else.
entries.nudge_text: Who taught you to look at the shoulder curve like that?

entries.initial_entry:
Still at the studio. Eun-bi came by to pick up her tools and asked about the streetwear thing. I told her I'm not doing it and she looked at me like I'd said I don't like money. She said I should be practical, that these collaborations are how young artists build a name now. I told her I already have a name, it's just small.

She means well. But there's something about turning the craft into content that sits wrong with me. Halmeoni spent forty years in the same workshop selling to the same neighborhood. Nobody filmed her. Nobody asked her to put her glazes on handbags. She just made things that people used, and when she died the funeral was standing room only. That's the kind of career that makes sense to me.

End of the day. Checked my account and the last gallery payment was smaller than expected — they took a higher commission this quarter for "marketing costs." Didn't argue. Just transferred Umma's share and looked at what's left. The emergency cushion is thinner than I'd like going into winter.
entries.nudge_text: How thin is thin?
entries.response_text: Two months of rent and studio fees. Maybe two and a half if I skip materials. It's not nothing but it's not enough to stop the math running in my head at 2am.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday morning, studio's empty, just me and the space heater humming. Loading the kiln for the bisque fire — the moon jars plus a few smaller bowls I made for the lunar new year gifts. Every year I make a set for the family, rice bowls with a celadon glaze my grandmother used to mix by feel. I have her ratios written down in a notebook but I still adjust by eye the way she showed me, adding a pinch more calcium when the humidity is high.

Fired up the kiln, set the cone, sat on the floor with tea. The studio smells like dust and wet earth and something burning off the shelves. Nothing urgent. Nothing broken. The jars are in there doing what they'll do and I can't control it from out here. So I'm just sitting. The heater clicks off and on. That's the whole update.

My aunt called again about the civil service exam. She does this every Chuseok season, like clockwork — finds some cousin's daughter who just passed and drops it into conversation as though I asked. I said the same thing I always say. I'm fine. The studio is fine. Rent is covered.

She doesn't believe me. I know because she paused the way she does when she's holding something back, and then she said Umma worries. Which is just her way of saying she worries, but putting it on my mother so it lands harder. I didn't take the bait. Told her I had to go check the kiln.

There was nothing in the kiln. I just sat in the studio and threw a slab that I didn't need. The wheel was cold and the clay was too wet because I hadn't wedged it properly. It didn't matter. I wasn't making anything. I was just moving my hands so I wouldn't think about the conversation. Normal Wednesday.

I said I'd think about it but the whole walk home I kept hearing halmeoni's voice — that clay remembers what your hands were thinking when you shaped it. Felt cheap even considering it.

Woke up to a message from Ji-hye. She got into the residency in Arita. I know I should feel happy for her. She's my closest friend in the studio and she works harder than anyone I know. entries.response_text: That I'd come back with something my family could point to. Studying under a master in Japan — that's something even my aunt would understand. It would have bought me time.

But I held the jar up to the window light and the walls were even, the foot was clean, and the curve of the shoulder did exactly what I meant it to do. That matters to me even if it doesn't matter to anyone else.

entries.response_text: Two months of rent and studio fees. Maybe two and a half if I skip materials. It's not nothing but it's not enough to stop the math running in my head at 2am.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday morning, studio's empty, just me and the space heater humming. Loading the kiln for the bisque fire — the moon jars plus a few smaller bowls I made for the lunar new year gifts. Every year I make a set for the family, rice bowls with a celadon glaze my grandmother used to mix by feel. I have her ratios written down in a notebook but I still adjust by eye the way she showed me, adding a pinch more calcium when the humidity is high.

Fired up the kiln, set the cone, sat on the floor with tea. The studio smells like dust and wet earth and something burning off the shelves. Nothing urgent. Nothing broken. The jars are in there doing what they'll do and I can't control it from out here. So I'm just sitting. The heater clicks off and on. That's the whole update.

Had a meeting with Dr. Villanueva about the dissertation chapter on extractive policy in Peru. For once he actually engaged with the argument instead of just redirecting me toward his framework. He pushed back on my case selection, which was fair, but he did it by asking questions rather than just telling me to change it. That felt different. I left his office thinking clearly for the first time in weeks.

Walked to the mercado afterward and sat with a coffee going through my notes. The chapter outline is solid. The theoretical framing is mine -- not his, not borrowed from the lab's usual line. I've been circling this argument for months and it finally has shape. There's a version of this that could become a standalone article, which matters because I need publications that aren't just appendages to his projects.

I forget sometimes that I came here with a decade of professional experience. I wasn't some kid who wandered into a program. I chose this. I left a salary and a title because the work I was doing in Bogota hit a ceiling I couldn't break through without the credential. Days like today remind me the trade-off has a point.
entries.nudge_text: That chapter outline is yours -- what usually pulls you away from it?

entries.initial_entry:
Lab meeting today was three hours of performative agreement. Villanueva presented his new grant proposal and everyone nodded along like it was revelation. Half the methodology section is recycled from a paper we all know didn't replicate well. I sat there biting my tongue because the last time I raised a concern in front of the group he reframed it as me "not understanding the nuance yet." In front of the first-years. That was six months ago and I still feel it.

Marina, the other senior student, caught my eye during his presentation. She sees it too but she's smarter about it than I am -- she just stays quiet, publishes on the side, and keeps her head down. I can't do that. Something in me won't let me just sit there when the reasoning is sloppy. But speaking up costs more than staying silent, at least in this lab.

Came home and opened the Peru chapter again. The contrast is sharp. In Villanueva's lab I'm a student who should listen. In my own work I'm building something that could actually matter for how these countries manage their mining conflicts. The gap between those two versions of me is getting harder to hold.
entries.nudge_text: What does Marina's approach cost her that yours doesn't?

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from a former colleague in Bogota asking if I'd co-author a policy brief on lithium regulation for a think tank. Good money, my name on it, and the topic overlaps with my dissertation. I said yes immediately. Then spent the rest of the afternoon wondering if I should have checked with Villanueva first. The program technically doesn't require it, but there's an unspoken expectation that outside work gets cleared through him.

The thing that bothers me is the hesitation itself. I spent eight years writing policy documents without asking anyone's permission. Now I'm second-guessing a decision I'm fully qualified to make because someone with a title might disapprove. I don't know when I started treating his opinion as a gate I have to pass through, but I notice it more and more.

Same day, later. Called my mother in Cali to tell her about the policy brief. She was happy, asked how much it paid, then reminded me that my cousin Luis just got promoted to director at the ministry. She doesn't mean it as a comparison but it lands like one. Luis is 33, has a corner office, and never left the country. I'm 38, sharing a desk in a research lab.

But then she said something that settled me: "You always needed to understand why things work, not just that they work." She's right. That's why consulting felt hollow toward the end. I was writing recommendations I didn't fully believe in because the analysis was never deep enough. The PhD is slow and humbling but at least I'm doing the actual thinking now.
entries.nudge_text: Is the thinking deeper now, or just differently constrained?
entries.response_text: Different constraints, sure. But at least I chose these ones.

entries.initial_entry:
Villanueva told me today that he wants to be listed as co-author on the Peru chapter if I submit it to a journal. His reasoning is that the "intellectual environment of the lab" contributed to the work. I pushed back. Told him the chapter uses a different framework than anything the lab has produced, that the fieldwork contacts are mine from my Bogota years, that the data collection was funded by my own assistantship hours. He got quiet in that way he does -- not angry, just recalibrating.

He said we'd "revisit it later," which means he expects me to come around. I won't. This is the one piece of work in this entire program that is entirely mine. Every argument, every source, every late night rewriting the methodology section because his suggestions didn't fit. I'm not handing that over.

I know how this looks from outside. Grad student refusing to share credit with advisor. But this isn't about generosity. If I publish this with his name on it, it becomes part of his portfolio, his grant narrative, his legacy. My contribution disappears into his. I've watched it happen to Marina three times already. That's not going to be me.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if he doesn't let it go?
entries.response_text: I'll figure that out when it comes. Right now the chapter is mine.

entries.initial_entry:
Can't focus. Keep replaying the conversation with Villanueva. The way he leaned back in his chair. The pause before he said "revisit." I know that move because I used to do it in meetings in Bogota -- the deliberate silence that says I'm not done with this. He's not going to drop it. And the problem is he doesn't need to fight me directly. He just needs to delay things. Slow down feedback on my other chapters. Forget to sign off on conference funding. The machinery of a program has a hundred small levers.

Started the policy brief for the Bogota think tank. The writing came fast, like muscle memory. Two thousand words in three hours. It reminded me of what it feels like to work without someone standing over the output, deciding if it's good enough to carry their name.

Had lunch with Marina. She told me Villanueva asked her opinion about my "collaboration style." That's how he phrases it -- not that I'm difficult, just that my style needs adjustment. She told him I'm thorough. Diplomatic of her. We both know what he's doing. At least someone in that lab sees it clearly.
entries.nudge_text: What would it change if you and Marina pushed back together?

entries.initial_entry:
Later in the evening. Finished the first draft of the policy brief. Sent it to the think tank contact. Fourteen pages, clean argument, clear recommendations. Took me less than a week of evening work. The dissertation chapter, by contrast, has been in revision for four months because every piece of feedback from Villanueva sends me in a different direction.

Thinking about what Marina said at lunch, about his use of "collaboration style." It's a framing that puts the problem on me, on how I work, instead of on the fact that he's claiming credit for something he didn't build. Language like that is how people with institutional standing rewrite the narrative. I've seen it in government -- the person with the title gets to define the terms.

I need to decide soon whether to submit the Peru chapter independently or wait for whatever "revisit" means. Waiting gives him time to build his case or create friction. Submitting means the relationship might not recover. My mother would say "no pelees batallas que no puedes ganar." But I'm not sure this is a battle I can afford to lose either. The chapter is the best thing I've written. If it goes out with his name, the work is still good. But it won't be mine anymore.
entries.nudge_text: Which loss would sit with you longer?

entries.initial_entry: My sister-in-law made a comment at dinner about how she could never "just stay home." I smiled and said something about how it's not for everyone. Dan didn't say anything. Nobody said anything. I loaded the dishwasher after and just stood there gripping the counter.
entries.nudge_text: What were you gripping the counter about?
entries.response_text: I wanted to say something back. Something sharp. But that would've made dinner weird and Dan's mom was right there. So I just didn't. And now it's stuck in my chest.

entries.initial_entry:
I know people think staying home is easy or that I'm wasting my degree. I see it in the way my old coworkers talk to me at birthday parties, like they're being careful not to make me feel bad about my choices. But honestly, I don't think they could do what I do. Managing two kids, the house, Dan's travel schedule, appointments, groceries, meal prep, all of it on a rotating loop that never pauses.

And yeah, I chose this. Nobody forced me. When I was pregnant with Owen we sat down and looked at daycare costs and my salary and it didn't make sense. Dan makes more. That's just math. And I wanted to be home with them during these early years, I really did. I still do. But wanting something and never hearing anyone say "good job" or "thank you" are two different things.

My mom called this morning and asked if I'd thought about going back to work part-time. I told her I'm fine. She does this every few months, like she's checking if I've come to my senses. I know she means well. I just wish people would stop treating my life like a problem to solve.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'good job' look like coming from someone?
entries.response_text: I don't even know. Maybe Dan noticing that the house runs because I make it run. Or my mom saying she's proud of me without the follow-up question. But I think the real thing is I want to believe it myself. That this counts. That I'm not just filling time until the kids start school and I have to figure out who I am again.

entries.initial_entry: Playgroup today. One of the other moms asked my opinion on the new park hours and I just agreed with whatever she said. Drove home wondering why I do that. It wasn't even something worth disagreeing about but I noticed myself doing it anyway.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you'd disagreed?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing. She would've shrugged and moved on. But in my head it felt like it would've been this whole thing. Like I'd be the difficult one. I know that's ridiculous but it's automatic.

entries.initial_entry:
Dan's mom called this morning to ask if we're coming to Sunday dinner. I said yes. She asked if I could bring a salad and the rolls, same as always. I said sure. She mentioned that Dan's brother and his wife are bringing their new baby, so it'll be a full house. I said that sounds nice.

After I hung up I looked at my calendar. Sunday is the only day Dan is home this week. I had been thinking about asking him if we could skip dinner and just do something as a family, the four of us. Take the kids to the aquarium or even just stay home and order pizza. But I already said yes. And backing out would be a thing. His mom would call Dan, Dan would ask me what happened, and I'd have to explain that I just didn't want to go, which sounds selfish when there's a new baby to meet and everyone will be there.

So we'll go. I'll make the salad and the rolls. The kids will run around with their cousins and it'll be fine. It's always fine. I just noticed that I committed us before I even thought about what I wanted. Like my mouth answered before my brain caught up.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have wanted instead?
entries.response_text: Just a quiet day. Nothing dramatic. But it's fine, really. The kids love seeing their cousins.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, later. Dan called to check in and I mentioned Sunday dinner and he said "oh great, I was hoping we'd go." And something in me just deflated. Not because he did anything wrong. He didn't know I wanted something different because I never said it. I never say it.

I keep replaying the moment I picked up the phone with his mom. I could have said let me check with Dan first. That's a normal thing to say. But I didn't because saying that would've felt like making waves. And I can't stand making waves even when the water is perfectly calm and nobody would even notice.

I'm tired of being the person who says yes to everything and then sits alone in the kitchen feeling resentful about it.

Good class today. The students actually debated about the new coastal development project instead of just waiting for me to give them the "right" answer. These moments remind me why I chose this path.
entries.nudge_text: What made them engage differently today?
entries.response_text: I think they're just more comfortable with the format now. We've been doing discussions for a few weeks.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. Parent-teacher meetings are draining in a way that teaching never is. One parent spent twenty minutes explaining why her son shouldn't have to learn about "foreign problems" like ocean pollution. I tried to explain that the Arabian Sea is not foreign to Kerala, but I could see her eyes glazing over.

Then the principal pulled me aside afterward to suggest I "tone down the activism" in my lessons. Said some parents have been complaining. I nodded and said I'd think about it. Not sure what I'm supposed to think about, honestly.
entries.nudge_text: What did that nod cost you?
entries.response_text: Nothing. It's just politics. Part of working in any institution.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the weekend thinking about the principal's comment. I talked it over with Arjun and he made a good point - I can either fight every battle loudly and burn out, or I can be strategic. Pick what matters most and find ways to teach it that don't raise red flags.

I don't love the idea of softening my approach. But I also know that if I get pushed out, whoever replaces me will probably just teach straight from the textbook. At least I'm here. At least these kids are asking questions about their own coastline. That has to count for something.

Skipped the drive to Maman's yesterday. Told her the baby had a fever, which was half-true — she was warm, maybe teething. I just couldn't face the motorway again. Sat on the sofa with all three of them watching something stupid on the tablet and felt wrong the whole afternoon.
entries.nudge_text: What exactly felt wrong — skipping, or the excuse?
entries.response_text: Both, but differently. The excuse was just cowardice. The skipping though — it's like I broke something. We've gone every Sunday since before the kids were born. Papa sets the table for all of us on Saturday night. When I called to cancel I could hear him putting the plates away in the background and it made me want to throw up. One missed week and I'm already thinking, what if it becomes easy to miss the next one?

entries.initial_entry:
Clémence from the school pickup asked me why I bother driving out to my parents' every weekend when "nobody does that anymore." She said it like I was performing something. I told her it's just what we do, and she laughed and said her kids barely see their grandparents twice a year and everyone's fine. Good for her.

I don't know why it got under my skin so much. Maybe because part of me knows she has a point — the kids are exhausted by the time we get home, Hugo has football on Sunday mornings now and we keep pulling him out of it. My husband doesn't complain but I can feel his patience wearing thin when I insist we go even when it's pouring rain or one of them is sick.

But what's the alternative? We stop going and then what? My parents eat alone. My sister already moved to Bordeaux and barely calls. If I stop showing up, that table shrinks to two. I'm not going to be the one who lets that happen, even if Clémence thinks I'm being ridiculous about it.
entries.nudge_text: You're pulling Hugo out of football for this — does he get a say?

entries.initial_entry:
Called Maman tonight because Léa said something at dinner that nearly broke me. She said "Mamie's potatoes taste like your potatoes." She's five. She doesn't know what she's saying. But she connected it — the gratin, the way I do it with the nutmeg and the bay leaf exactly how Grand-mère taught Maman, how Maman taught me. Four generations of women and a potato dish and I'm crying over the phone like an idiot.

Maman laughed at me, which helped. She said Grand-mère would have said I was being dramatic, which is true. That woman didn't cry about anything. She just cooked and fed people and that was how she said everything she needed to say. I think I understand that now in a way I didn't when I was younger. The recipe box isn't just recipes. It's her entire way of being in the world, passed down in measurements and instructions.

I made Léa help me stir the béchamel tonight. She stood on the stool and held the wooden spoon with both hands. It was a small thing but it felt exactly right. Like I was doing what I'm supposed to be doing.

Sunday lunch went well this week. Papa made his cassoulet — the one he only does in winter because he says the beans need cold weather, which makes no sense but nobody argues. Hugo actually ate two bowls, which surprised everyone since he usually picks at things. My brother-in-law came without Sandrine, said she had a work thing in Bordeaux, and Maman pursed her lips but said nothing.

After lunch the kids played in the garden while the adults sat around the table with coffee. Papa told the story about his father's vineyard again — the one about the frost of '56 that killed everything and how they replanted by hand. Hugo was half-listening from the doorway. He's heard it before but I saw him lean in when Papa described his grandfather carrying the new vines on his back up the hill. These are the details that disappear if nobody passes them on.

Drove home in the dark. The baby slept the whole way. Hugo asked if we could come again next week and I said of course, that's what we do. It felt good to say it and mean it without any of the doubt from earlier this month.

Had coffee with my friend Nathalie this morning while the baby napped. She's going back to work full-time in March — her youngest starts maternelle — and she asked if I'd thought about it. I said not really, which is a lie. I think about it constantly. Before the children I worked at the préfecture, nothing exciting, but I was good at it. I had colleagues. I wore proper shoes.

But there's a voice in my head that sounds a lot like Maman, and Grand-mère before her, that says this is the work that matters. Being here when they come home. Cooking real food, not reheating things. Knowing their teachers' names, volunteering at the school. My mother did all of that and I never once came home to an empty house. I want that for my children too. But I also notice I've started counting the hours until bedtime some days, and that scares me.

Nathalie made it sound so simple — "You can do both." But can you? My mother didn't. Her mother didn't. And those families held together. I look at Clémence's kids eating canteen food five days a week and I think no, that's not what I want. But then I wonder if that's conviction or just fear of doing something different from what I know.
entries.nudge_text: What would actually change at home if you worked?

entries.initial_entry:
Father Michel announced the parish is cancelling the Mardi Gras procession this year. Not enough volunteers, he said. My parents marched in it when they were children. I brought Hugo for the first time two years ago and he still talks about the paper lanterns.

I told Maman on the phone and she sighed and said "that's how it starts." She meant it starts with one thing cancelled, then another, then there's nothing left. I signed up to help organise it for next year, which means more time I don't have. But someone has to, or it just disappears.
entries.nudge_text: You said you're already counting hours — where does this time come from?
entries.response_text: From sleep, probably. Or from the one evening a week I watch something for myself after the kids are down. I know that's not sustainable. But if I don't volunteer, who does? The procession just dies. I'd rather be tired than explain to Hugo why the lanterns aren't happening anymore.

entries.initial_entry: Finished the last piece for the Jabal Amman gallery order today. Pulled each one out of the kiln myself, wrapped them in tissue, lined them up on the shelf. Four months of work sitting there looking exactly the way I wanted them to look.
entries.nudge_text: What hit you when you saw them all lined up?
entries.response_text: Relief, honestly. That this batch came out right and the gallery check will clear next week.

entries.initial_entry:
My cousin Fadi was in town from Dubai and we had dinner at my uncle's place. He kept going on about his new project management role, the relocation package, the company car. My aunt turned to me at one point and said, "You see, Nadia, that is what happens when you follow a plan." I smiled and ate my kibbeh and did not say anything.

The thing is, I do have a plan. I have the gallery contract through October, the workshop income, the private commissions spreadsheet. It just does not look like a plan to people who need a company name on it. I left dinner early and drove home with the windows down and felt fine about it. I did.
entries.nudge_text: Did you actually feel fine?
entries.response_text: No. I kept replaying my aunt's voice the whole drive home. It is not that she is wrong about Fadi doing well — he is. It is that she cannot see what I have built because it does not come with a business card.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent most of the morning at the studio doing inventory. Ran the numbers on clay and glaze stock for the next quarter and placed an order with the Turkish supplier. The exchange rate is worse this month, so I bought less than I wanted to. I will need to reorder before October if the gallery pieces go ahead.

Afternoon was a walk-in workshop — six women from a corporate team-building group. They made pinch pots and seemed to enjoy it. Easy money, low creative effort on my part. I have three more of these booked through August, which helps with the monthly numbers.

Back again. Could not stop thinking about what I wrote earlier, about the workshops. I want to be clear — I am not ungrateful. The workshop income is what lets me say no to design freelance gigs I do not want. It is a trade I chose and I would choose it again.

It is just that sometimes the choosing gets heavy. Every month is a little negotiation between what I want to make and what I need to sell. I keep both ledgers and they rarely agree. But they do not have to agree. They just have to coexist.

Had a call with Reem from the Wekalat gallery in Beirut. She wants to include two of my pieces in a group show in November. No artist fee, just exposure and potential sales. I said I would think about it, but what I was really doing was calculating shipping costs from Amman to Beirut in my head while she talked.

I decided yes. The pieces she wants are ones I made for myself, not on commission. Letting them go to a show where someone might actually connect with them — that matters more to me than the logistics headache. I will figure out the shipping.
entries.nudge_text: Which pieces did she pick?

entries.initial_entry: Signed a six-month lease renewal on the studio today. Landlord kept it at the same rate, which almost never happens here. Called my mother afterward and she said, "Good, at least something is fixed."
entries.nudge_text: What did she mean by 'at least'?

entries.initial_entry:
One of the corporate workshop pots cracked in the kiln. Not a client piece, just a leftover someone forgot. But I spent twenty minutes staring at it anyway because the glaze had done something unexpected on the broken edge — this deep copper-green that I have been trying to get for months on my own work and it just showed up on a throwaway pot by accident.

I cannot explain why that made me so frustrated. All the careful testing, the temperature logs, the glaze ratios written out in notebooks. And the thing I have been chasing appears on a piece that no one will ever claim. I scraped the glaze residue off and saved it in a jar. I will try to figure out what happened. That is all I can do.

Shipped the two pieces for the Beirut show today. Wrapped them myself in three layers — bubble wrap, then foam, then a wooden crate I built from scrap lumber in the back of the studio. The shipping guy looked at me like I was overdoing it, but those vessels took me five weeks each and I am not trusting them to someone else's packing job.

After they left, the studio felt different. Emptier, obviously, but also lighter. I have been holding onto those pieces since April, moving them from shelf to shelf, never quite sure what they were for. Now they have a destination and I can stop hovering. Reem sent a photo of the gallery space where they will be placed — a corner with natural light from a high window. They will look right there.

Spent the rest of the afternoon mixing a new glaze batch based on the copper-green residue from last week. I adjusted the iron oxide ratio and ran a small test tile. Will not know until tomorrow. The kiln schedule is full this week with workshop pieces, so I wedged my test into the last corner of the bottom shelf where the temperature runs slightly cooler. We will see.

The test tile came out close. Not exact — the copper-green is more muted than the accident, and it does not have that sharp metallic edge I saw on the broken pot. But it is in the neighborhood. I mixed a second batch with slightly more copper carbonate and will fire again Thursday.

My mother called this morning. She sounded tired. She asked about money, which she does when she is worried about her own but does not want to say so. I told her the gallery payment came through and that the workshop bookings are steady through September. She seemed relieved, or at least she stopped asking follow-up questions. I wired her some money through CliQ after we hung up — not a lot, but enough that she will not have to ask again for a while.

Sometimes I think the reason I track every fils so carefully is not really about the studio at all. It is about being able to pick up the phone and say "everything is fine" and have it be true. Or close enough to true. If my numbers are solid, I can carry more than just my own weight, and that is the part that lets me sleep at night.
entries.nudge_text: When did you start carrying that weight?
entries.response_text: When my father died and the bills started arriving at her door. I was twenty-six. I remember sitting at the kitchen table in Beirut doing the math and thinking, I need to always be able to do this math, no matter what.

entries.initial_entry: Workshop cancelled last minute — the group rescheduled to next month. That is 400 dinars I was counting on for September's clay order. I sat in the studio for an hour doing nothing, just staring at the schedule board and rearranging numbers in my head.
entries.nudge_text: What are you going to do?
entries.response_text: I will figure it out. I always do.

entries.initial_entry:
September already. Pulled up the spreadsheet this morning and went through everything — studio rent, clay orders, kiln fuel, the shipping costs from the Beirut pieces. After the cancelled workshop, the buffer is thinner than I like. Not dangerous, but I can feel the margin. I have two options: pick up a small freelance design job to bridge the gap, or book an extra workshop weekend and push the clay order back two weeks.

I chose the workshop. Spent an hour writing a post for the studio's page, offering a weekend intensive for beginners. It is more of the same work I was just complaining about, but at least it is mine — my studio, my schedule, my terms. The freelance route would have paid better but I would have spent a week taking direction from someone else's brief, and I left that behind for a reason.

Reem messaged that the Beirut show opens next Friday. She said there has been some early interest in the vessels. I read her message three times, then put the phone down and went back to wedging clay. I do not want to get ahead of myself. If they sell, good — it changes the math. If they do not, the math still works, just tighter. Either way, I keep making the work.

Started doing the math again tonight. If I keep the delivery app running five days a week and pick up at least two handyman jobs on the weekends, I can cover everything with about twelve thousand pesos left over. That leftover is supposed to be for the motorcycle insurance renewal in May. I wrote it on a sticky note and put it on the fridge next to the old one that says "DO NOT touch savings."

Funny thing is, I spent Saturday at the parrilla with Cacho and Pocho like always, and the bill came out heavier than usual because we ordered that tira de asado. I didn't blink. Six hours of good food and good talk, what am I going to do, skip it? Some weeks the numbers work, some weeks they don't. But I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should.

My sister Graciela keeps calling to tell me I should go back to a regular job. She doesn't get it. I'm not going to sit in some warehouse again answering to a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about the idea of going back that feels impossible?
entries.response_text: It's not impossible, I just don't want to. There's a difference. Look, I know what I'm doing. I've been managing fine for five years this way. Graciela worries because she's Graciela. She married an accountant, everything in her life has a line item. Not everyone lives like that.

entries.initial_entry:
Bad week. The delivery app lowered the per-trip rate again, no warning, no explanation. Just opened the app Monday morning and the numbers were different. I did the same twelve deliveries I always do on a weekday and came home with almost twenty percent less. Twenty percent. That's not nothing. That's a week of groceries, that's Negrita's vet visit I've been putting off.

The handyman side is slower too because people are tightening up. I had three jobs lined up this week and two of them canceled last minute — one lady said she'd "wait until next month" to fix her bathroom faucet and the other one just stopped responding. So now I'm sitting here Thursday night with the calculator app open, moving numbers around like I'm playing some kind of sad puzzle game, trying to figure out where the motorcycle payment comes from.

The thing that really gets to me is the feeling. It's not just about the money, it's the not knowing. Every month is a guess. Every week I wake up and I don't know what I'm going to make. I used to think that was the trade-off for not having a boss breathing down my neck. Lately I'm not so sure the trade-off is worth it. But what am I supposed to do, crawl back and prove Graciela right? No. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out.
entries.nudge_text: You said you're not sure the trade-off is worth it anymore. What would it look like if you really sat with that thought?

entries.initial_entry: Got a call from Don Alfredo across the street — he needs his whole kitchen faucet replaced plus some tile work. That's a good-sized job, probably two days of work. Felt like I could breathe again for the first time all week.

Motorcycle started making a noise yesterday. A grinding thing when I brake. I took it to Raul's shop and he looked at it and said the brake pads are gone and the disc is warped, it needs replacing. He quoted me a number that wiped out everything I'd saved from the Alfredo job. Every peso.

I stood there in Raul's shop and felt my stomach just drop. Because it's not optional — the motorcycle is how I make deliveries. Without it I'm on foot or taking the subte, which means fewer trips, which means less money, which means next month's rent gets tight. It's like this chain where one thing breaks and everything behind it starts wobbling. I told Raul to do it, because what choice do I have. But I walked home and I couldn't even eat dinner.
entries.nudge_text: When you walked home, what was going through your head?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I was thinking about my old man. He worked at the same factory for thirty-two years. Hated it, but every month the check came in. He never had to wonder if the floor was going to fall out from under him. I used to think that was a sad way to live. Now I'm not so sure. He always had a little envelope in the dresser drawer with cash for emergencies. I don't have that envelope. I don't have anything between me and the next bad surprise. And that thought — that I'm one broken motorcycle or one bad month away from not being able to pay rent — that sat on my chest the whole walk home.

entries.initial_entry: Couldn't sleep. Opened the app at 1am to see if there were any late-night delivery runs. There were two. I took them both, rode the bicycle I borrowed from my neighbor. Came home at 3am with barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas.

Picked up the motorcycle from Raul this morning. It rides clean now, brakes feel solid. That's something. But I'm running on maybe four hours of sleep from the last few days and I can feel it in my eyes, in the way I'm slow to react at intersections. Almost clipped a bus mirror on Corrientes.

Saturday is coming and Cacho already texted about the parrilla. Part of me wants to skip it this week, save the money. But another part of me — the louder part — says that's the one thing that keeps me sane. You take away the asado with friends and what do I have left? A cat, a calculator, and a leaking faucet in my own bathroom that I haven't even bothered to fix.
entries.nudge_text: When you say the parrilla is the one thing that keeps you sane — what does it give you that nothing else does?
entries.response_text: It's the only time during the week where I'm not counting anything. Not counting deliveries, not counting pesos, not counting hours. I sit down, the meat comes, somebody pours the wine, and for a few hours I'm just Roberto having a good time with his friends. Nobody's asking me to fix something or deliver something or be somewhere in fifteen minutes. It's the closest thing I have to feeling like my life is actually mine.

entries.initial_entry: Don Alfredo recommended me to his daughter for some painting work at her new apartment. Three rooms, good rate, she even offered to pay half upfront. I bought Negrita the fancy wet food she likes to celebrate.

Graciela came over unannounced today while I was between deliveries. She brought empanadas, which was nice, except then she sat at my kitchen table and started doing that thing she does where she looks around the apartment cataloguing everything that's wrong. The peeling paint above the window, the chair with the broken leg, the fact that I still don't have a proper desk and eat on the couch.

Then she said — and I quote — "Roberto, you're almost fifty, you can't live like a college student." And I just stood there holding an empanada feeling like I was fourteen again getting lectured. She doesn't see that I actually like my life most of the time. I like picking my own hours. I like riding through Palermo on a Tuesday morning with the sun out and nobody telling me where to be. But she looks at this apartment and she sees failure. And the worst part is that sometimes, when she leaves, I start seeing it through her eyes too.
entries.nudge_text: You said sometimes you see it through her eyes. What do you see when that happens?
entries.response_text: A guy who's been treading water for five years and calling it swimming. Someone who left a stable situation because of pride and never figured out the next step. I see the broken chair and I think, why haven't I fixed that? I'm literally a handyman. And the answer is because when I come home I don't want to fix anything else, I just want to sit down and not move. And that scares me a little because my old man never left things broken. He always had the house perfect. I can't even keep up with my own apartment and I live alone with a cat.

entries.initial_entry:
Painting job at Don Alfredo's daughter's place is done. She was happy with it, paid the balance, even tipped me a little extra. So the money situation is stable for the moment. Rent is covered, motorcycle payment is covered, Raul is paid. There's a small cushion again, not much, but it's there.

Went to the parrilla Saturday like always. Pocho brought his nephew, a kid of about twenty-five who does the same delivery app I do but also drives for a rideshare service on the side. He was telling me about his "strategy" — how he works the surge hours, how he tracks which neighborhoods tip best, how he's saving for a down payment on his own place. The whole table was impressed. I just sat there eating my chorizo and feeling old.

It's not jealousy exactly. The kid has energy and optimism and a plan, and good for him. But I was that kid once. I had the energy to work sixteen-hour days and still go dancing with my ex-wife on Saturday night. Now I do eight hours of deliveries and my back locks up. I eat the asado and drink the wine and it's still good, it's always good. But there's this thin layer underneath the enjoyment now, like a film on old coffee. Like I'm enjoying it because I know this might not last. Like I should be saving what I'm spending here but I can't bring myself to stop because this is the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival.
entries.nudge_text: You described a thin layer underneath the enjoyment. When did you first start noticing it?

entries.initial_entry:
Fixed the chair today. The one Graciela noticed. It took me twenty minutes. I don't know why I left it broken for so long. I also patched the peeling paint above the window. Not because she said anything but because once I started with the chair I just kept going. Negrita watched me from the couch like she was supervising.

After I finished I sat in the chair and it felt solid. Stupid thing to feel good about but I did. I texted Cacho and told him I'd bring a bottle of the good Malbec tomorrow — the Catena, not the box wine we usually get. Sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason. I've got the painting money still in my pocket. Next week is uncertain, it always is. But right now the chair is fixed, the wall looks clean, and tomorrow I'll eat well with people I like. That's not nothing.

Long week. Delivered the payment gateway migration three days early and nobody said a word about it. Just got folded into the next sprint like it was expected. Meanwhile Tomás presented his dashboard prototype at the all-hands and got a round of applause. A prototype. Not even in production.

I'm not bitter about it. That's just how things work. You do the plumbing, someone else gets to show off the faucet. I knew that when I chose backend over product years ago.

Camila made tamales tonight. Sat on the balcony eating them while the girls did homework inside. The jalapeños she planted last month are actually coming up. Small green shoots pushing through the soil. That's the thing about growing something—it doesn't need anyone to clap for it. It just grows.

Monday standup, same routine. Gave my update, kept it short. Marcos asked if anyone had concerns about the new API rate-limiting approach and I almost said something. Almost. Because I think the threshold they picked is going to choke under real load. I ran the numbers over the weekend, actually. But Diego already endorsed the approach and Diego is the architect, so.

Funny how that works. You have the data, you have the experience, but you also have a sense of when speaking up will just make things awkward. Diego doesn't take pushback well. Everyone knows it. Nobody says it.

Went to the gym after work. Deadlifts. There's something honest about weight on a bar. Either you lift it or you don't. No politics in iron.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you'd spoken up?
entries.response_text: Honestly, probably nothing dramatic. Diego would have nodded, said "good point, let's take it offline," and then nothing would change. That's the pattern. I've seen it enough times. It's not worth the energy to test it again. Better to document my concerns in the ticket comments so there's a paper trail when it breaks. That way I'm covered without making it a thing.

entries.initial_entry:
Performance review cycle opened today. I hate this time of year. You have to write a self-assessment that's basically a sales pitch for yourself, and I've never been good at selling. Camila says I undersell. My manager Fernando says I need to "make my impact more visible." Everyone has advice.

The thing is, I know what I've done. Seventeen production releases this year. Zero rollbacks. Mentored two junior engineers who both got promoted. Reduced our CI pipeline time by forty percent. But writing it all out in first person with action verbs and metrics feels like performing. "I spearheaded." "I drove." Those aren't words real people use.

My colleague Ana has the opposite problem—she writes these self-assessments that read like award acceptance speeches. And it works for her. She got promoted last year. I've been at the same level for four years. So maybe the system rewards the performance, not the work.

I'll write the self-assessment this weekend. I'll use the strong verbs. I'll quantify everything. It just feels like wearing a costume that doesn't fit. My papá was a carpenter in Medellín. He didn't have to write paragraphs about how well he built a cabinet. The cabinet spoke for itself.
entries.nudge_text: You say the cabinet speaks for itself—but does it?

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today, which I never do. But I sat down to draft the self-assessment after dinner and something shifted.

Started listing my projects and realized I've been carrying more than I thought. The payment gateway wasn't just a migration—it was six months of reverse-engineering undocumented legacy code while keeping the live system running. The mentoring wasn't casual either. I had weekly one-on-ones with Daniela and Esteban, reviewed their PRs line by line, talked them through architectural decisions. That's not a line item. That's shaping how two engineers think.

Maybe the problem isn't that I can't write about my work. Maybe it's that where I grew up, talking about yourself like that was considered feo—ugly, tasteless. You let your elders praise you. You didn't praise yourself. My mother would have given me a look if I'd walked around listing my accomplishments.

But I'm not in Medellín anymore. And the rules here are different. Not better or worse, just different. Fernando isn't going to guess what I've done. He has thirty direct reports. If I don't tell him, he genuinely won't know.

I wrote four paragraphs. Strong verbs and all. Read it back and it sounded like someone else, but maybe that's okay. Maybe learning a new professional language isn't the same as losing the old one.

Fernando scheduled a "career path conversation" for Monday. I don't know what that means. Could be good—could be the director track opening up. Could also be them telling me to lateral into some platform team nobody wants to join. I've seen both versions. People keep saying I should be hopeful but I don't want to get set up for another no.

Had the meeting with Fernando. It was... better than expected. He said the director position is opening again in Q2, and this time he's putting me forward as the internal candidate. Not a guarantee, but a real recommendation. He said my self-assessment was "the strongest you've written" and that the team's reliability metrics speak for themselves.

I thanked him. Shook his hand. Walked back to my desk and just sat there for a while.

The strange part is I expected to feel more. Elated, relieved, something big. Instead it was quiet. Like I'd been holding my breath for a long time and finally let it out, but the exhale was so slow I barely noticed. Maybe that's what it feels like when something you've wanted actually moves closer—not fireworks, just a slight loosening in the chest.

Camila was happy when I told her. She said "finally they're seeing what I've always seen." I almost corrected her—they're not seeing me, they're seeing a version of me that learned to write in their language. But I didn't say that because it would've turned a good moment into a philosophy seminar. And she deserved to just be happy for me.

Took the girls out for helado to celebrate. Lucía got three scoops. Sometimes that's enough.
entries.nudge_text: The version they're seeing—is that still you?
entries.response_text:
I've been thinking about that since I wrote it. And honestly... yes. I think it is. It's not the whole me. It's not the me who eats tamales on the balcony or argues about fútbol with my brother-in-law. But it's not fake either. It's the part of me that learned to operate in a system that wasn't built for people like me. A Colombian who came here with an accent and a degree nobody recognized at first.

Maybe I resented it for a long time because it felt like the system was asking me to become someone else. But writing that self-assessment—I wasn't lying. I was translating. And translation isn't betrayal. My papá spoke three regional dialects. He didn't lose himself switching between them.

entries.initial_entry:
Submitted my director application packet today. Fernando reviewed it yesterday and said it was solid. Included the self-assessment, three peer endorsements, and a one-page proposal for how I'd restructure the backend org.

The proposal was the hardest part. I had to suggest changes to the current structure, which means implicitly criticizing how things are done now. I kept it diplomatic—framed everything as "building on existing strengths" rather than pointing out problems. Ana said I should be bolder. Maybe she's right. But I know who's on the review panel, and bold doesn't play well with everyone.

Coached volleyball practice after work. The girls are getting better at their rotations. They're learning to trust the system—each person covers their zone, and the whole court gets covered. There's a lesson there that I keep noticing but I'm not going to spell it out.
entries.nudge_text: You keep it diplomatic—what are you protecting?

entries.initial_entry: Interview panel was today. Four people, ninety minutes. I said what I prepared. Didn't improvise, didn't overshoot. Answered the hard question about team conflict honestly—told them I default to listening first. They nodded. Whether that's enough, I'll know in two weeks. Going to bed early. Camila left soup on the stove.
