Camila left soup on the stove.

Finally told Andrés he can't keep showing up twenty minutes late to install pieces. I've been letting it slide for weeks because he's good with his hands and the clients like him, but this morning he rolled in while a collector was already waiting and I had to cover for him, again. So I pulled him aside after she left and laid it out: one more time and I'm finding someone else.

He looked genuinely startled, which tells me nobody's ever drawn that line with him. Good. I can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table. Felt solid doing it, too. Not angry, just clear. The gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set.
entries.nudge_text: Did the collector say anything about the wait?
entries.response_text: She didn't mention it directly, but she kept checking her phone while I was making small talk. Ended up buying the smaller piece instead of the one she'd asked about originally. Could be coincidence, could be she lost patience. Either way, that's exactly why I can't let things slide.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day finishing the commission for the Restrepo family. Got the last panel mounted and stepped back to look at the whole triptych together. It works. The proportions are right, the color transitions hold. I stood there for a minute just looking at it and felt something click into place, that rare moment when what I made matches what I saw in my head.

Called my mom after. She asked about the gallery rent, whether I'd paid on time. I had, obviously. I always do. She worries, but she doesn't need to — I've had the next three months covered since January. Told her the commission check clears Friday. She sounded relieved, which is enough for now.
entries.nudge_text: What was that click like?

entries.initial_entry: Met with a potential co-investor for the expansion into the second floor. He kept talking over me, pitching his own vision for the layout. I smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back, and now I'm sitting here irritated at myself.

Walked through the Saturday market in Provenza this morning, not to buy anything but just to look at how other people display their work. The ceramicist on the corner has started using linen backdrops and better lighting — she's learning. A year ago her booth looked like a garage sale. Now people stop.

I do this thing where I watch who commands a space and who just fills it. The ceramicist commands hers now. It reminded me why I fought so hard to get the gallery out of the shared lease arrangement. When I had to negotiate every wall placement with Camilo, nothing felt fully mine. Now every corner of that space reflects a decision I made, and collectors can feel it. That matters more than people think.

Lazy Sunday, which almost never happens. Woke up late, made arepas with the leftover hogao from Friday. Sat on the balcony eating and watching the street below. The neighbor's kid was kicking a ball against the wall again, steady thunk-thunk-thunk rhythm that's almost calming when you're not trying to concentrate.

Started going through my supply invoices because even on a day off I can't leave the numbers alone. Paints are up twelve percent from last year which is absurd, and I'm running through the cadmium yellow faster than usual because of the Restrepo palette. Need to either find a new supplier or adjust my pricing. Probably both. I've been meaning to rework the pricing sheet anyway — the last revision was August and costs have shifted.

Nothing urgent. The accounts are fine. I just like knowing exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out. My mother would say I got that from my father, who used to keep a paper ledger for his tire shop even after they had a computer. Different scale, same instinct.

Spent the afternoon finalizing the March exhibition lineup and it felt like orchestrating something, which is the part of this work I love most. Three emerging artists, each one I handpicked. Two of them came to me through referrals from collectors I've built relationships with over years. The third found me through Instagram, sent a portfolio that was raw but had something. I'm taking a risk on her, but a calculated one — I've already soft-committed two buyers who tend to go for that aesthetic.

The thing is, when I curate a show, I'm not just hanging art. I'm deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't. That responsibility sits well with me. I spent years being the one hoping someone would give me a shot. Now I'm on the other side. I've earned that.

Had a good call with Daniela, my accountant, beforehand. March projections are strong, the reserve fund is untouched, insurance renewal is handled. When the financial side is solid, I can actually think about the art. When it's shaky, everything else feels shaky too. I learned that the hard way during the first year after the split with Camilo, when I was burning through savings and couldn't focus on a single canvas.
entries.nudge_text: What was the something in her portfolio?
entries.response_text: Honestly, she reminded me of myself ten years ago. Same raw energy, same refusal to make the work palatable for easy sales. I almost didn't reach out because I don't usually go for sentimental reasons. But then I looked at her pieces again and they're genuinely good. I just didn't want to admit that the recognition was part of why I noticed her.

entries.initial_entry: The co-investor sent a revised proposal with his name listed as "co-director" of the new floor. I didn't agree to that. I wrote back saying we need to clarify terms before anything moves forward, but my hands were shaking when I hit send. Not from fear — from the idea that someone thinks they can just slide into a title I haven't offered.
entries.nudge_text: What were the hands shaking from, then?
entries.response_text: Just adrenaline. It's fine. The email's sent, the boundary is drawn. Moving on.

entries.initial_entry: March show opens Friday. All three artists delivered on time. Walked through the hung pieces this evening and everything is exactly where I placed it. Good feeling, knowing the room is set and the invitations went out to the right people.

Opening night went better than I planned. Sold four pieces in the first two hours, including one from the new artist — the one I took a chance on. A collector from Bogotá asked for my card and said he'd been hearing my name. That sentence alone made the whole month worth it. This is what building a reputation looks like, one show at a time, one handshake at a time.

Called my mom after closing up. She asked how much I made and I told her, which I don't usually do, but I wanted her to hear it. Wanted her to know the gallery isn't some reckless bet anymore. She said "qué bueno, mija" in that tone that means she's genuinely proud but won't say it bigger than that. That's enough. The numbers are good, the reserve is intact, and people are starting to know the name Villalobos in this city's art scene.

Someone on an art forum called my curation "commercially safe." I almost replied but closed the tab instead. My gallery, my choices, my track record. If making a living from art while keeping my lights on and my artists paid is "safe," fine. I'll take it.
entries.nudge_text: What were you going to type?
entries.response_text: Something about how I left a stable partnership to build this alone and nobody who's done that gets to be called safe. But then I realized I'd be arguing with a stranger who's never mounted a show or signed a lease. Not worth it. I know what I've built.

entries.initial_entry: The co-investor agreed to drop the co-director title but now wants approval rights on artist selection for the second floor. Told him I'd think about it. I won't give that up, but I also can't fund the expansion alone and the bank terms are worse. Feels like I'm slowly trading control for capital and I'm not sure where the line is.
entries.nudge_text: Where would the line be?
entries.response_text: I don't know yet. That's the whole problem. I'll figure it out before the next meeting.

entries.initial_entry:
Picked up Lotte's kids from school again because she had a bad flare. Mats had a meltdown about his shoelaces in the car park and I spent ten minutes crouched on the tarmac retying them while Eva sat in the back seat reading like nothing was happening. That girl is ice. Bought them both stroopwafels from the bakker on Voorstraat and dropped them home. Lotte was on the sofa looking grey. I made her tea and put a load of washing on before she could say she'd do it herself.

Then I had to sprint across town for a delivery window I'd nearly forgotten about. Some law firm on the Maliebaan wanted documents by five and I made it with three minutes to spare, sweating through my jacket. The receptionist didn't look up. Forty minutes of cycling for eight euros fifty. That's the maths of it.

Got home, fed Poes, sat down, and realised I hadn't eaten since a cheese sandwich at eleven. My knee is doing that clicking thing again. I should stretch more. I should do a lot of things. The fern on the windowsill is browning at the tips, which probably means I've been overwatering. Even the plants aren't getting my best these days. Not complaining, just noticing how the day went.
entries.nudge_text: What would complaining sound like?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday and I thought I'd have the morning to myself but Lotte rang at half seven because the downstairs tap was leaking and Pieter -- her ex -- isn't answering his phone as usual. Cycled over with a wrench and some thread tape. The washer was gone, ten-minute fix, but then she started crying at the kitchen table and I didn't know what to do so I just sat there. She said she's tired of asking me for things. That she feels like she's ruining my weekends. I told her that's stupid, she's not, but the word "stupid" came out too fast and she flinched.

I'm sitting on her front step now writing this. The kids are inside watching cartoons. I can hear Mats laughing. I want to go back in and say something better but I don't know what that would be. Sometimes being there isn't enough and you can't fix it with a wrench.

Later. Went back inside and Lotte had made coffee, which felt like her way of saying it was fine. We didn't talk about it. Played a board game with the kids -- Mats cheats spectacularly and Eva keeps a running tally of his crimes in a little notebook, which is both hilarious and slightly concerning. Normal afternoon. Good one, actually.

On the ride home I kept replaying the kitchen thing. She said "I feel like I'm ruining your weekends" and the truth is some weekends do feel like they disappear into errands for her. But I can't say that because it would confirm exactly what she's afraid of. So I carry it. It's not heavy most days. Today it's a bit heavy. Poes was waiting by the door when I got in, which helps more than a cat should.

Had a full delivery day for once. Eight runs between nine and four, mostly parcels for that organic food box service. My legs are cooked but the earnings are decent -- sixty-two euros before the platform cut. One of the customers, an older woman near Griftpark, invited me in for coffee. I said no because I was behind schedule, then felt guilty about it for the next two hours. She looked lonely. I don't know why that stuck.

My mate Jeroen called and asked if I wanted to come to his birthday drinks on Friday. I said yes but I'm already calculating whether Lotte will need me with the kids that evening. Haven't asked her yet. I should just go. Jeroen always shows up for me. I just need to show up back. That should be simple but somehow it never is.
entries.nudge_text: What makes showing up for Jeroen feel complicated?
entries.response_text: It's not complicated. I'll go. Just need to sort out the logistics first.

entries.initial_entry:
Lotte had a hospital appointment today. New neurologist, younger guy, very direct. He told her the progression is slow but real and that she should "plan her support network" for the next few years. She looked at me when he said that. Not at Pieter, who wasn't there. Not at our parents, who live in Friesland and visit twice a year. At me.

I sat in the corridor afterwards while she got dressed and I stared at a poster about hand hygiene and thought about what "the next few years" means. More school runs. More leaking taps. More weekends. That sounds terrible written down. I don't mean it that way. I mean I'm trying to understand the shape of what I'm signing up for, and whether it's something I'm choosing or something that's choosing me.

Rode home the long way through Amelisweerd. The trees are turning and the light through the leaves was that specific October gold that makes you slow down even if you don't want to. Stopped on the bridge over the Kromme Rijn for a few minutes. A heron was standing in the shallows looking patient. I don't feel patient. I feel like I'm holding something that keeps getting heavier and everyone assumes I'm fine because I keep showing up with the wrench.
entries.nudge_text: You keep showing up. What would it look like to put the wrench down?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Lotte has nobody else who shows up consistently. If I step back even a little, the gap isn't filled by someone else, it just stays empty. So putting it down doesn't feel like an option. It feels like leaving a hole.

entries.initial_entry: Went to Jeroen's birthday. Stayed three hours. Drank too many Jupilers, talked rubbish, laughed until my stomach hurt. He gave me a hug when I left and said he's glad I came. Lotte had texted to say the kids were fine at their dad's for once. I cycled home through the quiet streets feeling lighter than I have all week. Small thing. Good thing.

Mum called from Leeuwarden. Wanted to "check in" which always means she has something to say. She asked how Lotte was doing and I gave her the update from the neurologist. Long pause. Then she said maybe I'm "doing too much" and that it's "not sustainable." She suggested Lotte look into professional home care. I told her that costs money Lotte doesn't have. She said maybe I should focus on my own life, find a partner, think about what I want.

Right. What I want. As if I haven't thought about that. As if I'm some martyr running around Utrecht with a spanner because I haven't considered the alternative. I do think about it. I think about it on every solo ride home. But thinking about what I want doesn't change what Lotte needs, and nobody else is stepping in, least of all Mum who calls once a fortnight from three hours away.

I know she means well. I do. But there's something in the way she says it -- "focus on your own life" -- as if helping Lotte is separate from my life. It IS my life. She's my sister. Her kids call me Oom Sansen because Mats couldn't say Sander when he was two and it stuck. I'm not doing this out of obligation. I'm doing it because I'd rather be tired than be the kind of person who steps away when things get hard. That's not something I need advice about from someone who sends a card on birthdays.
entries.nudge_text: What kind of tired are we talking about?
entries.response_text: The kind where you wake up already behind. Not sleepy tired. More like the tank is always half-empty and everything costs a bit more than it should. I manage. But some mornings I sit on the edge of the bed for a while before I stand up.

entries.initial_entry: App keeps glitching today, lost two rides because of it. My head's all over the place.
entries.nudge_text: Sounds like a frustrating day. What's weighing on you most right now?

entries.initial_entry: Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay this month since Dad's physical therapy bills are piling up. Of course I said yes but then I looked at my account and it's like, where is this money supposed to come from? I'll figure it out, I always do.
entries.nudge_text: You said yes right away, then checked your account. What was going through your mind in that moment between?

entries.initial_entry:
Twelve-hour day yesterday and another one today. Holiday surge is good for earnings but my back is killing me from sitting in the car so long. At least I was able to drop off the pharmacy money to Mom on my way between zones. She tried to give me back half of it and I told her no, just keep it in case something else comes up.

Had a passenger tonight who was heading to the airport for some tropical vacation and I sat there thinking about how I haven't taken more than a day off in probably six months. Not complaining really, it is what it is. Just tired.
entries.nudge_text: Six months without a real break. If you could take a day off tomorrow, what would you actually do with it?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I'd probably sleep until noon. Maybe drive out to the mountains just to sit somewhere quiet without my phone buzzing. But then I'd probably spend the whole time calculating how much money I'm losing by not working, so maybe that defeats the purpose.

entries.initial_entry:
Sister texted asking if I'm coming to Christmas dinner at her place this year. I want to say yes but I'm looking at the schedule and Christmas Eve and Christmas Day are usually the best earning days because everyone else takes off. Last year I worked both and just stopped by Mom and Dad's for an hour between rides.

Part of me thinks I should just take the hit and be there for the whole dinner. Dad's been doing better but who knows how many of these we get, you know? But then another part of me is like, one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment. I don't know. I'll probably end up doing what I always do which is work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present anyway.
App keeps glitching today, lost two rides because of it. My head's all over the place.
entries.nudge_text: Sounds like a frustrating day. What's weighing on you most right now?

entries.initial_entry: Mom called asking if I could cover her prescription copay this month since Dad's physical therapy bills are piling up. Of course I said yes but then I looked at my account and it's like, where is this money supposed to come from? I'll figure it out, I always do.

entries.initial_entry:

Last year I worked both and just stopped by Mom and Dad's for an hour between rides.

But then another part of me is like, one good day of driving could cover half my car insurance payment. I don't know. I'll probably end up doing what I always do which is work until the last possible minute and then rush over stressed and half-present anyway.

Spent most of the afternoon sourcing fabric swatches with Diya at this tiny warehouse in Camp. The guy running the place kept talking about minimum order quantities and I could see her doing math in her head, getting stressed. I just wanted to touch everything, feel the weight of it. Found this insane indigo block-print that would be perfect for the bomber jackets we've been sketching.

On the rickshaw back I was thinking about how three months ago I was sitting in a lecture hall pretending to care about supply chain management and now I'm actually doing it, badly, in real life. The irony isn't lost on me. Mum called while I was out but I let it ring. Didn't feel like hearing about Priya's wedding venue shortlist again.
entries.nudge_text: What happens when you do pick up those calls?
entries.response_text: Same script every time. How's the business, when am I coming home, did I hear Priya's news. I just run out of ways to sound enthusiastic.

entries.initial_entry:
Pop-up at Koregaon Park went well. Sold eleven pieces including two of the bomber prototypes. One girl asked if we had an Instagram — we do, but it's barely set up. Diya handled the money stuff while I talked to people. That part I'm good at. Strangers telling me they like something I made with my hands, that's a specific kind of high.

Afterwards we went to this new rooftop bar Adi told me about. Spent way too much on cocktails but the music was perfect and the city looked ridiculous from up there. Diya said it was our "revenue celebration." We made maybe eight thousand rupees today and spent half of it on drinks and that felt exactly right.

Meeting with a guy who runs a boutique in Viman Nagar. He wants to stock five designs on consignment. The terms aren't great — he takes forty percent — but Diya says the visibility is worth it. I kept looking around his shop thinking about whether our stuff would even fit next to all that boho tourist stuff.

Adi came over after. We ordered biryani and watched some terrible thriller on Netflix. He mentioned his lease is up in April and sort of hinted at us getting a place together. I changed the subject to the boutique deal. Not sure why. It's not like I haven't thought about it.
entries.nudge_text: You've thought about it but changed the subject — what came up for you in that moment?
entries.response_text: Felt like if I said yes it would become this whole plan, this whole thing with routines and splitting rent and groceries. I like how we are now where it's easy.

entries.initial_entry: Late now, Adi left an hour ago. Finished stitching the last sample for the consignment order and my fingers are sore but the jacket looks exactly how I pictured it. Small win. Going to sleep actually satisfied for once.

Went home for lunch yesterday because Dad's birthday. Mum cornered me in the kitchen to ask if I've "thought about what comes next" if the brand doesn't work out. Priya was right there smiling with her ring and her Infosys lanyard still around her neck like some kind of prop. I know Mum means well but I'm so tired of being the cautionary tale at family gatherings.

I told her I don't have a backup plan because this is the plan. She got quiet in that way she does. Dad just ate his cake and said the jacket I brought him was nice, which honestly was enough. I don't need everyone to get it. The brand is growing, I'm paying my share of rent, I'm not asking anyone for money. I don't know what else there is to prove.
entries.nudge_text: You said Dad's comment was enough — was it actually?

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep, brain won't stop. Thinking about the consignment thing and whether it's a step up or just giving our margins away for the ego of seeing our label in a shop. Diya ran the numbers and we'd need to sell through at least seventy percent to break even after materials. That's tight. But then again if we don't get the stuff in front of new people it just sits in our flat.

Also still chewing on the Adi thing from the other night. It's not that I don't want to be with him, it's that the second you sign a lease with someone the whole shape of your days changes. You eat dinner at the same time, you watch the same shows, you wake up and there's no surprise about who's next to you. That sounds awful when I write it out but I don't mean it in a cruel way. I just like having space where I can decide at midnight to rework a pattern or go meet Diya for chai without explaining.

Maybe I'm overthinking this. He didn't bring it up again and maybe he won't. Part of me kind of wants him to, just so I know he actually means it instead of it being some lease-expiry convenience thing. I should probably eat something, I skipped dinner and my stomach is doing that acidic thing.

Diya wants to pause the brand for a month because she got offered a paid internship at some design studio in Bangalore. She says it's temporary and she'll be back but it felt like the floor shifted. Sat on the balcony after she told me just staring at traffic, not sure what I'm supposed to do with half a brand.
entries.nudge_text: What part of this scares you most — the business or doing it alone?

entries.nudge_text: What part of this scares you most — the business or doing it alone?

entries.initial_entry: The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time and I didn't even get a mention in the quarterly call. Kramer presented the numbers like they were his. Sat there chewing my pen while the board nodded along.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from speaking up during the call?
entries.response_text: Politics. Kramer reports to the CEO directly. Picking a fight on camera would only hurt me.

entries.initial_entry:
Still stewing over the call. Lotte opened a bottle of that 2019 Brunello we'd been saving for something and I couldn't even enjoy it properly. Kept replaying the moment where Kramer said "the regional team" instead of my name. The regional team. Like the numbers came out of nowhere.

It's not just vanity, though people would say it is. When you run three sites and you restructure the nightshift rotation yourself and you push back on corporate for months to get the new sorting line funded, having your name attached to the outcome matters. It's how you move up. It's how people know who to call when the next VP seat opens.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if the VP seat opens and they don't think of you?
entries.response_text: Then six years of building this region into the best-performing in the company would have been a waste. And I'd have to seriously think about whether I stay or find somewhere that notices.

entries.initial_entry: Two of my warehouse leads called in sick on the same day and I spent six hours on the floor sorting parcels like it's 2003. My back is wrecked. Cancelled dinner reservations at that new place in Katendrecht because I couldn't face sitting upright for two hours.

Had the one-on-one with Kramer today. Brought up the quarterly call, kept it measured. He said he appreciated my "team-first attitude" and that everyone knows who does the real work. Classic deflection. He even patted me on the shoulder on the way out, which I found condescending but smiled through anyway.

Lotte says I should document everything from now on. She's probably right. She also said I need to stop letting work ruin my evenings, because last weekend I spent the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails on my phone instead of actually being out there. She's definitely right about that one. I barely noticed the water.
entries.nudge_text: You said you barely noticed the water. How often does that happen — being somewhere you chose to be but not really being there?
entries.response_text: More than I'd like. But that's the deal when you run things, isn't it.

entries.initial_entry: Got the green light to hire two senior coordinators for the Antwerp hub. My budget, my picks, no committee. Finally something that feels like actual authority instead of managing upwards all day. Already have a shortlist.

Hosted twelve people for dinner last night. Lotte paired every course — she did the lamb with a Châteauneuf-du-Pape that made van der Berg's wife close her eyes when she tasted it. That's the kind of evening I live for. Good food, the right people, candles on the canal, Lotte in her element. Van der Berg even asked if we'd consider doing a supper club. I said maybe, but honestly I don't want to turn it into an obligation.

The thing is, half the guests were people from the industry. Not a coincidence. You build your network over a cheese course, not in a meeting room. Van der Berg sits on the advisory board at Port of Rotterdam. His wife runs procurement at Maersk Benelux. I like these people genuinely — I do — but I also know exactly what those relationships are worth. Lotte says I can never just have fun without an angle. She's not wrong, but I don't see why it can't be both.
entries.nudge_text: When Lotte says you always have an angle — does that bother you or does it feel like a compliment?
entries.response_text: Depends on the day. Sometimes I think she sees through me in a way that's useful. Other times it makes me feel like I can't just enjoy something without her questioning my motives.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. The Antwerp expansion hit a snag — permits delayed, which means the two coordinators I hired start in three weeks with nothing structured for them to do. I've been on calls with the municipal office, with legal, with the construction firm, trying to unstick things. None of it is technically my job. The site manager should be handling permits. But if I leave it to him we'll lose another month, and I can't afford that with the VP shortlist conversation supposedly happening in Q3.

Lotte and I were supposed to go to Bruges for the weekend. She booked a hotel with a rooftop terrace and a Michelin-star restaurant for Saturday night. I told her I'd need to work Saturday morning and she went quiet in that way she does. Not angry, just — recalibrating. She went anyway, took her friend Anneke. I stayed home and spent the day on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast. By four o'clock I realised the permit issue was probably going to take another two weeks regardless of whether I spent Saturday on it or not.

I could have been in Bruges. I could have been sitting on a rooftop with a glass of something excellent, watching the sun go down with the person I like most in the world. Instead I ate cheese toast. I don't know what to do with that information.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to have gone to Bruges instead?

entries.initial_entry: Lotte showed me photos from Bruges. The terrace, the food, the canal at sunset. She wasn't rubbing it in — she just wanted to share. I looked at the photos and felt something I can only describe as stupid. Not sad. Stupid.

Permits came through. Both coordinators start Monday and the fit-out begins next week. I walked the Antwerp floor this morning and rearranged the staging layout myself because the site manager's plan was going to create a bottleneck at dock three. He didn't love me overriding him but he'll see the throughput numbers in two weeks and come around.

Took Friday off. Actually took it off — phone in a drawer, sailed out to the Grevelingenmeer with nobody but the cats in my head for company. Grilled fish on the dock afterwards, drank a cold Riesling, watched the light change on the water for an hour. Didn't think about Kramer or permits or throughput once. Well, maybe once. Lotte would be proud. Didn't tell her about the once.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the phone in the drawer like it was a big deal. Was it?
entries.response_text: Yes. I check it probably forty times a day on weekends. Old habit from when I was on-call as a shift lead. Now it's just control.

entries.initial_entry:
VP shortlist leaked. Three names. I'm one of them. Kramer is not, which should make me feel vindicated but instead I just feel the weight of it. If I get this, I'm responsible for all five regions across the Benelux. Sixty direct reports through my managers. A seat at the executive table. Everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years.

But there's a clause in the offer framework that the role is based in Amsterdam and requires eighty percent in-office. Lotte's business is in Rotterdam. The canal house is in Rotterdam. The boat is twenty minutes from Rotterdam. I'd be commuting two hours a day or we'd need a second flat in Amsterdam, which means I'd be living half my life in a box near Zuidas eating takeaway alone while Lotte hosts wine tastings without me.

I keep doing the maths in my head. More status, more scope, more say in how the company runs. But also more time in transit, fewer evenings on the terrace, fewer dinners where Lotte pairs a wine so perfect it changes the conversation. I don't know how you weigh those things against each other. Every time I think I've decided, I flip. Lotte hasn't said what she thinks yet. I think she's waiting to see what I actually want before she tells me what she wants, and honestly that scares me more than the interview.
entries.nudge_text: If Lotte told you she wanted you to take it, would that make it easier or harder?
entries.response_text: Harder. Because then I couldn't blame the decision on protecting what we have. I'd have to admit I want it and deal with what that costs.

entries.initial_entry:
Four hundred bucks today, which is solid, but man my knee is screaming. Had this moment around 3pm where I was sitting in my car outside some guy's apartment waiting for him to come down and I just started laughing because I remembered this time in 2019 when I was rappelling into a canyon fire in New Mexico and thinking "this is it, this is what life should feel like." Now I'm waiting for some dude to get his Thai food.

It's not bad though. Like I genuinely don't hate this. The freedom part is real—nobody tells me when to start, when to stop, I can take a Tuesday off if I want to drive up to the mountains and just sit. Rachel thinks I'm avoiding something. Maybe I am. But four hundred bucks is four hundred bucks and the drone business hit $800 in sales this month. That's momentum.
entries.nudge_text: What does Rachel think you're avoiding exactly?
entries.response_text: She thinks I'm avoiding commitment. Like capital-C commitment—a real job with benefits, a retirement plan, maybe a ring. She's not wrong that I'm not chasing those things, but I don't think I'm avoiding them either. I just don't see them as the only path. The drone thing could actually become something real. I sold a Phantom 4 Pro yesterday to a real estate photographer who found me on Facebook Marketplace. That's a client, not just a sale.

entries.initial_entry:
Rachel's brother came over for dinner last night and the whole thing turned into this intervention I didn't ask for. He's a project manager at Lockheed, makes probably three times what I do, and he kept asking "but what's the long-term plan here?" like I'm some kind of lost teenager. I'm 38 years old. I've fought wildfires. I've pulled people out of avalanches. I've built a side business from nothing while working 50-hour weeks in a car. But sure, Kevin, tell me about your 401k match.

The thing that pissed me off the most is that Rachel didn't say anything. She just sat there nodding at some of his points. We talked after he left and she said she "understands both sides" which is code for "I agree with him but don't want to fight." I get that she wants stability. I do. But I'm not going to take some cubicle job just so Kevin can stop worrying about me at Thanksgiving.

I know I sound defensive. I am defensive. But there's a difference between not having a plan and having a plan that other people don't recognize as one. The drone business did $800 this month. If I can hit $2000/month by December, that's rent covered. That's not nothing. That's a runway.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned runway—but runway to what?
entries.response_text: To not having to drive for DoorDash anymore. To selling drones full-time, maybe training people to fly them, partnering with the real estate guy to do aerial shoots. There's a path here. It's just not a straight line with a pension at the end. It's more like—I build something piece by piece until one day I look around and I'm not hustling anymore, I'm running something.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about last night. Couldn't sleep. Kevin's voice in my head: "But what if the drone thing doesn't work out?" Like failure is this thing that only happens to people who try unconventional stuff. His job is so safe it's practically embalmed and he acts like that makes him wise.

I texted my old crew chief from the fire days, Dario. He's in Tucson now doing hazmat consulting. He said "bro you gotta stop explaining yourself to people who already decided you're wrong." He's right. But it's hard when one of those people sleeps next to you.
Worked 11 hours. Three apps running simultaneously. Knee is bad. Rachel and I haven't really talked since the Kevin thing and the apartment feels weird.
entries.nudge_text: Weird how?

entries.initial_entry:
She finally said it. "I don't know if this is working for me." Not the relationship—the whole thing. The uncertainty. The not knowing if I'll make $300 or $500 in a given week. The drone boxes stacked in the spare room. The fact that I'm 38 and we're still splitting rent instead of building toward something "together."

I told her I AM building toward something. She said "but it's YOUR something. Where am I in it?" And I didn't have a good answer for that. Because honestly I haven't thought about it that way. I've been so focused on proving that my way works that I forgot to check if she wanted to be on the ride.

But also—and I know this sounds cold—is it my job to make her feel secure? She knew who I was when we started dating. I was doing ski patrol then, seasonal work, no guaranteed paycheck. She thought it was exciting. Now it's a problem? People want the story of the adventurous guy but not the actual life that comes with it.

I don't know. Maybe I'm the one who's wrong here. But it doesn't feel wrong. It feels like I'm being asked to become someone else.
entries.nudge_text: Who would you become if you gave her what she's asking for?
entries.response_text: I don't even know what she's asking for. A different job? A proposal? A five-year plan with bullet points? She says "stability" but that means different things. My parents had stable jobs and they were miserable for 30 years. I'd rather be broke and building something than comfortable and dead inside.

entries.initial_entry:
Third entry today, I know. Can't stop spinning on this.

Rachel went to her sister's for the night. Said she needed space to think. I said okay and then I sat in the apartment alone eating leftover pad thai and feeling like the walls were closing in. Which is ironic because I literally fought claustrophobia for years—you can't do wildland work if you panic in tight spaces—but emotional claustrophobia is apparently different.

I pulled up my old photos from the fire days. There's one from a job in Oregon, 2017, where we're all standing in front of this blackened hillside at dawn after a 14-hour shift. Everyone's covered in ash and grinning. We'd just saved a subdivision. I remember feeling like I mattered, like the work mattered, like I was exactly where I was supposed to be.

I don't feel that now. The drone stuff is cool when it's working but it doesn't hit the same. And driving—driving is just time traded for money, nothing more. I keep telling myself it's temporary, it's a bridge, but bridges are supposed to lead somewhere and I'm not sure mine does anymore.

Maybe Rachel is right to question it. I just wish she questioned WITH me instead of AT me.

Rachel came back this morning. We talked for two hours. She's not leaving, but she's also not okay with things continuing exactly as they are. We agreed to try couples counseling, which I never thought I'd say yes to, but here we are.

She made one point that stuck: she said she doesn't want me to change who I am, she wants to feel like she's part of the plan, not just watching from the sidelines. That's fair. I've been treating the drone business like my thing and the relationship like our thing, but they're not separate. If I succeed, she's there. If I fail, she's there. She deserves to know the stakes.
entries.nudge_text: How'd it feel saying yes to that?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Terrifying. Counseling feels like admitting something is broken. But maybe that's the old me talking—the guy who thinks asking for help is weakness. Dario told me once that the smartest firefighters are the ones who call for backup early. Maybe relationships work the same way. I'd rather get help now than watch the whole thing burn down because I was too proud to call it in.

entries.initial_entry:
First counseling session was yesterday. Weird to have a stranger summarize your relationship in 50 minutes. She said we have "different risk tolerances" and "misaligned timelines." Which, yeah, that tracks. But she also said something that hit different: she said I might be using constant motion as a way to avoid examining whether I'm actually happy.

That's been rattling around in my head ever since. Because the thing is—I'm not sure I know what happiness looks like when it's not tied to a goal. Like, I was happy fighting fires because I was good at it and it was hard and people respected it. I was happy when the drone business made its first sale because it proved the concept worked. But am I happy just... existing? On a Tuesday afternoon with nothing to prove?

Rachel asked me later what I'd do if I couldn't work. Not retirement—like, if I got injured again, worse than the knee, and couldn't drive, couldn't rebuild drones, couldn't hustle. What would be left? I said "you" but even as I said it I wasn't sure I believed it. Because I don't know who I am when I'm not chasing something.

That's a problem, probably. The counselor would say it's a problem. Rachel would say it's a problem. But part of me wonders if it's just... how I'm built. Some people are wired to sit still. I'm wired to move. The question is whether that's a feature or a bug, and whether the answer even matters if it means losing someone I love.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to be still and not feel like you're dying?
entries.response_text: I genuinely don't know. My dad retired at 62 and was dead by 65—not physically, but like, the light went out. He used to build furniture in the garage and one day he just stopped. Said there was no point. That terrifies me more than anything Rachel's brother ever said about 401ks. So maybe stillness isn't the goal. Maybe it's about finding the right kind of motion—something that includes her, something that matters, something that isn't just running from the thing I'm afraid of.
