entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji made a comment at lunch about the indigo stains on my fingernails. She didn't say anything directly, just that thing she does where she looks at my hands and then looks at the dal and raises her eyebrows. Ravi was right there and said nothing. I know he thinks the dyeing thing is fine, he's told me so, but he won't say it in front of her. So I'm the one who looks like I'm neglecting things.

The thing is, the kitchen was spotless. Lunch was on time. Arjun's tiffin was packed before school. I don't know what more she wants from me. Or maybe I do know and I just don't want to give it, which is this complete surrender of any time that's mine. Like the house should swallow me whole and I should be grateful for it.

I scrubbed the indigo off after lunch. Took ten minutes with lemon and baking soda. I shouldn't have had to do that. But I did it anyway and I'm not sure what that says about me.
entries.nudge_text: Why did you scrub them off?
entries.response_text: Because it was easier than sitting through another silent dinner with her staring at my hands. I pick my fights and that one wasn't worth it. But it felt like erasing something.

entries.initial_entry:
Arjun's parent-teacher meeting today. The class teacher mentioned he draws during free period instead of playing cricket with the other boys and asked if everything is okay at home. I wanted to say, yes, everything is fine, my son likes drawing, why is that a problem? But I smiled and nodded and said I'd encourage him to be more social.

Came home and told Ravi about it and he said maybe Arjun should join a sport, that it would be good for him. And I thought, here we go. Everyone has an opinion about what the boy should be doing except the boy himself.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from saying that to the teacher?
entries.response_text: I don't know. You just don't, do you? Not in that setting.

entries.initial_entry: Made aloo paratha for breakfast the way Appa used to, with extra ajwain in the dough. The smell filled the whole kitchen and for a second I was back in our old house in Mysore, watching him roll them out, flour everywhere.

Spent the whole morning at the Diwali mela helping set up the community puja. I did the rangoli at the entrance the way my grandmother taught me, rice flour with turmeric borders, none of the acrylic paint shortcuts the younger crowd uses. Three women came up and asked me to show them how. Felt good.

Later, back home, I worked on a new indigo resist pattern. Nobody was around to comment on it. Ravi had taken Arjun and Vihaan to the cricket match. Two hours of silence and my hands doing what they wanted to do. I didn't even check my phone.
entries.nudge_text: Which part of the day stuck with you more?

entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji wants me to start teaching Vihaan the Vishnu Sahasranama. He's seven. She says she learned it by six. I said I'd start next week, which I will, because it's important, Appa made sure I knew it by heart and I'm glad he did. But there's this edge to the way she asks, like she's testing whether I'm doing enough.

Ravi's cousin Priya called in the evening. She started a small catering business last year and wanted to know if I'd be interested in supplying hand-dyed table linens. I said I'd think about it. Didn't mention it to anyone at home yet.
entries.nudge_text: Are you going to tell Ravi about Priya's offer?

entries.initial_entry:
Sat down this morning to teach Vihaan the first twenty verses. He's quicker than I expected. He kept asking what the words meant and I found myself explaining them properly, not just the rote way I was taught but actually talking about what each name describes. He asked why Vishnu has so many names and I said because one name is never enough for something that big. He seemed satisfied with that.

It reminded me of sitting with Appa in the puja room, early mornings, the smell of camphor and sandalwood paste. He was patient. He never rushed me or made it feel like a chore. I want to give Vihaan that same feeling, not the pressure of getting it right, but the warmth of sitting together with something that matters. The sense that this is ours, this thread that connects us backward through generations, something nobody can take away or schedule over.

Mummyji passed by the room and listened for a while and actually smiled. She didn't say anything, didn't correct his pronunciation or mine. She just stood there and smiled. That was enough for today.

Spent the afternoon cataloguing my dye samples. I have fourteen shades of indigo now, ranging from almost-grey to a deep navy that I got by double-dipping with a fermentation vat I built from a recipe in an old Kalamkari manual. Labelled each one with the mordant used, the dip count, the fabric type. Cotton takes differently than silk, and I'm getting more consistent results with the cotton muslin I sourced from a weaver in Solapur.

Priya messaged again about the linens. She has a wedding order for February. Twelve table runners, twenty napkins. The money would be decent. I told her I need to think about scale. My terrace setup can handle maybe four meters of fabric at a time. I'd need to figure out drying space and timing around the kids' schedules.

Vihaan came up to the terrace after his homework and asked if he could dip his hand in the vat. I let him. His palm came out blue and he ran downstairs to show everyone. Mummyji was not pleased.
entries.nudge_text: So are you doing it?
entries.response_text: Haven't decided. Need to work out the logistics first.

entries.initial_entry:
Told Ravi about Priya's proposal over chai after the boys went to bed. He was more supportive than I expected. He said if I wanted to try it, he'd help me figure out the money side. He even suggested converting part of the back store room into a workspace. I hadn't thought about that.

What surprised me was my own hesitation. He was offering exactly what I thought I wanted, backing, space, permission. But when it was right there in front of me I felt this pull backward, like, who am I to run a business? My mother didn't. Mummyji didn't. All the women I grew up watching ran homes, not enterprises. And I love running this home. That's the part that gets lost when people hear I want to do something else. I don't want to leave, I want to also.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'also' actually look like day to day?
entries.response_text: Mornings with the boys, school drop-off, puja, cooking. Afternoons when they're in school or doing homework, that's my window. Three, maybe four hours. It's not a lot but it's mine. I just need people to stop treating those hours like leftover time that belongs to the house.

entries.initial_entry:
Mummyji brought up the store room idea at dinner, in front of Ravi's parents who were visiting. She said something like, "Meera is thinking of opening a factory in the house." She was smiling but it was the wrong kind of smile. Ravi's father asked what she was talking about and I had to explain the dyeing and the linen idea in front of everyone while trying not to sound like I was defending myself.

Ravi stepped in eventually and said it was just a small project. The word "small" stuck. I know he was trying to help but it felt like he was shrinking it to make it palatable. I didn't say anything after that. Finished dinner. Cleared the plates. Went upstairs.

Can't sleep. Ravi's parents left after dinner and everyone is being normal now but I keep replaying the table conversation. The worst part isn't what Mummyji said, it's that nobody asked me a real question about it. Nobody said, what kind of fabric, what's the process, how did you learn. They only wanted to know if it would interfere with anything.

I keep thinking about Appa's workshop in Mysore. He repaired watches. Tiny, patient, precise work. Nobody ever questioned whether he should be doing it. It was just what he did. I want that. Not the permission, not the approval. Just the quiet assumption that of course I do this, it's mine.
entries.nudge_text: What would Appa say about the indigo?

entries.initial_entry:
Took the boys to the Ganesh temple this morning for the monthly abhishekam. Vihaan recited the first twenty verses of the Sahasranama on the auto ride there, unprompted. I didn't even know he'd been practising on his own. The priest noticed and said something to me about keeping these things alive. I felt proud but also this strange defensiveness, like I needed to prove I wasn't only about the dyeing, that I still carry all of this too.

Why do I feel like I have to choose? Like every hour I spend with the indigo is an hour stolen from something else. Nobody told Ravi he has to choose between his consulting work and being a father. It's just assumed he can be both.
entries.nudge_text: Who's actually making you choose?

entries.initial_entry:
Called Priya back tonight and said yes to the February order. Twelve runners, twenty napkins, indigo with a kalamkari-inspired border block I've been working on. She sounded happy. I felt terrified and then calm, in that order, like jumping into cold water.

Haven't told Mummyji yet. Told Ravi, who said congratulations and then asked if I needed help setting up the store room this weekend. I said yes. Vihaan overheard and asked if he could help with the blue. I said yes to that too. I don't know if I'm doing the right thing. But I know I'm doing my thing, and right now that has to be enough.

Got the renewal confirmation from Formosa Plastics this afternoon. Three-year contract extension, same volume, small price adjustment we can absorb. I sat with the letter for a while before telling anyone. Twenty-three years of this company and these moments still feel like exhaling after holding your breath. The terms are not exciting. No growth clause, no expanded scope. But when I walked through the warehouse floor afterward, I counted the stations — forty-two people whose paychecks run through what I decide. That number has not changed in four years and I am fine with that.

My son Jia-hao asked why I did not push for a bigger increase in the unit price. He pulled up competitor rates and showed me a spreadsheet. He is sharp, I will give him that. But he has not sat across from a procurement manager who controls a quarter of your annual revenue and watched them consider whether you are worth the trouble. You do not squeeze someone who keeps coming back. You make it easy for them to stay.

Mei-ling and I had tea after dinner and she said I looked lighter than I have in weeks. She is right. A locked-in contract means the next three years have a floor under them, and that matters more to me than a ceiling I might not reach.
entries.nudge_text: What did Jia-hao say after that?

entries.initial_entry:
Second time writing today, which I almost never do. After the tea with Mei-ling I went back to my study to look over the Q3 projections and ended up just sitting there. Not anxious, not relieved — more like noticing the room. The desk lamp I have had since the company's first year. The filing cabinet that still has paper copies of our original business registration. Mei-ling keeps telling me to digitize everything but there is something about opening a drawer and seeing the actual stamps on the actual paper.

I called my younger brother Weijun in Kaohsiung. He asked about the Formosa deal and I told him it went through. He said congratulations and then started talking about his daughter's wedding plans. Normal conversation. But I realized I had called him specifically so someone outside the company would know the ground is solid. Like I needed a witness. Not sure what that is about. Maybe just habit — our father always reported good news to the family first, bad news never.

The neighborhood was quiet tonight. Mrs. Lin across the alley had her laundry out late again. I can see it from my study window. Small things like that — the rhythm of the block staying the same — I notice them more than I used to.

Had lunch with old colleague Lao Zhang today. We worked together at the engineering firm before I left to start the company. He retired last year from the same desk he sat at for thirty-one years. Full pension, government health coverage, the works. We ate at the beef noodle place on Zhongxiao East Road that has not changed its menu since we were in our thirties.

He told me he is bored. Gets up, reads the paper, watches his grandchildren on video calls, goes to bed. Said he envies me for still having something to wake up for. I told him I envy his pension. We both laughed but I think we both meant it. The difference is I chose the path where nothing is guaranteed month to month, and some part of me has never fully forgiven myself for that, even though the company has been stable for over a decade. I still check the accounts receivable every Monday morning before anyone else arrives.

I suppose that lunch reminded me why I keep the reserve fund where it is, even when Jia-hao argues we should reinvest. Lao Zhang's safety net was built into his employment. Mine I had to build by hand, brick by brick, and if I pull bricks out to build something taller, the foundation gets thinner.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to forgive that choice?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I do not think I can. The choice was right — the company employs forty-two families. But the version of me that wanted a predictable life, that version does not care about being right. He just wanted to stop worrying. And I still have not given him that.

entries.initial_entry:
Jia-hao came to me with a proposal to take on a new product line — biodegradable packaging for the food service sector. He had the whole pitch: market research, supplier contacts, projected margins. Spent weeks on it apparently. And the numbers looked reasonable on paper. But "reasonable on paper" and "reasonable when you are the one signing the purchase orders" are different things entirely.

I told him we would revisit it after the Formosa contract settles in. He did not argue but I could see the frustration. He walked out of my office the way I used to walk out of my father's study — jaw tight, hands in pockets. I know what that walk means. But I also know what happens when you stretch into a new market before you have confirmed your existing revenue is truly locked down. I have seen it take companies apart. I am not going to let that happen to ours.
entries.nudge_text: Did you tell him any of that?

entries.initial_entry:
Mei-ling brought it up after dinner. She thinks I was too quick to shut Jia-hao down this morning. She did not say it that way — she said maybe I should look at the proposal more carefully before deciding. But I know what she means. She has been the translator between me and the children for decades.

She also mentioned that Jia-hao talked to her about possibly starting his own company if there is no room for his ideas here. She said it casually, while washing dishes. I do not think it was casual. I told her I would read the proposal again this weekend. Then I went to my study and closed the door.

Spent the weekend with Jia-hao's proposal spread across my desk. Read it three times. The margins are tighter than he presented — I found some cost assumptions that do not account for certification delays in biodegradable materials, which can take six to eight months in Taiwan. But the core idea is sound. The food service sector is moving that direction whether we participate or not.

I kept thinking about what Mei-ling said, about him starting his own thing. My father ran a hardware store in Tainan for forty years. Same location, same inventory categories, same suppliers. When I told him I was leaving the engineering firm to start my own company, he did not speak to me for two weeks. Not out of anger — he genuinely could not understand why anyone would walk away from a monthly salary. I swore I would never do that to my children. And here I am, not refusing to speak, but maybe doing something worse — keeping him inside a structure that is shaped entirely around how I see the world.

I circled three sections of his proposal where the numbers need work and left it on his desk with a note that said "Let's talk Monday." That is further than I wanted to go. But the alternative — watching him leave the way I left my father's orbit — I cannot do that either. I keep turning it over. I agreed to look, not to leap. But I am not sure the distinction matters to anyone but me.
entries.nudge_text: Are you opening the door or just cracking it?

entries.initial_entry:
My sister Huda called again about the retirement fund thing. She has this way of bringing it up like she is just asking a casual question but we both know where it is going. "Just curious, do you have anything saved for when you cannot drive anymore?" She knows the answer. She has known for years.

What she does not understand is that I did the office thing for fifteen years and it nearly killed something in me. I am not going back to counting someone else's hours so I can sit in a flat at seventy with money and nothing to show for actually living. Maybe I am wrong. But right now the idea of giving up my mornings at Abu Khalil's cafe to stack dirham in an account I might never touch makes me feel worse than the uncertainty does.
entries.nudge_text: What does the uncertainty actually feel like?
entries.response_text: It feels like Tuesday. Anyway, Huda worries enough for both of us.

entries.initial_entry:
Good week for rides. Had a couple of airport pickups that paid well and a half-day tour with a German family visiting Jerash. They wanted someone who could explain things properly, not just point at columns. Felt good using that part of my brain again. Have not done a proper tour in a while and I forgot how much I like it.

Spent the extra money on a proper dinner at Sufra with Tariq and Walid. Lamb mansaf, the real kind. We sat there for three hours talking about nothing important. Drove home with the windows down. Warm night still, even in October.
entries.nudge_text: What made those three hours worth more than saving it?

entries.initial_entry: Long day, five rides back to back because I said yes to everything on the app. Knees ache and my shoulders are stiff. Going to bed early for once.

Cannot sleep. Lying here thinking about that German family from last week, how the father kept asking me questions about Umayyad architecture like I was a professor. I knew every answer. Fifteen years driving tourists around will do that. Strange how that one afternoon made the whole week feel worthwhile.
entries.nudge_text: Is it the knowing, or someone caring that you know?
entries.response_text: Both, honestly. But if nobody asks, the knowing just sits there.

entries.initial_entry: Woke up still tired from yesterday's marathon. App is showing surge pricing downtown but my back says no. Skipped it. Felt relieved for ten minutes then started calculating what I am losing.
entries.nudge_text: Which number matters more — what you lost or what your back saved you?
entries.response_text: Ask me again when rent is due.

entries.initial_entry: Went out for coffee instead of driving. Sat at Abu Khalil's and watched the street for an hour. Sometimes that is the whole day and it has to be enough.

Third time writing today which is unusual for me. Tariq stopped by the cafe this afternoon and mentioned his son got him set up on one of those delivery apps, the bicycle ones. He is sixty-two and cycling around Amman in this heat delivering shawarma. He laughed about it but I could see he was embarrassed.

That could be me in a few years if the driving work dries up. Or it could not. Hard to know. Tariq still ordered the expensive coffee though, the Turkish one with cardamom they do properly. We split a knafeh. He said the delivery money is not bad and he picks his own hours. Same thing I always say about driving. Maybe we are both just telling ourselves the right story.
entries.nudge_text: What would the wrong story sound like?
entries.response_text: That we gave up something real for comfort that does not last. That choosing the nice afternoon over the stable job was not brave, it was just easier. I do not fully believe that. But I hear it in Huda's voice sometimes.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a fare today, a businessman from Riyadh who spent the whole ride talking on his phone about quarterly targets and staff problems. Tipped well but looked miserable. Suit that cost more than my rent and he did not once look out the window at the citadel lit up at night.

People assume I ended up here, like gig work is where you land when everything else falls through. Maybe for some. For me it was a door I walked through on purpose. I know what a Monday morning in an office smells like. I know what it costs. I chose this, and today, watching that man bark into his phone while Amman glowed outside his window, I felt sure about it.
entries.nudge_text: Do you always feel this sure, or just on nights like this?
entries.response_text: Not always. The sure feeling comes and goes. But tonight it is here so I am writing it down.

entries.initial_entry:
Rent went up. Landlord sent a message this morning, very polite about it, but two hundred dinars more starting January. I sat with the phone in my hand for a while doing the math. It is tight. It was already tight but now it is the kind of tight where one bad week matters.

Part of me wants to call Huda and tell her she was right, just get it over with. But right does not mean I know what to do differently. I cannot go back to an office. I do not mean I refuse, I mean something in me genuinely cannot. The last time I tried I lasted four months and stopped sleeping. So what is the alternative. Pick up more rides, skip the cafe, eat at home every night. Become the version of me that survives but does not particularly want to.

Later in the evening now. Calmed down a bit. Called Walid and he said his cousin has a spare room in Jabal Amman, smaller place but cheaper, and the neighborhood is actually nicer for walking. Might look at it this weekend. Moving is not ideal at fifty-eight but it is not the end of the world either.

I keep thinking about what I wrote earlier about not being able to go back to office work. That is true but it is also a wall I have built. The real question is whether the wall is protecting something important or just keeping me stuck. I think it is protecting something. The mornings where I sit with coffee and watch the light hit the old stones. The afternoons where I take the long route home because the view from the fourth circle is good at sunset. The dinners that go three hours because nobody has anywhere to be.

These are small things. Nobody would list them on a CV or count them as assets. But they are the thread running through every good day I have had in the past fifteen years. If I have to move to a smaller flat to keep them, that is an easy trade. If I have to drive more shifts, I can do that too. What I cannot do is give them up entirely and still recognize myself.
entries.nudge_text: When did you first realize those small things were the thread?
entries.response_text: Probably the week I quit the logistics job. I walked out at lunch and sat in a park eating falafel and thought, this is the first time in years I have actually tasted my food. That was fifteen years ago and I still remember the taste.

entries.initial_entry: Spent the whole evening comparing fixed deposits on three different bank sites while the kids fought over the remote. Anil says we should lock in the rate before it drops again. I know he is right but I kept thinking about that weekend trip to Alibaug we never took. We had the dates picked and everything.
entries.nudge_text: What happened to the Alibaug trip?
entries.response_text: We pushed it. First it was school fees, then Anil's mother needed new glasses, then he said let's wait till Diwali. Diwali came and went. The dates are still saved in my calendar like some kind of joke.

entries.initial_entry: My sister Meera called and asked when we are finally going on a proper holiday. I told her we went to Lonavala in June. She laughed and said "that does not count, it was one night at your cousin's farmhouse." I do not need her opinion on how we spend our time or money.
entries.nudge_text: Did it bother you because she was wrong, or because she wasn't?

entries.initial_entry:
Took Aarav and Rhea for their dental checkups. No cavities, which is a relief because the last round cost us eleven thousand rupees. Came home, made dal and rice, helped Aarav with his Hindi homework. He is struggling with the matras again. Called the tuition teacher and she can take him Tuesdays and Thursdays for two thousand a month.

Anil came back late. I had kept his food covered on the counter. He ate while scrolling his phone and went straight to bed. I watched two episodes of my show and finished the leftover gulab jamun from Sunday. The house was quiet. It should have felt peaceful but it just felt flat.

My mother-in-law brought up the gold investment scheme again at lunch. She wants us to put money into one of those jeweller savings plans, says every family needs gold as a safety net. Anil looked at me like I was supposed to agree. I smiled and said we would think about it.

Later I told him I do not want more gold sitting in a locker I forget the code to. He said it is about long-term protection, not about wearing it. Fine. But we have insurance, we have the FD, we have his provident fund. At what point do we stop saving for some imagined disaster and use the money to actually live a little? He got quiet and changed the subject.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'living a little' look like right now?
entries.response_text: Nothing extravagant. Maybe booking that resort in Goa I bookmarked three months ago. Getting the good cheese from the fancy store without feeling guilty. Taking an auto instead of waiting twenty minutes for the bus with two cranky kids. Small things that do not require a committee meeting.

entries.initial_entry:
I had a full breakdown over a cake. Not even a real breakdown, just stood in the kitchen with my hands shaking after I realized I forgot to order Rhea's birthday cake. Her party is Saturday. I had the tab open on my phone for two weeks, kept meaning to customize it, kept getting pulled away. She wanted the one shaped like a castle with purple icing and her name in gold. Now the bakery says they need five days minimum and I have three.

Called four other bakeries. One can do it but it costs almost double. I said yes without even checking with Anil because I could not bear the thought of my daughter's face when there is no castle cake.

Then I sat on the kitchen floor and cried. Not because of the cake. Because of everything the cake represents. How my days dissolve into errands and appointments and packed lunches and I cannot hold onto a single thing for myself. I used to coordinate events for two hundred guests. I used to keep spreadsheets and timelines and vendor contacts organized across six months. Now I cannot order a cake on time.

Anil found the charge on the credit card and asked about it. I told him the original bakery fell through. He said okay, no problem. He did not ask why I was upset. I do not think he noticed.

The worst part is that I know the party will be fine. Rhea will love the cake. The kids will run around the flat screaming. The mothers will sit in the living room and talk about school admissions. And I will clean up afterwards and load the dishwasher and think about the next thing. That is what bothers me. Not that it went wrong. That even when it goes right, there is nothing in it for me.
entries.nudge_text: You said 'nothing in it for me' — what would something for you look like?
entries.response_text: I honestly do not know anymore. That scares me more than the money stuff. At least with money I know what the number should be. But when I try to picture what would make me feel like I got something out of a day, I come up blank. I used to know. I used to have answers. When did I stop?

entries.initial_entry:
Rhea's party went well. She wore her purple lehenga and ate cake with both hands. I took about sixty photos. At one point she grabbed my face and said "best mummy" and I felt it go straight through me.

After everyone left and the kids were asleep, I poured myself a glass of wine I had hidden behind the pickle jars. Sat on the balcony with my feet up. Pune was noisy below me — auto horns, someone's TV, a dog barking. I just sat there drinking slowly and not thinking about anything. Forty-five minutes of nothing. It was the best part of my week and it cost me two hundred rupees and zero planning.

I need more of that. Not wine specifically. Just moments that belong to me.

Long week. Aarav had a stomach bug Monday through Wednesday so I was home with him while Rhea went to school. Spent three days washing sheets, making khichdi, and checking his temperature. He is fine now, already back to leaving Lego pieces everywhere.

Anil's company announced a restructuring. Nothing definite yet but he has been tense. Came home Thursday and said we should hold off on any big expenses for a few months, just in case. I agreed because what else would I say. We ordered in biryani that night and watched a movie together on the couch. The biryani was from the place near the station, the one that uses too much oil but tastes right. Anil fell asleep halfway through the film. I finished it alone and cleaned the containers.
entries.nudge_text: What else would you have wanted to say?

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday. Took both kids to the park in the morning. Rhea did the monkey bars for the first time without help and I clapped so hard another parent looked at me. Aarav just wanted to sit on the bench and read his comic book, which is fine, he has always been like that.

Came home and looked at my recipe blog for the first time in weeks. I have a draft post about paneer tikka that I never finished. The photos are on my phone, the notes are in a notebook somewhere. I thought about sitting down to write it but then the pressure cooker started whistling and Rhea needed help with her project and the moment passed. My phone buzzed with a notification that my blog traffic dropped forty percent this month. I closed the app.
entries.nudge_text: What does the blog mean to you these days?
entries.response_text: It used to feel like proof I still had a brain. That I could make something and put it out there with my name on it. Now it just feels like another thing I am failing at. One more tab I keep meaning to get back to.

entries.initial_entry:
Renewed our health insurance today. Bumped up the coverage to ten lakhs because Anil's colleague had a surgery that wiped out his savings. Felt right to have that cushion even though the premium went up. Also renewed Aarav's accident cover since he is starting football classes next month.

Made butter chicken for dinner using my grandmother's recipe. The one with the kasuri methi at the end. Both kids asked for seconds. Anil said it was the best I have made in months, which I will take as a compliment and not an insult. After they went to bed, I sat down and actually finished the paneer tikka blog post. Published it at eleven PM. Nothing fancy, but it is out there. Felt good to hit that button.

My hands are still shaking from the critique session. Professor Mansour put my naskh panel up on the wall next to everyone else's work and asked the class, "Where is the artist in this?" Like I was just copying. Like three months of grinding ink and cutting reeds and adjusting every single letter weight was nothing. Somebody said it looked like something you'd find in a mosque gift shop. People laughed.

I didn't say anything. I sat there and took it. Walked straight to the bathroom after and just stood there gripping the sink. The worst part is I keep replaying it and I still can't figure out what I should have said. I know they don't get it. But that doesn't make the laughing stop echoing in my head.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said if you could go back?
entries.response_text: That the script isn't decoration. That every stroke has a genealogy — my jiddo's hand over mine, guiding the qalam for years. That I didn't choose this style from a catalogue. But I don't know if saying that would have changed anything. They'd probably still just see an old alphabet on paper.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning helping Teta Hana sort through jiddo's old papers again, she wants to frame some of his practice sheets, the ones with his warmup strokes, hundreds of little alifs in neat rows fading where the ink ran dry. She held one up and just smiled and didn't say anything. Put it aside for me.

Then I go to studio and Layla is laser-cutting plexiglass and Rami's doing something with projectors and sand and I'm sitting there with my inkwell and bamboo and I keep thinking do I look like a museum exhibit to them, like am I doing something real or am I just holding onto something because letting go would feel like — I don't know. I keep not finishing that thought.
entries.nudge_text: Feel like what?
entries.response_text: Like forgetting him. Like those practice sheets stop meaning anything. I know that doesn't make logical sense, I could still remember jiddo without doing calligraphy. But it would feel like I walked away from the table where he taught me and never came back.

entries.initial_entry:
Professor Mansour pulled me aside after class and said she sees potential in my work but wants me to "break the script apart." She used words like deconstruct, fragment, subvert. She wants me to take the naskh letters and tear them up, layer them over industrial textures, make them unrecognizable. She said it would be a strong thesis direction.

I told her I'd think about it. Walked home the long way through Gemmayze. Something about the idea makes my stomach tight. Not because it's technically hard. I could do it. I know how to cut letterforms apart and reassemble them. It's that I keep picturing jiddo watching me do it. And Teta seeing the result on a gallery wall and not being able to read a single word. I said I'd think about it and I will, but the thinking itself feels wrong.
Everyone keeps acting like I'm afraid of experimenting. I'm not afraid. I just don't think ripping something apart is the only way to prove you understand it.
entries.nudge_text: What would proving it look like for you?

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner at Uncle Fadi's last night and my cousin Reem asked about my thesis. I said calligraphy and she nodded like that was obvious, like of course Nadia's doing that, and I wanted to snap at her. I don't know why. She wasn't being mean. She was being supportive. But the way she said "of course" made me feel like a fixed point everyone's already mapped. Like no one wonders what I'd do if I chose freely.

Then mama brought up jiddo and told the story about him lettering the sign for the pharmacy on Rue Hamra, how the owner cried when he saw it. Everyone smiled. I smiled too. It's a good story. I love that story.

But I sat there holding my plate thinking about Professor Mansour's suggestion and how if I told this table I was going to fragment the script and collage it over concrete textures, my grandmother would go quiet in that particular way she does when she's hurt but won't say so. And my mother would change the subject. And I'd sit there knowing I made a room full of people uncomfortable for the sake of a grade and a gallery slot. So I won't do it. But I keep circling back to the fact that I want to, or at least part of me does, and I can't tell if that part is ambition or betrayal.
entries.nudge_text: You said part of you wants to — which part?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Can we not do this right now.

entries.initial_entry:
I did something small today that I keep thinking about. I was in the studio early, nobody else there yet, and I pulled out a fresh sheet and started writing the bismillah in naskh the way jiddo taught me. Perfect form, clean lines. Then without really deciding to, I took a second sheet and wrote it again but let the letters drift apart, stretched the connections, let the lam curve wider than it should. It still read as the phrase. Just looser.

I stared at both sheets for a long time. The second one wasn't ugly. It wasn't disrespectful. It was the same words with more air in them. Teta could still read it. Jiddo would have recognized the hand.

I don't know what this means for my thesis. I rolled both sheets up and put them in my bag. I haven't shown anyone. Part of me thinks if I show Professor Mansour she'll want me to go further, push harder, and I'll end up somewhere I didn't agree to. And part of me thinks if I show Teta she'll look at the second sheet and ask why I'm being careless. So for now they're mine. Two versions of the same prayer, sitting in my bag, and I'm the only one who knows about both.

Staff meeting today went sideways. The principal announced they're changing the Class III syllabus mid-term and everyone just nodded along like this was normal. I sat there wanting to say something because I've already prepped my unit plans through June and this throws everything off. But Mrs. Kulkarni was agreeing, and she's been teaching twenty-three years, so what do I know. I just kept quiet and wrote notes.

After the meeting Neha—she joined the same year as me—asked if I thought the change made sense. I said it was fine, it'll be fine. She looked at me like she knew I was lying. I probably was. The thing is, even if I did speak up, what would I say? "Excuse me, ma'am, I disagree"? That's not how it works here. You're new, you keep your head down, you follow the lead of people who've been around longer. That's what Amma always says.

But walking home I kept replaying it and feeling this tight, hot thing in my chest. Not anger exactly. More like frustration at myself for not even trying. I had a valid point and I swallowed it whole. I do this constantly. In meetings, at family dinners, everywhere. I just smile and go along with things and then come home and feel this restless itch like I've betrayed something. I don't even know what.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think you betrayed?
entries.response_text: Maybe my own opinion? Like the fact that I even had one. Everyone assumes I'm just agreeable by nature but sometimes I have things I want to say and I just... don't. And then it's like those thoughts don't count because I never let them out. It's exhausting pretending you don't have a voice.

entries.initial_entry: Quiet week at school. Adjusted the unit plans to fit the new syllabus. Amma said she was proud of me for not making a fuss.

Parent-teacher meetings ran until seven tonight. I'm so tired I can barely think straight. Had a difficult conversation with one father who was upset about his son's handwriting marks. He kept raising his voice and I kept saying "I understand, uncle" over and over like a broken toy. Mrs. Kulkarni was in the next room, she could hear everything, and afterwards she said I handled it well. "You didn't escalate," she said, like that's the highest praise.

And honestly, part of me is glad I stayed calm. That's how you're supposed to handle these things. You listen, you don't react, you let the other person feel heard. Amma taught me that. Baba would say the same—don't give anyone a reason to complain about you.

But another part of me wanted to tell that man his son's handwriting isn't poor because of me, it's because the boy doesn't practise at home. I had the words right there. I didn't say them. I never do. Came home, ate dinner in silence, helped Bhabhi clear the dishes. Now I'm lying in bed typing this and I can still hear his raised voice in my head. I wish it didn't stick.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about raised voices that sticks with you?
entries.response_text: I think it's because I associate them with something going wrong. Like if someone raises their voice at me, I've failed at keeping things smooth. Which I know is ridiculous because he was being unreasonable. But my whole body just goes into this mode where I have to fix it, calm it down, make it okay. I've been like this since I was small. Loud voices in the house meant someone was unhappy and it was everyone's job to make it stop.

entries.initial_entry:
Dadiji called today to discuss Rakshabandhan plans even though it's months away. She wants everyone at her place in Nashik, full family, all the cousins, the usual production. I said of course, yes, we'll be there, before even checking with Baba or Amma. Didn't even occur to me to say maybe or let me check. It was automatic, like a reflex I didn't know I had.

Neha invited me to a weekend workshop on creative teaching methods in Mumbai that same weekend. I said I couldn't go without even thinking about it. She asked why and I just said family commitments. She didn't push but I could tell she wanted to.
entries.nudge_text: Did you want to go to that workshop?
entries.response_text: It doesn't really matter, does it? Dadiji's plans are set. You don't skip family things for a workshop. That's just how it is.

entries.initial_entry:
Something happened at lunch today. A group of the senior teachers were discussing a student in my class—Arjun, the one who's been acting out. They were saying he needs stricter discipline, maybe a note home to his parents. I know Arjun. His parents are going through a separation and he's been coming to school with unwashed uniforms. He doesn't need punishment, he needs someone to be patient with him.

I almost said something. I opened my mouth and Mrs. Deshpande looked at me and I just said "yes ma'am, I'll send the note." I felt sick walking back to my classroom. Still do.
entries.nudge_text: You said you felt sick—what part exactly?
entries.response_text: Because I know I'm right about Arjun and I still didn't say it. It's one thing to stay quiet about syllabus changes or meeting agendas—that's just politics. But this is a child. I'm supposed to be looking out for him. Instead I folded because a teacher with more years looked at me a certain way. What kind of teacher does that make me?

entries.initial_entry:
Writing again because I can't sleep. I keep thinking about Arjun and the note I'm supposed to send. I haven't written it yet. Part of me wants to just talk to his mother directly, gently, without framing it as a disciplinary thing. I could say something like "I've noticed he seems distracted, is everything okay at home." That's not disobeying anyone. That's just being a teacher.

But then I imagine Mrs. Deshpande finding out I went around her suggestion and the thought makes my stomach drop. She's the one who writes my probation review. She's been teaching since before I was born. Who am I to decide my approach is better?

And yet I keep coming back to Arjun's face when I told the class to take out their notebooks yesterday. He didn't have his. He just sat there looking small. I smiled at him and quietly gave him a spare. Nobody noticed. That felt right. The note home doesn't feel right. But doing what feels right and doing what you're told are two different things and I don't know when I got so afraid of the gap between them.

Spoke to Arjun's mother after school today, just casually near the gate. Kept it light, asked how things were going. She got teary and said it's been a hard few months. I told her I'd keep an eye on him and she held my hand and thanked me. I didn't mention the note.

Mrs. Deshpande asked if I'd sent it. I said I spoke with the mother directly and she seemed satisfied. Mrs. Deshpande paused, then said "fine." It wasn't warm but it wasn't a reprimand either. I survived it. My heart was pounding the entire time though.

Friday and I'm sitting on the balcony while everyone watches that serial on TV. Bhabhi asked if I wanted to join and I said in a minute but I just want to sit here with the noise from the street and not talk to anyone for a bit.

This week has been strange. The Arjun thing—I keep turning it over. I did something different. I didn't just follow orders. I found a way to do what I thought was right without directly confronting anyone. Maybe that's the only way I can operate. Not rebellion, not silence, something in between. Like, I bent the expectation without breaking it. And nothing bad happened. Mrs. Deshpande didn't yell at me, Arjun's mother was grateful, the world didn't end.

I think I've been telling myself a story that any deviation from what's expected will result in disaster. Like if I push back even slightly, everything will collapse—my reputation, my relationships, my family's opinion of me. But today I'm sitting here and nothing collapsed. The serial is still playing, Bhabhi is still laughing, Amma just brought me chai without asking. Everything is the same except I feel a tiny bit less compressed. Like there's a millimeter more room inside my chest.
entries.nudge_text: That millimeter of room—what does it feel like?

entries.initial_entry: Cousin Meera announced she's moving to Bangalore for a job. Whole family is upset. Dadiji called it selfish. I sat there thinking she's brave but I said nothing because everyone was already piling on.
entries.nudge_text: You thought she was brave?
entries.response_text: I mean, it takes guts to do something your family doesn't approve of. That's all. I'm not saying she's right or wrong. It's not my place.

entries.initial_entry: Amma asked me at breakfast if I'd ever "do something like Meera." I said no, of course not. She looked relieved. I don't know why that relief felt like a small door closing.

End of term assessments all week. I'm marking papers every night until midnight and still helping Bhabhi with the kids in the morning because she has a cold. I don't mind helping—that's what family does. But I haven't had an hour to myself in I don't know how long. Every minute is either school or home duties.

Neha asked me to grab chai after school and I said I couldn't because I had marking to finish. That was true but also I knew if I went home late Amma would ask where I was and I'd have to explain and it just felt like more effort than it was worth. Sometimes the path of least resistance is the only path I have energy for.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have talked about with Neha if you'd gone?
entries.response_text: Probably everything I can't say at home. She's the only person who gets it—being new, being young, trying to figure out if you're a good teacher or just an obedient one. Last time we talked she said she argued with the vice principal about textbook choices and won. I was equal parts impressed and horrified. I wanted to ask how she does it, how she speaks up without her whole body shutting down. But I ran out of time.

entries.initial_entry:
Report cards went home yesterday and a parent emailed the principal to say I graded her daughter too harshly in Hindi. The principal called me in and asked me to "reconsider." Not directly, not as an order—just that little tilt of the head and "maybe you could take another look." I know what that means. It means change the grade.

The grade is accurate. I checked three times. The girl is struggling with grammar and I wrote detailed comments about how to improve. But the parent is on the school trust and the principal doesn't want problems. So now I'm sitting here trying to decide whether to bump a B+ to an A- to keep everyone comfortable.

I hate this. This is exactly the kind of thing I got into teaching to fight against—marking a child on who her parents are instead of what she's learned. But if I refuse, the principal remembers. My probation review is in August. Is one grade worth the trouble?

I already know what I'm going to do. I'm going to change it and tell myself it doesn't matter in the long run, and feel this same sick feeling I felt about Arjun, and go to bed and wake up tomorrow the same person who keeps the peace at any cost. And one day I'll wonder when I stopped trusting my own judgment. Or maybe I'll just stop noticing. That's the scarier thought.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you not to change that grade?
entries.response_text: Someone to tell me it's okay to hold my ground. Which is pathetic because I shouldn't need permission. But I've spent my whole life being told that keeping things smooth is the right thing to do, and when every single person around you believes that, it's hard to trust the one voice saying otherwise—especially when that voice is just mine.

entries.initial_entry: My cousin Luc called to tell me his wife is pregnant and they just closed on a house in Barrhaven. Good for them. I told him things are going well with the courier gigs and he got quiet in that way people do when they're trying not to say what they actually think.
entries.nudge_text: What part of that silence bugged you most?
entries.response_text: Probably that he didn't even bother arguing with me about it anymore. Like he's decided I'm a lost cause or something. A year ago he would've pushed back, told me to finish school. Now it's just quiet. I don't know if that's worse.

entries.initial_entry:
Picked up a double shift on the app Thursday because rent's due. Did about fourteen hours between rides and deliveries. Ate a shawarma in the car between pickups around Plateau. Phone died at 9pm so I had to call it early anyway. Made enough to cover rent plus a bit extra.

Maman texted asking if I'm coming to Tante Diane's birthday thing in October. It's in Sudbury obviously. I said I'd try. She sent back a thumbs up which from her basically means she already knows I won't make it. I'll probably go though. Diane's turning seventy and she's the one who taught me to skate.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if you skip Diane's seventieth?
entries.response_text: I'm not going to skip it. I just said I'd try.

entries.initial_entry:
Switched my Tuesday route to cover the McGill area because a couple of the regulars tip well over there. Figured out that if I stage near the Roddick Gates between 11:30 and 1 I can grab three or four lunch deliveries without killing my legs on the hills. Small optimization but it adds up over the week.

Called Grand-mère after my shift. She was watching her game show and half-listening but that's fine, that's how she is. Told her about the McGill route and she laughed and said her father used to deliver ice blocks on Sherbrooke Street with a horse cart. She tells me that every time. I don't mind hearing it again.

Guy at a red light today rolled down his window and asked if I was hiring for my "company" because of the courier bag. Made me laugh. Told him it's just me and my bike. But it stuck with me after. I've been doing this almost two years now and I never once had to ask anyone's permission to take a day off or switch a route. That part still feels right even when the money doesn't.

There's something about the rhythm I've built here. Mornings I pick my own routes. Afternoons I can stop if I want to or push through. When I was in the business program in Sudbury every hour was accounted for by somebody else's schedule. I couldn't breathe in that. Maybe some people need that structure. I just know I'm not one of them.
entries.nudge_text: When the money doesn't feel right, what does that actually look like?
entries.response_text: It's not dramatic or anything. Just some weeks are tight. I figure it out.

entries.initial_entry:
Maman called tonight and it turned into a whole thing. She wants me to come home for Thanksgiving and also stay the extra week to help Uncle René move out of his apartment because his knees are bad. I said I can't do a week and a half off the app, I'll lose my momentum and the algorithm buries you if you go inactive. She hit me with the "family is more important than an app, Marcus" line and I kind of lost it.

I didn't yell or anything but I got sharp with her. Told her she doesn't understand how this works, that it's not like taking vacation days from a normal job, there's no paid leave, no one covers your shifts. She went quiet. I hate when I do that to her. She's not wrong that René needs help. I just can't be the one who drops everything every time. But saying that out loud to her felt awful.
entries.nudge_text: You said she's not wrong. So who is?
entries.response_text: Nobody's wrong, that's the problem. She's right that René needs the help and that family should show up. I'm right that I can't just vanish for ten days without it costing me real money I don't have a cushion for. It's just one of those things where both sides are true and somebody still ends up disappointed.

entries.initial_entry: My cousin Luc called to tell me his wife is pregnant and they just closed on a house in Barrhaven. Good for them. I told him things are going well with the courier gigs and he got quiet in that way people do when they're trying not to say what they actually think.
entries.nudge_text: What part of that silence bugged you most?
entries.response_text: Probably that he didn't even bother arguing with me about it anymore. Like he's decided I'm a lost cause or something. A year ago he would've pushed back, told me to finish school. Now it's just quiet. I don't know if that's worse.

entries.initial_entry:
My mother-in-law came by today and made some comment about the stack of translation documents on the kitchen table. Something about how I should focus on the kids instead of "outside projects." I smiled and said I'd clear them after lunch. But it sat with me the rest of the afternoon.

She doesn't understand what those documents are. Each one is someone's asylum application or work permit or medical form that they can't read because nobody thought to print it in a language they actually speak. I can do that. I can sit at my kitchen table after the kids are asleep and make that one thing less terrifying for someone. And my kids are fed, they're healthy, they're fine. I don't need to justify this to anyone, but I keep feeling like I have to.
entries.nudge_text: You said you don't need to justify it. So why does it still sit with you?
entries.response_text: Because she's not wrong that there are only so many hours. I chose to stay home for the kids. But the work I left behind didn't stop needing to be done. I just can't shake the feeling that I'm supposed to pick one and be at peace with it, and I haven't figured out how to do that yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Residents association meeting tonight about the new recycling sorting rules the district is rolling out. I volunteered to run the information session for our building because nobody else would and the flyer they sent was confusing — mixing up the plastic codes and getting the compost pickup schedule wrong. Spent two hours after the kids' bedtime redoing the flyer in Korean and English since we have several non-Korean families in the complex.

Hyun-woo got home late again and asked why I was still up. I showed him the flyer and he said "can't someone else do this?" He wasn't being mean. He just doesn't get why I'd spend a free evening on something that could theoretically be someone else's problem.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you'd just left the flyer as it was?
entries.response_text: The wrong information would go out and our building would get fined again, and the two Filipina families on the fourth floor who can barely read the Korean notices would have no idea what changed. I keep thinking about Mrs. Reyes carrying her bags to the wrong bin last month looking confused and embarrassed. Nobody helped her. I can't see that and not do something. It physically bothers me to walk past a problem I could fix.

entries.initial_entry:
Took the kids to the park near the river this morning. Eun-bi wanted to feed the ducks and Jun-ho wanted to throw rocks, so we compromised — ducks first, then rocks at a different spot. Standard negotiation. It was actually nice until I noticed a group of volunteers doing a river cleanup downstream. They had those grabber tools and black trash bags and matching vests from some environmental group I didn't recognize.

I stood there watching them for probably five minutes while the kids played. Part of me wanted to walk over and ask how to join. I used to do cleanups like that every month before Jun-ho was born. Our old NGO partnered with a river conservation group in Mapo-gu and I'd spend whole Saturdays hauling plastic out of the Cheonggyecheon tributaries. Felt like I was doing something tangible, something I could point at and say today this stretch of water is cleaner because I was here.

Now I watch from the playground. I'm not complaining about my life — the kids are wonderful and I chose this. But there's this gap between what I used to be able to do and what my days look like now. I don't know what to do with that gap except notice it and move on. Jun-ho threw a rock into the water and it made a perfect splash and he was so proud. I clapped and said "good one" and we went home for lunch.

Can't sleep. Kept thinking about the river cleanup people. Found their group online — they meet every Saturday, which is the one morning Hyun-woo is reliably home with the kids. I could go. But then I'd be giving up the only morning I have for grocery shopping, meal prep, and the laundry pile that's been staring at me all week.

My friend Ji-yeon texted asking if I wanted to join her yoga class on Saturday mornings. Everyone keeps offering me ways to spend my time that are about me. Yoga, self-care, "you need a break." But I don't want a break. I want to be useful beyond these walls. That probably sounds ungrateful. I have a warm apartment and two healthy children and a husband who comes home every night. But I keep reading the news about the factory fire in Gimpo where those migrant workers were locked in because the exits were blocked and I can't just — I can't just do yoga about it.

Went to the refugee resettlement office for my translation shift. They had a backlog of about forty documents — medical intake forms, mostly. One was for a family from Myanmar with three kids under ten. The mother had written additional notes in the margins in Burmese that I couldn't read, so I flagged them for the Burmese translator. Just small handwritten lines. Probably questions she was too afraid to ask out loud. That kind of thing breaks something in me every time.

Came home and Eun-bi had drawn a picture of our family at school. She drew me holding a big stack of papers. Hyun-woo laughed and said "even she knows." I laughed too but it landed weird. Like even my six-year-old sees me as the woman with the papers instead of — I don't know. I don't know what I expected her to draw. I am the woman with the papers. I just wish that didn't feel like it needed defending.
entries.nudge_text: What did you want her to draw instead?

entries.initial_entry: Both kids had fevers last night so I was up every two hours with wet towels and children's Tylenol. Jun-ho finally slept at 4am. I skipped the recycling committee meeting and I feel guilty about it even though I know that's absurd.
entries.nudge_text: Why does skipping it feel like a failure?
entries.response_text: Because I told Mrs. Park I'd present the updated sorting schedule and now she has to scramble. I know she'll manage. I know my kids come first. But letting someone down — even for a completely valid reason — just eats at me. It always has.

entries.initial_entry:
Kids are better. Jun-ho's fever broke around noon and Eun-bi was already bouncing off the walls by breakfast. Hyun-woo took the afternoon off which almost never happens, so I had about three hours to myself. I should have napped. Instead I sat on the balcony with my laptop and read through the news.

There's a piece about a proposed bill that would make it harder for undocumented workers to access emergency medical care. I read it twice. Then I started drafting a letter to our local representative, citing cases I'd seen at the resettlement office — anonymized, of course. I wrote about the Myanmar family's intake forms and the gap between what the system promises and what reaches people. It took me two hours and I don't even know if anyone will read it.

Hyun-woo came in and saw me typing and said nothing. He just made me tea and left it on the desk. I think he's given up trying to understand why I spend my rare free hours this way. He's not angry. He's just resigned. And I can feel the distance in that resignation. Like he's accepted that this is who I am but he'd prefer a version of me who could sit on the balcony and just look at the sky. Some days I'd prefer that version too. But I open the news and there's another factory fire or another deportation story or another kid who fell through the cracks and I can't look at the sky. The sky is not the problem.

Sent the letter. Also emailed the river cleanup group about joining their Saturday rotation starting August. Hyun-woo agreed to handle Saturday mornings. Feels like a small step but at least it's forward.
First Saturday cleanup. Hauled three bags of plastic from a 200-meter stretch near Banpo Bridge. Got home muddy and sunburned. Eun-bi asked if I was "saving the river" and I said I was trying.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel being back out there?

entries.initial_entry:
Had my quarterly review today. My director said I "exceeded expectations" and hinted at a senior manager role opening up in Q2. I should feel relieved—this is exactly what I've been working toward for two years. And I do feel good about it. But there's this part of me that immediately started calculating: if I get promoted, how long until I'm considered for the director track? It's like the goalpost moves the second I reach it.

I called Baba tonight to tell him. He was proud, I could hear it in his voice even though he just said "Alhamdulillah, keep your head down and work hard." That's his way. I think he worries I'll get complacent. I won't.
entries.nudge_text: What would reaching the 'final' goalpost actually look like for you?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't know if there is one.

entries.initial_entry: Long week. Two team members called in sick, had to cover their workload on top of mine. Hit all deadlines anyway. Didn't sleep much but the project shipped on time. Director noticed.

I found out today that Ahmad—who joined the company six months after me—is also being considered for the senior manager position. We have the same title. Same level. But he's been here less time, has less experience, and somehow we're competing for the same promotion.

I know I shouldn't compare. I know the advice is "focus on your own race." But how can I not? We sit in the same open office. I see his calendar, his meetings, how he operates. He's good at being visible. Always volunteering for the high-profile projects that get executive attention, while I'm handling the operations that actually keep this company running.

Maybe that's the game. Maybe I've been playing it wrong. I thought doing excellent work would speak for itself. But excellent work that nobody sees might as well not exist.

I couldn't focus on anything else today. Kept replaying the conversation with HR, analyzing every word. They said "both strong candidates." What does that mean? Is it 50-50? Or are they just being diplomatic?

My stomach has been in knots since morning. I hate that this affects me this much.
entries.nudge_text: It sounds like doing excellent work and being recognized for it might require different strategies. How do you feel about that?
entries.response_text: Frustrated. It feels unfair. But I need to adapt.

entries.initial_entry:
End of year push. Everyone wants everything done before the holidays. I've been in the office until 9pm every day this week. My flatmate asked if I still live here—I've barely seen him.

The promotion decision got pushed to January. "Too much going on, we'll revisit in the new year." I don't know if that's good or bad. More time to prove myself, or more time to overthink everything?

Mama called asking about my holiday plans. I told her I'm staying in Dubai—too much work, flights are expensive. She was quiet for a long time. I could tell she was disappointed. My sister's engagement party is on the 27th. I should be there. But the thought of being away while Ahmad is here, possibly making moves... I can't.

I don't even know if staying will make a difference. But leaving feels like a risk I can't take.
entries.nudge_text: Missing your sister's engagement for work that might not even matter—how does that sit with you?
entries.response_text: It's just one event. I'll make it up to her later.

entries.initial_entry:
I video-called into the engagement party. It was awkward—the connection kept dropping, everyone was loud and happy and I was sitting alone in my apartment with my laptop. Layla smiled when she saw me but I could tell she'd wanted me there in person. Mama barely looked at the camera.

My cousin Tariq asked why I couldn't "just take a few days off." I said I had responsibilities. He laughed and said "Dubai changed you."

He doesn't understand. None of them do. They have stable government jobs back home, houses their families helped them buy. I'm building from nothing in a place where your visa is tied to your employment. If I lose this job, I have 30 days to find another or leave. That's not paranoia, that's reality.

But sitting here tonight, in the quiet after the call ended, I wonder if I'm using that reality as an excuse to avoid other fears.

New year. Everyone's posting about fresh starts and intentions. I don't know what mine are yet. Get the promotion, obviously. But what else?

I went for a walk along the Marina last night at midnight. All these people celebrating, fireworks going off over the water. I stood there alone and felt... nothing? Not sad, not happy. Just watching. Like I was outside of my own life looking in.

I keep thinking about what Tariq said. "Dubai changed you." Maybe it did. Or maybe this is just who I become when I'm in survival mode. Head down, eyes on the goal, don't look around too much because the periphery is full of things that will slow you down.

Is that bad? Or is that just what it takes?
entries.nudge_text: When you say 'survival mode'—what are you surviving?
entries.response_text: The uncertainty. The feeling that nothing is really mine.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a one-on-one with my director today. She was more candid than usual. Said she appreciates my "operational excellence" but that senior leadership roles require "strategic visibility." She suggested I present at the next all-hands meeting.

Part of me bristled at the feedback. I've been doing critical work, keeping the machine running while others polish their personal brands. But another part of me heard what she was actually saying: the game has rules, and I've been refusing to play by some of them.

I've always thought self-promotion was distasteful. Where I come from, you don't brag about your work—you let it speak. Boasting is seen as shallow. But I'm not where I come from anymore. I'm in a place where speaking up isn't bragging; it's survival.

I signed up for the presentation slot. It felt uncomfortable, almost wrong. But I did it.

I also booked a flight home for Mama's birthday in February. Non-refundable. I need to stop letting work consume everything. The promotion matters, but it can't be the only thing that matters.
entries.nudge_text: What made you decide to book that non-refundable ticket?
entries.response_text: I realized I was building a life I wouldn't want to live.

entries.initial_entry:
Did the all-hands presentation. Nervous beforehand but it went well. Several people came up after to compliment the insights. Ahmad was one of them—said he learned things about the operations he didn't know.

Strange to think of him as just a colleague now, not a threat. The competition hasn't changed. But my relationship to it has, slightly.

I got the promotion.

The email came this afternoon. Senior Operations Manager, effective February 1st. A 15% salary increase and a team of twelve direct reports instead of five.

I sat in the conference room alone for ten minutes after reading it, just letting it settle. Two years of work. All those late nights, the stress, the sacrifices. It paid off.

I called Baba first. He said "This is good. Keep going." Classic Baba. Then I called Mama and she cried, which made me tear up too even though I tried not to. "I'm so proud of you, habibi. When are you coming home?" I told her February, for her birthday, and she made me promise three times.

Tonight I'm going to dinner with colleagues to celebrate. I picked a nice place, not the most expensive because I'm still me, but nice enough to mark the occasion.

Sitting here now, I'm thinking about what I wrote a few weeks ago—about not knowing what the final goalpost looks like, about whether there even is one. I still don't know the answer. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe that's okay.

What I do know is that I can't let the pursuit of the next thing erase the moment when I actually get something I worked for. Tonight, I'm going to let myself feel this. Tomorrow the new worries will start: proving myself in the senior role, saving for the apartment, thinking about what's next. But tonight, just tonight, I made it.
