I didn't know that about my grandmother. All those years cooking her recipes and I never knew she had a dream that got shut down.

Doesn't change the practical reality though. The film industry is brutal and unpredictable. I told Vikram I'd look into some programs with him, actual structured ones, not YouTube tutorials. If he's serious, there should be a proper path. Priya thinks I'm trying to control it and maybe she's half right, but I can't just say go for it with no plan. That's not how I'm built.

Work was uneventful. Accepted the payments integration lead role. Raghavan seemed relieved. Seventeen years and I'm still the person they hand things to when they need it done without drama.
Board approved the new distribution hub in Monterrey. I'll be flying up there twice a month starting in August to oversee the build-out. Finally something different on the calendar.
entries.nudge_text: Twice a month to Monterrey—are you relieved or just restless?
entries.response_text: Restless, if I'm honest. The office routine was starting to feel like wearing a shirt that's too tight. Even the commute, the same faces at the same intersections. Monterrey gives me something to look forward to.

entries.initial_entry:
First scouting trip to Monterrey was yesterday—flew in early, drove around the industrial park, met with the contractor, walked the empty warehouse space. The floor was just concrete and dust and that particular echo you get in big empty buildings. Something about it felt like possibility. Like the first page of a notebook.

Had dinner at a little place the contractor recommended, carnitas that reminded me of a trip I took to Michoacán twenty years ago. Called Lucía from the hotel balcony and told her about the mountains visible from the site. She said she might come on the next trip. That made the whole day feel complete.
entries.nudge_text: Would having Lucía there change how you experience it?
entries.response_text: Probably. She notices things I miss. But honestly I was just glad she's interested—she's been busy with her own work lately and I wasn't sure she'd want to. Anyway, the site is the main thing right now.

entries.initial_entry: Back in the office. Same view from my desk. Already counting days until the next Monterrey trip.
entries.nudge_text: What's so unbearable about the view from your desk?

entries.initial_entry:
My nephew Tomás visited for lunch today. He's 28, just got promoted at the same insurance company where he started as an intern six years ago. Same company, same building, probably the same lunch spot every day. He was showing me photos from his vacation—Cancún, the same resort his parents always went to—and talking about his ten-year plan. A house in the suburbs. Maybe a cabin somewhere for weekends.

I nodded and said the right things but inside I felt this churning. Not judgment exactly, more like... incomprehension? How does someone his age already know where they want to be in ten years? When I was 28 I was on my third city and had just quit a job because my boss wanted me to specialize.

He asked me for career advice. I didn't know what to say. What works for me would be terrible advice for him. "Keep moving" doesn't help someone who wants to stay put. So I told him to trust himself, which felt hollow. The whole conversation left me feeling like I'm from a different species.

Lucía says Tomás reminds her of her brother, who's very happy with his predictable life. Maybe that's true. Maybe I'm the odd one. But walking back to the office after lunch, the city felt gray even though the sun was out.
entries.nudge_text: The gray city—is that about Tomás or something else?
entries.response_text: Both, probably. I don't know. Sometimes I think I'm allergic to contentment, if that makes sense. Never mind, it's probably just that I need another coffee.

entries.initial_entry: Someone at the board meeting asked if I'm "committed to staying" with the company through the Monterrey project. As if my track record of getting things done isn't enough. I've never left a project half-finished in my life.
entries.nudge_text: But you've thought about leaving, haven't you?
entries.response_text: Of course I have. I always think about what's next. That doesn't mean I'll leave before the work is done. They're two different things. The question just felt like an accusation.

entries.initial_entry: Took Lucía dancing last night at that new salsa club in Condesa. We stayed until 1am. Good reminder that variety doesn't always require a boarding pass.
My brother called to tell me our father's old house in Guadalajara sold. He implied I should feel sad about it. I don't. It was just a building—the memories are in my head, not in the walls.
entries.nudge_text: Does it bother you that it doesn't bother you?
entries.response_text: A little, actually. Everyone else in the family seems to treat places like anchors. I've always treated them like way stations. Sometimes I wonder if I'm missing something they feel, or if I just feel it differently.

entries.initial_entry: Third trip to Monterrey. The warehouse walls are up now. I stood where the loading dock will be and watched trucks pass on the highway. Different view from two weeks ago.
entries.nudge_text: What does it feel like, watching something you started take shape?
entries.response_text: Good. It feels good. Though honestly, this phase—when it's under construction—is always my favorite part. Once it's running smoothly, that's when I start getting restless again. But that's a problem for later.

entries.initial_entry:
Long day. Flight delayed, then a three-hour meeting about supply chain redundancies that felt like it could have been an email. Sat through presentation after presentation about backup plans and contingencies and worst-case scenarios. Everyone nodding seriously.

I get it. Redundancy is important. Contingencies save companies. But my God, the energy in that room was like a waiting room at a dentist's office. No one asked what opportunities we might be missing, what new routes we could try, what experiments might be worth running. Just risk, risk, risk, mitigation, mitigation, mitigation.

Came back to the hotel and just sat on the bed for twenty minutes without moving. Not tired exactly—more like deflated. Lucía texted asking how it went and I didn't know how to answer. "Fine" felt like a lie but "soul-crushing" felt dramatic. Said "long" and left it at that.

She's meeting me here tomorrow for the weekend. We're going to drive up to a canyon she read about. That's what I'm holding onto right now.
entries.nudge_text: Is the canyon trip rescuing you or just postponing something?
entries.response_text: Rescuing. Definitely rescuing. I don't think there's anything to postpone—the meetings are what they are, I just need something to balance them out. The canyon is balance. Lucía is balance. Without those counterweights I'd probably quit every job within six months.

entries.initial_entry:
The canyon was worth it. We drove two hours on winding roads, stopped at a mirador where you could see layers of rock going back millions of years. Lucía had brought a picnic—tortas and fruit and those little marzipan candies I like. We sat there for an hour just watching shadows move across the canyon walls.

On the drive back she asked me what I wanted to do when I retire. I laughed and said "not retire." But she pressed, and I tried to actually think about it. What would I do if I didn't have to work?

I think I'd travel. Not vacation-travel but exploratory travel—learn a language, live somewhere for a few months, move on. Maybe write about what I see. The problem is, I've been saying some version of that for thirty years, and somehow there's always another project, another opportunity, another reason to stay in the game a little longer.

Lucía said she'd come with me. She wasn't being hypothetical—she was offering. That felt like something. I'm still thinking about it.

Performance reviews this week. I gave mostly good feedback to my team, but one conversation stuck with me. Daniela, one of the younger project coordinators, asked me how I've "stayed motivated for so long." The way she said it made me sound like some kind of marathon runner who refuses to stop.

I told her the truth—that I've never felt like I was running a marathon. More like a series of sprints toward interesting things. She looked confused. I tried to explain that what keeps me going is that each project, each challenge, feels genuinely different, even when the surface looks similar. The Monterrey hub isn't like anything I've done before because the people are different, the problems are different, the city is different. She nodded but I could tell she didn't quite get it.

Later I wondered if I was making sense or just rationalizing. Maybe to someone like Daniela, who's building expertise in one domain, my approach looks scattered. Maybe it is scattered. My brother would say so—he's been with the same company for twenty-five years and can't understand why I don't value that kind of depth.

But when I think about doing one thing for twenty-five years, I feel a kind of panic. Not fear exactly, more like suffocation. Is that a character flaw or just how I'm wired? I've never been able to answer that question. Maybe it doesn't matter as long as I'm still curious about what comes next.
entries.nudge_text: What would change if you decided it's just how you're wired?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'd stop second-guessing myself every time someone looks at me like I'm an alien for not wanting to settle down. Or maybe the second-guessing is part of the wiring too. Anyway, the Monterrey hub opens in October. That's what matters right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Today was the monthly temple committee meeting and I just cannot shake this strange feeling. Meena aunty said something about how "some people like to take charge of everything" and I know she was talking about me. Everyone was looking at their phones or the floor. I have been running this committee for six years and we have never had a failed event, never run out of food, never had volunteers not show up. But suddenly I am the problem?

I stayed quiet. What else could I do? If I defended myself it would look exactly like what she was accusing me of. But sitting there pretending I did not hear her felt like swallowing glass. My husband said I should just ignore it, that Meena aunty is just jealous because her daughter-in-law moved her out of the main house. Maybe. But that does not explain why no one else spoke up for me.

The thing is, I take charge because if I do not, things fall apart. Remember the Holi celebration last year when I was at Ravi's parents' house? Complete disaster. Cold food, wrong decorations, crying children. But sure, I am the one who is too controlling.
entries.nudge_text: What held you back from responding?

entries.initial_entry: Dropped off three containers of biryani to the Sharma family down the street. Their son just had surgery and the mother looked like she had not slept in days. She cried when she saw me at the door. This is what it is all about, really. Showing up when people need you.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel when she cried?
entries.response_text: Honestly, it reminded me of when Amma had her first stroke and neighbors brought us food for weeks. You never forget who showed up. I want to be that person for others. Sometimes I think this is the only thing I am truly good at.

entries.initial_entry: Priya came home crying from school because her friend group excluded her from a birthday party. I spent an hour consoling her but the whole time I kept thinking about that committee meeting. Is this what it feels like? To be pushed out? Too tired to think about this more.
entries.nudge_text: Are you thinking about yourself or Priya?
entries.response_text: Priya, obviously. She is thirteen and everything feels like the end of the world at that age. I am just tired.

entries.initial_entry:
Ravi's sister called asking if we can host his parents for six weeks instead of two this summer. She has a work trip to Singapore that "cannot be rescheduled." I said yes before even thinking about it. What else could I say?

Now I am mentally reorganizing everything. Priya's room will have to be cleared for them. I will need to cook separate low-sodium meals for Appa. The bathroom schedule becomes a nightmare with seven people. But Lakshmi made it sound like I was the only solution, and I suppose I am.
entries.nudge_text: Did you want to say something else?
entries.response_text: Wanting and doing are different things. Lakshmi works, I do not. She has meetings in Singapore, I have school drop-offs. The math is simple. But yes, fine, I wanted to say "let me check with Ravi first" like a normal person. Instead I just absorbed it like always.

entries.initial_entry:
Nothing happened today, just the usual. But I have been thinking about something my cousin Anita said when she visited last month. She asked why I "gave up" my career to stay home, as if I am sitting here watching television all day. I manage a household, a family, a community. I coordinate schedules for five people, handle three different school systems' requirements, manage all the finances, plan every festival, and support whoever needs me.

Ravi could not find his passport last week. Who found it in twenty minutes? Me. Priya needed a costume for her Bharatanatyam recital. Who stayed up sewing until midnight? Me. Vikram's college applications needed someone to proofread seven essays. Me.

But because there is no salary, it is not real work. Because I do not have a LinkedIn profile anymore, I have "given up." Anita has a corner office and takes two vacations a year. Good for her. She also calls her mother once a month and sends birthday cards late. I am not saying one is better. I am just saying they are different, and I am tired of having to defend my choice.

Committee met again. I volunteered to step back from leading the Navratri planning. Let someone else handle the vendor contracts and volunteer schedules. Meena aunty looked surprised. Everyone did, actually.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel to step back?

entries.initial_entry: Made chai for myself this afternoon and sat on the balcony for fifteen minutes doing nothing. The house was quiet. Just me and the crows arguing in the mango tree. Cannot remember the last time I did this.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different?

entries.initial_entry:
Vikram called from university just to talk. Not because he needed money or help with something. Just to tell me about his professor who reminds him of his grandfather, and about the dosa place he found near campus. We talked for forty-five minutes.

I think I did something right with him. He is kind. He notices people. Last month he organized a food drive at his dorm because he saw international students struggling during spring break when the dining hall closed. He did not tell me about it - his roommate's mother mentioned it. That is the part that makes me proudest. He does not do it for recognition.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think shaped him that way?
entries.response_text: I wish I could take credit but honestly, I think he just watched. Watched me drop off food to the Sharmas. Watched his grandmother live with us for three years. Watched Ravi stay late at office but still make every recital and cricket match. Children learn what they see, not what you tell them.

entries.initial_entry: Second entry today. In-laws arrived this evening, four days early. Lakshmi's flight got moved up. I had just finished cleaning but the guest room mattress has not been aired. Amma looked at the sheets and I know she noticed. Too tired to care right now.

Amma has been here four days and I am already losing my mind. She reorganized my spice cabinet. MY spice cabinet that I have arranged the same way for fifteen years. She said she was "helping" because it was "inefficient." I have managed three children, a husband, a house, and an entire temple committee with that "inefficient" cabinet.

I did not say anything. What would be the point? She is seventy-two and she is a guest. But Ravi saw my face when I opened the cabinet door and he just shrugged. Shrugged! As if his mother rearranging my kitchen is completely normal and I should just accept it.

The worst part is that she is not wrong. Her arrangement probably is more efficient. But that is not the point. The point is that this is my home, my space, the one domain where I should have some say. But apparently even here I am meant to just smile and adjust. I made everyone's favorite dinner anyway because what else am I going to do, let everyone starve over cumin placement?
entries.nudge_text: What would having a say look like?
entries.response_text: It does not matter. Four more weeks and then things go back to normal. I just need to survive until then.

entries.initial_entry:
Priya asked me today why I always have to be "in charge of everything." She was annoyed because I reminded her three times about her homework. Three times! Because she was on her phone and the deadline was in two hours. But apparently I am controlling.

I told her that someone has to keep track of things. She rolled her eyes and said "you could just let me fail once." What kind of mother lets her child fail on purpose? I did not hover over Vikram and look how he turned out - responsible, organized, successful. But Priya is different. She needs more reminders. If I do not push her, she will coast.

Ravi thinks I should "give her space." Easy for him to say. He is at office twelve hours a day. He does not see the daily negotiation of getting this child to do anything. When she fails a test, no one will ask where her father was. They will ask what I was doing.

But maybe everyone is right and I am the problem. Maybe I should just stop caring. Let the laundry pile up, let the homework go undone, let the spice cabinet stay however Amma wants it. Then we will see how things run.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you did stop?
entries.response_text: Chaos. Or maybe nothing. Maybe everything would be fine and I would realize I have been making myself essential when I am actually just in the way. That thought is worse than the chaos, honestly.

entries.initial_entry:
Amma and I had an argument today. Or not an argument exactly - she does not argue. She just makes observations. "In my house we always did it this way." "You work so hard, beta, but maybe if you organized differently..." "Priya seems stressed, have you thought about letting her breathe?"

I finally said something. I told her that I appreciate her concern but this is my house and my family and I have been managing fine for twenty years. She got very quiet and went to her room. Ravi says I was too harsh. Maybe. But she has been here two weeks and every day there is a new suggestion, a new correction, a new way I am falling short.

The thing is, she ran her own household for forty years. I understand the need to feel useful, to have a role. When she moved in with Lakshmi she lost all of that. So she comes here and tries to find her place by improving mine. I see the pattern. I am not blind to it.

But seeing the pattern does not make it easier to live with. And I keep wondering - is this my future? Will I be the mother-in-law who cannot stop fixing things because fixing things is all I know how to do? Will Priya write journal entries about me reorganizing her kitchen?
entries.nudge_text: What would you want to be remembered for?
entries.response_text: Not for being right. Not for having the most organized spice cabinet or the best-run committee. I want Vikram and Priya and even little Arun to remember that I showed up. That when things fell apart, I was there. That I fed people not because I needed to control the menu but because I believe you cannot love someone properly if you let them go hungry. That is why I do all of this. I think I forgot that somewhere along the way.

entries.initial_entry:
So I just got back from the department mixer and my head is swimming with possibilities. Priya from the neuroscience lab was talking about this crazy project they're starting with VR and neural mapping and I kept thinking why am I not doing THAT. My current project is fine, it's perfectly fine, it's just—sequencing data, more sequencing data, writing scripts to process sequencing data. Rinse and repeat for the next three years apparently.

Then again I did just switch labs six months ago so maybe I should stay put? But Dr. Mehta's stuff is so much more interesting. He was at the mixer too and mentioned they're doing fieldwork in Costa Rica next semester. COSTA RICA. I haven't been anywhere since Vietnam and my feet are literally itching.

I talked to Aditya after and he thinks I'm insane. "Bro you can't switch labs again, what will your committee think?" He's probably right. He's always right and it's annoying. But also he's been in the same lab since day one doing the same protein folding stuff and seems perfectly happy which I genuinely don't understand. How does someone just... do one thing?

Anyway the mixer had good samosas at least. Small wins.
entries.nudge_text: What's the Costa Rica project actually about?
entries.response_text: Honestly? I don't even fully know. Something about tracking disease vectors in rainforest ecosystems. I think what got me was just... the image of it. Being in a rainforest with equipment, collecting real samples, not just sitting in front of a terminal. I've been in climate-controlled rooms for two years straight. Maybe it's not about the science at all, maybe I just need to feel like I'm actually DOING something with my body, not just my brain. That's probably a terrible reason to uproot everything again.

entries.initial_entry:
I can't believe my parents called during lab meeting. Like yes I know I told them to call anytime but I didn't mean DURING LAB MEETING. Had to decline and now I have three missed calls and a WhatsApp message that just says "call us urgent" which probably means they found out about the Vietnam credit card bill I thought I'd hidden.

But that's not even the real issue. The real issue is that Dr. Sharma asked me about my thesis timeline today and I had nothing to say. Literally nothing. I don't know what my thesis IS yet. Everyone else seems to have these perfectly formed research questions and I'm over here like "I don't know, something with genomics maybe? Can I take a gap year?" Obviously I didn't say that last part out loud.

The worst part is I know I'm smart enough to do this. I know I could focus if I wanted to. But I sit down to work on my analysis and within twenty minutes I'm looking at flights to New Zealand or researching pottery classes or wondering if I should learn to surf. My brain just doesn't want to stay in one place. Sometimes I feel like there's something wrong with me, like everyone else got a stability gene I missed.

Amma's going to be so disappointed when she finds out I haven't figured anything out. She keeps telling everyone back home that her son is getting a PhD in America. What she doesn't say is that her son might not actually finish it.
entries.nudge_text: That stability gene thing—do you actually believe that?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Sometimes. Like when I watch Aditya work on the same problem for eight hours straight and I can barely sit still for forty-five minutes without needing to get up, get chai, check my phone, look out the window. It makes me feel broken. But then I also think—maybe I'm just not doing the right thing? Like maybe if I found the RIGHT project I'd be obsessed with it? I've been obsessed before. I once spent three straight days learning to solve a Rubik's cube when I was fifteen, didn't eat properly, didn't sleep. So it's possible. I just haven't found the research equivalent of that yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Okay so something weird happened today. I was in the library trying to read papers and I got a LinkedIn message from this startup in Singapore. They saw my GitHub and want to interview me for a bioinformatics engineer role. Not a PhD program, just a job. With a salary. And the office is in Singapore which—I've never been to Singapore.

I know I should just delete it. I'm in the middle of a PhD. You don't just leave a PhD for a random job opportunity. That's what my parents would say. That's what Sharma would say. That's what everyone would say.

But I haven't deleted it yet. It's just sitting there in my inbox like a little door that's cracked open. The company does something with personalized medicine and CRISPR delivery. Actually kind of interesting? I watched their YT videos for like an hour instead of reading papers. The office has this rooftop garden and the team seems young and the founder used to be at MIT before she moved back to Asia.

I messaged Rani about it—she's my cousin in Mumbai who actually works in tech—and she was like "bro at least do the interview, what do you have to lose?" And I was like "my entire academic career?" And she was like "your career isn't a vase, it won't break if you explore options."

I don't know. I'm probably making this into a bigger thing than it is. It's just one LinkedIn message.
entries.nudge_text: You spent an hour on their videos—what kept you watching?
entries.response_text: I think it was the founder's story actually. She was in a top PhD program, left after her second year, started this company, and now they're actually helping people? Like real patients. Not in ten years when a paper gets published and maybe influences a treatment, but NOW. She kept talking about "velocity" in one of the videos—how fast they move from idea to implementation. And I realized I've never experienced that. Academia is so slow. Everything takes years. Peer review takes months. I feel like I'm moving through honey while the rest of the world is running. Maybe I'm just impatient. Maybe impatience is a character flaw I need to fix. Or maybe... I'm in the wrong environment.

entries.initial_entry:
I did the interview. First round, just a thirty-minute video call with their head of engineering. I was so nervous I almost backed out twice but Rani talked me off the ledge. And it went... well? Like, really well? She asked me about my GitHub projects and actually seemed interested in the weird evolutionary algorithm thing I built for fun. In my PhD no one cares about that stuff, it's not "publishable."

She mentioned they're building something new—can't share details yet—but they need someone who can "think sideways." I've never been told I think sideways as a compliment before. Usually it's more like "Arjun, can you stay focused on the problem at hand?" There's a second round next week. I haven't told anyone except Rani.
entries.nudge_text: Why keep it secret?
entries.response_text: Because the moment I tell someone in the program, it becomes real. And then I'll have to defend it. Explain it. Justify it. And I don't know if I can. How do I explain to Dr. Sharma that I might leave his lab—the lab I specifically transferred into six months ago—for a startup job? He'll think I'm not serious. Everyone will think I'm not serious. And maybe they're right? Maybe I'm just chasing the next shiny thing again and this isn't any different from switching labs or going to Vietnam or any of the other times I've... anyway. One interview at a time.

entries.initial_entry: Second interview scheduled. My heart is doing weird things. Also I definitely bombed my committee meeting today because I couldn't focus but whatever.
entries.nudge_text: What happened in the committee meeting?

entries.initial_entry:
Timeline of the past week:
- Monday: Second round interview. Went four hours. Met six people. Took a technical assessment that was actually fun.
- Tuesday: Couldn't focus on lab work. Kept checking email.
- Wednesday: They asked for references. Panicked. Gave Rani's contact and my undergrad advisor.
- Thursday: Dr. Sharma asked if everything was okay. Apparently I've been "distracted." Lied and said family stuff.
- Friday: Offer came in.

They want me to start in January. Salary is more than my stipend obviously. Relocation assistance. Singapore work visa they'll sponsor. The role is basically building their data pipeline from scratch.

I haven't responded yet. I have until next Friday. I've been sitting in my apartment for three hours just staring at the wall. I called Amma and she asked about my research and I said it was going great. I'm a terrible son.

Facts I'm trying to process:
1. If I leave, I won't have a PhD.
2. If I stay, I might still not get a PhD (dropout rates are real).
3. Singapore is 13 hours by flight from Bangalore. The US is 22 hours.
4. I've never lived in Southeast Asia long-term but I loved traveling there.
5. My apartment lease ends in January anyway.
6. I don't know what I want.
entries.nudge_text: Forget the facts for a sec—what's your gut say?
entries.response_text: Gut is overrated. Gut is what made me switch labs twice. Gut is what made me book a flight to Hanoi three hours after a breakup. My gut has historically terrible judgment and everyone in my life would agree. Maybe that's the problem—I've always followed my gut and look where it got me: 24 years old, no finished degrees past undergrad, no savings, and a LinkedIn message away from blowing up my life again.

entries.initial_entry:
Entry number two today because I can't shut my brain off. Went for a walk around campus and ended up sitting by the fountain for like two hours. This undergrad came up and asked if I was okay and I was like "yeah just having a quarter-life crisis, normal stuff."

The thing is—and I've been avoiding thinking about this—the thing is that even if Singapore goes badly, even if it's a huge mistake, at least I'll KNOW. I'll know what it's like to work at a startup. I'll know what it's like to live in Singapore. I'll have that data. Right now I'm operating on pure hypothesis. "Maybe academia isn't for me" is a theory, not a fact. The only way to get facts is to run the experiment.

But experiments have costs. If I leave and it fails, I can't just come back and pick up where I left off. The PhD window closes. And I'm not sure I can start over again at 26 or 27. There's something about the timing that feels like now or never.

Aditya texted me asking if I wanted to grab dinner. I said I was busy. I've been saying I'm busy a lot lately. I think he knows something's up. I'm a terrible friend and a terrible son and maybe just a terrible person who can't commit to anything or anyone.
entries.nudge_text: Terrible person for... considering options?
entries.response_text: When you put it like that it sounds ridiculous. But it's not just this one decision, it's the pattern. It's always something with me. New city, new project, new plan. My parents have been patient for years and I keep changing directions. Aditya sticks with things. My cousin Rohan finished his MBA in two years flat, married his college girlfriend, stable job at Deloitte. Everyone else seems to have figured out how to commit and I'm over here treating my life like a buffet, sampling everything, finishing nothing. At some point that stops being "exploration" and starts being "avoidance," right?

entries.initial_entry: Third entry today. Decided to accept the offer. Sent the email. Going to Singapore in January.
entries.nudge_text: How do you feel right now?

entries.initial_entry:
Told Dr. Sharma yesterday. He was... actually really nice about it? I expected disappointment or anger but he just said "Arjun, you've always seemed like you were searching for something. I hope you find it." Which made me cry a little bit, not gonna lie. He said if I ever want to come back to academia, he'd write me a letter. That was generous. More generous than I deserved probably.

Told Amma and Papa last night. That was harder. Amma went quiet for a long time and then said "so no PhD?" and I said "not right now" and she said "okay" and passed the phone to Papa who talked about health insurance for twenty minutes. I think it's their way of processing. They didn't yell which is something. Rohan apparently defended me—said tech is where the money is anyway, maybe this is smart. I'll take whatever allies I can get.

Now I'm in this weird in-between space. Still going to lab every day because technically I haven't withdrawn yet, but everyone knows. Aditya hugged me when I told him and said "you crazy bastard, I knew it." We got biriyani delivered and he didn't even lecture me about being impulsive which is growth for him.

I'm scared. I should say that. I'm really scared. But also there's this fizzy feeling in my chest that I haven't felt in a long time. Like when I booked the Vietnam trip, or when I switched to Sharma's lab. It's the feeling of something NEW coming. I know that feeling can't be trusted completely but god it's good when it's here.
entries.nudge_text: What makes that feeling untrustworthy?
entries.response_text: History, I guess? Every time I feel like this, I tell myself "this is it, this is the thing, this will be different." And then six months later or a year later I'm feeling stuck again and looking for the next exit. So I don't know if this is genuine alignment with what I want or just my brain's addiction to novelty dressed up as insight. But I guess I'll find out. Worst case, I get a year in Singapore, learn some stuff, and figure out my next move. Best case... I don't know what best case looks like yet. That's kind of exciting too.

entries.initial_entry: Packing is harder than I thought. Every item is a decision. Also found my old journal from undergrad where I wrote "I will definitely finish my PhD" in all caps.
entries.nudge_text: That undergrad version of you—what would he think?
entries.response_text: Probably that I'm a flake. But also that kid had never tried and failed at anything substantial yet. He didn't know what two years of incremental research feels like. He just knew PhDs sounded impressive. I'm trying not to be too hard on either version of myself—the one who wrote that and the one who's walking away from it now. We're both just trying to figure it out.

entries.initial_entry: People keep asking if I'm "sure" about this. Yes I'm sure. I mean, I signed the contract, booked the flight, told everyone—what else is "sure" supposed to look like?
entries.nudge_text: Is anyone asking that you haven't already asked yourself?
entries.response_text: No. That's the thing. I've asked myself a thousand times. Am I running away? Am I being impulsive? Am I disappointing everyone? What if I hate it? What if I'm just fundamentally incapable of staying anywhere? I've interrogated this decision from every angle. But at some point you just have to move. Uncertainty doesn't go away by thinking harder. You have to actually live through the thing to know if it was right. So yeah, I'm sure—not sure it's the right choice, but sure that I need to make A choice. Staying in a PhD I'm not committed to isn't safety, it's slow suffocation. Whatever Singapore is, at least it'll be something different.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning reviewing the pitch deck for the Malmstrom account. Anna prepared it, and it's solid work—maybe better than what I would have done. That should feel good. Mostly it does. But I caught myself wanting to change things anyway, just to put my fingerprints on it.

Held back. Let her present it. We got the work.

There's this line I'm trying to walk. I built this company because I wanted to do things my way, make choices that made sense to me. But somewhere along the way "my way" has to include other people's ways too, or I'm just running a one-man show with extra chairs.

Drove home through the first real snow of the season. The city looked good.
entries.nudge_text: What made it hard to hold back? Was there something specific about her version that felt different from what you would have done?
entries.response_text:
It wasn't that her version was wrong. It was actually the right tone for this client—softer, more collaborative than my usual approach. I think what made it hard was realizing I might have been doing it wrong all these years. Or not wrong, but... narrower than I needed to be.

I keep thinking about what it means to build something. I started this so I could make decisions. But decisions aren't just about me anymore. And that's uncomfortable in a way I didn't expect.

entries.initial_entry:
Board meeting for the industry association today. Three hours of people who've never started anything telling founders how to run our businesses. Compliance updates. Risk mitigation frameworks. Standard recommendations for standard companies.

I know they mean well. I know some of this matters.

But I sat there thinking about all the things I could be doing instead. Actual work. Actual decisions that affect actual outcomes.

Left early. Probably shouldn't have.

Marcus called today. My old business partner, from the agency days. He's still there—made partner eventually, corner office, the whole thing. Wanted to know if I'd consider coming back. Not as an employee, as a "strategic partner." They'd acquire the consultancy, I'd run a division, get their resources, their clients, their benefits package.

I said I'd think about it, which is what you say when you don't want to say no immediately.

But I already know.

The thing is, Marcus sounds tired. He sounds like someone who traded the thing that matters for the thing that lasts. Stability. Predictability. A clear path to retirement with a good pension. And I don't judge him for it—we're different people, wanted different things.

What I can't stop thinking about is whether I'm fooling myself. The consultancy had a rough year. We lost the Bergqvist account in March, spent too much on that software project that went nowhere. I'm fine, we're fine, but it's not the growth trajectory I imagined when I started.

Maybe Marcus is right and I'm just stubborn. Maybe there's a version of partnership that would give me what I need without taking what I can't give up.

Or maybe I just can't stomach the idea of someone else making decisions about my work again.

Went for a run after the call. Didn't help as much as I wanted it to.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the rough year and the growth trajectory you imagined. What would you be giving up if things stayed exactly as they are now?
entries.response_text:
That's the wrong question, I think. It's not about what I'd be giving up—I know what that list looks like. Less travel, fewer client dinners, probably working until I'm seventy.

The question is what I'd be giving up if I took the deal. And I don't think I can explain that to someone who hasn't built something. It's not about money or title. It's about... walking into a room and knowing that whatever happens next, I chose it.

entries.initial_entry:
Year-end push. Everyone's trying to close projects before the holidays, which means I've been in back-to-back calls since seven this morning. Just finished the last one—client wanted changes to something we finalized two weeks ago, and my first instinct was to push back hard.

Didn't. Let Erik handle the diplomacy while I drafted the revision scope. We'll bill for the extra work, client's happy, team's intact.

Small victory, I guess. Learning to pick battles.

Emilia asked if I was coming to the Lucia dinner tomorrow. I'd forgotten it was Lucia already. Said yes before I could think of reasons to say no. Sometimes that's the right approach.

The consultancy is going to make it through another year. Not where I wanted to be, but still here. Still mine.

Closed the office for the holidays today. Handed out bonuses—smaller than I wanted, but real. Anna cried. Erik made a toast that went on too long but meant something.

I don't know why I'm getting emotional about this. It's just another December.

But I keep thinking about Marcus's offer, which I finally declined this morning. Kept the email short—appreciated the thought, not the right fit, let's catch up in the new year. He'll understand, or he won't.

What I couldn't write in the email is that the offer made me realize something. I don't want what he has. I'm not even sure I want what I thought I wanted when I started this—the growth, the big clients, the industry recognition. What I want is to keep making choices. To wake up and know that whatever problems I face today, they're problems I chose.

The rough years are mine. The small bonuses are mine. The team that stays anyway is mine.

Drove home through snow again. Stopped at that overlook near Djurgarden, the one Sara and I used to go to. Sat there for a while. Felt okay.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned Sara. Is that weight you're still carrying, or something you've set down?
entries.response_text:
Set down, mostly. We split four years ago—she wanted more certainty than I could give her, and she was right to want it. I just couldn't be that person.

Stopping at that spot wasn't about missing her specifically. It was more like... checking in with a version of myself that made a choice. The choice cost me something. I wanted to remember that and still feel okay about it.

I think I do.

entries.initial_entry:
Got an email from the procurement office in Mafraq saying they want to switch to a cheaper Chinese filter unit. Half the cost, sure, but the failure rate data I have seen from field tests in East Africa is not good. I started drafting a reply with the test results attached, then stopped. Because what if they just need to save money this quarter and I am the one being difficult.

Rania said at dinner that Noor needs new textbooks and the car registration is due. She did not say it as a complaint, just facts, and that somehow made it worse. I kept thinking about those filters in Mafraq and whether I am fighting the right fight or just being stubborn about something that is not mine to decide.
entries.nudge_text: What makes it feel like stubbornness vs. the right call?
entries.response_text: When it is the right call I feel steady about it. This time I keep second-guessing, which probably means I know the cheaper option might actually work fine and I just hate the idea of cutting corners on something that goes to people who cannot complain if it breaks.

entries.initial_entry:
Cannot sleep. Went back to my laptop after everyone was in bed and found a message from Youssef, my old university friend who still works at the consultancy I left. He forwarded a job listing -- senior engineering director, Dubai office, salary that would cover both girls' tuition in one year. He wrote "just in case" with a smiley face. I know he means well.

The thing that got me was not the money. It was the simplicity. Show up, solve technical problems, go home. Nobody asking me to justify why clean water matters more than a lower unit cost. Nobody expecting me to write another grant narrative about "impact metrics" while families in Azraq are drinking from storage tanks that have not been serviced in months. I sat there staring at the listing for maybe twenty minutes.

Then I closed the tab. But I did not delete the email, and I am not sure what that means. Rania would not pressure me either way, but she would be relieved, and I can picture exactly how her shoulders would drop. That image keeps coming back. I feel like I chose this path and I keep choosing it, but tonight the choosing feels heavier than usual.
entries.nudge_text: Why didn't you delete it?
entries.response_text: Because part of me wanted to keep the option visible. Not to take it, probably. But to know it exists. Like proof that I could still walk away if this gets too hard. That sounds pathetic written down.

entries.initial_entry: Drove out to the factory in Sahab this morning. Watched the team assemble thirty units in two hours, tested every seal myself. Loaded them into the truck for the Irbid delivery and the driver, Abu Faris, said his cousin's family has one of our filters and the kids stopped getting sick last winter.

Board call this afternoon, mostly routine. Layla from the Beirut office mentioned a school in Bekaa that wants to pilot our units in their water system. My mind wandered to whether we could scale the housing design to fit institutional pipes.
entries.nudge_text: Did you bring up the school idea on the call?
entries.response_text: No. I have learned not to pitch things until I have run the numbers. Last time I jumped ahead on a project in front of the board, the finance guy asked about margins and I had nothing. Embarrassing. Now I keep ideas to myself until they are bulletproof, which means some of them die quietly in my notebook.

entries.initial_entry:
A report came out from an NGO I respect showing that three water projects in southern Jordan were suspended due to funding cuts. Three. Just gone. Families who were relying on those systems now have to go back to buying trucked water at five times the cost. I read it on my phone during Leila's basketball game and could not focus on the match after that.

It makes me furious in a way I cannot really explain to anyone at the game. The other parents are talking about weekend plans and I am sitting there doing math in my head about how many households that affects. Rania noticed and squeezed my hand but did not ask. She knows the look by now.
entries.nudge_text: Does Rania ever say anything about the look?

entries.initial_entry:
Met with a potential corporate sponsor today, a petrochemical company that wants to fund fifty filter units for a camp near Zarqa as part of their CSR portfolio. Good meeting on paper. They want their logo on the units, photos for their annual report, a "brand ambassador" visit where executives tour the camp.

I said yes to all of it because we need the money. Fifty units is fifty families with clean water, and that is not nothing. But I spent the drive back feeling uneasy. The camp is not a backdrop. The people there are not props for someone's sustainability page. I know how it works -- I have done these partnerships before and the water still gets delivered -- but every time I shake hands on one of these deals I lose a small piece of something.

Told Rania about it over tea and she said, "You always do this. You take the deal and then you punish yourself for taking it." She is probably right. I just wish there was a way to fund this work that did not require performing gratitude for people who are writing it off on their taxes.

Noor asked me at dinner why we do not live in a bigger house like her friend Dina's family. She is fifteen and it was not a complaint, more like genuine curiosity. I told her we chose to put our money into the business. She asked what the business does exactly, and I realized I have never really sat down and explained it to her properly.

So I did. I told her about the filters, about the camps, about how water-borne illness is the thing that keeps kids out of school more than anything else in those communities. She listened, then asked, "But does it actually change anything or does it just help for a while?" I did not have a quick answer for that. It is the question I carry around constantly and hearing it from my daughter in her school uniform at our kitchen table hit differently than when it comes from a grant reviewer.

I said something about how helping for a while still matters. She shrugged and went to do homework. I sat there for a long time after. The honest answer is I do not know if what I am building will outlast the next funding cycle, and some nights that uncertainty feels like it is eating through the floor under me. But I also cannot imagine doing anything else, which maybe is its own kind of trap.

Long day at the factory fixing a seal press that jammed. Hands smell like silicone and my back is done. But we got the Zarqa order out on time and Abu Faris brought his wife's knafeh to share with the crew, which made the overtime feel less grim.
entries.nudge_text: The Zarqa batch -- that the corporate sponsor one?
entries.response_text: Yeah, that one. Fifty units, all sealed and loaded. Anyway the knafeh was really good, she puts extra cheese.

entries.initial_entry:
My brother Faisal called to tell me our cousin Samir just got promoted to VP at his bank in Riyadh. The way Faisal said it -- "Samir is doing very well" -- had that particular family tone that means "and what about you." I said congratulations and changed the subject but it sat with me the rest of the day.

I do not need validation from Faisal. He has never understood why I walked away from the consultancy and he is never going to. What I do know is that last week a mother in Irbid told our field coordinator that her son's eczema cleared up two months after we installed the filter. That is real. Samir's title is a line on a business card. I should not have to keep defending this to myself but apparently I still do.
entries.nudge_text: If you don't need it, why's it still sitting with you?
entries.response_text: Because somewhere in the back of my head there is still a version of me that measures success the way my family does. Cars, titles, where your kids go to school. I thought I outgrew that but it surfaces when I am tired or when someone like Faisal pokes at it. It passes. It just takes longer to pass than I would like.

entries.initial_entry:
Okay so today was a lot and I'm still processing everything honestly. Got to the welcome center this morning and there was this family from Syria, father and two kids, and the little girl was maybe six years old and she had this tiny sketchbook she'd been carrying since they left Damascus. She showed me her drawings. Houses on fire. People running. A boat with stick figures packed so tight they looked like a fence. And then the last page was just colors—blue and orange and yellow—and she said that was what she wanted to see now. I nearly lost it right there but you can't do that in front of them so I just smiled and said her colors were beautiful.

Then I had to go straight from there to meet Pieter about the factory wall commission and he was talking about branding and "visual impact metrics" and I just sat there thinking about that little girl's sketchbook and how my murals are supposed to mean something but here I am discussing color palettes that will make people want to buy sustainable sneakers. Like what am I even doing? The sneakers are made ethically apparently, fair wages, recycled materials, so it's not like I'm selling my soul to Shell or whatever, but still. The whole meeting felt hollow.

Walked around the harbor for an hour after instead of taking the tram home. The water was that grey-green it gets in April and I kept thinking about the fishing boats in Lisbon and Maria who helped me there and how she said art that changes nothing is just decoration. Am I just decorating? The mural will be seen by thousands of people every day. Maybe one person thinks differently about what they consume. Maybe nobody does and it just looks cool and gets likes.

I don't know. I'm tired and I smell like spray paint and I can't stop seeing that little girl's drawings. Tomorrow I have to give Pieter an answer about the commission. I should probably say yes because I need the money but something in my chest is tight about it.
entries.nudge_text: What would Maria say about the sneaker thing?
entries.response_text: Ha. She'd probably tell me I'm overthinking it and that any wall is better than no wall. But then she'd also ask me what story I want to tell on it, and right now I don't have a story. I just have a brand deck with hex codes and a brief that says "inspire conscious consumption." Maybe that's the problem—it's their story, not mine.

entries.initial_entry:
I said no to Pieter. Told him yesterday and he was actually pretty understanding about it, said he'd keep me in mind for future projects, but I could tell he was disappointed. Whatever. I can't be responsible for everyone's disappointment.

What I didn't expect was how RELIEVED I felt after. Like I'd been holding my breath for a week and finally exhaled. Spent all of today at the welcome center instead of in my studio and it was exactly where I needed to be. We did an art session with some of the kids—just paper and crayons, nothing fancy—and I sat with Amira (the Syrian girl from before) and we drew together for two hours. She's so quiet but her hands never stop moving across the paper. She drew her grandmother today. A woman in a blue dress standing in a kitchen with yellow walls. She told me her grandmother makes the best knafeh but she doesn't know if she's still alive because they haven't been able to reach her since January.

I don't know how to hold that. I don't know what to do with the fact that I worried for a week about a sneaker mural while this kid doesn't know if her grandmother is alive. It makes everything I do feel small and pointless but also somehow more urgent? Like if I'm going to make things then they have to MATTER. They have to reach people the way Amira's drawings reach me.

I talked to Bram on the phone tonight—he's still in Berlin, been there four months now doing projections on the Reichstag for some climate group—and he said something that stuck with me. He said we can't measure ourselves by the size of the problem. That if everyone waited until they could fix everything, nothing would ever get made. I know he's right but it doesn't make the gap between what I want to do and what I can actually do feel any smaller.

Anyway. I'm going to start something new. Not sure what yet. Maybe something with the kids at the center, collaborative, their images mixed with mine. Something that tells THEIR story instead of a brand's story. No money in it probably but at this point I think I'd rather be broke and making something real than comfortable and decorating.
entries.nudge_text: What would the kids' piece look like?
entries.response_text:
I've been thinking about it all night actually. What if it wasn't a mural at all? What if it was something that moved? The boats in Lisbon worked because they existed in water, in motion—people saw them differently depending on the tide, the light. Amira's drawings are so full of movement even on flat paper. Houses falling, people running, boats tipping. What if we made something together that could actually move? Projections maybe, like what Bram does, but with their drawings animated somehow. I don't know how to do that technically but Bram might.

The more I think about it the more I realize the static murals were starting to feel like cages. I'd finish one and immediately want to be somewhere else, doing something else. Maybe that restlessness was telling me something—not just that I need to travel or whatever, but that the medium itself wasn't right anymore. I want to make things that breathe. That change. Things people can't just walk past and forget.

entries.initial_entry:
Got back from the studio late and Baba was already asleep but Mama was waiting up. She didn't say anything about me being late, she just set out the leftover kibbeh and sat across from me while I ate. The whole time I could feel her watching me and I knew she wanted to ask about the commission meeting. I told her it went fine, which it did, mostly. The client wants something for his hotel lobby, large scale, blues and golds, very safe. I said I'd do it.

But the thing is I'd spent the whole morning working on something else entirely, this series I've been sketching for months. Faces in crowds, people looking away from each other. Disconnection in tight spaces. It's the most honest work I've done in years and I can't show it to anyone. Not because it's bad but because every time I imagine hanging it somewhere or putting it in a show I can already hear my uncle asking why I'm painting something so depressing, or my cousin making that face she makes when she doesn't understand something but doesn't want to say so. So instead I took the hotel lobby job and told myself the other work can wait. It can always wait.

I keep doing this. The safe thing first, then the real thing never.
entries.nudge_text: You said the real thing never happens. What would have to change for it to?
entries.response_text: I'd need to stop caring what people think, which is basically asking me to be a different person. Or I'd need someone to tell me it's okay, which is pathetic at my age.

entries.initial_entry:
Friday lunch was the usual production. My aunt brought her friend who has a daughter studying dentistry and somehow the conversation kept circling back to me and whether I'm seeing anyone. Mama smiled the whole time and said nothing, which is her way of agreeing without being the one to push. I just sat there eating and deflecting. "I'm focused on work right now." "Inshallah, when the time is right." All the correct answers.

My sister pulled me aside after and said I handled it well, which felt more like pity than praise. She knows I've been seeing someone casually, someone who isn't from the community, and she knows I'll never bring it up at Friday lunch. Not because I'm ashamed but because I can already map out the exact sequence of reactions and it's exhausting to even think about. My father asking quiet questions. My mother suddenly having a headache. My uncle making a joke that isn't a joke.

People think I'm easygoing because I never push back. The truth is I've just gotten very efficient at predicting which battles aren't worth starting. That's not the same thing as being at peace with it.
entries.nudge_text: You said that's not the same as being at peace. What does the gap feel like?
entries.response_text: Like holding your breath in a room full of people who think you're breathing normally. It's fine until it isn't.

entries.initial_entry:
Had a conversation with Rami at the studio today that's been sitting with me all evening. He got accepted into a group show in Beirut, contemporary stuff, political, boundary-pushing. They asked if I wanted to submit something too. I told him I'd think about it, which we both knew meant no.

He didn't push, which I appreciated, but later he said something offhand while cleaning his brushes. He said, "You're the most talented person I know who makes the least interesting work." He wasn't being cruel. He actually looked sad when he said it. I didn't know how to respond so I just laughed it off and changed the subject.

The thing is he's right. The hotel lobby commission is technically proficient and completely forgettable. The faces series sitting under the tarp in the corner of my studio is the best work I've ever done and no one has seen it. I keep telling myself I'm being strategic, building a reputation with the commercial work first, earning credibility. But at some point strategy becomes avoidance and I think I passed that line a while ago. I just don't know what to do about it because every option I can think of involves disappointing someone or drawing attention I'm not ready for.

Long day finishing preliminary sketches for the hotel commission. The client wants revisions already, more warmth, more welcoming. I nodded through the whole meeting and made notes. On the drive home I realized I'd been clenching my jaw for hours and my neck is completely locked up.

Mama called to remind me about my cousin's engagement party this weekend. I said I'd be there. Of course I'll be there. Everyone will be there. I'll wear the right shirt, say the right things, congratulate the right people. I'm good at this part.

Engagement party was fine. Loud. Lots of people I hadn't seen in months asking the usual things. How's the art going, meaning are you making money. Are you eating enough, meaning you look tired. When are you next, meaning why haven't you settled down. I smiled through all of it because that's what you do.

Danced with my mother at one point, which was actually nice. She was happy, genuinely, and I like seeing her like that. My father sat in the corner with the other men drinking coffee and nodding. He looked proud of my cousin. I wonder if he looks at me and feels something different, not disappointment exactly but maybe confusion. His son the painter. He's never said a negative word about it but he's also never asked to see my work. That silence says more than any criticism would.

After everyone left I helped stack chairs and my aunt squeezed my arm and told me I was such a good boy for always helping. I'm thirty-nine years old and I'm still being called a good boy. The worst part is I felt relieved by it. Like that's still the thing I'm chasing, the approval, the confirmation that I'm doing it right. Whatever "it" is.
entries.nudge_text: You noticed feeling relieved at being called 'a good boy.' What does that tell you?

entries.initial_entry:
Rami brought up the Beirut show again. He said the deadline was extended and there was still time to submit. I told him I appreciate him thinking of me but the timing doesn't work with the hotel commission. Which is technically true. But also I could find the time if I wanted to. I just don't want to deal with the aftermath of showing work that actually says something.

It's not cowardice. I know what it looks like from outside but it's more complicated than that. My family has a certain idea of what I do and who I am and disturbing that comes with a cost. Not a dramatic one, no one's going to disown me. It's subtler than that. It's the shift in how people look at you when you stop being predictable. I've seen it happen to other people and I've decided I'd rather keep things smooth. That's a choice, not a failure. At least that's what I keep telling myself.

Can't sleep. Spent the last two hours looking at Rami's work online, the pieces he's submitting to Beirut. They're raw and unfinished-looking on purpose and there's this energy in them that I used to have. I pulled out the faces series from under the tarp and laid them out on the studio floor and just stood there staring at them. They're good. I know they're good. That's not ego, that's honest assessment after twenty years of doing this.

I took a photo of the pieces laid out and almost posted it. My thumb was literally on the button. Then I imagined my mother scrolling past it and asking why the faces look so lonely, or my uncle forwarding it with a comment about how art these days has no beauty. So I put my phone down and covered everything back up.

I keep thinking about what Rami said. The most talented person he knows who makes the least interesting work. It's like a splinter I can't get out. If I showed the real work and people responded to it, everything would change. But if people in my life reacted badly, that would also change everything. I don't trust myself to handle that without losing something I need. The approval, the ease, the feeling of fitting in. People act like fitting in is small but when you've built your entire life around it, it becomes the foundation. I'm terrified of what happens if I pull a brick out.
entries.nudge_text: What would pulling that brick out actually look like, concretely?
entries.response_text: Submitting to a show, probably. Or just leaving the tarp off the canvases when my family visits the studio. Small things. Anyway, I need to sleep.

entries.initial_entry:
Something shifted today, or maybe it's been shifting for a while and I only noticed now. I was working on the hotel commission, painting this massive blue gradient that's supposed to evoke the sea, and I caught myself adding tiny figures along the bottom edge. Almost invisible unless you're close. Faces turned away from each other, like my other series. I painted over them before anyone could see but for a few minutes they were there and the piece was better for it.

My sister came by the studio to drop off food Mama had made. She lingered, looking at canvases against the wall. She pointed at one of the commercial pieces and said, "This is pretty." Then she pointed at the corner where the faces series was partially visible under the tarp and said, "What's that one?" I told her it was nothing, just experiments. She said it looked interesting and I should show her sometime.

That's the thing about my sister. She asks without pushing. If I ever show the real work to anyone in my family, it'll start with her. Not because she'll necessarily understand it but because she won't make me feel wrong for having made it. That counts for more than most people realize.

I drove home feeling lighter than I have in weeks. Nothing has actually changed. I still didn't submit to Beirut. But someone in my family expressing even mild curiosity about the work I actually care about made me feel less like I'm carrying this alone.
Delivered the hotel commission today. Client was thrilled. Shook my hand and said it was exactly what he envisioned, which I think is the most depressing compliment I've ever received.
entries.nudge_text: Why does hearing 'exactly what I envisioned' feel depressing to you?

entries.initial_entry:
Took my sister up on her offer. Showed her three of the faces pieces at the studio today. She looked at them for a long time without saying anything. Then she said, "These are different." I waited for the follow-up, the concern, the suggestion to maybe not show Baba. Instead she said, "You should make more of these."

I didn't know what to do with that so I made us tea and we sat on the studio floor talking about other things. But the pieces stayed uncovered the whole time. I didn't put the tarp back until after she left. Small thing. But I noticed it.
entries.nudge_text: You noticed you left the tarp off. What do you think that means for you?
entries.response_text: Maybe I'm getting tired of hiding the work. Or maybe I just needed one person to see it and not flinch. Either way the tarp felt heavier putting it back on than it used to.

entries.initial_entry:
People keep telling me I take on too much. The vice principal said it again today after I stayed past six finishing the mid-term analysis spreadsheets. But someone has to track which students are falling behind, and nobody else is doing it systematically. It's not like I'm trying to be a martyr. I just know what happens when these kids slip through the cracks -- they end up in the same cycle their parents were stuck in.

Anyway, the reading club had its best turnout this week. Fourteen students. Two of them are reading a full grade level above where they started in September. That's not nothing. That's the whole point.
entries.nudge_text: You say it's not martyrdom. What is it then?
entries.response_text: I think it's that I can't separate myself from the outcome. Like if Ravi in 8B fails his English paper, I take it personally because I saw the warning signs in October and I had the data. If I'd just done one more review session, maybe. I know that's not entirely rational but that's how it sits in my chest.

entries.initial_entry: Got the district assessment results back. My class average is fourth out of nine sections. Fourth. I worked harder than anyone in that staffroom and fourth is what I get.
entries.nudge_text: Fourth out of nine is above the middle. What number would have felt okay?
entries.response_text: First. Or at least second. I know how that sounds. But I mapped every student's weak areas, I differentiated the worksheets, I came in on Saturdays. The sections that beat me have teachers who've been doing this for fifteen years. I've been at it for three. Part of me knows I should be proud but the other part just sees the gap.

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about the rankings. Came home and opened my laptop to look at the breakdown by question type and I can see exactly where my students lost marks -- the inference questions, the ones that require them to read between lines. And honestly that tracks because half my class comes from homes where there aren't books, where nobody reads to them, where the language of the test isn't even the language they think in. So the test is measuring something, sure, but it's measuring something that has more to do with privilege than with whether I taught well.

Which makes it worse in a way because now I'm angry at the system and at myself simultaneously. Angry at the system for pretending this is a level field. Angry at myself for caring about the ranking anyway when I know it's flawed. I spent two hours building supplementary inference exercises tonight instead of eating dinner. Amma would lose her mind if she knew.

I keep circling. If I focus on the ranking I feel like I failed. If I focus on the injustice I feel helpless. There's no version of this where I just sit with it and feel fine.

Had the NGO water-mapping session today and it hit different this time. We tested samples from a borewell near the school and the iron content was nearly three times the safe limit. Three times. These kids drink this water. They fill bottles from this tap during lunch break. I've watched them do it.

I reported it to the block office last month and the response was basically a form letter saying it's been "noted for review." Noted for review. Meanwhile Lakshmi in 7A has been complaining about stomach aches every other week and I don't have proof it's the water but I also can't not think about it.

The grounding part is that at least now I have data. Six months of readings from twelve sites. The NGO coordinator said we have enough to go to the press if the block office keeps stalling. It feels like something I can actually push on, unlike the test scores where I'm just one teacher against a whole structure. Small difference, maybe, but it's mine.

Sunday and I told myself I'd take the day off but here I am at the kitchen table with three stacks of papers. One is the inference worksheets I'm piloting Monday. One is the water quality report I'm formatting for the NGO. One is the mid-year self-evaluation the school wants by Wednesday.

Amma called while I was in the middle of all this and asked when I'm coming home to visit. I said maybe February. She said I said that in November too. She's right. I haven't been back to Thrissur since Onam. Seven months. My cousin got engaged and I sent a gift instead of showing up. I keep telling myself I'll go once things settle down but things never settle down because I keep finding more things that need doing.

The self-evaluation is funny because they want me to rate myself on a scale of one to five across these categories and I genuinely don't know what to put. On effort I'd say five without hesitation. On results -- by the district's measure -- maybe a three? And then there's this whole other dimension they don't even ask about, like the fact that Ravi can now write a paragraph without freezing up, or that the water report exists at all. None of that fits in a checkbox.
entries.nudge_text: Seven months away from home. What do you miss most?
entries.response_text: My grandmother's rice kanji in the morning. The sound of the rain on the tin roof extension my uncle built. Amma not having to ask me through a phone screen if I'm eating properly. I miss being in a place where nobody's evaluating me and I'm not evaluating myself. I just exist there. But then I think about Monday's lesson plan and the water report deadline and the missing feels like something I can't afford right now.

entries.initial_entry:
Colleague asked me at lunch if I'd considered just "letting the NGO stuff go for a bit" since the term is getting intense. She meant well, I think, but it rubbed me the wrong way. Letting it go means those water readings sit in a folder and nothing happens. Letting it go means the block office gets to keep ignoring it. That's not something I can just put on pause because my marking load went up.

But I will say this -- I did manage to set a boundary this week. Told the NGO coordinator I can only do fieldwork every other Saturday instead of every week. It felt wrong at first, like I was abandoning something, but I also got through the week without that Sunday-night dread for the first time in a while. That's worth something.
entries.nudge_text: You felt relief cutting back but also guilt. Which one do you trust more?

entries.initial_entry:
Barely functioning today. Slept maybe four hours because I was up redoing the inference module after I realized the reading passages I'd chosen were too culturally specific -- references to summer camps and snow days that my students have zero frame of reference for. So I rewrote three of them using contexts they'd actually recognize. Market scenes, monsoon flooding, cricket matches. It took until 2 AM but when I tested the first passage on a few students during break today, the difference was immediate. They were actually engaging with the inference questions instead of getting stuck on vocabulary.

I'm exhausted but I'm also weirdly satisfied. This is what the district rankings don't capture. The test uses standardized passages that assume a particular kind of childhood. I can't change the test but I can build the bridge to it. My students scored low on inference not because they can't infer -- they do it constantly in their daily lives, navigating languages, reading social cues -- but because the test's version of inference lives in a world they've never been to.

I need to sleep more. I know that. But nights like last night are also when I do my best thinking because nobody's pinging me and I can just sit with the material. It's a trade-off I keep making and I'm not sure it's sustainable but right now it's working.

Three things happened today worth noting.

First, the block education officer called about the water report. They're sending someone to re-test the borewell next week. Not a full commitment to fixing anything, but the fact that they called instead of sending another form letter means the pressure is working. The NGO coordinator wants to submit the full dataset to the state environmental board as a parallel track. I agreed.

Second, I ran the revised inference module in two of my sections. Pass rate on the practice set went from 38% to 61%. That's with the same students, same difficulty level, just different cultural framing in the passages. I documented everything -- the original scores, the revised passages, the new scores. If I'm going to argue that the standardized test is biased, I need evidence, not just frustration.

Third, the vice principal flagged me for the district's "Emerging Educator" recognition. I didn't ask for it. Apparently my mid-term data tracking caught someone's attention higher up. I should feel good about it but mostly I feel like the recognition is for the wrong thing. They noticed the spreadsheet, not the Saturday reading club. They noticed the data, not the kids.

Amma called again. She's upset I'm not coming for Pongal. I tried to explain that February is when I need to prep the students for boards and I genuinely cannot take three days off right now. She said "you always have a reason" and I could hear that specific disappointment in her voice, the kind that doesn't argue, just goes quiet.

I don't know how to make her understand that the reason I can't come home is the same reason she wanted me to become a teacher in the first place. She raised me to care about this. She's the one who drove me to the library every Saturday when I was ten. She's the one who cried when I got the appointment letter. So how is it that doing the thing she was proud of is also the thing that keeps me away from her? I know she doesn't see it that way. She just misses me. But I can't hold both of those things at the same time without something cracking.

Week in review. The borewell got re-tested by the block office team. Results confirmed what our data showed -- iron at 2.8 times the limit, plus elevated nitrates we hadn't caught with our field kit. They've issued a notice to the school to install a filter system within 60 days. It took six months of volunteer hours to get to this point. Six months for something that should have been caught by routine inspection.

On the school side, I submitted my Emerging Educator portfolio. Included the inference module data alongside the standard metrics. I wrote a one-page note arguing that student performance gains should be measured against where students started, not against an absolute benchmark that ignores context. Don't know if anyone will read it. But it's on record.

Personally, I'm in a strange place. I should feel like I'm winning. The water issue is moving. My students are improving. I got recognized by the district. But I missed Pongal. I haven't called my grandmother in three weeks. I ate dinner at my desk every night this week. There's this gap between the things I'm accomplishing and how I actually feel, and I don't know what to do with it. I keep performing well by every external measure while something internal stays unsorted.
entries.nudge_text: That gap you mentioned -- what would it take to close it?
entries.response_text: I honestly don't know. Maybe it's not something you close. Maybe it's just the cost of caring about things that are bigger than your own life. The water report matters more than my comfort. My students' futures matter more than my sleep schedule. I believe that. But believing it doesn't make the tiredness go away, and it doesn't make Amma's silence on the phone hurt less. I think what I need is for someone to tell me it's okay to be proud and depleted at the same time. That those aren't contradictions.
