entries.initial_entry:
Made the kheer for Amma's birthday exactly how Nani used to make it. The cardamom from the Indian shop on Belgrave Road, not the supermarket stuff. Sat there stirring for forty-five minutes, watching the milk thicken, and it felt right. Like the kitchen back home, like Nani's hands guiding mine when I was eight years old and barely tall enough to reach the stove.

Called Amma after. She cried a little when I described the smell. Said she could almost taste it through the phone. She asked when I was coming next, and I told her maybe Diwali if I can get the time off. She said she will save her sari for me to help her drape it, like I always do.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about draping the sari together?
entries.response_text: It is the only time I feel like her daughter again, not just someone who calls and sends money. She cannot do the pleats properly anymore, her hands shake. But she waits for me instead of asking the neighbour. It is our thing.

entries.initial_entry:
Double shift again because Meena called in sick. I do not mind covering, but by hour fourteen my feet were just throbbing and I kept thinking about how I had promised to video call Appa before his morning walk. He waits by the phone like a child waiting for sweets. I texted him instead, said I was still at work, and he just replied "okay beta" with that period at the end that always makes me feel like I have disappointed him even when he says nothing is wrong.

Reena stopped by the nurses' station around two in the morning with tea. She said I look tired, which is her way of saying I look like death. I laughed it off, but she is right. I have been covering too many shifts. The money is good—I put extra aside for Meera's wedding fund this month—but I cannot remember the last time I sat down for a proper meal that was not eaten standing in the break room.

The new doctor on rotation asked why I work so much. I did not know how to explain that it is not really a choice. My brother's shop in Ahmedabad is struggling, my parents' medical bills keep growing, and Meera's wedding will not pay for itself. This is what daughters do. But then I missed the call with Appa, and I wonder if I am getting the balance wrong. He will not be around forever, and I keep choosing the extra shifts.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually think you're getting it wrong, or is that the exhaustion talking?
entries.response_text: Both, maybe. Sometimes I think the money is the excuse. Easier to send the transfer than to sit with the silence on the phone when Appa forgets what he was saying mid-sentence. The shifts fill the time I would otherwise spend worrying.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunita auntie came over uninvited, which is very her. She brought that horrible orange mithai from the temple and sat in my kitchen for two hours talking about how her nephew just got married to "a good Gujarati girl." Then she looked at Meera's photo on the fridge and asked if there was "any news."

I told her Meera is focusing on her career right now, and Sunita auntie made that face. The one where her eyebrows go up and she says "career is good, but..." I just kept refilling her chai until she left. Did not argue, did not defend. Just smiled and nodded because that is what you do. And honestly, I was not even angry. This is just how aunties are. Meera will find someone when Meera finds someone, and no amount of auntie pressure will change that. I have made my peace with it even if they have not.
entries.nudge_text: How did you get to that peace?
entries.response_text: Watching Meera with her patients. She is good at what she does, really good. I did not have that when I was her age—I went into nursing because my mother did and her mother did. Meera chose it. That matters more than a wedding date.

entries.initial_entry:
Hospital was busy with the usual winter rush. Three admissions on my shift, two respiratory cases and one fall. Paperwork took longer than the actual care, which is frustrating but normal. The new electronic system is meant to make things faster but everyone is still learning it.

Brought leftover rotli from yesterday for lunch. Ate at my desk while filling in charts. Raj called to say he would be late picking up the groceries so I stopped at Tesco on the way home. Nothing interesting, just milk and bread and the biscuits Appa likes for when he visits next.

Back-to-back shifts this week. Monday bled into Tuesday and now it is whatever day it is. Slept for four hours between them and dreamed I was still at work, which felt cruel. Woke up confused about whether the patient in room 4B had actually been discharged or if I had just imagined it in the dream.

Made daal for dinner because it is the only thing that requires no thought. Watched something on telly but could not tell you what. Raj asked if I wanted to go for the walk we used to take around the park but I just wanted to sit. He sat with me instead. Neither of us said much. Sometimes that is enough.

Skipped the temple this morning. Told Raj I was too tired, which was true, but also I just did not feel like sitting through the puja with everyone asking about the family and when we are going to India next. Felt guilty about it all day.
entries.nudge_text: What's the guilt actually about?

entries.initial_entry:
Had a patient today who reminded me why I do this. Mrs. Okonkwo, eighty-three, recovering from a hip replacement. Her family is all in Nigeria and she kept asking when someone would come. I sat with her during my break, just talking about nothing—the food, the weather, whether the NHS would survive another winter. She held my hand and called me "daughter."

I know I cannot replace her actual children, but I can be there for the fifteen minutes between her medication rounds. Brought her a cup of chai from the nurses' station even though it is not exactly protocol. She said it reminded her of something her mother used to make. We are not so different, Mrs. Okonkwo and I. Both far from home, both making do.
entries.nudge_text: What does 'making do' look like for you?

entries.initial_entry:
Had the conversation with Meera about Canada again. She has a job offer in Toronto, a good one, at a hospital that specializes in what she wants to do. I should be proud, and I am proud, but I also spent the whole dinner feeling like I was swallowing something sharp every time she talked about visa applications and apartment hunting.

She said I was being quiet and I said I was just tired. Which was not a lie, but it was not the whole truth either. The truth is I keep thinking about how I moved to England and left my parents behind and now my daughter wants to do the same to me. It is not fair to think that way. She has every right to build her life. I did the same thing.

But Amma stayed. My grandmother stayed. All the women in our family stayed close, within calling distance, within sari-draping distance, within "come over for chai" distance. And I broke that chain when I came here, and now Meera is breaking it further, and I cannot decide if this is progress or loss. Raj says I should be happy she is ambitious. I am happy. I am also something else that does not have a word.
entries.nudge_text: What's the something else?

entries.initial_entry:
Meera and I cooked together this evening. She asked me to teach her the dhokla recipe, the one from my mother's side that we only make for special occasions. Said she wanted to learn before she goes. We stood in the kitchen for two hours, me showing her how to test the batter, her writing everything down in that little notebook she keeps.

She asked about Nani, questions she has never asked before. What songs she used to sing, what her hands looked like, whether she was strict or soft. I found myself telling stories I had not thought about in years. The way Nani would wake before sunrise to pray. The way she saved the best pieces of mango for the grandchildren. Meera listened like she was memorising it all, and maybe she was. Maybe this is how it continues—not by staying in the same place, but by carrying the recipes and the stories wherever you go.
entries.nudge_text: Do you believe that—that carrying the stories is enough?
entries.response_text: Ask me again in a year when she is actually gone.

entries.initial_entry: Sent the dhokla recipe to my mother. She called immediately, laughing, saying I had written down the measurements when she never measures anything. Told her Meera made her promise to keep it exactly as written. Three generations passing the same batter between their hands.
entries.nudge_text: How did it feel, sending it?
entries.response_text: Like handing over something I did not know I was holding. Heavy and light at the same time.

entries.initial_entry: Third week of summer break and I'm already crawling out of my skin, spent the whole morning refreshing job boards for literally anything that isn't sitting at home, maybe I should just book a flight somewhere, anywhere, the walls are closing in.
entries.nudge_text: What happened this morning that set this off?

entries.initial_entry: Principal Chen called about leading the new student orientation committee for August. I said yes before she finished explaining the scope.
entries.nudge_text: You didn't even let her finish?
entries.response_text: Honestly no. The moment she said "lead" I was already in. I've been rotting at home for three weeks with nothing to run, nothing to decide. My mom keeps asking why I don't just relax like a normal person during break. But sitting around feels like drowning. At least now I have something with my name on it.

entries.initial_entry:
I'm so frustrated I could scream. The orientation committee met today on Zoom and Ms. Liu, who's been at the school for fifteen years, kept interrupting me to "add context" every time I proposed something. Context! As if I don't know our own students. She literally said "Wei-Lin, you're still new here" in front of everyone when I suggested we move the welcome assembly outdoors to the courtyard.

It's my committee. Principal Chen asked ME to lead it. But every time I try to actually lead, someone older swoops in to remind me I'm twenty-three. I left banking so I wouldn't have to wait in line behind people who got there first. I thought teaching would be different.
entries.nudge_text: Different how?
entries.response_text: I thought... I don't know. That being good at something would be enough. At the bank I was fast, accurate, never made mistakes, but promotions went by seniority. I figured teaching would reward initiative—you put in the work, you earn the room. But maybe every institution is the same. Maybe I'm just chasing something that doesn't exist, some place where being capable matters more than being old. That's a depressing thought. I don't want to believe it.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, different mood I guess. After that Zoom call and venting here I went for a long walk around the harbor, watched the fishing boats come in, and something clicked. Ms. Liu wasn't trying to undermine me—she's been running orientations since before I was in middle school myself, and she probably felt sidelined when Chen handed the lead role to someone who's been there less than two years.

That doesn't mean I was wrong to want to shake things up. The outdoor assembly IS a better idea. But maybe I came in too hot, treating the meeting like a presentation where I had all the answers instead of a collaboration where people needed to feel heard. My dad used to say I sprint before people have tied their shoes. He meant it as a flaw. I always took it as a compliment.

I think both can be true? I move fast because slow feels unbearable, but fast doesn't have to mean alone. I messaged Ms. Liu after the walk and asked if she wanted to grab boba tomorrow to talk through the logistics together. She sent back a smiley face. Small win. I'll take it.

The harbor smelled like diesel and salt and I took some good shots of the cranes against the sunset for the gram. Feeling more like myself now. Sometimes I just need to move to think.

Picked up a bubble tea order from Toa Payoh and the auntie at the shop asked if I ever get tired of riding around all day. Told her it beats sitting in an air-conditioned office watching my soul leak out through Excel spreadsheets. She laughed, said her son is trying to get her to hire a manager so she can rest. "Rest is for the dead," she said. I think about that sometimes—how some people need the motion, the doing, even when the body is telling them to stop.

Delivered to a condo in Newton. The guy who took the order was maybe mid-twenties, looked like he hadn't slept in days. Developer, probably. I remember that life—the midnight oil, the takeout containers stacking up. He tipped twenty bucks, which is rare. Made me wonder if he saw something in me, recognized the uniform of someone who opted out of the grind. Or maybe he was just too tired to count properly.

The thing about this work is you see everyone's lives in fragments. A birthday cake for a kid in Clementi. Medicine from a pharmacy to an elderly woman in Bedok who asked me to wait while she found exact change. Each delivery is a tiny window into something bigger. I don't know if I'm helping or just moving products around. Both, maybe. The city needs its circulatory system.
entries.nudge_text: What do you mean by "opted out of the grind"?
entries.response_text: Opted out is probably too clean a phrase for what happened. I didn't make some grand declaration. After the restructuring, I took a few weeks off, started driving for Grab "temporarily," and then the temporary turned permanent. The truth is I was scared to go back. Scared I'd slip back into caring about quarterly targets and performance reviews. Scared I'd become someone who measures his worth in promotions again. So I kept riding. Five years now. Priya says I'm running from something. Maybe she's right. But at least on the road, I'm running toward something too—even if I don't know what it is yet.

entries.initial_entry: Brother-in-law made a comment at family dinner about "still doing delivery." I told him not everyone needs a business card to feel important. He didn't say anything after that, but Priya gave me that look.
entries.nudge_text: What was that look about?
entries.response_text: The look that says I embarrassed her in front of her family again. That I could have just let it go instead of making things awkward. She's right, probably. But something in me couldn't let him sit there feeling superior. He sells insurance—half his job is convincing people to be afraid of the future. At least when I hand someone their food, they're genuinely happy to see me.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning at the shelter helping sort donations—winter clothes that people send even though we're in the tropics, which says something about how little attention donors pay but also something about the impulse to give, that even misguided giving is still a reaching-out. Ramesh was there, the Bangladeshi construction worker I've been teaching English to on Saturdays. His vocabulary is getting better. Today he said "bureaucracy" correctly and I felt stupidly proud.

Then back on the bike by 2pm for the dinner rush. The contrast is jarring sometimes—one moment you're sitting with men who send half their salary home to families they see once every two years, the next you're delivering premium sashimi to someone who complains the wasabi is on the wrong side of the box. I try not to judge. Everyone is fighting something. The sashimi guy might be going through a divorce for all I know. But still.

Route took me through Geylang. The old shophouses are getting demolished one by one. Another one gone since last month, replaced by scaffolding and a sign promising "luxury living." I don't know why I keep track. It's not like I can do anything about it. But someone should be witnessing it, I think. Someone should notice what we're losing.
Rain all day. Soaked through three times. App crashed twice during peak hours so I lost the surge pricing bonuses. I need to remember days like this when I romanticize "freedom from the cubicle."
Twelve-hour shift but hit my weekly target. Priya and I had dinner together for the first time in two weeks—she made fish curry, the Kerala style her mother taught her. Felt like us again.
entries.nudge_text: What do you mean by "felt like us again"?
entries.response_text: I mean we weren't performing. No tension underneath the conversation about money or my "career choices." Just two people who've been together fifteen years, eating fish curry, talking about nothing important. Her laugh came easy. Mine too. I forget sometimes that this is what I'm working for—not the money itself, but these moments. The ability to sit across from her without apology. To be present without calculating what I should be doing instead.

entries.initial_entry:
Year-end party season means everyone wants everything delivered everywhere. Did a corporate gift run today—thirty identical gift baskets to thirty different addresses across the island. Whoever organized this clearly didn't think about the human being who'd have to actually execute it. By basket twenty-two, my back was screaming. Sat in a void deck in Tampines for ten minutes just breathing.

The weird thing is, I had this flash of missing my old job. Not the job itself, but the end-of-year bonuses. The company dinners at nice restaurants. The illusion of security. Priya's been hinting that her sister's husband (yes, the business card guy) might have a position opening at his agency. I said I'd think about it. I'm not sure if I said that to keep the peace or because part of me is actually considering it.

New year. Priya wanted to watch the fireworks at Marina Bay but I couldn't face the crowds. We watched from the rooftop of our HDB instead, the view half-blocked by the neighboring tower. She fell asleep before midnight. I stayed up, counting the distant pops of light.

Made the usual resolutions in my head—save more, drink less, be more patient. But underneath all that, a different question kept surfacing: is this the life I want for another year? Not the delivery work specifically, but the uncertainty, the lack of a plan. I used to think planning was a trap. Now I'm wondering if having no plan is its own kind of trap. The kind where you wake up at fifty still "figuring things out."
entries.nudge_text: What would having a plan even look like for you?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I don't know. That's the problem, isn't it? Every time I try to picture a "plan," it looks like going backward—back to the office, back to the hierarchy, back to measuring life in KPIs. Maybe I just need to find a different kind of plan. One that doesn't feel like surrender.

entries.initial_entry: Someone on a riders' Telegram group posted an article about gig workers "lacking ambition." Had to stop myself from typing a rant. Ambition isn't one-size-fits-all—some of us just refuse to let corporations define what success looks like.

Priya finally said it out loud: she's scared. Not angry, not disappointed—scared. About what happens if I get injured, if the gig economy regulations change, if we never have enough for a real retirement. I wanted to argue, to list all the reasons this life is worth the risk. But looking at her face, I couldn't.

The thing is, I know she's not wrong. I've been telling myself that freedom is worth the instability, that I'm building something even if it doesn't look like what everyone else is building. But am I? Or am I just afraid of commitment dressed up as philosophy? The men at the shelter—they don't have the luxury of choosing instability. They're here because they have no choice. I'm here because I walked away from choices. There's a difference, and I'm not sure it's a flattering one.

Rode around Jurong West after we talked, just to clear my head. Ended up at a hawker center, eating prata and watching families come and go. A father helping his daughter with homework. An elderly couple sharing a plate of carrot cake. Ordinary scenes. The kind of life that requires showing up, day after day, even when it's boring. Especially when it's boring. Maybe that's its own kind of courage. Maybe I've been confusing restlessness with meaning.

Told her it beats sitting in an air-conditioned office watching my soul leak out through Excel spreadsheets. She laughed, said her son is trying to get her to hire a manager so she can rest. Got the async handler working today. Took me three days of reading documentation and watching my screen like it owed me money, but when those tests finally went green, I stood up and made myself a cup of tea. Just stood there in the kitchen, holding the warm mug, looking out at the grey Berlin sky.

Lukas, one of the junior devs, sent a thumbs-up emoji in Slack when he saw my commit. He has no idea what that little green checkmark cost me. But that is fine. I know.
entries.nudge_text: What did it feel like, standing there with the tea?
entries.response_text: Like I had earned five minutes of quiet. Not celebration, just stillness after fighting something.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent most of the day in meetings. Sprint planning, architecture review, a call with the client about edge cases nobody had thought about. By the time I closed my laptop it was already dark outside. Made some hummus from a jar and ate it with stale bread while scrolling through news I did not really read.

Fatima called but I let it ring. Texted her later that I was in a meeting. I was not. I just did not have anything to say.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have said if you'd picked up?
entries.response_text: Probably just the usual. Work is fine, eating okay, miss you. The same loop. Sometimes I do not want to perform that script.

entries.initial_entry:
The new hire, Emma, asked me to pair program today. She needed help understanding the ownership model in Rust. I spent two hours explaining borrow checker errors, drawing diagrams on my tablet, watching her face shift from confusion to clarity.

By the end, she said, "Tariq, you should do a lunch-and-learn for the whole team." I laughed and said maybe. But I meant it. That felt like the best two hours I have had in weeks.
entries.nudge_text: Are you going to do it?

entries.initial_entry: Flying home tomorrow. Already packed my bag, already checked in online. I keep refreshing the weather app for Amman like it is going to change.
Back in Berlin. The flat smells stale. I opened all the windows even though it was cold. Fatima and I spent four days together and I still feel like I missed something.
entries.nudge_text: What did you miss?

entries.initial_entry: Same day, later. Could not sleep. My brother called to ask when I am coming back for good. I said I do not know. He said our mother keeps asking about me. I told him I would call her soon. I probably will not.
entries.nudge_text: What stops you from calling her?
entries.response_text: Because she will ask questions I do not have answers to. When are you retiring? Why are you not here? I left at 56 and I cannot explain that to her in a way that does not sound like running away.

entries.initial_entry:
Did the lunch-and-learn. Fifteen people showed up, including the CTO. I walked them through memory safety concepts, showed some examples of common pitfalls, answered questions for twenty minutes after.

Afterwards, Marco from the backend team said, "You should have been teaching this whole time." I do not know if that was a compliment or a comment on my code contributions. Either way, I felt useful in a way I have not felt in months.
Got feedback in my one-on-one that I should "lean into documentation more." I think that means they want me to write rather than code. I nodded and said I would think about it.
entries.nudge_text: How did that land?
entries.response_text: Like being put on the shelf. Maybe I am reading too much into it, but it felt like being redirected.

entries.initial_entry: Saturday. Went to Mauerpark, watched people sing karaoke in the amphitheater. A guy my age was doing a terrible version of a song I did not recognize. His friends were cheering like he was winning something.
entries.nudge_text: What were you thinking while you watched him?
entries.response_text: That he did not care if he was bad. He just wanted to be up there. I have not felt that kind of careless in a long time.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, evening. Walked back along the canal, bought a kebab from a place near Eberswalder, ate it sitting on a bench. The light was that soft gold that Berlin gets sometimes before sunset.

I have been here six months. Half a year of takeaway containers and video calls and learning syntax. Half a year of proving I could start over. I do not know what comes next. The lease on my flat is up in August. The project I joined will probably ship by then, or it will not. Either way I will have to decide something.

The kebab was good. The guy remembered my order from last time. That felt like more continuity than anything else in my life right now.
entries.nudge_text: What are your options?

entries.initial_entry:
My manager asked if I had considered moving into a developer advocate role. Travel, conferences, representing the company to external developers. "You have the experience and the presence," she said.

I told her I would think about it. What I did not tell her is that it sounds like being put in front of people because they do not know where else to put me. I came here to build things. Not to talk about building things.

But maybe I am being stubborn. Fatima would say I am being stubborn. She would say any role is a role if you do it well. Maybe she is right. I do not know.
entries.nudge_text: What would you lose if you took it?

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with Lukas and Emma last night. They took me to some trendy place in Kreuzberg with exposed brick and cocktails that cost too much. We talked about the project, about their weekend plans, about nothing in particular.

At some point Emma asked how I ended up in Berlin. I told them the short version: got bored, saw a job posting, applied on a whim, got the offer, said yes. Lukas looked at me like I had told him I climbed Everest. "At 56?" he said. "My dad is 55 and he complains about learning new apps."

I did not know how to explain it to him. That staying in Amman felt like watching the same episode of a show I had already seen. That I needed to not know what was going to happen next. That the discomfort of being the oldest person in the room is better than the comfort of being exactly where everyone expected me to be.

I just shrugged and said, "Different people want different things."
entries.nudge_text: What did you want to say instead?
entries.response_text: That I was terrified when I got on that plane. That I still am, most days. But the fear feels more alive than the certainty I left behind.

entries.initial_entry:
The exhibition proposal went through. Three years of work, and the municipal committee finally said yes. I spent the morning just sitting with the letter, reading it over again. The installation will be about the rising waters—the families in Bangladesh, the villages disappearing, but also what it means here, for us, watching from our safe harbors.

I called my mother to tell her. She asked if it meant I wouldn't have to worry about rent anymore. I said yes, for a while. She sounded relieved. So am I, honestly. The grant covers materials and a modest stipend. It's not wealth, but it's enough to stop checking my account every morning.

Strange to feel both grounded and ambitious at once. Usually those pull in different directions for me.

Spent the whole day researching for the installation and now I can't sleep. The numbers are overwhelming—millions displaced, coastlines eroding, and we're here arguing about parking spaces and bicycle lanes. Not that those don't matter. They do. But the scale of it.

My neighbor Hans stopped by, wanted to borrow sugar of all things, and I ended up talking at him for twenty minutes about sea level projections. Poor man just wanted to bake a cake. He nodded politely and escaped as soon as he could. I do this. I know I do this. The work consumes me and then I become insufferable.

But also: what are we supposed to do? Make art that no one sees? Make art that everyone sees but changes nothing? I keep thinking about that Adrienne Rich line about diving into the wreck. The thing itself and not the myth. I want to make something true. Something that matters. But I also want to pay my electricity bill and not have a panic attack every time my card gets declined.

The residency helps. God, it helps so much. But it's three years and then what? Back to the hustle. Back to applying for everything, hearing nothing. I'm too old for this uncertainty. Or maybe I'm exactly old enough to know it never goes away.
entries.nudge_text: You mention being 'too old for this uncertainty' but also describe being consumed by work that matters to you. How do you sit with both of those truths?
entries.response_text: I suppose I've always thought I'd have to choose eventually. That at some point the responsible thing would be to give up the art and get a real job, whatever that means. My father certainly thought so. But here I am at fifty-one, and I haven't chosen. I just keep doing both—worrying and working. Maybe that is the choice. Maybe the tension is the point, not something to resolve. The discomfort keeps me honest. Keeps me from getting too comfortable with my little grants and my little installations while the world burns.

entries.initial_entry:
Studio day. Cut seventeen panels for the base structure. Hands are sore. The plywood is heavier than I remembered, or I'm weaker. Probably both.

Got a call from the arts center about scheduling. They want to move my opening back two weeks. I said fine. It doesn't really matter.

Leftovers for dinner. Early bed.

The news today. Another boat capsized in the Mediterranean. Forty-seven people. They had names. They had families waiting for them. And I'm here in my warm studio, making art about their suffering while drinking expensive coffee.

I called Ingrid and cried on the phone for half an hour. She said the work matters. She said bearing witness matters. I want to believe her but some days it feels like decoration. Like we're all just rearranging deck chairs.

My mother called later, worried about me. She heard something in my voice this morning. I told her I was fine. I'm not fine, but what can she do? She's eighty-three. She doesn't need my existential crises.

The panels are half-finished. Tomorrow I'll keep cutting. That's all I know how to do.
entries.nudge_text: You describe feeling like the work is 'decoration.' Has there been a time when your art felt more connected to real impact?
entries.response_text:
Yes. In 2019, I did an installation at a refugee center in Berlin. Nothing fancy—just photographs and recorded voices, stories from people who'd made the crossing. A woman named Fatima came to the opening. She'd been one of the voices. She stood in front of her own image and wept, and then she hugged me. She said no one had ever asked her to tell her story before. That it mattered that someone wanted to listen.

I think about Fatima often. Whether she'd think this new work was useful or just... European guilt made pretty.

entries.initial_entry:
Two days before Christmas. Studio closed for the holiday. I should be relieved but I don't know what to do with myself.

Bought presents for my mother. A scarf, some chocolate, a book about Danish gardens. Small things. Safe things. She doesn't want much anymore.

My back hurts. I've been hunched over those panels for weeks. Need to stretch more. Need to take care of this body that insists on getting older.

Sleep.

Christmas with my mother was quiet. We ate roast duck and watched old films. She fell asleep on the couch around eight and I just sat there, watching her breathe. Thinking about how many more of these we might have.

She asked about the installation. I tried to explain the concept—the rising waters, the displaced families, the way I want viewers to feel implicated. She nodded and said, "You've always cared too much." Not a criticism. Just an observation.

Maybe that's true. Maybe caring too much is the only way I know how to be in the world. The alternative—not caring—seems worse. Deadening.

The new year approaches. I don't make resolutions anymore. I just try to keep working. Keep showing up. Keep paying attention to what hurts.
entries.nudge_text: When you say you 'keep paying attention to what hurts'—are you referring to the world's pain, your own, or both?
entries.response_text: Both, I suppose. Though I'm not sure there's much difference anymore. The work has a way of blurring those lines. When I'm deep in research about displacement, I feel displaced. When I'm cutting panels until my hands ache, the ache feels earned. Like penance, maybe. Or communion. I don't know. The question feels too large for the last day of the year.

entries.initial_entry:
Got feedback from Katrine at the arts center today. She thinks the installation is "too heavy." Says audiences want hope. Says I should consider adding something uplifting at the end.

I smiled and said I'd think about it. What I wanted to say: hope is a luxury. Hope is what you get to feel when you're not the one drowning. But she controls my funding, so I nodded.

Ingrid says I should compromise. Pick my battles. She's probably right. She's usually right about these things. But every compromise feels like a small death. Another edge sanded down. Another truth made palatable.

Maybe I'm being stubborn. Maybe stubbornness is all I have left. The work will be what it is. If they don't like it, they can find someone else. Someone younger, cheaper, more optimistic.
entries.nudge_text: You mention that 'every compromise feels like a small death,' yet you also nodded and smiled. What helps you decide which compromises to make?

entries.initial_entry:
The base structure is done. All thirty-four panels, cut and sanded and ready for paint. I stood back today and looked at the skeleton of the thing—this rising wave that will eventually hold video projections, sound, the voices of people I've never met whose lives hang in the balance.

I thought about Katrine's feedback. The hope question. And I realized I was wrong to dismiss it entirely. Not because she's right that audiences need comfort, but because hope isn't the opposite of truth. Fatima in Berlin—she had hope. Impossible, stubborn hope. The families I've interviewed for this project, the ones who lost everything and started over—they have hope too. It's not naive. It's a form of resistance.

So I'm adding something. Not a happy ending, but a thread. A wall of handwritten messages from people who've rebuilt their lives after displacement. Their words, their handwriting. Small acts of continuation.

My mother would say I'm softening. Maybe I am. Maybe softening isn't the same as surrender.

The residency runs until 2027. Two more years of this stability. Two more years of being able to worry about the work instead of worrying about money. I'm grateful in a way I couldn't have articulated ten years ago. Grateful and still restless. Still wanting to make something true.

Outside, the harbor is gray. Winter light. The water rises imperceptibly, day by day. We're all just trying to stay afloat.

Amma called this morning while I was waiting for an order at the hawker center and she was crying before she even said hello. Priya's college fees went up again, some new lab component they're adding to the program. I told her not to worry, that I would figure it out, but my hands were shaking a bit when I hung up. The order came through right after and I had to ride with that sitting in my chest.

I ran the numbers on the way home. If I pick up the late night slots Monday through Thursday on top of the regular daytime rides, I can probably cover the gap. That's four extra hours a day though. My back has been acting up from all the riding already. Kumar keeps telling me to see the doctor at the polyclinic but when exactly am I supposed to go. Every hour I sit in a waiting room is money not coming in.

I keep thinking about what Appa would have done. He worked the rubber plantation six days a week and never complained, at least not where we could hear. But I also remember how tired he always looked. I don't want to end up like that but I also can't imagine calling Amma back and saying actually we need to find another way for Priya's fees.

Something good happened today. Mrs. Lim from the fourth floor introduced me to her son-in-law who runs a small renovation company. He said he's been looking for someone reliable to do basic plumbing and electrical work for his residential jobs, not full-time but steady weekend work. The pay is better per hour than delivery riding.

I told him about the things I've been fixing around the block and he asked if I could come by a site in Bedok on Saturday to show him what I can do. It feels like the first real opening in months. If it works out, I could drop some of the late night delivery slots and still cover Priya's fees without grinding myself into the ground.

Called Amma to tell her and she immediately started worrying about whether the renovation work is safe, whether I'll be climbing ladders, whether the boss is trustworthy. That's how she shows she cares, I know that. I just wished for one moment she could say that sounds great, Rajesh, I'm glad. But I could hear the relief underneath the worrying, so I'll take it.
entries.nudge_text: What would it mean to you if she had said that?
entries.response_text: It would mean she sees me, not just what I provide. Amma has always been practical, it's how she survived after Appa died. Everything gets filtered through is it safe, is it sustainable, will it last. I understand why. But sometimes I wish someone in my family would just be proud of me for figuring things out here on my own. Priya does, actually. She sent me a voice note last week saying she couldn't believe I fixed the neighbor's water heater. Small thing but I replayed it twice.

entries.initial_entry: Good day on the road. Hit my target by 4pm which almost never happens. Used the extra time to pick up some curry puff for the uncles who play chess downstairs, they always wave at me when I ride past and I figured why not. Uncle Rajan tried to teach me a Sicilian opening again. I'm terrible at chess but he laughs every time I make a bad move and honestly that's worth more than winning.
entries.nudge_text: What draws you to spending your free time with them?

entries.initial_entry:
Priya called at midnight her time which is never a good sign. One of her classmates had some kind of breakdown during clinicals and Priya was the one who stayed with her at the hospital for three hours while the girl's family was contacted. She sounded rattled. I stayed on the phone for forty minutes just letting her talk, even though I had an early morning slot booked.

Now I can't sleep. Partly because I'm worried about Priya and partly because I keep thinking about what happens if she burns out before she even finishes the degree. She's like me, she can't watch someone struggle and just walk past. I don't know if that's a strength or a problem. Probably both. I should have told her to look after herself first but I didn't because honestly I don't follow that advice either so who am I to give it.

Saturday site visit went better than I expected. Mr. Tan, Mrs. Lim's son-in-law, watched me fix a leaking pipe joint and re-wire a light switch and said he'd give me two days a week starting next month. Shook my hand properly, looked me in the eye, said "you clearly know what you're doing." I haven't heard that from a boss figure in a long time.

Came back and told my flatmate Kumar and he said we should celebrate so we went to the roti prata place near the MRT. I kept doing the math in my head over dinner. Two days renovation plus four days delivery is still a lot but the hourly rate is almost double, so I could actually rest on Sundays for once. Might even have time to call home when I'm not half asleep.
entries.nudge_text: You mention resting on Sundays as something you 'could' do. What usually wins when you have a free day?

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Did the late night slots three days in a row because one of the other riders, Suresh, had to fly back to Chennai for a family emergency and I told the coordinator I could cover. Stupid probably but Suresh helped me when I first started and didn't know the routes. You don't forget that. My knee is swollen and I've been icing it with a bag of frozen peas that Kumar keeps threatening to eat.

Mrs. Lim knocked on the door today with a container of herbal soup. She said I looked thin the last time she saw me and that I remind her of her late husband who also never knew when to stop working. I didn't know what to say to that so I just thanked her and ate the whole thing standing in the kitchen. It was really good. I can't remember the last time someone cooked for me who wasn't being paid for it.

Priya sent money. Not much, maybe fifty dollars equivalent, from her part-time tutoring. She said it was for me to see the doctor about my back. I almost sent it right back but something stopped me. Maybe the soup. Maybe being tired enough that my stubbornness ran out of fuel. I booked the polyclinic appointment for Monday. We'll see if I actually go.
entries.nudge_text: What made you keep Priya's money instead of sending it back?
entries.response_text: Because she would have been hurt. She's working that tutoring job on top of her studies specifically to send me something, and sending it back would be like saying her effort doesn't count. I know that feeling. When I first started sending money home Amma tried to refuse and it made me feel small, like I couldn't contribute. So I kept Priya's fifty dollars because I remembered what it felt like to have someone reject what you're trying to give them. The back appointment is real though. I need to actually go.

entries.initial_entry: Can't sleep. Kept redoing the sketch for the Suba district mural because the committee wants something "uplifting" and every version I draw keeps drifting toward the river contamination. My hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you showed them your real sketch?
entries.response_text: They'd smile politely and tell me to try again. That's how these things work — you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive. It's not about my sketch.

entries.initial_entry: Good day at the workshop. One of the teenagers, David, showed up with a collage about his neighborhood flooding last year — garbage and river water mixed together. He made it beautiful without sanitizing it. I wish I had his nerve sometimes.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think gives him that nerve that you wish you had?
entries.response_text: He doesn't have a committee reviewing his work. He doesn't have a landlord or a mother who calls every Sunday asking if the grants are coming through. David just has cardboard and glue and something he needs to say. There's a freedom in that. I used to have it too, I think, before I learned all the reasons to hold back.

entries.initial_entry:
Meeting with Patricia from the environmental collective went better than expected. She's organizing a march next month about the deforestation in Guaviare and wants muralists to paint temporary pieces along the route. No committee, no brief, no polite rewrites — just paint what you see and feel. I said yes before I finished my coffee.

Spent the afternoon looking at satellite images of the cleared areas. It's staggering how fast the green disappears between one photo and the next. I started sketching a piece that layers the satellite view underneath a portrait of a family I met two years ago when I traveled there for a residency. They had this small farm at the edge of the forest. I keep wondering if that farm still exists.

The march is February 28. Patricia asked if I'd also speak at the rally beforehand. I told her I'd think about it. Speaking is different from painting — you can't hide behind brushstrokes. My mother would probably tell me to just do the mural and not draw attention to myself. She still worries about artists getting into trouble. But Patricia's right that the work means more when the person behind it shows up.

The Suba mural committee approved a revised design. I compromised — kept the river but softened the contamination imagery into something more abstract. Flowing blues and greens instead of the murky brown I originally wanted. They loved it. Said it was "inspiring." I nodded and thanked everyone and then sat in my car for ten minutes staring at the steering wheel.

I keep telling myself this is how it works. Public art is negotiation. You serve the community's vision, not just your own. And maybe the abstracted version will still land — maybe someone will look at those blues and sense that something is wrong underneath. That's the hope, anyway. But part of me keeps hearing David's collage in my head, all that directness, no translation required.

Called my cousin Marcela tonight. She's a lawyer in Medellin, very proper, always knows the right thing to say at family dinners. I told her about the march and she got quiet for a long time. Then she said, "Lu, you know how tia Rosa talks. Just be careful what you attach your name to." And I know she means it with love. I know the family worries. But careful has a cost too, and I'm starting to wonder how much it's adding up.
entries.nudge_text: When you say careful has a cost — what does that cost feel like day to day?
entries.response_text: Like a low hum. This background noise of things I chose not to say, images I softened, opinions I kept to dinner with close friends. None of it is dramatic on its own. But when I look at my portfolio from five years ago versus now, the work is technically better but quieter. The edges got filed down somewhere. I'm not sure when it happened exactly.

entries.initial_entry:
Family group chat exploded today. My uncle Hernando posted a news article about the Guaviare deforestation — same issue the march is about — and wrote "sad but what can you do, these people need to eat." I started typing a response three times. Deleted all of them.

It's not that I disagree entirely — the economics of it are real and I've seen the poverty firsthand. But the framing drives me insane. "What can you do" as if there are no choices, no policies, no one responsible. I wanted to write about the logging companies, the lack of enforcement, the families who actually live there being displaced. But Hernando is my mother's brother and I can already picture the awkward silence at Easter dinner if I go off in the group chat.

So I sent a sad emoji and closed the app. Mature. Professional. Utterly hollow. Patricia would have posted a five-paragraph essay with citations. I admire that and also cannot imagine being that person in my own family. The mural for the march — that I can do. Words in the chat where my grandmother can see them — that's different somehow, and I hate that it's different.
Finished the base layer of the march mural today. It's honest work. Stood back and felt something I haven't felt in a while — that the paint is saying exactly what I mean.
entries.nudge_text: Sounds like something clicked. What's different about this piece?

entries.initial_entry: My mother called. Someone told her about the march mural. She didn't yell — she never yells — she just got very quiet and asked "is this going to be a problem for you?" That question does more damage than yelling ever could.
entries.nudge_text: What does her worry do to you?
entries.response_text: Makes me want to reassure her, mostly. I told her it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true. But she doesn't need to carry that. She has enough.

entries.initial_entry: Long day prepping supplies for Friday. Shoulders ache. Didn't think about the committee or my mother or the march. Just mixed pigments and cleaned brushes. Sometimes that's enough.

Two days until the march. Patricia confirmed I'm on the speaker list. I told her yes three weeks ago and have been quietly hoping she'd forget or the schedule would change. It didn't. So now I'm sitting here at midnight trying to figure out what to say to a crowd when I couldn't even type a paragraph in my family's group chat.

The mural is done. I'm proud of it — really proud. It shows the forest canopy dissolving into ash at the edges, with small figures of families walking along the border between green and grey. It's direct without being preachy, I think. But speaking is a different exposure. My name attached to words, recorded on someone's phone, shared. My uncle could see it. My mother definitely would hear about it. Marcela's voice keeps playing in my head: "be careful what you attach your name to."

I keep going back and forth. One minute I think: this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine. The next minute I think: who am I to stand on a stage when I can't even push back on a mural committee? Maybe I should just let the painting speak and stay behind it where I'm good. But Patricia asked me specifically because she said artists need to be visible, not just their work. And she's not wrong. I just wish being visible didn't feel like standing in traffic.
entries.nudge_text: If you speak and your family hears — what's the worst that actually happens?

entries.initial_entry:
The march was last Friday. I spoke. It was short — maybe three minutes — and my voice shook the entire time. I talked about how art can hold what policy reports can't, the texture of a place disappearing, the face of someone losing their home to a decision made in an office they'll never enter. Patricia squeezed my arm when I sat down. The crowd was bigger than I expected.

My mother hasn't mentioned it. Marcela sent a one-word text: "brave." I don't know if that's approval or a warning. Hernando liked a photo of the mural on Patricia's Instagram, which is confusing. The sky didn't fall. The family didn't fracture. I feel lighter than I have in weeks, and also a little raw, like a layer of skin is missing. Not sure if I'd do it again. But I'm glad I did it once.
entries.nudge_text: What surprised you most about how it felt?
entries.response_text: That nobody was angry. I'd built this whole catastrophe in my head — my mother crying, my uncle calling me irresponsible, the family closing ranks. None of it happened. The worst outcome was silence, and silence turned out to be bearable. The actual surprise was that the shaking in my voice didn't make me less credible. People came up to me afterward and said it made them listen harder. I don't know what to do with that yet.

entries.initial_entry:
Same day, later. The Suba committee emailed asking if I'd be interested in a second mural for a park renovation. They mentioned they saw "my recent public work" and want something with that "raw quality." I read the email four times.

On one hand — this is exactly what I wanted. Someone seeing the unfiltered version and asking for more of it. The kind of work where I don't have to translate myself into something palatable. But then I read the rest of the email and there are guidelines, approval stages, a heritage review board. The same machinery. And I started wondering whether they want the rawness or the idea of rawness, something that looks honest but still fits neatly into a park renovation plan.

I haven't replied yet. Part of me wants to say yes and fight for the real version from inside the process. Part of me wants to say no and keep doing the kind of work where I answer only to the paint. And there's a third part — the loudest one, honestly — that says: just be grateful someone asked, don't be difficult, say thank you and show up and do good work within the lines. That voice sounds a lot like my mother. I don't think she's wrong. I don't think she's entirely right either. Going to sleep on it.

Got the feedback from Dr. Weiss today about the cardiac arrest protocol I revised. He actually read the whole thing. Said it was thorough, well-researched, that he'd pass it to the department head for review. I stood there nodding like it was no big deal but inside I was buzzing. This is the first time anyone senior has taken something I wrote seriously.

I've been working on that protocol for three months. Stayed after shifts, dug through research papers during lunch, ran the numbers on our ward's response times. Most of it I did on my own time because nobody asked me to. I just wanted to prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care. That I'm not just someone who checks vitals and changes IV bags.

After he left I went to the break room and just sat there for a minute. Didn't even get coffee. Just needed a second to let it land. It felt real. Like something I made mattered.

Picked up two extra shifts this week. Covered for Anja on Tuesday and stayed late Thursday for the new grad's first code situation. She froze at the monitors and I had to talk her through it step by step while doing compressions. Afterwards she thanked me and I told her that's just how it goes the first time.

Thomas made a comment about never seeing me anymore. Fair. I've been home maybe four evenings this month. But the ward is short-staffed and I'm trying to show I can handle the load. Being reliable matters when you're trying to move up.

Normal week. Two admissions, one discharge, one transfer to ICU. Ran the morning handoffs all five days since Monika was out sick. Nobody commented on it, which is fine. That's the job.

Had coffee with Klara from the surgical ward. She asked how I was doing since "the thing" — meaning Frau Hartmann getting the charge position instead of me. I said fine. She looked at me like she didn't believe it. I changed the subject to her holiday plans.
entries.nudge_text: What would believing it have looked like?

entries.initial_entry: Checked the intranet again. Still no updates on the junior ward manager posting. Refreshed it three times during break like that would change anything. Felt stupid afterwards.
entries.nudge_text: Why stupid?
entries.response_text: Just impatient I guess. Nothing unusual.

entries.initial_entry:
Thomas said something last night about me always being on my phone even when I'm home. He wasn't wrong, but the way he said it — like I'm doing something wrong by checking work emails — rubbed me the wrong way. I didn't pick a fight about it. Just said okay and put the phone down. But I kept thinking about it.

It's not like I want to be checking emails at 9pm. There's just always something. A shift swap request, a question from one of the students, updates about policy changes. If I don't stay on top of it I fall behind. And falling behind means being invisible.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if you're invisible?
entries.response_text: Then what was the point of any of it. The overtime, the extra certifications, the protocol work. I can't just be average.

entries.initial_entry:
Double shift yesterday. Started at 6am, didn't leave until almost midnight. We had a patient crash twice within four hours — Herr Müller, 67, the one with the triple bypass last week. Both times I was the first to respond. Both times we got him back. The second time I couldn't even feel my hands afterwards, just this hollow buzzing in my arms.

Dr. Weiss pulled me aside at the end. I thought he was going to say something about the protocol, maybe some feedback finally. Instead he asked if I was okay. Told me I looked exhausted. That I should take my full breaks. I nodded and said I was fine.

On the U-Bahn home I stared at my reflection in the dark window. Couldn't remember the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift. Or the last time I left on time. There's a performance review coming up next month and I keep thinking if I can just get there, if I can just hold on until then, it'll be worth it. But I don't know anymore.
entries.nudge_text: What would make it worth it?
entries.response_text: The title. The recognition. I don't know, proof that it mattered.

entries.initial_entry:
Frau Hartmann asked me to train the new hires on the cardiac monitoring system next week. She said I'm the best on the ward with the equipment and the documentation. Part of me was annoyed — I trained her on half of this stuff two years ago, and now she's delegating it back to me as if she invented it.

But I said yes. What else am I going to say? It's exposure. It's a chance to show the department head what I can do. Even if it means staying late again. Even if Thomas will eat dinner alone again. This is how it works. You don't get anywhere by saying no.
entries.nudge_text: What would saying no feel like?

entries.initial_entry: Thomas asked if I wanted to go to his cousin's wedding in August. I said I'd have to check my shifts. He didn't respond. Just went to bed. I stayed up looking at the calendar knowing I could probably make it work but not wanting to commit.
entries.nudge_text: What are you waiting for?

entries.initial_entry: Finished the training sessions. Got positive feedback from all four new hires. One of them said I made the defibrillator protocols actually make sense. Small win.

Realized I forgot our anniversary was yesterday. Thomas didn't say anything. Just made dinner like normal. I should have remembered. I had a reminder in my phone but I dismissed it during a shift and never looked again.
entries.nudge_text: What did you feel when you realized?

entries.initial_entry: Performance review scheduled for Friday. Spent the evening updating my self-assessment document. Listed all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work. Looks good on paper. Should be enough.
entries.nudge_text: Enough for what?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the morning glazing the new bowl series — that deep blue with copper undertones I've been chasing for months. Watched the light shift across the wet surface and forgot about everything else for a while. This is why I do this. The moment when the color catches and something ordinary becomes something worth looking at.

Then Mama called to ask if I could take her to the pharmacy. Of course I said yes. Packed up the brushes, washed my hands, drove her across town. We stopped for knafeh after and she held my hand in the car on the way back, said she's grateful I came home.

I am too, most days. But I also think about how I used to have whole weeks where nobody needed me for anything, and I could just make things until my hands hurt. I don't know if I miss that or if I just think I should miss it.

My cousin Nadia asked me to paint something for her new apartment. Not commissioned — just asked, the way family asks. Like my time and materials are a gift I owe her because we share blood. I said yes because what else do you say when your aunt is standing right there and Nadia's going through a divorce and everyone's been worried about her.

I don't even know what she wants. Something cheerful, she said. Blue maybe. Something to look at in the morning.

So now I'm supposed to make something cheerful and blue on top of the three workshop sessions I'm prepping, the gallery order that's due next week, and driving Mama to her appointments. And the worst part is I can't even be properly upset because Nadia's having a hard year and I should want to help. I do want to help. I just also want someone to ask me what I need instead of what I can give.

Had a glass of wine on the balcony after everyone left tonight. The city noise, the warm air, the taste of it — that small hour was the best part of my whole week. And I feel stupid admitting that because it's nothing, it's just sitting there, but everything else is for other people and that was just for me.
entries.nudge_text: What would you need right now if someone asked?
entries.response_text:
I think I'd want someone to take over the pharmacy runs for a week. Just one week where I don't have to be anywhere at a specific time. I'd finish the gallery order early and maybe drive to the Dead Sea with Reem and Dana, just for a day. Float in the water, eat too much, laugh at nothing. Come back tired in a good way instead of the way I usually come back tired.

It sounds selfish when I write it out. But I used to do things like that more often, and I was better company. I was a better artist too, I think. Less resentful. I don't want to become someone who counts favors.

entries.initial_entry:
Workshop went well today. Eight kids, ages seven to twelve. We made pinch pots and one girl named Sana made hers into a turtle. She was so proud she carried it to her mother with both hands like it was made of glass.

My brother Tariq said he might take Mama to the pharmacy on Thursday. We'll see if he actually follows through.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if he doesn't follow through?

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep. Tariq texted to say he has a work thing Thursday and can I cover after all. Of course I can. Of course I will.

I keep thinking about what Reem said last month, that I should just say no sometimes and see what happens. But she doesn't have a mother who needs her, and she doesn't have a brother who conveniently forgets his commitments, and she doesn't understand that if I don't do it then it just doesn't get done or Mama has to take a taxi alone and feel like a burden.

The thing is I don't even want to stop helping. I want to stop being the only one who helps. There's a difference but it gets lost in translation when I try to explain it to anyone in my family.

Started a new sketch tonight though. Something abstract, just shapes and warmth. No one asked for it. It's ugly so far but it's mine.

Finished Nadia's painting yesterday. Took it to her apartment and she cried when she saw it — not because it was good, I think, but because she needed someone to do something soft for her and I was the one who did it. We sat on her empty floor surrounded by moving boxes and she told me things about her marriage I didn't know. How small she'd felt. How invisible.

I stayed longer than I planned. Made her tea with the one pot she'd unpacked. Told her about the residency I turned down two years ago and how I sometimes wonder if I made the right call coming back. She said at least you had a call to make. At least you have something that's yours.

That stayed with me on the drive home. I do have something that's mine. The studio, the clay, the color mixing — all of it. Even when I'm tired and resentful and covered in glaze at midnight, I'm still making things that didn't exist before I made them.

Treated myself to good coffee this morning. The expensive kind, with cardamom. Sat in the garden and drank it slow while the light came up. Small luxuries. I'm learning they're not small at all — they're how I keep going. Without the good coffee and the Dead Sea trips and the wine on the balcony, I'd hollow out. I need those things as much as my family needs the pharmacy runs. Maybe that's okay.

Spent the morning glazing the new bowl series — that deep blue with copper undertones I've been chasing for months. Watched the light shift across the wet surface and forgot about everything else for a while. This is why I do this. The moment when the color catches and something ordinary becomes something worth looking at.

Then Mama called to ask if I could take her to the pharmacy. Of course I said yes. Packed up the brushes, washed my hands, drove her across town. We stopped for knafeh after and she held my hand in the car on the way back, said she's grateful I came home.

I am too, most days. But I also think about how I used to have whole weeks where nobody needed me for anything, and I could just make things until my hands hurt. I don't know if I miss that or if I just think I should miss it.

My cousin Nadia asked me to paint something for her new apartment. Not commissioned — just asked, the way family asks. Like my time and materials are a gift I owe her because we share blood. I said yes because what else do you say when your aunt is standing right there and Nadia's going through a divorce and everyone's been worried about her.

I don't even know what she wants. Something cheerful, she said. Blue maybe. Something to look at in the morning.

So now I'm supposed to make something cheerful and blue on top of the three workshop sessions I'm prepping, the gallery order that's due next week, and driving Mama to her appointments. And the worst part is I can't even be properly upset because Nadia's having a hard year and I should want to help. I do want to help. I just also want someone to ask me what I need instead of what I can give.

Had a glass of wine on the balcony after everyone left tonight. The city noise, the warm air, the taste of it — that small hour was the best part of my whole week. And I feel stupid admitting that because it's nothing, it's just sitting there, but everything else is for other people and that was just for me.
entries.nudge_text: What would you need right now if someone asked?
entries.response_text:
I think I'd want someone to take over the pharmacy runs for a week. Just one week where I don't have to be anywhere at a specific time. I'd finish the gallery order early and maybe drive to the Dead Sea with Reem and Dana, just for a day. Float in the water, eat too much, laugh at nothing. Come back tired in a good way instead of the way I usually come back tired.

It sounds selfish when I write it out. But I used to do things like that more often, and I was better company. I was a better artist too, I think. Less resentful. I don't want to become someone who counts favors.

entries.initial_entry:
Workshop went well today. Eight kids, ages seven to twelve. We made pinch pots and one girl named Sana made hers into a turtle. She was so proud she carried it to her mother with both hands like it was made of glass.

My brother Tariq said he might take Mama to the pharmacy on Thursday. We'll see if he actually follows through.
entries.nudge_text: What happens if he doesn't follow through?

entries.initial_entry:
Can't sleep. Tariq texted to say he has a work thing Thursday and can I cover after all. Of course I can. Of course I will.

I keep thinking about what Reem said last month, that I should just say no sometimes and see what happens. But she doesn't have a mother who needs her, and she doesn't have a brother who conveniently forgets his commitments, and she doesn't understand that if I don't do it then it just doesn't get done or Mama has to take a taxi alone and feel like a burden.

The thing is I don't even want to stop helping. I want to stop being the only one who helps. There's a difference but it gets lost in translation when I try to explain it to anyone in my family.

Started a new sketch tonight though. Something abstract, just shapes and warmth. No one asked for it. It's ugly so far but it's mine.

Finished Nadia's painting yesterday. Took it to her apartment and she cried when she saw it — not because it was good, I think, but because she needed someone to do something soft for her and I was the one who did it. We sat on her empty floor surrounded by moving boxes and she told me things about her marriage I didn't know. How small she'd felt. How invisible.

I stayed longer than I planned. Made her tea with the one pot she'd unpacked. Told her about the residency I turned down two years ago and how I sometimes wonder if I made the right call coming back. She said at least you had a call to make. At least you have something that's yours.

That stayed with me on the drive home. I do have something that's mine. The studio, the clay, the color mixing — all of it. Even when I'm tired and resentful and covered in glaze at midnight, I'm still making things that didn't exist before I made them.

Treated myself to good coffee this morning. The expensive kind, with cardamom. Sat in the garden and drank it slow while the light came up. Small luxuries. I'm learning they're not small at all — they're how I keep going. Without the good coffee and the Dead Sea trips and the wine on the balcony, I'd hollow out. I need those things as much as my family needs the pharmacy runs. Maybe that's okay.

The department meeting today went as expected. Principal wanted us to adopt the new digital assessment platform by January. Several younger teachers were enthusiastic, but I noticed Meera sitting silently, looking overwhelmed. She is only in her second year and already struggling with the curriculum changes from last term.

After the meeting, I walked with her to the staff room and showed her how to break down the transition into smaller steps. She seemed relieved. I did not mention this to anyone else because it might embarrass her. These small moments make me feel I am contributing something worthwhile, even if no one else notices.
Amma's blood pressure was high again this morning. Cancelled my NGO session to take her to the clinic. Feeling stretched thin.
entries.nudge_text: What's weighing on you most about today?
entries.response_text: The children at the tutoring center were expecting me. I had promised to bring the geometry worksheets I prepared. Now another volunteer will have to scramble, and the children will wonder why I didn't come. At the same time, Amma needed me and there was no question about staying. But sitting in that clinic waiting room, I kept thinking about those children waiting too, and feeling like I was failing someone no matter what I chose.

entries.initial_entry:
Staff lunch today, celebrating Lakshmi ma'am's retirement after thirty-five years. Everyone contributed dishes, told stories. The principal made a speech about her dedication. I sat there eating my rice and watching her accept a shawl and a plaque, thinking about how she spent three decades in the same building, teaching the same subject, attending the same festivals. She looked content.

My brother called afterward asking when I am planning to retire. His son is getting married next year and he wants to know if I can take extended leave to help with arrangements. I said I would see what is possible. The truth is I have not thought seriously about retirement. Teaching still feels meaningful most days, and I do not know what I would do with empty hours. But my brother sounded like he was already counting on my availability, and I did not want to disappoint him by saying I prefer to keep working.

There is also the question of my pension and whether the timing would be right. I have been careful with money but there are always uncertainties. Maybe I should start considering it more seriously. The conversation left me feeling unsettled in a way I cannot quite name.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned not wanting to disappoint your brother. What about what you want?

entries.initial_entry: Finished grading the Class 10 exams. Most students improved from last term. Ravi, who was failing in September, scored 72%.

My colleague Anand said something today that I cannot stop thinking about. We were discussing the new reservation policy for faculty hiring, and he made a dismissive comment about how it lowers standards. I disagreed but only said "these policies have complex reasons" and changed the subject to exam schedules.

Later I felt angry at myself. I have taught students from every background for nearly three decades and I know that talent is not distributed by birth. But I did not say any of this to Anand. I worried about creating an awkward atmosphere in the department. Now I am sitting here wondering if my silence made it seem like I agreed. The children I tutor on weekends would not recognize this version of me, the one who says nothing when it matters.
entries.nudge_text: You mentioned the children wouldn't recognize this version of you. Which version feels more like the real one?

entries.initial_entry:
New year, same routines. I find comfort in this. The school reopened today and there is something settling about walking through the gates, hearing the morning assembly, smelling chalk and floor cleaner.

I brought sweets for the staff room, as I do every January. Small gestures maintain the fabric of a workplace. I noticed the new Hindi teacher, Priya, looking uncertain during break time. I invited her to sit with our group. She seemed grateful. I remember how difficult my first year was, how much it mattered when senior teachers acknowledged me. If I can make someone's path a little easier, that feels like enough.
entries.nudge_text: What do you remember most about that first year?
entries.response_text:
I remember feeling like a fraud. I had my qualifications, my training, but standing in front of a classroom full of fourteen-year-olds who could sense every hesitation, I felt exposed. There was one day, maybe three months in, when a student asked me a question I could not answer. I panicked, gave some vague response, and spent the whole night convinced everyone knew I was not qualified to be there.

What saved me was Mrs. Subramanian, who taught physics. She never made a fuss, just started including me in her lunch conversations, asking my opinion on small matters, treating me like I belonged. I never told her how much that meant. She passed away six years ago. I suppose I keep doing for others what she did for me, hoping it matters the way it mattered to me.

entries.initial_entry:
My sister-in-law visited today and made a comment about how I spend too much time on "outside activities" instead of focusing on the family. She was referring to the NGO work. I explained calmly that I only go twice a month and always ensure Amma is cared for. She nodded but I could see she was not convinced.

I should not have to justify myself. I have managed this household for fifteen years while working full-time. I have never once complained about cooking for her when she visits or adjusting my schedule around her preferences. The tutoring gives me purpose beyond daily obligations. But I did not say any of this. I just served tea and changed the subject to her daughter's college applications.
entries.nudge_text: What does that purpose feel like when you're there with the children?
entries.response_text:
It feels like my skills matter beyond grades and syllabi. These children, most of them are first-generation learners. Their parents work long hours in construction or domestic help. When I explain a concept and I see understanding appear on a face that was confused a moment before, it is the same satisfaction I felt in my first years of teaching, before it became routine.

At school, I am Padmini ma'am, the department head who files reports and attends meetings. At the tutoring center, I am just someone who knows something useful and is willing to share it. The children do not care about my title or my seniority. They only care that I showed up and that I can help them understand fractions. It is simpler and somehow more honest.

entries.initial_entry:
Reading the news this morning about the air quality crisis in Delhi. Schools closed, children wearing masks, hospitals filled with respiratory cases. I kept thinking about the children there who have no choice but to breathe that air, whose families cannot afford air purifiers or trips to cleaner places.

We complain about Chennai traffic and summer heat, but this is different. This is a slow catastrophe that everyone sees but no one seems able to stop. The article mentioned that the poorest neighborhoods have the worst air quality because they are near industrial zones. Of course they do. It is always the same pattern, those with the least resources bearing the heaviest burdens.

I mentioned this to a colleague during lunch and she shrugged, said "what can we do, these things are beyond us." I understand the impulse. The scale of the problem makes individual action feel pointless. But I kept thinking about it through my afternoon classes. The students I teach will inherit this world. What am I preparing them for, if not to care about things larger than their exam scores?
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to prepare them to care about things larger than exams?
entries.response_text: That is the question, isn't it. I am not sure I have an answer. The syllabus is the syllabus, the exams are the exams. But sometimes I try to use real examples in my word problems. Calculating pollution levels, or ratios of resources. Small things. Maybe it plants a seed, maybe it does not. I cannot solve the crisis but I can teach the children who might.

entries.initial_entry:
Difficult day at the tutoring center. One of the regular students, Kavitha, did not come. I asked around and learned her family is moving back to their village because her father lost his job. She is twelve and had been making such progress in mathematics. Now she will attend the government school there, which may not have the same resources.

I keep thinking about all the Kavithas, the children whose potential depends on circumstances they did not choose. I can help a few, a handful each year, but there are so many more. The NGO coordinator asked if I could take on additional sessions. I said I would think about it, but I already know my family obligations make this difficult. Amma needs more care as she gets older, my brother's wedding preparations are starting, and I cannot ask others to carry my responsibilities so I can spend more time elsewhere. But it sits heavily, knowing there are children who need help and I am not able to give it.
entries.nudge_text: What would it feel like to say yes to those additional sessions?
entries.response_text:
It would feel like choosing. And I have spent my whole life arranging things so I do not have to choose, so everyone gets enough of me, even if no one gets all of me. If I said yes to more sessions, I would have to say no to something else, and then I would have to explain that no, and see the disappointment, and feel like I am being selfish.

The strange thing is, I know I am not selfish. I have given and given for decades. But somehow the idea of asking for something, of prioritizing what feeds my soul over what is expected, still feels like a transgression. Kavitha's face keeps appearing in my mind. I cannot save her from moving away, but maybe I could have helped more children like her if I had been braver about asking for time that was truly mine.
