entries.initial_entry:
Had a small win today. Closed a bulk deal with a buyer in Shenzhen, margins were solid, the whole thing came together in like two hours from first DM to payment confirmed. These are the moments where I feel like I actually know what I'm doing. Not pretending, not faking it. Just reading the situation right and moving.

My mum asked about it at dinner and I gave her the short version. She nodded but didn't say much. A year ago that would've stung. Tonight it just felt fine. I don't need her to get it. The deal speaks for itself and I know what it took.
entries.nudge_text: What's different about tonight?

entries.initial_entry: Spent the afternoon sourcing new drops, scrolling through pages of the same stuff everyone else is flipping. Getting harder to find edges. Might need to branch into vintage or accessories, something less saturated. Called James about it and he's keen to explore Taipei suppliers. Brain's moving fast today, not in a bad way, just restless.
entries.nudge_text: Bored or scared of stalling?
entries.response_text: Neither really. Just how my brain works, always scanning for the next angle. Anyway I need to finalize the Taipei logistics before I spiral into planning mode.

entries.initial_entry:
Auntie May came for dinner. The usual questions: when am I going back to school, do I have a real plan, what happens when this "internet thing" stops working. I smiled and gave polite non-answers. Changed the subject to her son's new job because I knew that would keep her busy for twenty minutes.

It's a skill at this point, steering conversations away from anything that makes them uncomfortable about me. I don't even get annoyed anymore. It's just the cost of sitting at that table without ruining everyone's evening. My cousin Jason does the same thing with his music career. We exchanged a look across the table. That was enough.

Dad asked me this morning if I'd consider taking a part-time role at Uncle Raymond's logistics company. Stable income, benefits, "good for your resume." I said I'd think about it. He looked relieved, which is probably the part that's bothering me most.

The business is actually growing. March numbers are tracking 40% above February. I have a potential partnership with a streetwear brand in Seoul that could change the whole trajectory. But none of that registers with him because it doesn't look like a job. I didn't bring up the Seoul thing. No point until there's a signed contract he can hold in his hands.
entries.nudge_text: Are you thinking about it or just buying time?

entries.initial_entry:
Been sitting with the uncle's company thing all day. Part of me knows the smart move is to say no and keep building. The numbers back me up. But there's this other part, the part that watched my dad's face relax when I said I'd consider it, and that part wants to just take the damn job so everyone can stop worrying.

It's not even about the money or the role. It's about what saying no does to the room. Every holiday, every family dinner, I'm the loose thread. The one who doesn't fit the pattern. And I can handle that most days but sometimes I wonder if I'm burning through goodwill I'm going to need later. Like what happens when I actually need their support for something and they've already written me off as the kid who wouldn't listen.

The Seoul partnership could be real. If it lands, it validates everything. But if it doesn't, I'm just the dropout who turned down a stable offer to chase another long shot. I keep toggling between these two versions of how this plays out and neither one lets me sleep.
entries.nudge_text: What support are you actually afraid of losing?
entries.response_text: Honestly, probably not financial. They'd help if things went really bad regardless. It's more like... being taken seriously. Right now I'm tolerated. If I keep going against what they want and it doesn't pay off spectacularly, I become the cautionary tale they tell my younger cousins. That's the version I can't stomach.

entries.initial_entry:
Mum just came into my room to say dad's feelings were hurt that I haven't given an answer about uncle's company yet. It's been twelve hours. Twelve. I told her I was still thinking and she gave me the look, the one that means she's already decided what the right answer is and is waiting for me to arrive at it.

I'm not being ungrateful. I know they're looking out for me. But every time they frame some fallback option as "just in case," what they're really saying is they don't think this works. And maybe they're right, but I need to figure that out on my own terms, not because I got guilted into hedging. Anyway. I told her I'd talk to dad tomorrow. That bought me another day at least.

And maybe they're right, but I need to figure that out on my own terms, not because I got guilted into hedging. Anyway. I told her I'd talk to dad tomorrow. That bought me another day at least.

End of the year crunch is starting to show. Had three classes back to back this morning and by fourth period I was basically on autopilot. The juniors are doing their neighborhood oral history projects, which is the one part of the semester I still genuinely look forward to, but even that felt like pushing a boulder today.

Stayed late grading because I refuse to bring that stack home again. The custodian, Marco, stopped by and we talked for twenty minutes about his kid's Little League team. Probably the best conversation I had all day. Sometimes the building feels more like mine after everyone leaves. Quieter. No one asking me to fill out forms or attend alignment meetings.

Came home, heated up leftover chili, and sat in the garage for a while not really doing anything with the lathe. Just sitting. The dog parked herself at my feet. That was enough for tonight.

Could not sleep. Kept replaying the staff meeting from this afternoon. Reynolds stood up there with her slides about "instructional coherence" and basically told us the district is adopting a packaged curriculum for all social studies classes starting next fall. Everyone just nodded. I raised my hand and asked what happens to the electives I built. She said we would "discuss individual cases" later. That is code for no.

Twelve years of that program. I designed every unit, every field trip route, every community partner relationship. And some vendor's binder is supposed to replace it because test scores dipped two points in a grade level I do not even teach. I should have pushed harder in the meeting. Or maybe I should not have said anything at all. I do not know which one sits worse.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from pushing harder?
entries.response_text: Honestly, the room. Everyone had already accepted it. I looked around and realized I was the only one who cared enough to object, and that made me feel old and stubborn rather than right.

entries.initial_entry:
Had the juniors present their oral history drafts today and it reminded me exactly why I do not want to hand this over to a shrink-wrapped curriculum. Daniela interviewed her grandmother about the Vanport flood. She brought in a photograph she had never seen before, one her grandmother kept in a shoebox for sixty years. The whole class went quiet.

You cannot buy that in a textbook package. You cannot script it. It comes from giving kids room to chase something that matters to them and getting out of the way. I told Daniela after class that her project was one of the best I have seen in nearly three decades, and she looked genuinely shocked, like no one had told her she was good at anything academic before.

Drove home with the windows down even though it was drizzling. Put on the Coltrane record Linda used to play on Friday evenings. Poured a glass of wine. Felt like myself for the first time all week. Whatever happens with the curriculum decision, today was proof that my way of teaching works. That has to count for something.

Reynolds pulled me aside after school and offered me a role as "curriculum liaison" for the new program. Basically wants me to help implement the thing I spent the last week arguing against. I said I would think about it but the whole walk to the parking lot felt like swallowing something sharp.
entries.nudge_text: What part of saying yes scares you most?

entries.initial_entry:
Been thinking about the liaison offer all weekend. My daughter called Sunday and I mentioned it. She said take it, that it is better to be at the table than shouting from outside. She is probably right from a strategic perspective. But something about it feels like agreeing that the packaged curriculum is acceptable as long as I get a title and a small say in how it rolls out.

I keep thinking about the first year I built the civic history elective. Nobody approved it. I just wrote the proposal, walked it into the old principal's office, and made my case. He let me try it for one semester. That was 2013. It has run every year since. The idea that I would now become the person helping dismantle what I created on my own terms feels backwards.

But I am also fifty-seven. I have maybe eight years left before retirement. Part of me wonders if fighting this is just stubbornness dressed up as principle. Linda would have told me to pick my battles. She was better at knowing which hills were worth it. I keep reaching for that kind of clarity and coming up empty.
entries.nudge_text: If Linda said take it, would you listen?

entries.initial_entry:
Told Reynolds no on Friday. She took it well enough, said the door stays open. I do not think it does, but that is fine. Spent the weekend not thinking about school for the first time in weeks. Built a cutting board out of walnut and maple for my neighbor's birthday. Spent four hours on the glue-up alone because I kept adjusting the pattern. Nobody was timing me.

Saturday morning I went to the farmers market and ran into two former students, both from the civic history elective, one from 2016 and one from 2019. The 2016 kid, well she is twenty-five now, told me she works for the city planning office and still thinks about the neighborhood mapping project. I did not bring up the curriculum stuff. But it sat with me the whole drive home.

Graded the last round of oral history presentations tonight. Gave Daniela's project full marks and wrote her a recommendation letter she did not ask for. I figure if the program disappears next year, at least this cohort got the real version. The garage smelled like fresh-cut walnut when I came inside. The dog was asleep on the sawdust pile again. Quiet house, but not an empty one.

Spent most of the morning at the hospital with Mother again. Her blood pressure readings have been erratic and the doctors want to adjust her medication. I sat in that waiting room scrolling through my literature review notes, pretending I could focus on methodology while watching the clock. By the time we got home and I made sure she ate lunch and took her pills, half the day was gone.

I signed up for this PhD knowing it would be challenging. What I didn't account for was how often life would demand I put it aside. My advisor sent a gentle reminder about the chapter draft due next week. I haven't written a single new paragraph in twelve days.

Strange to be the oldest person in every seminar, still learning to say "I need more time" without feeling like a failure.
entries.nudge_text: What does failure look like to you here?
entries.response_text:
I suppose it looks like proving everyone right — my former colleagues who raised eyebrows when I resigned, my brother who thinks this degree is a vanity project. They expected me to enjoy retirement properly: travel with my husband, babysit grandchildren, maybe volunteer at the temple. Instead I'm chasing citations at fifty-eight.

But if I'm honest, the deeper fear is wasting whatever sharp years I have left. There's a clock now that wasn't there at thirty. Every delay feels heavier.

entries.initial_entry:
My daughter called crying at eleven last night. Her husband is traveling for work again and the baby has been colicky for three days straight. She asked if I could come stay for the weekend to help. I said yes before she finished the sentence, and then hung up and stared at my calendar.

The weekend is when I was supposed to finally sit down and write. I had blocked off Saturday and Sunday entirely — no hospital visits, no groceries, no family dinners. Just me, my laptop, and the quiet of an empty apartment. I was looking forward to it the way you look forward to a small holiday.

Now I'm packing a bag instead. And I feel terrible for resenting it, even a little. She's my daughter. She's exhausted and alone and I'm her mother. What kind of person calculates their writing time when their child needs them?

But another part of me is tired of always being the one who drops everything. My husband won't go — he says he's "not good with babies," as if that's a permanent condition and not something you can simply choose to do. So it falls to me. It always falls to me.

I'm bringing my laptop. I'm telling myself I'll write at night after the baby sleeps. We both know that's not going to happen.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you said no, just this once?

entries.initial_entry:
Back from my daughter's. Wrote nothing. Slept maybe four hours each night. The baby finally settled on Sunday evening, just in time for me to catch the train home.

I keep thinking about the pork belly buns from that little shop near her apartment. We walked there Saturday afternoon while the baby napped in the carrier. Twenty minutes of sunshine and soft dough and my daughter laughing at something I said. Those moments felt like breathing.

Maybe that has to be enough for now.

Submitted the draft. Not my best work but it exists. My advisor responded within an hour with comments — mostly structural, nothing devastating.

Made congee for Mother tonight. She said it tasted like my grandmother's, which is the highest compliment she gives. Small victories.
entries.nudge_text: How are you feeling now that it's submitted?
entries.response_text: My cohort is organizing a hot pot dinner for next week. First time they've included me in something social — usually they forget the old lady in the corner. I'm looking forward to it more than I probably should. Haven't had good hot pot in months.

entries.initial_entry:
The hot pot was wonderful. Crowded restaurant, too loud to hear anything, steam fogging my glasses every time I reached for the lamb slices. Xiao-Wei sat next to me and we discovered we both grew up eating the same brand of sesame paste that nobody makes anymore.

Funny how food carries so much. My mother used to mix that paste into noodles on cold days. Now I'm in a room full of people young enough to be my children, fishing mushrooms out of boiling broth, and for two hours I wasn't thinking about deadlines or hospital appointments or whether I'm falling behind.

I ordered a second round of the taro dumplings even though nobody asked. Sometimes you have to be the one who says "let's keep going." They didn't seem to mind.

Walked home through the night market after. Bought a bag of pineapple cakes I definitely don't need. Didn't feel guilty about it even once.
entries.nudge_text: This sounds different from the weekend at your daughter's.
entries.response_text:
It is different. No one needed me to be anywhere else. No responsibility I was avoiding by being there. I was just having dinner because I wanted to, with people who expected nothing from me except to pass the chili oil.

Maybe that's the permission I've been missing — not from anyone else but from the situation itself. When there's no competing obligation, the enjoyment feels clean.

entries.initial_entry:
My brother called this morning to remind me that Mother's 80th birthday is in three weeks and "everyone is expecting a proper celebration." By everyone he means himself and the cousins who haven't visited her in months. By proper celebration he means I'll be the one booking the restaurant, organizing the guest list, and making sure the gift is tasteful enough to photograph.

I told him I'm in the middle of my comprehensive exams and he laughed. Actually laughed. Said something about how I could take a break from "playing student" for one day.

I hung up on him. First time I've ever done that. My hands were shaking.

I know I should call back and smooth things over. That's what I always do. But I'm so tired of being the one who manages his feelings while he dismisses mine.

Three hours later and I still haven't called him back. My husband thinks I'm overreacting. "He didn't mean it that way," he said. "You know how he is."

Yes. I know exactly how he is. I've known for fifty years. That's precisely the problem.

When I gave up my career advancement to take care of our father during his illness, my brother sent flowers. When I spent six months coordinating Mother's hip surgery and recovery while working full time, my brother called on weekends. When I finally, finally decided to do something entirely for myself — to pursue this degree I've wanted since I was thirty — he treats it like a hobby I can set aside.

I'm not even angry at him specifically anymore. I'm angry at the role I've been assigned in this family, the one I keep accepting because the alternative feels like abandoning people I love. The reliable one. The one who handles things. The one whose own aspirations come last because someone has to hold everything together.

My chest hurts. I should make tea and calm down but I don't want to calm down. I want to stay angry long enough to remember this feeling the next time I'm tempted to say yes to something I don't have room for.
entries.nudge_text: What would it look like to not be the reliable one?
entries.response_text:
Terrifying. And also, maybe, free?

I think about my classmate Hui-Fang who went to Portugal last month just because she wanted to see the ocean. She didn't ask permission or coordinate schedules. She just booked a ticket and went.

I can't even imagine making a decision that clean. Every choice I make has to pass through a filter of who else it affects, who will be disappointed, who will need me to be somewhere else. I've been doing it so long I don't know if I'd recognize myself without it.

But there's something appealing about the idea of someone else handling Mother's birthday. Just this once. Just to see what happens.

entries.initial_entry:
Called my brother back. Told him I'd handle the restaurant booking but the guest list and gifts are his responsibility this year. He sputtered but eventually agreed.

Then I made myself a proper cup of oolong and sat on the balcony watching the city lights come on. It's cold but I wrapped myself in the blanket my daughter gave me and stayed out there until the tea was gone.

Small rebellion. Small pleasure. Both felt earned.

Comprehensive exams tomorrow. Studied at the library until closing, then walked the long way home through the park where the winter jasmine is blooming.

Stopped for beef noodle soup at my usual place. The owner remembered my order — extra green onion, light on the chili. There's something comforting about being known.

Feel as ready as I'll ever be.
entries.nudge_text: What's going through your mind tonight?

entries.initial_entry:
Passed. The committee seemed genuinely interested in my research, asked questions I hadn't anticipated but could answer because I've been living inside this topic for two years. Dr. Huang said my clinical experience brought a perspective the literature lacks. Coming from him, that means something.

Called my mother first. She asked if this meant I was finally finished and I had to explain that no, there's still the dissertation, probably two more years. She sighed and said at least I'd be a doctor eventually and she could tell her mahjong friends. I'll take it.

My daughter sent flowers. Pink lilies and a card that said "So proud of you, Mom." I cried in my office for a solid five minutes.

Tonight my husband is taking me to the French restaurant we've been saying we'll try for a year. White tablecloths, a tasting menu, the whole indulgence. I bought a new dress — dark green, silk blend, probably too expensive. I'm going to enjoy every course without thinking about calories or cost or whether I should be home checking on Mother.

For once, I'm just going to let myself have the moment.

This degree might be a vanity project. It might be impractical at my age. It might mean missing things, disappointing people, constantly negotiating between who I want to be and who everyone needs me to be.

But today, sitting in that conference room, defending ideas I've been developing for two years — I felt like the version of myself I imagined when I was twenty-five and too afraid to try. She showed up after all. Just took her a few decades.

Spent most of the morning at the hospital with Mother again. Her blood pressure readings have been erratic and the doctors want to adjust her medication. I sat in that waiting room scrolling through my literature review notes, pretending I could focus on methodology while watching the clock. By the time we got home and I made sure she ate lunch and took her pills, half the day was gone.

Strange to be the oldest person in every seminar, still learning to say "I need more time" without feeling like a failure.

This degree might be a vanity project. It might be impractical at my age. It might mean missing things, disappointing people, constantly negotiating between who I want to be and who everyone needs me to be.

But today, sitting in that conference room, defending ideas I've been developing for two years — I felt like the version of myself I imagined when I was twenty-five and too afraid to try. She showed up after all. Just took her a few decades.

Twelve-hour shift and my feet are just wrecked. But honestly a decent day. One of my kids on the ward, Milo, he's seven and has been in and out for weeks with a recurring infection. Today he finally got the all-clear for discharge. His mom was crying and I was trying not to cry and he just kept asking if he could keep the stickers I'd been putting on his IV pole every morning.

I told him yes obviously. Grabbed a handful of extras from the supply closet and stuffed them in a ziplock for him. His mom hugged me on the way out and said I was the only nurse who remembered he was scared of the blood pressure cuff and always warned him first. I do remember that stuff. It matters.

Got home and Mom had made mac and cheese from a box, which she was very proud of. Her hip is doing better, she managed the kitchen without the walker. We ate on the couch watching Chopped reruns. I didn't have the energy to make anything real. But sitting there with the mac and cheese and Mom laughing at the contestants, I just felt like okay. This is fine. This is actually good.
entries.nudge_text: Is that what good usually looks like?

entries.initial_entry:
Lisa from work asked me again about the charge nurse thing. Said the manager is "disappointed" I turned it down and that I'm "wasting my potential." I told her I appreciate the concern but I'm good where I am. She gave me that look, the one where people think you're being lazy or afraid when really you just know what works for you.

I'm not afraid of more responsibility. I covered the entire unit by myself that time two nurses called out during the flu surge. I can handle it. I just don't want to lose my Saturdays and trade my shift for nights. Mom needs someone home in the evenings and I need at least a couple days a month where I do absolutely nothing useful. That's not a character flaw.
entries.nudge_text: Does Lisa's opinion actually get to you or is it just annoying?
entries.response_text: It gets to me more than I want it to. She's not wrong that I could do the job. I just don't want it right now. But hearing "wasting potential" sticks in my head longer than it should.

entries.initial_entry:
Mom had a rough night, woke up at 3am with hip pain and I ended up sitting with her for two hours doing ice packs and finding the right pillow arrangement. Then went straight to work at 6:30. Running on maybe three hours of sleep.

The thing that's eating at me is that at one point she was apologizing for waking me up and I snapped. Not badly, just a quick "Mom it's fine, please stop saying sorry." But I could hear the edge in my voice and she went quiet. She didn't deserve that. She didn't ask for any of this. I just — I was so tired and I had work in a few hours and the frustration came out sideways.

Day off. Slept in until 10 which felt almost criminal. Mom was already up, puttering around with her walker, watching the morning news at a volume that could wake the neighborhood. Made us both a proper breakfast — scrambled eggs with chives from the little herb pot on the windowsill, sourdough toast, fresh orange juice. She said it was better than the diner we used to go to on Sundays when I was little. I don't think that's true but it was sweet.

Spent the afternoon doing laundry and reorganizing the hall closet, which has been bothering me for weeks. Mom sat in her chair and directed me on where everything should go, like she was running a construction site. It was actually kind of fun. Found a box of old photos from when my brother Kevin and I were small. Mom got teary looking at them but in a happy way.

Kevin called in the evening. He lives in Denver now. Asked how Mom was doing and I gave him the update. He said he's sorry he can't help more and that he'll try to visit in August. I said that would be great, and I meant it, but I also know "try" usually means it won't happen. I'm not angry about it. He has his own life, two kids, a wife with a demanding job. It just is what it is. I'm the one who's here and that's the arrangement we've settled into.
entries.nudge_text: When Kevin says 'try to visit' — what do you actually expect?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Nothing. And I've made my peace with that mostly. He'll call, send money sometimes, maybe show up for Thanksgiving. But the day-to-day stuff is mine. It's been that way since Mom's surgery and probably won't change.

entries.initial_entry:
Picked up an extra shift for Dani because her kid had a fever. Sixteen hours on my feet. I can barely think straight right now. Got home and Mom had waited up for me even though it was almost midnight. She'd left a plate of pasta in the microwave with a little note that said "eat something, mija."

I feel bad because my Saturday is supposed to be this weekend and I don't think I can do it. Mom has a follow-up appointment Saturday morning and Kevin was supposed to call her PT coordinator back last week and never did, so now I have to sort that out too. I was going to do the sushi-and-movie thing. I've been looking forward to it all week. But I keep giving those days away and then wondering why I feel like a wrung-out dishrag.
entries.nudge_text: What if you kept the Saturday anyway?

entries.initial_entry:
Called Kevin about the PT coordinator thing. He apologized, said it slipped his mind. I told him it's fine but could he at least handle the insurance paperwork that's due next week. He said sure. We'll see.

I rescheduled Mom's appointment to Monday and I'm keeping my Saturday. I made the decision and I feel weird about it. Like I'm being selfish even though rationally I know a two-day delay on a routine follow-up doesn't matter. But there's this voice in my head that sounds a lot like Mom's voice — not the real Mom, the imaginary guilt-trip Mom — saying I should just handle it and stop complaining. Real Mom would probably tell me to go enjoy my day. She's not the problem. I'm the problem.

Took my Saturday. Hot bath, ordered the good sushi from Kiko's, watched that French movie I've had saved for a month. Nobody needed anything from me for eight whole hours and I didn't check my phone once.
entries.nudge_text: How'd it feel compared to what you expected?
entries.response_text: Better. Genuinely better. I kept waiting for the guilt to kick in and it mostly didn't. Just felt like myself again.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Mom to the rescheduled follow-up. PT says she's progressing well, might be able to transition to outpatient therapy in a couple months. That would free up a lot of evenings for me which I didn't let myself think about too much while the doctor was talking, but it was the first thing that popped into my head.

Started thinking about what I'd do with those evenings. Join a pottery class or something, go to that wine bar Hannah keeps inviting me to. Then immediately felt gross about it because my mom is still recovering and here I am mentally redecorating my free time. I don't know why I can't just want two things at once without feeling like one of them makes me a bad person.
entries.nudge_text: Who told you wanting your own life makes you bad?

Mom sat in her chair and directed me on where everything should go, like she was running a construction site. It was actually kind of fun. Found a box of old photos from when my brother Kevin and I were small. Mom got teary looking at them but in a happy way.

Kevin called in the evening. He lives in Denver now. Asked how Mom was doing and I gave him the update. He said he's sorry he can't help more and that he'll try to visit in August. I said that would be great, and I meant it, but I also know "try" usually means it won't happen. I'm not angry about it. He has his own life, two kids, a wife with a demanding job. It just is what it is. I'm the one who's here and that's the arrangement we've settled into.
entries.nudge_text: When Kevin says 'try to visit' — what do you actually expect?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Nothing. And I've made my peace with that mostly. He'll call, send money sometimes, maybe show up for Thanksgiving. But the day-to-day stuff is mine. It's been that way since Mom's surgery and probably won't change.

entries.initial_entry:
Picked up an extra shift for Dani because her kid had a fever. Sixteen hours on my feet. I can barely think straight right now. Got home and Mom had waited up for me even though it was almost midnight. She'd left a plate of pasta in the microwave with a little note that said "eat something, mija."

I feel bad because my Saturday is supposed to be this weekend and I don't think I can do it. Mom has a follow-up appointment Saturday morning and Kevin was supposed to call her PT coordinator back last week and never did, so now I have to sort that out too. I was going to do the sushi-and-movie thing. I've been looking forward to it all week. But I keep giving those days away and then wondering why I feel like a wrung-out dishrag.
entries.nudge_text: What if you kept the Saturday anyway?

entries.initial_entry:
Called Kevin about the PT coordinator thing. He apologized, said it slipped his mind. I told him it's fine but could he at least handle the insurance paperwork that's due next week. He said sure. We'll see.

I rescheduled Mom's appointment to Monday and I'm keeping my Saturday. I made the decision and I feel weird about it. Like I'm being selfish even though rationally I know a two-day delay on a routine follow-up doesn't matter. But there's this voice in my head that sounds a lot like Mom's voice — not the real Mom, the imaginary guilt-trip Mom — saying I should just handle it and stop complaining. Real Mom would probably tell me to go enjoy my day. She's not the problem. I'm the problem.

Took my Saturday. Hot bath, ordered the good sushi from Kiko's, watched that French movie I've had saved for a month. Nobody needed anything from me for eight whole hours and I didn't check my phone once.
entries.nudge_text: How'd it feel compared to what you expected?
entries.response_text: Better. Genuinely better. I kept waiting for the guilt to kick in and it mostly didn't. Just felt like myself again.

entries.initial_entry:
Took Mom to the rescheduled follow-up. PT says she's progressing well, might be able to transition to outpatient therapy in a couple months. That would free up a lot of evenings for me which I didn't let myself think about too much while the doctor was talking, but it was the first thing that popped into my head.

Started thinking about what I'd do with those evenings. Join a pottery class or something, go to that wine bar Hannah keeps inviting me to. Then immediately felt gross about it because my mom is still recovering and here I am mentally redecorating my free time. I don't know why I can't just want two things at once without feeling like one of them makes me a bad person.
entries.nudge_text: Who told you wanting your own life makes you bad?

entries.initial_entry:
Christmas holiday but the school WhatsApp group is still buzzing with admin sending schedule reminders. Ignored all of it. Spent the morning working on the creative writing syllabus instead—finally nailed down the module on personal narratives. Used that Chimamanda essay collection I've been sitting on for months.

Ma called to ask if I'd "at least consider" the biodata from that engineer in Pune. Told her I'm not doing the arranged meeting circuit right now, I have actual work to do. She did her disappointed sigh thing but didn't push. Small victory.

The thing is, people act like I'm being difficult when I say I want to figure out my own path. But today felt right. I mapped out exactly how I want this program to run, on my terms. No committee approval needed because it's extracurricular. That's the whole point—I carved out a space where nobody gets to tell me what works and what doesn't. If Principal Mehta wants to be involved later, fine, but the vision is mine.

Rohan texted asking if I wanted to join the cousins for drinks. Didn't go. Needed this time alone with my ideas. Maybe that makes me antisocial but I genuinely don't care right now.

Back to lesson prep. The regular curriculum stuff—grammar worksheets, comprehension passages. Mind-numbing but has to be done before term starts.

Had chai with Ananya in the evening. She's dealing with wedding planning stress (March date confirmed) and wanted to vent about caterers. Listened mostly. She asked about the writing program and seemed genuinely interested, which was nice. Said she wished she had something like that when she was in school.

New year. Ma insisted on temple visit in the morning, then the extended family lunch happened. Usual scene—aunties asking about marriage, uncles making jokes about my "career phase." Nodded through most of it.

Uncle Vijay pulled me aside to say he knows someone at a CBSE school in Delhi with "excellent growth prospects" and would I be interested. Government affiliated, good pension, stable. Everything I specifically walked away from. Told him I'm happy where I am. He looked at me like I'd said something strange.

What bothers me is that I couldn't fully explain why. In the moment I just wanted to get away from the conversation. But it's not just about being stubborn. I chose this path. The after-school program, the flexibility to try new things in class, the fact that I can actually experiment—that's worth something. Worth more than a pension honestly.

But sitting here now, I wonder if I should have pushed back harder instead of just... retreating. Like, made him understand instead of just escaping the conversation. I sort of feel like I let myself down by not articulating it better.
entries.nudge_text: What would you have wanted to say to him?

entries.initial_entry:
Term starts Monday. Spent the day at school setting up classroom—put up the new bulletin board with student work from last term, rearranged desks into that U-shape configuration I like for discussions. The janitor looked at me weird but helped anyway.

Thinking about the first week back. Class 9 will be fine, they're used to my style by now. Class 7 might be tricky—new batch, they came from a very traditional English teacher before me, lots of rote learning. Going to have to ease them into actual discussion.

Also the creative writing program. Need to finalize the registration process. Was thinking maybe just a simple Google form, keep it informal. But then if it gets popular I might need to actually track attendance and all that. Ugh, the admin side of things. Part of me wants to just keep it casual and let kids show up when they want, but that might not fly with the school if they ever want to formalize it.

Ordered biryani for dinner. Treating myself since the real work starts again soon. Ma called twice, didn't pick up. Will call her tomorrow when I have the energy.
entries.nudge_text: What's the worst that happens if you keep it casual?
entries.response_text: Probably nothing honestly. Mehta doesn't pay attention to extracurriculars unless parents complain. I should just stop overthinking.

entries.initial_entry:
First day back was fine. The Class 7 kids are shy but one girl—Meera—actually raised her hand without being called on. Asked if we'd be reading any poetry this term. Told her yes, we have Kamala Das on the syllabus. She looked excited. Those moments make it worth it.

But then the staff meeting happened. Principal Mehta going on about board exam prep and standardized assessments and how we need to "align" better with the other schools in the trust. Forty-five minutes of jargon. I could feel my eyes glazing over.

Here's what I keep circling back to: I know I'm a good teacher. The kids respond, they engage, they actually want to be in class. But in these meetings I feel like a fraud because I'm not doing what everyone else is doing. They're all talking about test scores and I'm thinking about how to get the kids to care about what they're reading.

And the thing is, I believe I'm right. I believe my approach matters. But when you're sitting in a room full of people nodding along to metrics and "learning outcomes," it's hard not to feel like maybe you're the one who's missing something.

I guess I'm just tired. It's only day one.
entries.nudge_text: Do you actually think you're missing something, or does the room just make you feel that way?
entries.response_text: The room definitely. When I'm with my students I know exactly what I'm doing. Meetings just bring out this performative anxiety or something.

entries.initial_entry: Good teaching day. The Class 9 debate on whether social media is ruining language went better than expected—even the quiet ones participated. This is why I do this.

Furious right now. Just got off the phone with Ma and I need to write this down before I explode.

She forwarded me a LinkedIn profile. An ENGINEER from her friend's son's network who "seems compatible" based on absolutely nothing. When I told her I wasn't interested, she started crying. Actual tears. Said she's worried I'm "throwing my life away" by being so "particular about everything."

Particular. Like wanting to choose my own life partner is some unreasonable demand. Like deciding to leave a government job to do work that actually means something is being difficult.

I told her I'd call her back when I was calmer. She said "don't bother, you never listen anyway." So that's where we are now.

The thing that really gets me is that she knows. She knows I'm happy with my work. She's seen me come home excited about a lesson that went well. She visited the school during last year's annual day and saw the kids perform the scenes we'd been working on for months. She hugged me after and said she was proud.

But somehow that doesn't count? Somehow I'm still failing at life because I'm 29 and single and not in a government job with a pension?

I don't know how to make her understand that I need to do things my own way. I've tried explaining it. Maybe I need to just accept that she'll never get it.
entries.nudge_text: Is that actually what you want though—her just not getting it forever?
entries.response_text: No, obviously not. But what's the alternative? I can't change who I am to make her comfortable.

entries.initial_entry:
Creative writing program had its first session today. Seven kids showed up, which is more than I expected honestly. Mix of classes 8-10. We did a freewriting exercise and then shared—nobody had to but four of them volunteered.

One boy, Arjun, wrote about his grandmother's hands. Just this vivid little piece about how she makes rotis and the way she hums while she works. Nothing fancy but so specific and real. I told him it was beautiful and he got embarrassed but also clearly pleased.

This is the thing I built. Mine. Nobody assigned it to me or approved a budget or gave me a template to follow. I figured out what I wanted to create and I made it happen. Finally something that feels fully mine in this job.
Second creative writing session done. Ten kids this time—word is spreading. Meera from Class 7 joined and already has a notebook full of poems she wanted to show me.

Lost the Grupo Modelo commission to Fernanda. They said they wanted "something bolder" for their new headquarters. I've done six projects for them over the years. My proposal fit their brand guidelines perfectly.
entries.nudge_text: What does losing this one mean to you?
entries.response_text: It's not about the money, though that hurts too. It's that I thought I knew what they wanted. I've spent years learning to give clients exactly what they ask for, and now that's not enough? Fernanda does these chaotic, aggressive pieces — nothing I would hang in my own home — and suddenly that's what serious companies want. I don't even know how to make work like that anymore. I'm not sure I ever did.

entries.initial_entry:
My mother called to tell me my cousin Rodrigo just got promoted to partner at his firm. She mentioned it three times during the conversation, which means she was making a point. Rodrigo and I used to be compared constantly growing up — he went the safe route, law school, the path my father would have chosen for me. When my paintings started selling, the comparisons stopped. Now I wonder if they'll start again.

The strange thing is I found myself defending my career to her even though she didn't attack it. I mentioned the board position at the collective, the new studio space, the clients I've worked with. I was listing credentials like she was interviewing me. My father would have been proud of the list. But sitting here after hanging up, I keep thinking about a canvas I stretched three months ago that's still blank in the corner. I bought the paint for it. I had an idea. I just haven't had time between commissions to work on anything that isn't already sold.

Lost the Grupo Modelo commission to Fernanda. They said they wanted "something bolder" for their new headquarters. I've done six projects for them over the years. My proposal fit their brand guidelines perfectly.
entries.nudge_text: What does losing this one mean to you?
entries.response_text: It's not about the money, though that hurts too. It's that I thought I knew what they wanted. I've spent years learning to give clients exactly what they ask for, and now that's not enough? Fernanda does these chaotic, aggressive pieces — nothing I would hang in my own home — and suddenly that's what serious companies want. I don't even know how to make work like that anymore. I'm not sure I ever did.

entries.initial_entry:
My mother called to tell me my cousin Rodrigo just got promoted to partner at his firm. She mentioned it three times during the conversation, which means she was making a point. Rodrigo and I used to be compared constantly growing up — he went the safe route, law school, the path my father would have chosen for me. When my paintings started selling, the comparisons stopped. Now I wonder if they'll start again.

The strange thing is I found myself defending my career to her even though she didn't attack it. I mentioned the board position at the collective, the new studio space, the clients I've worked with. I was listing credentials like she was interviewing me. My father would have been proud of the list. But sitting here after hanging up, I keep thinking about a canvas I stretched three months ago that's still blank in the corner. I bought the paint for it. I had an idea. I just haven't had time between commissions to work on anything that isn't already sold.
Completed the Q1 review presentation today. Numbers came in 12% above target for my division. Will send to regional director tomorrow morning.
entries.nudge_text: What does hitting that 12% mean to you?
entries.response_text: Honestly? It means I'm still relevant. At 57, there's always this question in the back of my mind—can the younger managers do what I do? Seeing those numbers tells me I still bring something they can't match yet. Experience has value.

entries.initial_entry: Found out Carlos from Guadalajara got invited to the leadership summit and I didn't. Thirty-four years with this company and they invite someone with eight. I don't even know what to say.
entries.nudge_text: What would that invitation have proven?
entries.response_text: It's not about proving anything. It's about respect for a track record. But maybe I'm just tired today. There's a lot of context the higher-ups might have that I don't know about.

entries.initial_entry:
Spent some time this weekend thinking about the Carlos situation. I realize I was more upset than the situation warranted. He's doing good work in Guadalajara, and the summit is about digital transformation—an area he's genuinely stronger in than I am.

What bothered me wasn't Carlos. It was feeling like my contributions are becoming invisible. I've hit every target for six quarters straight, but somewhere along the way, targets stopped feeling like enough. The goalposts moved and nobody told me.

Had my monthly one-on-one with my director. I asked directly about the VP track and where I stand. She said my operational numbers are "exemplary" but that they're looking for leaders who can "build capability in others." She mentioned mentorship programs.

I've trained dozens of supervisors over the years. Most of them are running plants now. But apparently that doesn't count unless it's in some formal program with tracking metrics. Now I need to figure out how to make what I already do more visible, or start doing something new at 57.
entries.nudge_text: How does it feel to quantify mentorship?

entries.initial_entry: Lucia told me I've been distant at home. She's right. I keep thinking about work even at dinner. Need to find some balance but also don't want to lose momentum on the VP push.

Today was difficult. The quarterly town hall happened and they announced promotions across the region. Three VPs named—none of them me. One of them is someone I trained back in 2018. Daniel, the kid who used to ask me basic questions about inventory systems. Now he's VP of Logistics for Central America.

I should be happy for him. Part of me is. But mostly I feel like I'm watching my own career highlights reel being played by someone else. All those late nights, all those targets exceeded, all those crises managed—what was it for? I built a machine that runs so smoothly they forgot someone built it.

Lucia saw my face when I came home. She didn't say anything, just made me coffee and sat with me. I couldn't talk about it. What would I say? That I'm jealous of my own mentee? That feels shameful to even write.

I keep thinking about my leather notebook. Every promotion, every bonus, every recognition. When did the entries stop mattering? Or maybe they always mattered less than I thought. Maybe what mattered was that I thought I was building toward something, and today I realized I might have already arrived—and this is it.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'enough' look like now?

entries.initial_entry:
Woke up early today, couldn't sleep. Started thinking about what my director said about building capability in others. Then about Daniel. He's calling me next week to "pick my brain" about some challenges in his new role. He didn't have to do that.

Maybe that's the thing. I keep score one way—titles, metrics, recognition from above. But there's another scoreboard I've been ignoring. The people who learned something from me and kept climbing. The plants that run well because someone I trained is running them. That doesn't get me VP. But maybe VP isn't the only way to measure thirty-four years. I don't know. Still thinking.

Look, I know some teachers think the community garden visits are a waste of instructional time. We spent two hours there today and yes, that's time away from test prep. But watching Marcus Jr. (not named after me, just a coincidence) actually engage with something for the first time all year? Seeing him carefully transplant seedlings and ask questions about composting? That matters.

Mrs. Patterson made a comment in the break room about my "field trip habit" but I'm not going to apologize for it. These kids need to understand they're part of something bigger than standardized tests. They need to touch the dirt, see the earthworms, understand where food actually comes from. That's education too.
Didn't call Grandma back again. She left another voicemail about the restaurant anniversary dinner planning and I just... let it go to voicemail. I'll call her tomorrow.
entries.nudge_text: What made you hesitate to pick up?
entries.response_text: Honestly, I know she's going to ask me to give a speech at the 60th anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. My Cantonese is terrible and I keep meaning to practice but I never do. It feels like I'm letting her down every time we talk and she hears how rusty I've gotten.

entries.initial_entry:
Principal wants me to use the new standardized curriculum materials for social studies. They come from the district. They're fine, technically. Cover all the state requirements.

But they're so sanitized. The unit on immigration literally has two paragraphs on the Chinese Exclusion Act. Two paragraphs. My great-grandfather couldn't enter this country for decades because of that law. I told the principal I'd supplement with additional materials. She said to be careful about "going off curriculum." Said it in that way that means don't.
entries.nudge_text: So what will you do with those materials?
entries.response_text: I'm going to use the curriculum. But I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story during the personal narrative unit. They can't tell me I can't share my own family history. At least I don't think they can.

entries.initial_entry:
The kids blew my mind today. We were talking about the water cycle and Jordan raised her hand and asked if the water we drink is the same water dinosaurs drank and I said yes, basically, and then she asked if that meant we were connected to dinosaurs and I said yes again and she got this look on her face like something clicked.

Then Carlos said if we're connected to dinosaurs through water we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees and I just stood there for a second because yes, exactly, that's exactly it. These ten-year-olds just figured out in five minutes what some adults never understand. We spent the rest of the period talking about interconnection and nobody complained and nobody looked at the clock.
entries.nudge_text: What did that moment mean to you?
entries.response_text: It reminded me why I do this job. Not for the test scores or the evaluations. For moments like that where kids discover something true about the world. That we're all connected, that what we do matters beyond ourselves. That's what I want them to carry with them.

entries.initial_entry:
Second entry today because I just need to get this out. Went to the grocery store after school, totally normal trip, and I just stood in the produce section for way too long looking at the plastic clamshells of strawberries. They're from Chile. In January. Wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years so I can have strawberries in the middle of winter.

I bought them anyway. I bought the stupid strawberries because I was tired and I wanted something sweet and the farmer's market doesn't open until Saturday. And I know one person buying or not buying strawberries doesn't change the global supply chain but I still felt like a hypocrite putting them in my cart.

This is what I mean when I say it's exhausting to care about everything all the time. My students look at me like I have answers but I'm just as stuck as everyone else. I teach them about sustainability and then I buy Chilean strawberries in plastic. I tell them every choice matters and then I make choices that don't align with that. I don't know how to live in this world without constantly feeling like I'm failing at something.
Called Grandma. We talked for forty minutes. I'm going to give the speech at the anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. She's going to help me practice.
entries.nudge_text: How do you feel about that decision?
entries.response_text: Nervous but good. It's important to her. The restaurant is important to the whole family, to the community really. Dad worked there every summer through high school. So did I. People need to hear what it means to us, in the language it's always been spoken in.

entries.initial_entry:
The diversity assembly actually worked! I've been pushing for this for two years and today we had speakers from five different community organizations talking to the whole school. Mrs. Okonkwo from the Nigerian cultural center, Mr. Patel from the Hindu temple, someone from the tribal council, and two others.

The kids were so engaged. They asked real questions. One of my students asked Mrs. Okonkwo what she missed most about Nigeria and she started crying a little and then laughed and said "the food, always the food" and everyone laughed and it was this beautiful human moment. This is what school should feel like. Not test prep and discipline charts. Actual humans connecting with each other, learning that the world is bigger than their own street.
entries.nudge_text: What made you push so hard for this?
entries.response_text: When I was a kid there was nobody who looked like me in the history books or the assemblies. I remember how alone that felt. I don't want any of my students to feel that way. I want them to see themselves in school, and I want them to see each other too.

entries.initial_entry:
Made dumplings with Mom today for Lunar New Year prep. My hands remember the folding even when my brain forgets the right way to pleat them. She corrected me three times on the same fold and I wanted to be annoyed but honestly I was just grateful she's still here to correct me.

She told me stories about the restaurant again, the early days when Grandpa worked eighteen hour shifts and Grandma handled the front while raising Dad. Stories I've heard a hundred times but they never get old. There's something about flour on your hands and family around you that makes everything feel solid. Like no matter what else is happening in the world, this is real. This is what matters.

Parent-teacher conferences all day. Twelve families. My voice is gone but one mom thanked me for showing her son that being Chinese-American is something to be proud of. That one sentence made the whole exhausting day worth it.
entries.nudge_text: What did it mean to hear that?
entries.response_text: More than I can explain. I spent so many years feeling caught between two worlds, not Chinese enough, not American enough. If I can help even one kid skip that confusion, help them feel whole from the start... that's why I became a teacher. That's the whole point.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Just long. A lot of grading, a lot of meetings, a lot of everything. The community garden got a small grant from the city though, so that's something. We can finally fix the broken irrigation system and maybe add a few more raised beds for the school partnership program.

Practiced my speech with Grandma over video call. My Cantonese is getting better but still rough. She says the important thing is not perfect pronunciation but speaking from the heart. I'm trying to believe her. The anniversary dinner is in two weeks and I keep rewriting what I want to say.
entries.nudge_text: What do you keep changing in the speech?
entries.response_text: I keep trying to capture what the restaurant means. Not just to our family but to the town. It's where people celebrated graduations and mourned losses. Where the same families have come for sixty years. It's history. It's proof we belong here. I don't know how to say all that in five minutes, in a language I barely speak, without crying.

entries.initial_entry:
I know I should have stayed for the whole faculty meeting but I had to leave early for the speech practice. Principal noticed. She didn't say anything but I saw her write something down. I'll deal with whatever comes from that.

Some things are more important than faculty meetings about parking lot protocols. The anniversary dinner is this Saturday and my grandmother is counting on me. The whole family is counting on me. And maybe that sounds dramatic but the restaurant isn't just a business, it's proof that we made it here, that we stayed, that we built something. I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that. I'm just not.

Everyone keeps asking me why I'm taking on the Chocó expedition when the company is already stretched thin. But they don't understand—this is exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice. The regional government contacted us specifically. They want sustainable tourism in the Pacific corridor, and we're the ones they trust to do it right.

My sister called to say Mamá is worried I'm "doing too much again." What does that even mean? I've built this company from nothing. Three employees became twenty-two. We survived the pandemic when half the tourism operators in Colombia closed. I know what I'm doing. I'm not going to apologize for wanting to grow, for wanting something more than just coasting.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'coasting' actually look like for you?

entries.initial_entry: Scouting day in Nuquí went better than expected, the community leaders were receptive, the river routes are navigable. Already picturing the first group coming through in February. This is the part I love—standing somewhere new, seeing what could be.

Back in Bogotá and immediately into the Sunday routine. Made sancocho because that's what Abuela always made in October when the weather turns. Fourteen people around the table today—cousins, their kids, my mother with her opinions about my salt levels. It's chaotic and loud and sometimes I think about how much easier it would be to just... not. To have my Sundays free for once.

But then my nephew Tomás asked me to tell him again about the time I got lost in Leticia and had to barter my watch for a boat ride. He's heard it fifty times. Still loves it. And there's something about seeing the whole family there, passing around the bowls the way we always have, that feels like it matters more than whatever else I could be doing.

Had a moment in the kitchen where I just stood there holding the wooden spoon Abuela used. The same one. It's cracked now but I can't replace it. Weird how objects carry weight like that. Anyway. Good day overall, even if I was tired from the week. The expedition proposal is due Friday and I haven't touched it yet. That's tomorrow's problem.

Sent off the proposal. Clean, professional, ambitious. I asked for twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer, and I want to bring in local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought. That costs more but it's the right way to do it. My father would have called it impractical—he always wanted me to play it safe, to stay in numbers and spreadsheets.

Thinking about him today. He never understood why I left the firm, never stopped hoping I'd come back. He died still thinking I'd made a mistake. But I think he would have liked seeing the company now, even if he wouldn't admit it. The way we do things matters to me in a way I couldn't articulate when I was younger. Maybe that's his influence after all—just expressed differently.
entries.nudge_text: What would he have noticed first if he saw the company now?
entries.response_text: The order. How organized everything is, the systems I built. He'd pretend not to be impressed but he'd notice. The irony is I use everything he taught me about running a business—I just pointed it at something he never would have chosen. Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like. You take the tools and build something your parents wouldn't recognize, but couldn't exist without them.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday again. Smaller group today, just eight of us. My mother wasn't feeling well so I drove out to pick her up from Soacha myself. She complained the whole way about the traffic, then about my driving, then about how I never call enough. Classic. But when we got to my place she went straight to the kitchen and started organizing things her way, moving my spices, telling me my onions were too big. And honestly it was fine. More than fine.

There's a rhythm to this. She's seventy-three and still trying to teach me things. I'm forty-eight and still letting her. We don't talk about feelings or whatever, but this is how we do it. She comes, she criticizes my cooking, she eats three helpings, she tells the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987.

Started thinking about what happens when she can't make the drive anymore. When the table gets smaller. The company keeps me running in six directions but this—this is the anchor. Nuquí is exciting. The Chocó project could be the biggest thing we've done. But it's not actually about that, is it? It's about having something to come back to. Otherwise you're just moving for the sake of moving.
entries.nudge_text: Does the running ever feel like coming back to something too?
entries.response_text: Sometimes, yes. When I'm navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time—there's a feeling like I'm returning to who I'm supposed to be. Like the version of me that sits still too long starts to decay and the version that moves is more real. Both things can be true I think. The anchor and the current.

entries.initial_entry: Regional government approved the proposal, full funding. I cried a little in my office with the door closed. Texted my sister, called my mother, then spent two hours just staring at topographic maps like a kid planning a treasure hunt.
entries.nudge_text: Tears of what?
entries.response_text: Relief mostly. And maybe grief that Papá isn't here to see it. Complicated tears. The kind where you're happy and sad and proud all at the same time and you can't separate any of it out.

entries.initial_entry: Camilo quit today. Just walked in and said he found something "more stable." Five years with the company and he leaves a month before our biggest project. I don't even know what to feel right now—betrayed, scared, angry, all of it.
entries.nudge_text: What scares you most about losing him?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent the last week scrambling to redistribute Camilo's responsibilities. Ended up giving some of it to María who's only been with us eight months but she's sharp, hungry, reminds me of myself at that age. Told her this is a trial by fire and she just nodded and said "Bueno, vamos." I like that. No drama, just forward motion.

The thing about losing someone is it forces you to see what you've been leaning on. I'd been leaning on Camilo more than I realized—his steadiness, his institutional memory. But the company isn't Camilo. It's bigger than any one person. That includes me, eventually. Something to think about.

Had dinner with my cousin Esperanza who runs a restaurant in Usaquén. She's been doing it for twenty years, same location, same menu basically, same suppliers. I asked her if she ever gets bored. She laughed and said "The food is the same, the people are different. That's enough." I don't know if I could do it. But I respect it. There's honor in tending the same thing year after year. I just don't think it's my kind of honor.

Sunday was Day of the Dead. Set up the ofrenda the way my family always does—photos, marigolds, Papá's favorite rum. Tomás helped me light the candles. He asked if Bisabuelo could really see us from wherever he is. I said I don't know but we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters. That felt true when I said it.

First planning trip to Chocó with the full team next week. Should be excited but I'm mostly anxious—what if the reality doesn't match the vision? What if I've oversold this to everyone including myself?
entries.nudge_text: What would 'matching the vision' actually look like on the ground?
entries.response_text: Community members who see themselves in the project, not just as employees. Routes that feel discovered rather than manufactured. Guests leaving changed. That's the vision. Whether we can actually build that... I guess we'll find out. The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying, sometimes like falling.

entries.initial_entry: Second entry today because I couldn't stop thinking. The anxiety from earlier settled into something more like anticipation. Called my mother to tell her about the trip and she said "Cuídate, mijo, pero también diviértete." Take care, but also have fun. Simple, but it landed.
entries.nudge_text: Why did that land today?
entries.response_text: Because usually she just worries. This time she acknowledged that I need this—the adventure part, not just the work part. Like she finally sees it's not recklessness, it's how I'm built. Took her long enough, but also: took me long enough to stop being defensive about it.

entries.initial_entry:
Just back from four days in Chocó. My body is wrecked—humidity, mosquitoes, sleeping on a cot in a community center. But my mind is alive in a way it hasn't been in months. We mapped three potential routes, met with eleven different community contacts, ate more fish and coconut rice than I thought possible.

María was incredible. She handled logistics like she'd been doing this for years. At one point we had a miscommunication with a boat operator and she talked him down in a Chocó dialect I didn't even know she spoke. I told her she has a future here if she wants it. She said "I want it."

The landscape. I keep thinking about the landscape. Rivers that split into a dozen channels, mangroves that go on forever, communities that have lived there for generations doing things their own way. There's a rhythm to life there that we're going to have to respect if any of this works. We can't bring tourists in like we own the place. We have to earn the right to be there, again and again.

Came home to seventeen emails, a broken water heater, and a message from my sister saying Sunday is at her place this week because my nephew has a school thing. Everything keeps moving. That's okay. I know how to move with it now. Or maybe I always did—I just spent too many years feeling guilty about it instead of accepting that this is who I am. Someone who needs both the roots and the river.
entries.nudge_text: When did the guilt start to lift?
entries.response_text: Honestly? Probably when I stopped expecting everyone else to understand it. My mother's blessing last week helped. But mostly I think you just get tired of fighting yourself. The guilt was exhausting. At some point you realize you can honor where you came from and still keep moving. They're not enemies. They never were.

entries.initial_entry:
Everyone keeps asking me why I'm taking on the Chocó expedition when the company is already stretched thin. But they don't understand—this is exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice. The regional government contacted us specifically. They want sustainable tourism in the Pacific corridor, and we're the ones they trust to do it right.

My sister called to say Mamá is worried I'm "doing too much again." What does that even mean? I've built this company from nothing. Three employees became twenty-two. We survived the pandemic when half the tourism operators in Colombia closed. I know what I'm doing. I'm not going to apologize for wanting to grow, for wanting something more than just coasting.
entries.nudge_text: What would 'coasting' actually look like for you?

entries.initial_entry: Scouting day in Nuquí went better than expected, the community leaders were receptive, the river routes are navigable. Already picturing the first group coming through in February. This is the part I love—standing somewhere new, seeing what could be.

Back in Bogotá and immediately into the Sunday routine. Made sancocho because that's what Abuela always made in October when the weather turns. Fourteen people around the table today—cousins, their kids, my mother with her opinions about my salt levels. It's chaotic and loud and sometimes I think about how much easier it would be to just... not. To have my Sundays free for once.

But then my nephew Tomás asked me to tell him again about the time I got lost in Leticia and had to barter my watch for a boat ride. He's heard it fifty times. Still loves it. And there's something about seeing the whole family there, passing around the bowls the way we always have, that feels like it matters more than whatever else I could be doing.

Had a moment in the kitchen where I just stood there holding the wooden spoon Abuela used. The same one. It's cracked now but I can't replace it. Weird how objects carry weight like that. Anyway. Good day overall, even if I was tired from the week. The expedition proposal is due Friday and I haven't touched it yet. That's tomorrow's problem.

Sent off the proposal. Clean, professional, ambitious. I asked for twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer, and I want to bring in local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought. That costs more but it's the right way to do it. My father would have called it impractical—he always wanted me to play it safe, to stay in numbers and spreadsheets.

Thinking about him today. He never understood why I left the firm, never stopped hoping I'd come back. He died still thinking I'd made a mistake. But I think he would have liked seeing the company now, even if he wouldn't admit it. The way we do things matters to me in a way I couldn't articulate when I was younger. Maybe that's his influence after all—just expressed differently.
entries.nudge_text: What would he have noticed first if he saw the company now?
entries.response_text: The order. How organized everything is, the systems I built. He'd pretend not to be impressed but he'd notice. The irony is I use everything he taught me about running a business—I just pointed it at something he never would have chosen. Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like. You take the tools and build something your parents wouldn't recognize, but couldn't exist without them.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday again. Smaller group today, just eight of us. My mother wasn't feeling well so I drove out to pick her up from Soacha myself. She complained the whole way about the traffic, then about my driving, then about how I never call enough. Classic. But when we got to my place she went straight to the kitchen and started organizing things her way, moving my spices, telling me my onions were too big. And honestly it was fine. More than fine.

There's a rhythm to this. She's seventy-three and still trying to teach me things. I'm forty-eight and still letting her. We don't talk about feelings or whatever, but this is how we do it. She comes, she criticizes my cooking, she eats three helpings, she tells the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987.

Started thinking about what happens when she can't make the drive anymore. When the table gets smaller. The company keeps me running in six directions but this—this is the anchor. Nuquí is exciting. The Chocó project could be the biggest thing we've done. But it's not actually about that, is it? It's about having something to come back to. Otherwise you're just moving for the sake of moving.
entries.nudge_text: Does the running ever feel like coming back to something too?
entries.response_text: Sometimes, yes. When I'm navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time—there's a feeling like I'm returning to who I'm supposed to be. Like the version of me that sits still too long starts to decay and the version that moves is more real. Both things can be true I think. The anchor and the current.

entries.initial_entry: Regional government approved the proposal, full funding. I cried a little in my office with the door closed. Texted my sister, called my mother, then spent two hours just staring at topographic maps like a kid planning a treasure hunt.
entries.nudge_text: Tears of what?
entries.response_text: Relief mostly. And maybe grief that Papá isn't here to see it. Complicated tears. The kind where you're happy and sad and proud all at the same time and you can't separate any of it out.

entries.initial_entry: Camilo quit today. Just walked in and said he found something "more stable." Five years with the company and he leaves a month before our biggest project. I don't even know what to feel right now—betrayed, scared, angry, all of it.
entries.nudge_text: What scares you most about losing him?

entries.initial_entry:
