entries.initial_entry: Finished the last batch of grading for the semester and walked home the long way through campus. Snow was piling up on the quad and I could see my breath and I just felt good about it, the routine of it. Same walk, same coat, same apartment waiting with the soup I made this morning.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about the same walk that does it for you?
entries.response_text: I think it's that nothing is asking me to figure anything out. The route is automatic. I just get to exist in it without calculating.

entries.initial_entry: My landlord mentioned he might sell the building in the spring. Just casually, at the mailbox, like it was nothing. I said something like "oh interesting" and went inside and sat on my bed for twenty minutes staring at the wall.
entries.nudge_text: That sat with you, huh?

entries.initial_entry:
Still thinking about what Doug said yesterday. I looked up what happens when a landlord sells a rental property in Ontario and the tenant protections are there on paper but I've heard enough stories from people in my program to know that "renoviction" is basically a verb now. My rent is $1,150 which is absurd for Toronto, everyone tells me that, and if I had to find a new place I'd be looking at $1,800 minimum for something comparable. That's an extra $650 a month I literally do not have.

I keep running the numbers. I could technically absorb it if I picked up more TA hours but then my thesis timeline stretches and my funding window is fixed. I mapped it out on a napkin at the kitchen table and none of the scenarios land well. One of my roommates from undergrad said I should "just deal with it when it happens" and I wanted to throw my phone across the room. Easy to say when your parents own a condo downtown.

The worst part is I chose this apartment specifically because it was cheap and close and I knew the landlord and it felt locked in. I turned down a nicer place in Kensington two years ago because this one felt more predictable. And now the thing I picked for being steady might be the thing that falls out from under me.

Got the renewal notice for my car insurance today. It went up $40 a month. I should call around and compare quotes but I've been putting it off because the semester starts next week and I don't have the bandwidth to sit on hold with insurance companies.

Told myself I'd do it over the break and now the break is basically gone. The payment comes out on the 15th so I'll probably just let it auto-renew and deal with it later. It's not a huge amount but it's another thing eroding the margin I built into my budget.
entries.nudge_text: What usually happens when you push stuff like this down the road?
entries.response_text: I mean it's forty bucks. It's not going to ruin me. I'll get to it in February when things calm down.

entries.initial_entry:
My advisor floated the idea of submitting our paper to a conference in Chicago in April. Which is great for the CV, sure, but that means registration fees, flights, hotel. He said the department might cover part of it but "might" is doing a lot of work in that sentence. I nodded and said sounds good and now I'm sitting here wondering why I didn't just ask what "part of it" means.

The thing is I know I should go. Everyone says conferences are how you build a network and it would be my first one. But dropping $800-1,200 on a maybe-partially-funded trip when my housing situation is already shaky feels like standing on a wobbly table and deciding to juggle. I told myself I'd look into it tomorrow but I know that means I'll look at the conference website, get stressed about the costs, and close the tab.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to actually ask him about the funding?

entries.initial_entry:
Doug — the landlord — stopped me in the hallway again. He said he's "had some conversations" with a developer. Not a done deal, his words. But the fact that there are conversations at all means the clock is ticking on something I can't control. I asked if he had a timeline and he shrugged and said these things take months. Months is not a timeline. Months is a fog.

I spent the evening going through rental listings on Kijiji and Facebook Marketplace. Everything in my price range is either a room in a shared house with strangers or a studio above a convenience store forty minutes from campus by transit. The places that are actually livable are all $1,700 and up. I made a spreadsheet comparing options but halfway through I just stopped because none of them felt like real options. They felt like damage control.

My mom called and I didn't tell her about any of it. She worries and then she offers money she doesn't really have and then I feel worse. I said school was going fine and we talked about my cousin's wedding. Hung up and sat in the kitchen doing nothing for a while. I keep trying to plan my way through this but there's nothing to plan around yet. It's just this low hum of something coming and I can't pin it down enough to solve it.

Submitted my thesis chapter draft to my advisor today. Two months ahead of the internal deadline. He seemed surprised, said most students don't get chapter two in until March. I don't know, I just can't function when something is hanging over me unfinished so I blocked out time every morning since November and chipped away at it.

Felt good walking out of his office. Like at least this one thing is where it's supposed to be. The housing stuff is still a mess and the conference money is still a question mark but the thesis is the one part of my life right now where I set a plan and the plan actually held.
entries.nudge_text: What does it feel like when a plan actually holds?
entries.response_text: I mean it feels normal. That's kind of the whole point, it's not supposed to be remarkable that things go the way you set them up to go. I just wish more things worked like that.

entries.initial_entry:
My friend Priya asked if I wanted to move in with her and her partner when their lease comes up in May. They're looking at a two-bedroom near Bloor and Ossington and they need a third person for the den. Rent would be $1,300, which is more than I pay now but way less than anything I've been finding on my own. And I know Priya, I've known her since first year, she's consistent. She pays bills on time, she's quiet, she doesn't bring chaos into shared spaces.

I spent the evening going over my budget to see if $1,300 works. It's tight but it works if I keep the TA position next semester, which my advisor basically confirmed. I'd lose my parking spot but I barely drive in winter anyway. The neighborhood is farther from campus but it's on the subway line, door to door maybe thirty-five minutes. I wrote out the pros and cons and the cons are all minor inconveniences. The pros are that I'd know where I'm living in June and I could stop refreshing Kijiji at midnight.

I told Priya I'm in. She sent a thumbs up emoji and said she'd send me the lease details this weekend. It's not the same as having my own place, and it's not the cheap locked-in situation I had before. But I can see the shape of it. I can budget around it. And right now that's enough to let me actually sleep.
entries.nudge_text: Is it actually enough, or is it just better than not knowing?

entries.initial_entry:
Spent most of Sunday lesson planning for the week. Marcus's mom sent another email asking if I could provide extra worksheets for him to practice at home. I said yes before I even finished reading it. That makes four families now with custom homework requests.

Mom called during lunch. Same conversation we always have. She asked if I'm eating enough, if I'm sleeping. I told her I'm fine. She doesn't need to worry about me when she has her own stuff going on with Dad's knee surgery coming up.

Aiden finally raised his hand today. First time all year. He got the answer wrong, but I could tell he'd been thinking about it. I just smiled and asked a follow-up question that let him get partway there. Small moment, but I keep replaying it.
entries.nudge_text: Why does that one stick with you?
entries.response_text: He's been so withdrawn since his parents split up this summer. Seeing him try, even knowing he might be wrong—felt like he trusted the room enough to risk it. That's the whole point of what I'm trying to build in there.

entries.initial_entry:
Mrs. Patterson cornered me after pickup today. Spent twenty minutes telling me her daughter needs more challenging work and that the current curriculum isn't "stimulating enough." I nodded through the whole thing. Told her I'd look into differentiated assignments.

I wanted to tell her that her daughter is actually right at grade level and doing fine. That I have eight kids reading below benchmark who need my attention more. But I didn't. Just smiled and said I understood her concerns.

Now I'm drafting a custom enrichment packet at 9pm. For a kid who doesn't need it. Because her mom is loud.
entries.nudge_text: Does that sit okay?
entries.response_text: Not really. I keep telling myself it's just easier this way, that pushing back would make the rest of the year harder. But mostly I think I'm scared she'll go to Principal Davis. And I hate that I care more about avoiding that conversation than standing up for what I actually think is right for the classroom.

entries.initial_entry: Staff meeting ran late. New reading assessment mandate for October. Everyone nodded along even though it means losing two instruction days. I didn't say anything either. Grabbed coffee with Lisa after—she's frustrated too but says there's no point fighting it.

The SEL committee met today and somehow I'm now also in charge of the parent workshop in November. I don't remember volunteering. Principal Davis just looked at me during the meeting and said "Sarah, you'd be great at this" and everyone turned and smiled and I heard myself saying sure.

I already run the book club. I already do morning bus duty twice a week. I don't know how to say no when everyone's looking at me like I'm the one who always says yes.

Called Mom after to vent and she just said "Sweetheart, you can't pour from an empty cup." Thanks, Mom. Very helpful.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you'd said no?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Probably nothing terrible. Someone else would've done it. But then they'd all be doing me a favor and I'd feel like I wasn't pulling my weight. It's fine. I'll figure it out.

entries.initial_entry:
Jaylen brought me a drawing today. Just a crayon picture of me and him and the classroom hamster. He said I'm his favorite teacher ever. Put it in my desk drawer with the others.

Some days that's enough. Today it was.

Sunday again. Grading. Always grading. Thinking about next week's parent conferences. Need to figure out how to tell the Millers their son might need an evaluation without them getting defensive. They're nice people, they just don't want to hear it.

Made pasta for dinner. Watched half a movie. Can't remember what it was called. Some romantic comedy. Fell asleep on the couch before it ended.

This is fine. This is just September. It always gets easier by October.

Parent conferences ran until 8pm. Smiled through all of them. Told every parent their kid was making progress, even when I had concerns, because who has the energy to have those conversations back-to-back for four hours?

The one I couldn't avoid was with Eli's dad. Had to tell him Eli's been struggling with focus and we might want to explore supports. He got quiet. Asked if I was saying something was wrong with his son. I backpedaled so fast. Said no, no, just thinking ahead, just wanting to help, never mind, he's doing great actually.

I know I should have held my ground. The kid needs help. But the look on his dad's face—I couldn't do it.
entries.nudge_text: What stopped you from holding that line?
entries.response_text: He looked scared. And hurt. And I just thought—this is his kid. His whole world. And I'm telling him something's wrong in a fluorescent classroom at 7pm on a Thursday. I couldn't be the one to make him feel worse. Even if staying quiet means Eli doesn't get the help he needs until someone else brings it up. That's on me now.

entries.initial_entry:
Caught up on emails. Planned next week's lessons. Went for a walk around the neighborhood. Nothing remarkable. Just a Saturday that felt like a Saturday for once.

Lisa texted about getting brunch tomorrow. Said yes. Probably the first social plan I've made in three weeks that isn't work-related.

Brunch with Lisa was good. We talked about school stuff but also about her sister's wedding and the trip she's planning for winter break. Normal friend things.

She asked how I was really doing. I gave the usual answer—busy, tired, fine. She just looked at me for a second and said "You know you don't have to be everything for everyone, right?"

I changed the subject. But I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I don't know how to be any other way. I don't know if I want to be, really. The kids need someone who shows up. The parents need someone who listens. Maybe that's just what teaching is. Maybe that's just what I am.
entries.nudge_text: What did you change the subject to?

entries.initial_entry:

Brunch with Lisa was good. We talked about school stuff but also about her sister's wedding and the trip she's planning for winter break. Normal friend things.

She asked how I was really doing. I gave the usual answer—busy, tired, fine. She just looked at me for a second and said "You know you don't have to be everything for everyone, right?"

I changed the subject. But I've been thinking about it all afternoon. I don't know how to be any other way. I don't know if I want to be, really. The kids need someone who shows up. The parents need someone who listens. Maybe that's just what teaching is. Maybe that's just what I am.
entries.nudge_text: What did you change the subject to?

entries.initial_entry:
Look, the pivot makes sense. Everyone at the Thursday pitch night kept asking why we dropped the furniture line when we had traction, but traction is not the same thing as momentum. We had retailers interested, sure, but the margins were getting squeezed and the whole supply chain was becoming a nightmare with shipping costs out of Shenzhen. I am not going to sit there manufacturing the same shelving unit for the next ten years just because some shops in Eindhoven like them.

Pieter thinks I pulled the trigger too fast. He said that in the meeting, in front of Anouk and Daan, which I did not appreciate. He has been with me since the Philips days and sometimes he acts like that gives him permission to question every call I make publicly. Maybe it does. I do not know. But I also know that if I had listened to the cautious voices three years ago we would still be making Bluetooth speakers nobody asked for.

The urban farming kits — there is something there. I can feel it in the conversations I am having. The market is shifting, people want to grow things, they want control over food, they want something tangible. We just need six months to prove the concept. That is not reckless. That is reading the room.
entries.nudge_text: What bothered you most about Pieter saying it?
entries.response_text: That he might be right. Not about the pivot being wrong, but about me not giving things enough time. I have heard it before from other people. It is a pattern I do not love seeing pointed out.

entries.initial_entry:
Two days of back-to-back calls with potential suppliers in Portugal and a prototype manufacturer in Ghent. My voice is shot. Ate a cheese sandwich at my desk around three and that was it until I got home and microwaved leftover stamppot that tasted like nothing.

The boys called. Sven wants to go to a music festival in Belgium and needs money. Matteo got a B+ on his physics exam and sounded genuinely proud, which made me proud too, even though I had nothing to do with it. Katrien is the one who sat with him while he studied. I was in Lisbon looking at terracotta planters.

I told Sven I would think about the festival money. What I actually thought was: I cannot keep saying yes to everything just because I feel guilty for not being there. But I will probably say yes anyway because that is what I do — throw money at the gap where my presence should be.

The Ghent manufacturer can do small runs. That is good. Keeps us lean. I should be more excited about that than I am right now. I think I am just tired.
entries.nudge_text: What would being there actually look like?
entries.response_text: I do not know. Sitting in their kitchen helping with homework? Driving Sven to football practice? It sounds so ordinary but I have built my whole life to avoid ordinary, and now ordinary is the thing I cannot reach.

entries.initial_entry:
Woke up at four again, brain already running. Kept thinking about whether the modular design should be stackable or side-by-side. Side-by-side takes more floor space but stackable has structural issues — the weight of wet soil plus the grow lights, it is not trivial. Sketched something on the back of an envelope before I even brushed my teeth.

Then my mind jumped to the lease. Studio lease is up in September. Pieter wants to renew for two years. Two years feels like a prison sentence. What if we need to move closer to a port? What if we need less space? What if we need more? I said let us do one year with an option to extend. He gave me that look. The one that says here we go again.

Had coffee with an old colleague from Philips, Joost. He is still there. Same building, same floor, different title. He seemed content but when I described what I was doing his eyes got wide and he said he could never do that. I used to find that flattering. Today it just made me feel alone.

Then back to the planters. Emailed three more suppliers. One in Valencia looks promising. Set up a video call for Friday. Kept going until my neck hurt and I realized it was already dark and I had not eaten. Ordered Thai food and ate it standing in the kitchen scrolling through competitor listings.
entries.nudge_text: Which of those things is actually keeping you up at night?
entries.response_text: The lease. Not because of the space, but because signing anything for a fixed term makes my chest tight. I know that is not rational. It is just how I am wired.

entries.initial_entry:
Katrien called. Not the boys, Katrien herself. She never does that unless something is wrong or she needs to tell me something I will not like.

Matteo has been acting out at school. Skipping classes, talking back to teachers. She thinks it started around the time I cancelled the last weekend because of the Lisbon trip. She did not say it is my fault, but she did not have to. The silence after she described what happened was louder than anything she could have said.

I sat in my car for twenty minutes after that call. Just sat there in the parking garage under the studio, engine off, staring at the concrete wall. I wanted to call Matteo but what would I say? Sorry I was looking at planters in Portugal instead of watching your football match? Sorry I keep choosing the next interesting thing over the predictable thing that happens to include you?

He is fourteen. He does not understand that I am trying to build something. Or maybe he understands perfectly and just does not think it is a good enough reason. Maybe he is right.

I am going to Utrecht this weekend. No excuses. I already told Pieter to handle the Friday call with Valencia.

Weekend in Utrecht was hard. Matteo barely looked at me. Sven was fine, acted like nothing was wrong, which somehow felt worse. We went to get frites and walked along the canal and I kept trying to say something meaningful but everything came out flat.

On Sunday I took them to a climbing wall. Matteo actually engaged for the first time. Something about the challenge of it, figuring out the next hold, the height. He was grinning at the top. For about an hour he forgot he was angry with me.

Drove home and cried in the car. Not the dramatic kind, just the quiet leaking kind where you keep driving and wiping your face.
entries.nudge_text: Why do you think the climbing wall worked?
entries.response_text: Because it was not sitting across a table pretending everything is normal. It was doing something together that required focus and a bit of nerve. Maybe that is the only language I know how to speak. Maybe Matteo is the same way and I never noticed.

entries.initial_entry:
A whole week gone and I cannot tell you what I accomplished. Meetings, emails, a prototype review that raised more questions than it answered. The Valencia supplier fell through — their minimum order is ten times what we need. Back to square one on that front.

Pieter brought up the furniture line again. Casually, over lunch, like he was just thinking out loud. "We still have the moulds," he said. "The retailers would take us back." I did not respond. Just kept eating my soup.

I spent the evening reading about vertical farming startups in Copenhagen and Stockholm. Some of them are doing incredible things. Modular, scalable, beautiful design. Part of me got fired up. Part of me thought: they are already there. You are late. You are always starting things and by the time you get somewhere, someone else has already built it better.

Anouk sent me a link to a design competition in Milan. Entry deadline is August. I opened the page, looked at the brief, felt that old kick of energy. Then I closed the tab because I know what happens — I spend three weeks on it, neglect the actual business, and end up with a beautiful submission and no revenue.

I do not know what I am doing. I mean I know what I am doing day to day. I just do not know what it adds up to.
entries.nudge_text: What would it need to add up to?

entries.initial_entry: Took a walk along the Maas after work. Watched the barges pass. Realized I have been in fight mode for weeks and forgot to just look at things. The water helped.

Pieter handed in his notice today. Twenty minutes before our morning standup, just walked into my office and put a printed letter on my desk. After eight years. Eight years of me dragging him through pivots and late nights and "trust me on this one."

He said he has taken a position at a consultancy in Amsterdam. Better pay, predictable hours, no more wondering whether the next quarter will exist. He was calm about it. Almost gentle. Said it was not personal, that he just needs stability, that he has a mortgage and a kid on the way.

I shook his hand and said I understood. And I do. I genuinely do. But the second he left my office I wanted to throw something. Not at him. At the wall, at the situation, at the fact that I am apparently the kind of person people eventually need a break from.

Anouk came in later and asked if I was okay. I said fine. She did not push it. I spent the rest of the day reorganizing the supplier spreadsheet, which did not need reorganizing, because I needed my hands to be doing something that did not require thinking about what happens next.

Katrien would say this is the cost. My mother would say this is the cost. Pieter leaving is just the latest version of someone choosing the stable path while I keep chasing whatever is around the next corner. But what is the alternative? Do what Joost does? Same floor, same desk, different title? I would rather fail at something new every two years than succeed at the same thing for twenty.

That sounds brave when I write it. It does not feel brave right now.
entries.nudge_text: What does it feel like right now?
entries.response_text: Tired, mostly. I will figure it out. I always do. Just need a few days to regroup.

entries.initial_entry: First full week of spring semester done. Managed to get through the group project meeting without making a fool of myself. The other three in my group are all in their mid-twenties. One of them, Priya, actually asked for my opinion on the policy brief outline. Small thing, but I didn't fumble the answer. Drove home to check on Mom after. She'd left the porch light on for me.

Professor Vasquez handed back the first reading response today. I got a B-minus. Fine. I expected that. But the comment in the margin said "push beyond summary -- what's YOUR position?" and I've been turning that over since 2pm. I summarized the readings accurately. I cited everything properly. The formatting was correct. I don't understand what she wants that I didn't give.

The younger students all seem comfortable throwing out half-formed opinions in class. This one guy, Marcus, will say "I think the entire framework is wrong" and then Professor Vasquez nods and says "interesting, say more." If I said something like that I'd need to be sure it was right first. You don't just declare something wrong without evidence. That's how I was taught. You do the reading, you report what it says, you don't pretend you're smarter than the author.

My ex-wife Linda used to say I was allergic to having opinions. That's not fair. I have plenty of opinions. I just don't see the point of broadcasting them when nobody asked. The paper asked me to respond to the readings. I responded. If "push beyond summary" means I'm supposed to disagree with published experts for the sake of classroom performance, then I don't know what I'm doing here.
entries.nudge_text: What would happen if you did push beyond summary?

entries.initial_entry: Group meeting went sideways. The other three want to argue in the brief that the county should eliminate paper records entirely. I think that's reckless -- I've seen what happens when digital systems crash and there's no backup. But they all agreed so fast I just nodded. Sat in the parking ramp for twenty minutes after. Should have said something. Didn't.
entries.nudge_text: You had thirty years of evidence. What stopped you?

entries.initial_entry:
Mom had a fall this weekend. Nothing broken, thank God, but she bruised her hip badly and the visiting nurse said she needs to use a walker now. Spent all of Saturday rearranging her living room furniture so she has a clear path. She kept saying not to bother, that she was fine, that she'd been walking in that house for forty-three years without help. I moved the ottoman anyway.

My sister Karen called from Phoenix to "check in" which meant asking me if I'd looked into assisted living yet. I said no because Mom doesn't want that and I'm not going to force her. Karen said I was "enabling her stubbornness." I said okay and changed the subject. It's easy to have opinions about what Mom needs when you live two thousand miles away. I could have said that but I didn't. Not worth the phone call it would turn into.

Drove back to campus Sunday night. Missed the reading for Monday's class. I'll skim it before 9am and hope she doesn't cold-call me. I keep telling myself I can handle all of this -- school, Mom, the drive, the apartment -- but weeks like this I feel like I'm running on fumes. Not complaining, just noting it. Everybody has a lot going on.

Office hours today, first time I actually went. Sat outside Professor Vasquez's door for maybe ten minutes before I knocked. She was eating a sandwich, which made me feel bad for interrupting, but she waved me in. Asked about the reading response grade, what she meant by "your position." She said something about the difference between understanding an argument and engaging with it. That you can agree with a reading and still have something to add.

I nodded a lot. She asked me what I thought about the Ostrom reading specifically and I said I thought the commons framework made sense but I'd seen it fail in county-level resource sharing. She lit up at that, said that was exactly the kind of thing she wanted in my papers. Real-world pushback grounded in experience. I said I didn't think that counted as academic.

She laughed, not unkindly. Said "Gerald, your experience IS a source." I felt something shift, not sure what. Like being told a door I thought was locked was actually just stuck. Walked back to my apartment and reread my B-minus paper. She's right, it reads like a book report. I could have written about the three times our county tried shared digital archives and what actually happened. I knew all of that. I just didn't think it was my place to say it.

Group meeting again. We're finalizing the policy brief draft this week. Priya divided the sections and I got the implementation risks portion, which is fine because that's where my actual knowledge lives. Started writing it last night and it came out faster than anything I've written for this program so far. Three pages in two hours.

I included the thing about our county's failed digital archive migration. The one where the server crashed during a property tax audit and we had to pull paper backups from a warehouse in Proctor that hadn't been climate-controlled. Lost about six weeks of records. The kind of thing that doesn't show up in policy papers because the people who lived through it don't write policy papers.

Sent the draft to the group chat. Marcus replied with a thumbs-up emoji. Priya said "this is really good, Gerald." I stared at that message for longer than I should have. It shouldn't matter this much, a twenty-six-year-old telling me my writing is good. But the work I did at the county for three decades -- nobody ever said anything about it. You filed things right, nobody noticed. You filed things wrong, everybody noticed. Priya's two words did something the county never did.
entries.nudge_text: Why does Priya's 'really good' land harder than thirty years of doing it right?
entries.response_text: Because at the county, doing it right was just the minimum. Nobody thanks you for the minimum. Priya read what I wrote and responded to it like it mattered. Like what I thought was the point, not just whether the form was filled out correctly. I didn't know I needed that. Apparently I did.

entries.initial_entry:
Karen called again. She's been talking to Mom's neighbor, Barb, behind my back. Barb apparently told Karen that Mom left the stove burner on last Tuesday and didn't notice until the pot was dry. Karen said we need to "have a family conversation" about next steps. I said I'd handle it. She said I always say that and then nothing changes.

She's not wrong. But the way she frames it -- like I'm failing at this -- makes it impossible to hear. I know Mom needs more help. I've been the one driving back every other weekend, moving furniture, filling prescriptions, bringing groceries. Karen sends a check once a month and calls that "doing her part." I could say that. I won't. It would start something that can't be unsaid and then every holiday, every phone call would have that underneath it.

So I said I'd look into some options and we'd talk next weekend. I already know what will happen. She'll send me links to places in Arizona. I'll say Mom won't leave Duluth. She'll say I'm being unreasonable. I'll fold the conversation up and put it away and nothing will change. The whole thing makes me feel like a coward, which I know isn't the right word, but I can't find a better one. I just don't want to be the person who starts the fight.
entries.nudge_text: What's the word if it's not coward?
entries.response_text: Careful, maybe. Or just tired. I don't know. Coward implies I'm scared and it's not exactly that. It's more like I've calculated the cost of every version of this conversation and none of them end well. So I pick the one where at least nobody is yelling. Even if I'm the one absorbing it all. I've been doing that math my whole life.

entries.initial_entry: Valentines Day was yesterday and I thought about Linda for the first time in a while. Not missing her exactly. Just remembering how she'd get frustrated when I wouldn't pick the restaurant. She'd say "just choose something, Gerald." I never could. Felt like if I picked wrong she'd be disappointed. Easier to let her decide and go along. She left. So maybe going along wasn't working either.

Policy brief presentation today. I was supposed to present the implementation risks section. Practiced in my apartment last night, had the whole thing mapped out, knew the material cold. Then I got to the classroom and Professor Vasquez asked who wanted to start and Marcus volunteered our group and suddenly I was standing at the front with three people half my age and a room full of strangers.

I got through it. Mostly read from my notes, which I know isn't great. My voice did that thing where it goes flat and monotone, the same voice I used to use in staff meetings at the county. Just get through it, say the correct things, don't draw attention. Priya jumped in during the Q&A and built on one of my points about legacy system failures. She cited my section like it was a published source. Professor Vasquez asked me a follow-up and I gave a real answer -- not a summary of what I'd read, an actual answer from what I know. It came out awkward but it came out.

Afterward Marcus said "dude, you killed it" and I think he was being generous but I'll take it. Something about surviving the thing you dread. Doesn't make you less afraid, just makes you slightly less certain you'll fail. Which I suppose is progress. Incremental, barely visible progress.

Long day. Drove to Duluth after class because Barb called and said Mom seemed confused about what day it was. Got there at 9pm. Mom was fine, watching Jeopardy, knew every answer in the history category. Made me a grilled cheese without me asking. I sat at the kitchen table and for a second it was like being twelve again.

Had the conversation. Told her Karen was worried, Barb was worried, I was worried. She said she was fine and that people should mind their own business. I said we might need to think about someone coming in during the day, just to help. She said "I don't need a babysitter, Gerald." I said okay. Drove back at midnight. The conversation Karen wanted me to have lasted about ninety seconds. I don't know if I tried hard enough or if trying harder would have just been cruel.
entries.nudge_text: What would trying harder have looked like?
entries.response_text: Sitting there longer, I guess. Listing reasons. Making her feel like she can't take care of herself. I don't want to do that to her. She's my mother. There's a difference between helping someone and cornering them.

entries.initial_entry:
Got the revised reading response back from Professor Vasquez. B-plus. She wrote "much better -- this is your voice" in the margin. I read that comment three times. Slid the paper into the folder where I keep things. Not sure why I'm keeping a paper folder of graded assignments like a kid bringing home a report card. But I am.

Group turned in the policy brief. Final version was solid. Priya formatted it, Marcus did the executive summary, I did the implementation risks, and our fourth member Aiden did the literature review. It reads like something a real policy shop would produce. I know that because I've read hundreds of those in my career. Nice to be on the writing end for once instead of the filing end.

Karen called. I told her about the conversation with Mom. She said ninety seconds wasn't enough and I needed to be firmer. I told her she was welcome to come to Duluth and be firm herself. There was a long pause. She said that was unfair. I said maybe. But I said it. First time in a long time I didn't just absorb what she threw and move on. Don't know if it changed anything. Probably made next month's phone call harder. But I was tired of calculating the cost of every sentence before I said it. Sometimes the math isn't worth it.
entries.nudge_text: You stopped doing the math. What did you feel instead?
entries.response_text: Lighter. Just for a second. Then immediately guilty, like I'd broken a rule I didn't know I was allowed to break. But the lighter part came first. That's new.

entries.initial_entry:
Signed Arjun up for the Math Olympiad prep course at Kumon even though his regular tuition teacher said he wasn't ready. The prep costs $480 a month on top of his existing classes and I didn't tell Ramesh yet. Not because he'd say no -- he never says no to anything for the kids -- but because I don't want to explain why I pushed for it. The tuition teacher was careful about it, said Arjun is solid but not exceptional, that maybe we should wait a year. And I heard myself saying he'll rise to the challenge, that's how we've always done it, throw him in and watch him swim.

But the car ride home was quiet. Arjun was in the backseat reading some manga and he didn't ask a single question about what just happened. He doesn't know I enrolled him. I was sitting at the traffic light near Bukit Timah and I thought, am I doing this for him or am I doing this because Kavitha aunty's son got into the Olympiad last year and I spent three months hearing about it at every family gathering. Priya brought it up at the temple too. Her Rohan this, her Rohan that.

I used to be the one people talked about. CA at twenty-three. First in the family to work at a Big Four. Now the only scoreboard I have is my children's report cards and I read them like they're my own performance reviews. Knowing something is off and stopping it are two very different things.
entries.nudge_text: What happens when Arjun finds out?
entries.response_text: He'll do what he always does. Nod, pick up the workbook, sit at the dining table after dinner. He's obedient like that. Sometimes I wish he'd push back so I'd have a reason to question whether I'm pushing too hard. But he just absorbs it, and I keep adding.

entries.initial_entry:
Amma made pongal rice this morning without being asked. Just woke up early, did the tempering with the curry leaves and cashews the way she's done it for sixty years, and had it ready by the time the kids came down. Arjun ate three servings. Even Divya, who's been on some ridiculous diet she read about on TikTok, had a full plate. And for a moment the kitchen smelled exactly like my grandmother's house in Mylapore, that particular combination of ghee and mustard seeds and the brass vessels heating up.

I sat there and I was furious. Not at Amma. At myself. Because I can't make it taste the same. I've watched her a hundred times, written the recipe down twice, and mine is always missing something. She says it's the hand -- amma kai paakkum -- that the food knows who is cooking it. Which is the most unscientific thing anyone has ever said to me and also the truest thing I've heard in years.

She's seventy-eight. Her knees are bad and she can't stand for long anymore. She did this today because Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice last week and she heard him. She heard him and she got up at five in the morning for it. I need to learn it properly this time. Not from a recipe card. From standing next to her and doing it until my hands remember.

Spent the whole morning reorganizing the prayer room shelf because Amma said the Lakshmi lamp was in the wrong position. Then drove Divya to violin, Arjun to Kumon, picked up Sanjay's cricket whites from the tailor. Calendar is a Tetris board this week.
entries.nudge_text: Was moving the lamp just another task or something else?
entries.response_text: It needed to be done. Amma asked, I did it. Not everything has to mean something.

entries.initial_entry: Ramesh asked why the credit card had a new recurring charge and I told him about Kumon. He just said okay, didn't even look up from his laptop. I don't know why I was bracing for a fight that was never going to happen. Maybe I wanted him to have an opinion about it.

Divya asked if she could skip the temple youth group meeting this Saturday for a friend's birthday party. I said no before I even thought about it. She went to her room and closed the door quietly, which was worse than slamming it.
entries.nudge_text: Why was the quiet door worse?
entries.response_text: Because slamming means she's angry and anger I can handle. Quiet means she's given up trying to negotiate. It means she already knows the answer before she asks and she's just going through the motions. I recognize it because I used to do the same thing with my own mother. Ask, get told no, retreat. I thought I'd be different but I'm running the same script.

entries.initial_entry: Pongal committee meeting at the community centre. Finalized the vendor list and stage layout. Kumaresh uncle questioned my budget for the second year in a row and I had the spreadsheet ready this time.

Arjun's first Olympiad practice test came back. Forty-second percentile. His regular tuition teacher called to check in and I could hear the I-told-you-so in her voice even though she was perfectly polite about it.
entries.nudge_text: What did you feel when you saw the score?
entries.response_text: It's one test. Baseline. That's the whole point of a baseline -- you start somewhere.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Sanjay had a cricket tournament in Jurong, two matches on Saturday and one Sunday morning. I was there for all of them, sitting in the pavilion with the other parents, making small talk about school rankings and enrichment classes. The usual tournament of its own, except we're the ones competing and the kids are just the scorecards.

Amma had a fall in the bathroom on Sunday while we were out. Nothing broken, but she bruised her hip and now she's moving even slower. Ramesh wants to talk about getting a helper but Amma won't hear of it. Says a stranger in the kitchen will disrupt the house. She means disrupt her routines -- the morning puja, the way she arranges the spice boxes, the specific order she hangs clothes on the bamboo poles. I understand it. I just don't know how much longer we can pretend she doesn't need help.
entries.nudge_text: Where do you land on the helper question?
entries.response_text: Stuck. I agree with Ramesh practically but I can't be the one who tells Amma her way of running a house isn't sustainable anymore. She built this life, this order. Taking it apart feels like telling her she's done. And she'll look at me like I've betrayed something. I'm not ready for that look.

entries.initial_entry:
Divya played her violin recital piece for us after dinner and it was genuinely beautiful. Mendelssohn. She's been grinding at it for months and tonight she played it clean, start to finish, no stops. Sanjay actually put his phone down to listen. Amma was in her chair nodding along. Ramesh recorded the whole thing on his phone.

And when she finished I clapped and told her it was wonderful and she smiled -- real smile, not the one she uses to get through family photos -- and I felt my chest crack open a little. This girl who closes doors quietly and rolls her eyes at temple and fights me on every single thing she can. She stood there with the violin under her chin and she was radiant. I wanted to tell her that but I couldn't figure out how to say it without making it about her exam grade or her college application. So I just said "beautiful, kanna" and hoped it was enough.

Arjun's Olympiad score went up to sixty-first percentile on the latest test. He showed me himself this time, brought the paper to the kitchen counter while I was chopping onions. I could tell he was pleased but trying not to make a big thing of it. I said good, keep going, and he nodded and went back to his room. Simple exchange but it sat with me.

I keep thinking about what my father used to say when I brought home my marks -- he'd scan the paper, circle whatever I got wrong, and say "next time, full marks." No praise for what I'd already done. Just the gap. I told myself I wouldn't do that to my kids but I catch myself doing a version of it. Keep going. As if where he is right now doesn't count. As if the sixty-one is just a waypoint to wherever I've decided he should end up. He looked happy at the counter. I should have said that's really good, Arjun. I should have let the moment be what it was.
entries.nudge_text: Could you still tell him?

entries.initial_entry:
Signed Arjun up for the Math Olympiad prep course at Kumon even though his regular tuition teacher said he wasn't ready. The prep costs $480 a month on top of his existing classes and I didn't tell Ramesh yet. Not because he'd say no -- he never says no to anything for the kids -- but because I don't want to explain why I pushed for it. The tuition teacher was careful about it, said Arjun is solid but not exceptional, that maybe we should wait a year. And I heard myself saying he'll rise to the challenge, that's how we've always done it, throw him in and watch him swim.

But the car ride home was quiet. Arjun was in the backseat reading some manga and he didn't ask a single question about what just happened. He doesn't know I enrolled him. I was sitting at the traffic light near Bukit Timah and I thought, am I doing this for him or am I doing this because Kavitha aunty's son got into the Olympiad last year and I spent three months hearing about it at every family gathering. Priya brought it up at the temple too. Her Rohan this, her Rohan that.

I used to be the one people talked about. CA at twenty-three. First in the family to work at a Big Four. Now the only scoreboard I have is my children's report cards and I read them like they're my own performance reviews. Knowing something is off and stopping it are two very different things.

entries.response_text: He'll do what he always does. Nod, pick up the workbook, sit at the dining table after dinner. He's obedient like that. Sometimes I wish he'd push back so I'd have a reason to question whether I'm pushing too hard. But he just absorbs it, and I keep adding.

She says it's the hand -- amma kai paakkum -- that the food knows who is cooking it. Which is the most unscientific thing anyone has ever said to me and also the truest thing I've heard in years.

She's seventy-eight. Her knees are bad and she can't stand for long anymore. She did this today because Arjun mentioned he missed her pongal rice last week and she heard him. She heard him and she got up at five in the morning for it. I need to learn it properly this time. Not from a recipe card. From standing next to her and doing it until my hands remember.

Spent the whole morning reorganizing the prayer room shelf because Amma said the Lakshmi lamp was in the wrong position. Then drove Divya to violin, Arjun to Kumon, picked up Sanjay's cricket whites from the tailor. Calendar is a Tetris board this week.
entries.nudge_text: Was moving the lamp just another task or something else?
entries.response_text: It needed to be done. Amma asked, I did it. Not everything has to mean something.

entries.initial_entry: Ramesh asked why the credit card had a new recurring charge and I told him about Kumon. He just said okay, didn't even look up from his laptop. I don't know why I was bracing for a fight that was never going to happen. Maybe I wanted him to have an opinion about it.

Divya asked if she could skip the temple youth group meeting this Saturday for a friend's birthday party. I said no before I even thought about it. She went to her room and closed the door quietly, which was worse than slamming it.
entries.nudge_text: Why was the quiet door worse?
entries.response_text: Because slamming means she's angry and anger I can handle. Quiet means she's given up trying to negotiate. It means she already knows the answer before she asks and she's just going through the motions. I recognize it because I used to do the same thing with my own mother. Ask, get told no, retreat. I thought I'd be different but I'm running the same script.

entries.initial_entry: Pongal committee meeting at the community centre. Finalized the vendor list and stage layout. Kumaresh uncle questioned my budget for the second year in a row and I had the spreadsheet ready this time.

Arjun's first Olympiad practice test came back. Forty-second percentile. His regular tuition teacher called to check in and I could hear the I-told-you-so in her voice even though she was perfectly polite about it.
entries.nudge_text: What did you feel when you saw the score?
entries.response_text: It's one test. Baseline. That's the whole point of a baseline -- you start somewhere.

entries.initial_entry:
Long week. Sanjay had a cricket tournament in Jurong, two matches on Saturday and one Sunday morning. I was there for all of them, sitting in the pavilion with the other parents, making small talk about school rankings and enrichment classes. The usual tournament of its own, except we're the ones competing and the kids are just the scorecards.

Amma had a fall in the bathroom on Sunday while we were out. Nothing broken, but she bruised her hip and now she's moving even slower. Ramesh wants to talk about getting a helper but Amma won't hear of it. Says a stranger in the kitchen will disrupt the house. She means disrupt her routines -- the morning puja, the way she arranges the spice boxes, the specific order she hangs clothes on the bamboo poles. I understand it. I just don't know how much longer we can pretend she doesn't need help.
entries.nudge_text: Where do you land on the helper question?
entries.response_text: Stuck. I agree with Ramesh practically but I can't be the one who tells Amma her way of running a house isn't sustainable anymore. She built this life, this order. Taking it apart feels like telling her she's done. And she'll look at me like I've betrayed something. I'm not ready for that look.

entries.initial_entry:
Divya played her violin recital piece for us after dinner and it was genuinely beautiful. Mendelssohn. She's been grinding at it for months and tonight she played it clean, start to finish, no stops. Sanjay actually put his phone down to listen. Amma was in her chair nodding along. Ramesh recorded the whole thing on his phone.

And when she finished I clapped and told her it was wonderful and she smiled -- real smile, not the one she uses to get through family photos -- and I felt my chest crack open a little. This girl who closes doors quietly and rolls her eyes at temple and fights me on every single thing she can. She stood there with the violin under her chin and she was radiant. I wanted to tell her that but I couldn't figure out how to say it without making it about her exam grade or her college application. So I just said "beautiful, kanna" and hoped it was enough.

Arjun's Olympiad score went up to sixty-first percentile on the latest test. He showed me himself this time, brought the paper to the kitchen counter while I was chopping onions. I could tell he was pleased but trying not to make a big thing of it. I said good, keep going, and he nodded and went back to his room. Simple exchange but it sat with me.

I keep thinking about what my father used to say when I brought home my marks -- he'd scan the paper, circle whatever I got wrong, and say "next time, full marks." No praise for what I'd already done. Just the gap. I told myself I wouldn't do that to my kids but I catch myself doing a version of it. Keep going. As if where he is right now doesn't count. As if the sixty-one is just a waypoint to wherever I've decided he should end up. He looked happy at the counter. I should have said that's really good, Arjun. I should have let the moment be what it was.
entries.nudge_text: Could you still tell him?

entries.initial_entry:
The end-of-year ceremony was today and I sat through three hours of speeches about commitment and building foundations and putting down roots. The headmaster went on about how teaching is about planting seeds and watching them grow over decades. Decades! I nearly laughed out loud. I've been here two years and already the walls feel like they're closing in. Same classroom, same view of the vineyard, same Tuesday staff meetings where Marie-Claire complains about the photocopier.

I know I'm supposed to feel grateful. The farmhouse is beautiful, the students are curious, the lavender fields smell like something from a perfume advertisement. But I keep thinking about that email from Kristina asking if I'd consider joining her school project in Kenya next spring. Teaching environmental science to kids who've never seen a glacier, helping build the curriculum from scratch. My heart raced just reading it.

Then I looked at my garden, the tomato plants I actually managed to keep alive this year, and felt this strange guilt. Like I'm betraying something by wanting to leave again. My daughter Elsa called last week and said, "Maman, don't you ever want to just... stay?" And I didn't know what to tell her because the honest answer is no. Not really. The staying is what makes me feel like I'm slowly turning to stone.
entries.nudge_text: What is it about building something from scratch that gets to you?

entries.initial_entry: Booked the flight to Nairobi for a preliminary visit in August, told the school I'm taking a sabbatical to "explore professional development opportunities," which isn't exactly a lie. The tomatoes will be fine, Elsa will roll her eyes, and I'll deal with the farmhouse question later.
entries.nudge_text: The farmhouse question?
entries.response_text: It's just logistics. Whether to rent it out or have someone check on it while I'm gone. Nothing dramatic.

entries.initial_entry:
Parent-teacher conference season again. I spent the whole afternoon choosing the right words for each report card comment. Mrs. Tan's son is struggling but she takes any criticism of him personally, so I wrote that he "shows potential for growth with additional practice." Mr. Lim expects detailed feedback, so his daughter got a full paragraph. Different families need different approaches.

Colleague Mei Ling asked why I spend so much time on these when most parents barely read them. I didn't have a good answer for her. I suppose I just feel that if I'm going to put my name on something, it should be done properly. No parent should feel embarrassed or caught off guard at the conference.
New vice-principal wants to "shake things up" with the curriculum. Suggested we try project-based learning instead of structured lessons. Everyone at the meeting nodded along. I nodded too.
entries.nudge_text: What were you thinking while you nodded?
entries.response_text: That the current curriculum has worked for fifteen years. That students need structure and clear expectations. That project-based learning sounds good in theory but creates chaos in practice. But new vice-principals need to feel heard, and nobody else was going to say anything, so why should I be the one.

entries.initial_entry:
Something happened today that's been sitting with me. During lunch, a student named Darren made a crude joke in the canteen about another teacher, Mr. Goh. The other students laughed. Some teachers nearby pretended not to hear. I was walking past with my tray.

I should have said something. Mr. Goh is a good man, near retirement, a bit old-fashioned but dedicated. Darren's joke was disrespectful. But I kept walking. Sat down at my usual table, ate my noodles, graded papers during lunch like I always do.

The thing is, if I had confronted Darren in front of everyone, it would have become a scene. The other teachers would have had to acknowledge they heard it too. It would have been awkward for Mr. Goh if he found out. Darren's parents are influential in the school community and I don't want to deal with that. So I did nothing.

I keep telling myself it was the practical choice. But Mr. Goh was in the staff room later looking tired in that way that means something happened. Maybe he did hear. Maybe I should have been the one to defend him. I don't know why I'm still thinking about this.
entries.nudge_text: What would have happened if you had said something?
entries.response_text: Everyone would have looked at me. The other teachers who ignored it would have felt judged. Darren's parents might have complained. The vice-principal might have gotten involved. And for what? The joke was already made. Mr. Goh's feelings were already hurt. Nothing I could say would undo that. But I still feel like I failed some kind of test.

entries.initial_entry:
Sunday. Called Mother at 7pm as usual. She asked about my health, about Mei's school results, about whether I'm eating properly. The same questions every week. I give the same answers. Mei is doing well. Yes I'm eating properly. Yes work is fine.

She mentioned that Cousin Huiying's son just got promoted to senior manager at some company. I said that's wonderful, congratulations to them. She waited a beat, the way she does when she wants me to say something else. I didn't say anything else. After we hung up I sat in the study for a while. The house was quiet, Ling had taken the kids to the mall. I thought about how I've been having the same conversation with my mother for thirty years. The questions change slightly but the shape of it stays the same. I ask nothing of her, she hints at things without saying them directly, we both pretend everything that needs to be said has been said. It's comfortable. Or at least it's familiar.

The Hamburg project is on hold. The city council voted to delay the commission review until March, citing budget reallocation concerns. I found out through my gallery contact, not from the committee chair who promised to keep me informed directly. That's two months of planning, three revised proposals, and a scale model my team spent five weeks fabricating.

I called Martina, the head of the cultural affairs department, and she said the delay was procedural. No guarantees about the final decision. When I asked about the competing submissions, she went quiet. There are competing submissions now. Nobody mentioned that before.

My assistant Werner suggested we could use the time to refine the proposal further. I told him the proposal doesn't need refining. What it needs is for people to honor their word. The original brief came to me because of my work in Frankfurt and Munich. Now there are suddenly other artists in the conversation.

Spent the evening alone in the studio. Didn't work on anything. Just sat with a glass of Burgundy and looked at the model. It's good work. The proportions are right, the way it catches light from the north side. Should have been straightforward.
entries.nudge_text: Why alone tonight instead of calling someone?
entries.response_text: Who would I call? Werner just tells me what I want to hear. My ex-wife would say I brought this on myself with my ego. My collector friends would offer sympathy but secretly wonder if I'm losing my touch. The wine was better company.

entries.initial_entry:
Meeting today with the Becker family about their estate commission. Third generation textile money, building a new country house in Baden-Württemberg. They want something for the entrance courtyard. Five meters minimum, abstract but with classical proportions. Budget is essentially open.

Herr Becker junior did most of the talking. Young man, maybe thirty-five, clearly trying to establish himself as the decision-maker now that his father has stepped back. He kept referencing installations he'd seen in Basel and New York. Wanted to know my thoughts on incorporating water features. I told him bronze and water require careful engineering, which adds six months minimum to the timeline.

His wife seemed more interested in the aesthetic conversation. She asked good questions about patina development over decades. I showed them photos of my Frankfurt piece from 2008 and how it's aged. She understood immediately what I was explaining about surface evolution.

Left the meeting feeling solid about the project. Private commissions like this are cleaner. One family, one vision, one contract. No committees, no budget votes, no competing proposals appearing from nowhere. The fee will be substantial. I'm already thinking about how to spend the spring - perhaps extend the Mallorca stay into April this year.
entries.nudge_text: Which of them do you think will actually decide?
entries.response_text: The wife. Herr Becker junior wants to prove he's running things, but she's the one with the eye. He'll defer to her judgment on anything visual. I've seen this dynamic before with these families. The money gives orders, but taste steers the ship. I'll make sure my next presentation speaks to what she responded to.

entries.initial_entry: Christmas dinner at my sister's in Cologne. Too many relatives, too much noise. Drove back late and opened a 2015 Barolo I'd been saving.

Received the formal letter from Hamburg today. The commission has been opened to a juried competition. My original invitation as the presumptive artist is now simply one submission among what they expect will be twelve to fifteen proposals. They frame it as fairness and transparency. I frame it as cowardice.

I built relationships with that cultural department for eight years. Hosted their delegation when they visited my Munich installation. Introduced Martina to collectors who later funded her gallery renovation. This is how it works in this world - you invest in people, build mutual obligation, and when opportunities arise, those investments pay returns. Now some junior bureaucrat has decided that giving me what I earned looks like favoritism. I may withdraw entirely. Let them have their competition with their fifteen proposals from artists who haven't done a single public piece over ten meters.
entries.nudge_text: What would withdrawing actually prove?
entries.response_text: That I don't need to compete for something that should have been mine by right. That my reputation speaks without having to perform for a jury of people who probably couldn't name three working sculptors. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'm just angry and this is how it comes out. The Becker commission will pay better anyway.

entries.initial_entry:
Had dinner with Joachim, an old friend from my academy days. He never pursued public commissions - works entirely through private galleries and collectors. Smaller pieces, more experimental. He mentioned he spent November in Portugal, just making work in a rented studio with no deadlines, no clients, no obligations. Came back with a series he's genuinely excited about.

I found myself envying that. Not the smaller scale or the lesser income, but the freedom from always positioning. Every conversation I have lately is a negotiation. Who gets credit, who controls the timeline, who has final say. Even the Becker project - I'm already calculating how to manage the son's ego while cultivating the wife's trust. Joachim just makes things. When did I stop doing that?
entries.nudge_text: Could you actually work that way now?
entries.response_text: I don't know. The studio, the assistants, the Mallorca house - they all require the income that comes from the positioning. But it's not just about money. I'm not sure I'd know what to make if nobody was watching. Joachim's work comes from somewhere inside him. Mine comes from briefs and site specifications. Maybe that's fine. Maybe that's just who I became. The question bothers me more than I expected.

entries.initial_entry:
New Year's Eve at the Düsseldorf Kunstverein event. Good turnout, the usual faces. I wore my grey Zegna and the Patek I bought myself after the Munich commission. People came over to talk, asked about my projects, laughed at my stories about difficult clients. The museum director mentioned they're planning a retrospective series next year featuring regional artists. My name came up in their planning meeting.

Drove home after midnight feeling satisfied. This is what the work is for - not just the objects themselves but the standing they create. The ability to walk into a room and have people know who you are and what you've accomplished. Joachim wouldn't understand this. He'd find it exhausting or empty. But there's genuine satisfaction in being recognized. I've earned that standing through decades of difficult work. Tonight felt like confirmation.

Spent the day in the studio actually working. Not managing, not planning, not reviewing contracts. Put on old clothes and spent six hours with clay, building maquettes for the Becker piece. Three different approaches to the water feature question - one that incorporates it, one that acknowledges the site's water table through the form without literal water, one that ignores it entirely.

My hands remembered things my mind had forgotten. The resistance of material, the way a form wants to go one direction but you have to convince it to go another. Werner came in at some point to ask about scheduling and I didn't even look up, just told him we'd discuss it tomorrow. By evening my back hurt and my shoulders were stiff and I felt better than I have in weeks. Opened a good Riesling and sat looking at what I'd made.
entries.nudge_text: What made today different from usual?
entries.response_text: No audience. Nothing to prove to anyone. Just me and the clay and the problem of how to fill that courtyard space. The Becker meeting, the Hamburg situation, even the Kunstverein evening - there's always a performance involved. Today was just doing the thing itself. I'd forgotten how much I used to need that. Maybe still do.

entries.initial_entry:
Presentation to the Beckers today. Brought all three maquettes. Herr Becker junior immediately gravitated toward the most literal interpretation - the water feature, the dramatic scale, the impressive engineering. He started talking about how visitors would experience it, the Instagram moments, the way it would announce the family's arrival into a new era.

His wife was quiet for a long time. Then she pointed to the smallest maquette - the one where the form echoes the water table beneath without any actual water. She said it felt inevitable. Like it belonged to the land rather than being imposed on it. Her husband looked irritated for a moment, then asked what I thought.

I told them the truth: the water feature would be magnificent and expensive. The subtle piece would be better art. I could see Herr Becker calculating - the subtle piece was also a third of the budget. For a moment I wondered if I'd just talked myself out of substantial money.

In the end they went with the subtle piece. The wife's choice. I'm genuinely pleased, which surprises me. A year ago I would have steered them toward the bigger commission without hesitation. The income loss is real but manageable. And I'll actually enjoy making this one. That matters more than I thought it would.
entries.nudge_text: What changed in the last year?
entries.response_text: Maybe nothing changed. Maybe I just got old enough to notice what was already there. The Hamburg situation reminded me that all the positioning and relationship-building can evaporate overnight. The status game has diminishing returns after a certain point. I have enough. The studio, the houses, the reputation - it's all established. What I don't have enough of is days like that one in January, alone with clay and no audience. The Becker piece might give me more of those.

entries.initial_entry:
Jia Hui came home with her spelling test today. 18 out of 20. The teacher wrote "Good effort!" at the top with a smiley face. I smiled and told her I was proud, put it on the fridge with a magnet.

But my mother-in-law saw it during dinner and said "Wah, only 18? Last time your husband always got full marks." I watched Jia Hui's face fall and I wanted to say something, anything, but I just changed the subject to the soup being too salty. I know she means well. I know comparison is how she shows she cares. Still scrubbing the wok now and can't stop thinking about it.

Registered Jia Hui for the enrichment center near the MRT. Three sessions a week, $800 a month. Told myself it's an investment, not an expense, but I keep opening the banking app to look at the balance.
entries.nudge_text: What would it take for you to feel okay about that $800?
entries.response_text: I don't know. Maybe if she comes home one day and gets full marks on something. Or maybe if I stopped needing her to prove that I made the right call leaving work. It's not really about the money, is it? It's about whether I'm doing enough. Whether staying home was worth it. Whether six years from now I'll look back and think I did right by her or I just threw away my career for nothing.

entries.initial_entry:
Ming keeps having nightmares. Third time this week I've been up at 2am holding him while he cries about "the shadow monster." Wei Hong sleeps through it all. I don't wake him anymore because he has that big presentation tomorrow, the one that might lead to the department head promotion.

I'm running on four hours of sleep and I still have to get both kids ready, meal prep for the week, and somehow fit in that phonics practice with Jia Hui because the enrichment teacher said she's "a bit behind the others." Behind. She's six. My hands are shaking from the coffee and I haven't even brushed my teeth yet.
entries.nudge_text: What does "behind" mean to you right now?
entries.response_text: It means I'm failing. It means other mothers are doing more flashcards, more reading, more something. It means by the time she sits for PSLE she'll already be playing catch-up. Everyone says don't worry, kids develop at their own pace, but then why does every other parent have their child in two or three different tuition classes? You can't have it both ways. Either it matters or it doesn't.

entries.initial_entry:
Parent-teacher conference today. Teacher said Jia Hui is "well-adjusted" and "a pleasure to have in class." Also said her math is strong but reading comprehension needs work. Suggested we read together more at home.

I nodded and wrote it all down in my notebook, the one I keep for tracking both kids' progress. Wei Hong couldn't come because of work, so I'll brief him tonight. Already ordered three new comprehension workbooks from Popular. Express delivery.

On the way home I saw one of the other mothers from the enrichment center. Her daughter is in Primary 2 already doing Primary 4 assessment books. She asked which tuition center we use for math and I lied and said we were still "exploring options." Didn't want to admit we only do the one enrichment class because I'm paranoid about the budget.

We have enough. I know we have enough. Wei Hong's bonus came in last month, we maxed out the CPF contributions, the emergency fund has eight months of expenses now. But I keep thinking about what happens if he loses his job, or if one of the kids needs medical treatment not covered by insurance, or if prices keep going up and suddenly eight months becomes four.

My mother used to say "save for nine winters" and I always thought it was superstition. Now I understand. You never know which winter will be the long one.
entries.nudge_text: You said you have enough. Do you believe it?
entries.response_text: Enough is relative. We have enough for now. But things change. I'd rather be overprepared than caught off guard. That's just practical, not paranoid. Can we talk about something else?

entries.initial_entry: Christmas gathering at my sister's place yesterday. All the cousins comparing which schools their kids got into, which scholarships they're aiming for. Jia Hui sat in the corner playing with her cousin's old toys while the adults talked about tuition fees and percentile rankings. I should have pulled her into the conversation. I should have let her be a child.
Wei Hong asked if we really need the third workbook. Said Jia Hui looked tired this week. I told him I'm the one here every day, I see what she needs. He dropped it. Maybe I shouldn't have snapped.
entries.nudge_text: What do you think she needs?
entries.response_text: The same thing every kid needs. To be prepared. To not struggle later. He doesn't see how the other kids are doing, he doesn't sit in those parent WhatsApp groups reading about everyone's tutors and schedules. He gets to be the fun parent who comes home for dinner. I'm the one who has to make sure she's ready for the real world.

entries.initial_entry:
New year. Everyone posting their resolutions online. "More quality time with family." "Less screen time." "Be present." I used to make those lists too. Detailed ones, with deadlines and KPIs like I was still writing performance reviews.

This morning Jia Hui asked if we could just play today. No workbooks, no practice. Just play. And I said yes. We built a blanket fort and read picture books inside it while Ming napped. She laughed so hard at the silly voices I made for the characters. For two hours I forgot about enrichment schedules and comprehension scores and whether we're saving enough.

Then tonight, putting her to bed, she said "Mama, today was the best day." And I don't know why but I started crying after I closed her door. Is this what I've been missing? Is this what she needed all along? I keep score so carefully but I'm not sure I'm measuring the right things anymore.
entries.nudge_text: What would measuring "the right things" look like?
entries.response_text: Maybe it's not about measuring at all. Maybe that's the problem. I spent so many years in a job where everything had metrics, targets, quarterly reviews. I brought all of that home with me. Milestone charts. Developmental benchmarks. Comparison tables. But she's not a project. She's a person. A small person who just wants her mother to be there, actually there, not checking boxes in her head. I don't know how to stop keeping score. But today felt like the first time in months I wasn't playing a game where I'm always losing.

entries.initial_entry:
